title: ‘The explosion is what I remember’: man nearly sucked out of Ryanair plane recounts ordeal | Air transport | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Ljubisa Karović tells Guardian the fact that he lived to tell the tale is nothing short of a miracle
Author: Helena Smith
Headline: Exclusive: Ljubisa Karović tells Guardian the fact that he lived to tell the tale is nothing short of a miracle
Content: ‘I don’t want anyone to suffer what happened to me’: Ljubisa Karović on the porch of his rented garden flat in Katerini, Greece.Photograph: Helena Smith/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘I don’t want anyone to suffer what happened to me’: Ljubisa Karović on the porch of his rented garden flat in Katerini, Greece.Photograph: Helena Smith/The GuardianAir transport‘The explosion is what I remember’: man nearly sucked out of Ryanair plane recounts ordealExclusive: Ljubisa Karović tells Guardian the fact that he lived to tell the tale is nothing short of a miracleHelena Smithin KateriniTue 21 Jul 2026 14.26 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIt is the explosion just before he was nearly sucked out of the aircraft that Ljubisa Karović remembers. The thunderous noise of the window shattering that roused him from slumber in 11F, his window seat on the early morningRyanairflight from Greece to Germany.Eleven days and 11 nights later, it remains the prelude “to the chaos, the catastrophe” that keeps him awake at night.“The explosion is what I remember,” says the Serbian entrepreneur in an interview with the Guardian.His head supported by a neck brace, his eyes staring into the middle distance, he adds: “It’s the noise before the chaos, the noise that is always there when I close my eyes to sleep.”Pain still haunts Ljubisa Karović, too, and it is easy to understand why. The burns and bruises livid on his right arm, ascending to his armpit and chest, are testimony to a fact that few will forget: that the 61-year-old was sucked out of an aircraft window – dislodged mid-flight in circumstances still under investigation – and survived.“I am lucky,” he says with some understatement as he swats a fly on the porch of the unassuming villa he has rented in Katerini, the booming coastal resort outside Thessaloniki. “I don’t remember much and my head and neck still hurt but I am still alive. It could have been the seatbelt, the fact that I was still strapped in. But maybe it is fate. I believe in God and I thank him every day.”Barely 100 hours have elapsed since Karović was discharged from hospital. The shock, he says, was overwhelming but the fact that he can tell the tale at all is nothing short of a miracle. His Greek lawyer, Vasilis Tsiaras, describes the wiry father of two as “being alive by mistake”.Woman speaks out on husband being pulled from Ryanair plane – video“Ljubisa dipped in and out of death,” he says. “When the cabin lost pressure he was hurled out of the window from his chest up and immediately lost consciousness. He was on that plane heading to Germany to celebrate his brother’s birthday. Instead, at 15,000ft he ended up with his head and right arm outside the aircraft as it moved at a speed of more than 600km/h. Imagine that! He looked at death, that other world, and came back.”Passengers on flight FR1879, including Karović’s wife, Svetlana, seated in 14C, three rows behind her husband,still have vivid recollections of the moment the window smashed, the oxygen masks coming down, the commotion that ensued as bottles and other objects flew through the air and fellow passengers grabbing Ljubisa, desperately trying to hold him in place.“I rushed over too. None of the cabin crew helped. It was the passengers,” she says. “One man, I think he was an Albanian, was incredible. He did all he could to bring him in and then tried to block the window, first with a bag that was immediately sucked out and then with a suitcase, which worked. We are eternally grateful to him.”The unbearable moments of seeing her husband “half in and out of the plane” are thought to have lasted between one-and-a-half and two minutes.“They tell me I fainted twice and again inside the plane and that my face was briefly swollen and deformed because of the pressure, the rush of air,” says the softly spoken Karović. “My wife reminds me about that.”Ryanair has defended the performance of its staff saying cabin crew did a “phenomenal job”. Tsiaras, the lawyer, takes issue with this. While Karović’s son, Danjell, who booked the flight, has been contacted by the company, neither Ljubisa nor Svetlana have been called or received any assistance or support, he says.View image in fullscreenA broken window and the interior of the Ryanair aircraft.Photograph: Despoina Papapavlou/Reuters“We have no complaint with the pilot, who followed the safety protocols and dealt with the situation very well,” adds Tsiaras referring to the rapid descent they are forced to make in the event of a plane losing cabin pressure. “My client fainted two or three times, once outside the aircraft when he was partially sucked out of the window, and twice inside when, again, he lost consciousness. At no point did the cabin crew offer to help.”Ryanair disputed that claim, saying that during the process of depressurisation and rapid decent to lower altitude, all passengers and cabin crew are required to fasten seatbelts and put on oxygen masks. “As soon as the aircraft descended to a safe altitude our cabin crew attended to and assisted Mr Karović, moving him to alternate seats (beside his wife) and arranged for a doctor onboard to sit with them until the aircraft landed safely back in Thessaloniki. They also arranged to have medical assistance standing by on landing in Thessaloniki,” the airline said in a statement to the Guardian.Citing witness testimony, Tsiaras claimed the cabin crew personnel were paralysed by fear, perhaps brought on by the belief that with the dramatic loss in altitude the aircraft was about to crash.“Everyone has agreed that the crew was in a state of shock, afraid that the plane was about to crash. It was the other passengers who rallied and gave Ljubisa water,” he says.The owner of an aluminium factory based in Serbia that for years has exported goods far and wide, Karović, long a frequent flyer, recoils at the thought of travelling by plane again.Doctors have warned that recovery will be slow, ordering him to wear the neck brace for at least six weeks when they will determine if surgery is necessary. “There are images that come back, the noise of the explosion, the chaos, but I want to recover,” he says. “And, yes, there should be compensation too. Not only emails that have focused on rebooking the flight.”Asked if there is a message he wants to convey, he allows himself to break into a smile. “Always make sure to wear your seatbelt. I don’t want anyone to suffer what happened to me.”This article was amended on 21 July 2026. An earlier version incorrectly said Ljubisa Karović is the only man to have been sucked out of an aircraft window and survived. In 1990, a British Airways pilot, Timothy Lancaster, also survived after being blasted halfway through a cockpit windscreen that blew out over Oxfordshire.Explore more on these topicsAir transportRyanairAirline industrynewsShareReuse this content

title: Could the World Cup be back in the US as soon as 2038? Absolutely | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Explosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue goes along with a desire by Fifa to recoup lost TV income in future deals
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: Explosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue goes along with a desire by Fifa to recoup lost TV income in future deals
Content: Gianni Infantino (left) and Donald Trump carry the World Cup trophy to the Spain team.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenGianni Infantino (left) and Donald Trump carry the World Cup trophy to the Spain team.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Could the World Cup be back in the US as soon as 2038? AbsolutelyExplosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue goes along with a Fifa desire to recoup lost TV income in future dealsMatt Hughesin New YorkTue 21 Jul 2026 15.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAs is often the case, it has been left to Donald Trump to say the quiet bit out loud. “We have to do this again and we have to do it while I’m around, you hear that Gianni?” the US president told Fox News in an interview broadcast in the buildup to theWorld Cupfinal.An American bid for the 2038 World Cup has been the talk of the hotel lobbies populated by administrators from around the world over the past month, who have watched this country’s seemingly limitless appetite for major events and willingness to pay whatever it takes to attend them.After this World Cup, here’s what I feel: a kind of messy hope | Jonathan LiewRead moreFifa has been quietly, but actively encouraging a US bid for some time and in a meeting at the Waldorf Astoria on Park Avenue in New York on Saturday it became clearer why.The Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, told representatives of the governing body’s 211 member associations that its revenue projections for the current four-year cycle has increased from $11bn (£8.2bn) to $15bn, with the vast majority of the growth coming from an explosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue.A Fifa source disclosed that such has been the demand in the US in particular that ticketing and hospitality sales have gone past $5bn, with that part of the market now worth more than the World Cup’s TV rights for the first time.“Fifa will want to get back here as quickly as possible,” said a senior figure at one of its hospitality partners.While ticket sales have exceeded expectations, helped by the dynamic pricing and secondary market resale models that are embedded in US culture, in one crucial respect Fifa left a huge amount of money on the table, another reason it is desperate to return.In an extraordinary deal, Fox Sports was awarded English-language TV rights in the US by Fifa without a bidding process, a precautionary move by the world governing body to prevent a lawsuit when Qatar 2022 was moved to the winter after the TV contracts had already been signed, creating a direct clash with the US domestic sports season, particularly the NFL and NBA.With no competitive tension, Fox was given a relative bargain, paying $485m for the 2026 rights, the same as in Qatar, despite the World Cup having an extra 40 matches and taking place on home soil. For context, the NFL’s domestic TV deal is worth $11bn a year, with the NBA bringing in around $7bn.Fox has capitalised this summer as a result, on the back of bumper viewing, with industry experts predicting it will make more than $1bn from TV adverts, whose volume and value has increased due to the addition of hydration breaks.Fifa is determined to recoup what many regard as lost income from the US market in future TV rights cycles.Having watched with frustration as Fox has cashed in, ESPN and NBC are expected to bid for the 2030 tournament, while initial conversations have also taken place with Netflix about them joining in the auction.View image in fullscreenHigh prices did not deter fans from packing out stadiums at the World Cup in the US.Photograph: Julio Cortez/APNetflix has already signalled its growing interest in football by buying American rights for the Women’s World Cup in 2027 and 2031, the latter being held in the US, and another men’s tournament seven years later would spark another boom.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionNo country has hosted the World Cup twice in 12 years, but Fifa’s statutes and recent staging decisions give it the flexibility for this to happen.By taking the 2030 World Cup to three continents and six countries – Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay – before Saudi Arabia hosts in 2034, a North American bid for 2038 is permitted under Fifa’s confederation rotation policy, along with one from Oceania.Soccer Australia had wanted to bid for 2034, but after being given less than a month to formally register its interest by Fifa, withdrew its candidacy leaving Saudi Arabia to be selected unopposed, Infantino’s preferred method.To complicate matters slightly, the North American confederation, Concacaf, is understood to have reservations about another US bid as it wants to give opportunities to other members.Costa Rica, Jamaica and Mexico have been added to the 2031 Women’s World Cup bid and another joint endeavour could emerge, although unsurprisingly Trump would prefer the US to be sole hosts. “Based on the numbers we will be requesting it again immediately,” he told Fox.Fifa’s timeline for opening the 2038 process is not yet known, but history suggests it could move quickly to secure the outcome it wants. The sudden opening of the 2034 bidding process in October 2024 took most confederations by surprise with Infantino announcing that Saudi Arabia would be the hosts just 25 days later, a decision ratified by all Fifa members at a bizarre virtual extraordinary general meeting two months later.Infantino will be re-elected for a fourth full term at next year’s Fifa Congress in Morocco, almost certainly unopposed, and then all eyes will turn to the 2038 contest.Barring what would be an unprecedented change to the US constitution to permit an additional term, Trump is due to leave office in January 2029, which may quickly become the deadline for Fifa.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupUS sportsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Big, brave Nige appears in public again with aimless speech to a few mega-fans | John Crace | The Guardian
description: Reform leader’s recycled moanathon included digs at Kemi, the media, immigrants and new PM. But no questions, of course
Author: John Crace
Headline: Reform leader’s recycled moanathon included digs at Kemi, the media, immigrants and new PM. But no questions, of course
Content: ‘Today was all about finding his feet. Indulging the narcissistic wound that would never heal. Hearing the live applause again.’Photograph: Joe Giddens/PAView image in fullscreen‘Today was all about finding his feet. Indulging the narcissistic wound that would never heal. Hearing the live applause again.’Photograph: Joe Giddens/PAThe politics sketchNigel FarageBig, brave Nige appears in public again with aimless speech to a few mega-fansJohn CraceReform leader’s recycled moanathon included digs at Kemi, the media, immigrants and new PM. But no questions, of courseTue 21 Jul 2026 11.36 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 16.51 EDTShareIs it safe? Is it safe? For the past couple of months,Nigel Faragehas been like Laurence Olivier in Marathon Man, wondering if he can come out of hiding. Tucked away in one of his five homes, living on handouts from benefactors and hoping the coast is clear. Free from troublesome reporters who insist on asking awkward questions about where his money comes from and what it’s for.It’s tough to be the Man of the People when some of your money is coming from a posh convicted fraudster. Then again, Nige is a man who seems quick to forgive criminal behaviour. But – slowly, slowly – he is beginning to show his face in public again. And on Tuesday he took the latest steps on his Long Walk to Freedom.Fed up with posting videos from random fields on social media, Nige was now ready to face a crowd of about 200 mega-fans in the garden of Kimberley Hall, a Norfolk country house often used as a wedding venue. For a speech, mind. Nothing more. There were to be no questions of any description. He might need his lawyers present for that.This was just baby steps. The first part of a lengthy rehab plan. Getting his story straight about what the £5m was actually for would come later. Especially now sources say he told senior figures in Reform UK hewould need “a million a year”to cover lost earnings if he stood for parliament in the 2024 general election. So today was all about finding his feet. Indulging the narcissistic wound that would never heal. Hearing the live applause again, for a dashed-off speech of little consequence. His bid to show he still counted for something, even when all eyes were on the new prime minister.First, the warm-up act: Colin Sutton, the new Norfolk police and crime commissioner. A man apparently hell bent on proving he was a lot stupider than he looked. All Colin – a former high-ranking police officer – seemed to care about was stopping equality and inclusion training. As if that alone would restore people’s trust in the police. He also managed to confuse the Lib Dems with the Tories before welcoming Nige to the platform. “I don’t know much about policing,” he said. “But I do know he’s an innocent man.” Phew. That’s good to know.On came Nige, relieved to have been given a pardon. Though he looked a bit bored. As if he couldn’t quite work out why he had agreed to do this. None of the main broadcasters were that interested, and he was just talking to a handful of the converted. The only plus side was that at least there was no sign of Count Binface. That would have been a humiliation too far.Life has not been kind to Nige recently. Not so long ago, he had been riding high. Leading the polls, being taken seriously as the next prime minister. Now it was turning to dust as details of his financial affairs began to unravel. Nige always seemed to have believed that scrutiny was for other politicians, not him. Worst of all, he had been the architect of his own downfall. He had been the one toresign his seat in Clactonto engineer a showdown with a comedian wearing a dustbin on his head.Nige began with a requiem for Keir Starmer. It had been Reform wot dun it. Reform that brought down the prime minister. It was also, unwittingly, a requiem for himself. Proof that you sometimes have to be careful what you wish for. Because in Andy Burnham, Reform now has a far tougher opponent. Someone who sounds credible. Someone to whom most of the country will give a decent hearing. You could almost hear the regret in Farage’s voice. Maybe he should have found a way of letting Starmer carry on as a lame duck leader.“We need a general election now,” he said. But do we? That doesn’t seem to be what most of the country wants. Burnham had become prime minister without a vote. Hmm. Much like Nige appointing himself leader of Reformweeks before the last election. Dicky Tice, everyone’s least favourite beta male, just rolled over when Nige told him he was no longer in charge. “Whatever you say, Nigel,” Dicky may have simpered. The latest in a never-ending stream of humiliations.The rest of the speech was just a reprise of some of Nige’s greatest hits. The sort of thing you used to be able to pay him £80 forin a personalised Cameo video. Nige didn’t even bother to pretend his heart was in it. This was Nige-by-Numbers. A man who sounded like he couldn’t wait to be somewhere else. He just wasn’t quite sure where. Everywhere he finds himself now seems wrong.There was a random attack on the Tories. Andthe rightwing press. Nige can’t forgive the Telegraph and the Daily Mail for talking about the Kemi bounce and for even mentioning his financial difficulties. It was also outrageous that the media kept bringing up George Cottrell’s time in prison on a money-laundering charge. What was wrong with offering to help out a few major drug dealers? Surely it was far worse that Lou Haigh had failed to report she had found her stolen phone. That was the sort of crime for which people deserved to go down.As the aimless ramble went on, the audience began to get as bored as Nige. Had they really made an effort to come to this? Then we had the “I’m not a racist, but” section. Nige loved immigrants. He really did. No one could love immigrants more. It was just a shame they were all criminals. London was now a no-go zone. No one dared to wear a watch any more, which is presumably why Nige’s own event had started 20 minutes late. Though it didn’t really explain how so many leading rightwing politicians had turned upat a conference in Londonthe week before without being mugged.Now things became desperate. Because Nige’s final complaint was that Burnham had used his notes 266 times during his half-hour acceptance speech for the Labour leadership. I mean, who cares? Surely what he said was what really counted. But Nige was having none of it. Style was all. Then again, none of us would really need notes to have made the speech he had just given. I’ve heard it so many times, I could have made it for him. Nige’s Mega Recycled Moanathon. He increasingly sounds like a man out of ideas. Whose time has come and gone. By the time he came to an end, half the crowd had beaten him to the exit.Explore more on these topicsNigel FarageThe politics sketchReform UKcommentShareReuse this content

title: ‘The Kardashian era is over’: TikTok’s ‘povvo king’ takes on the billionaires | Television | The Guardian
description: Shabaz Ali stormed to TikTok fame mocking the designer ice cubes of the privileged. Now he’s on the BBC stoking revolt against the superrich
Author: Michael Hogan
Headline: Shabaz Ali stormed to TikTok fame mocking the designer ice cubes of the privileged. Now he’s on the BBC stoking revolt against the superrich
Content: ‘People are so sick of wealth inequality’…Shabaz Ali.Photograph: Georgie WildingView image in fullscreen‘People are so sick of wealth inequality’…Shabaz Ali.Photograph: Georgie WildingTelevision‘The Kardashian era is over’: TikTok’s ‘povvo king’ takes on the billionairesShabaz Ali stormed to TikTok fame mocking the designer ice cubes of the privileged. Now he’s on the BBC stoking revolt against the superrichMichael HoganTue 21 Jul 2026 09.26 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIt was ice cubes that tipped Shabaz Ali over the edge. “A woman online spent her entire day making content about ‘designer ice cubes’,” he recalls. “She’d freeze flavoured waters or fruit juices and show the whole process in tedious detail. I was like, ‘There’s no way she’s got a proper job or has ever struggled for money. Only someone wealthy would have the time to do this.’ It vexed me, so on a whim, I made a reaction video saying ‘On today’s episode of I’m Rich, You’re Poor …’ Soon my TikToks were getting 30m views and thousands of comments. It blew my mind.”Ali, AKA @ShabazSays, has since become a cult celebrity for his sarcastic commentary on how the other half live. His scathing takedowns of the “rich and ridiculous” have won him more than 4 million followers. Now he’s transferring his viral fame to our TV screens.It all began when former chemistry teacher Ali, 32, took to his bedroom in Blackburn during lockdown and amused himself by mocking the smugly privileged. In his crosshairs were those who humblebrag about their perfect lives and woo-woo habits. Tongue firmly in cheek, he styled himself as “King of the Povvos” and leader of the “#PovvoGang”.Allow TikTok content?This article includes content provided byTikTok. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continue“It was reclaiming an insult,” he says. “I grew up working class and heard ‘povvo’ so much. The community we’ve built is warm, honest and self-deprecating. They’re saying, ‘We don’t have much but at least we’re here’. Povvo is a badge of honour now.”The disconnect between influencers and their audience is increasingly jarring. “They post about bespoke Bentleys and private jets. Who are you making this for? [US socialite] Becca Bloom really bothers me. Millions of people watch her cats eating out of £20,000 bowls. She has a £100k Louis Vuitton trunk full of Hermès bags. Yet her followers genuinely think they’re friends.”In Ali’s corner of the internet, a backlash was brewing: “People were tagging me in dozens of videos per day, pointing out tone-deaf displays of wealth.” He calls it “a movement” that is helping to move the dial on unrealistic standards. “There’s been a massive shift in the last few years,” says Ali. “The Kardashian era is over. We don’t want airbrushed reality any more. We want it raw, authentic and unfiltered.”double quotation markThe illusion of working hard and getting rewarded has been shattered. Young people dream of opulence but can’t have itIn these days of doomscrolling, aspirational figures fill a void. “As a teacher, I saw how social media warped teenagers’ perception of the world. Wealth influencers, trad wives and Andrew Tate provide something that’s missing from their lives. The illusion of working hard and getting your reward has been shattered. They’re seeing their parents and teachers struggle. Young people dream of opulence but can’t have it. They’d say, ‘I don’t need to study, I’m going into business. Richard Branson didn’t do his GCSEs.’ I was like, ‘He’s one person!’ There are 3,400 billionaires in a global population of 8.3 billion. That’s a lot of disappointed people.”Amid the cost-of-living crisis, his videos provided a reality check. “I was telling my students, ‘This lavish stuff online isn’t right. It’s not real’. That’s why I started taking the mickey. It snowballed.”His BBC documentary, Shabaz Asks: Are Billionaires Bad?, finds Ali investigating extreme wealth and its real-world impact. With his trademark playful wit, he asks whether the mega-rich are a symptom of a broken culture or if they can be a force for good.Allow TikTok content?This article includes content provided byTikTok. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueWealth hoarding gives him “the ick”. “What annoys me most is that the gap is widening,” he says. “We’re getting more billionaires, who are finding more tax loopholes and ways to hoard assets. I genuinely think 90% of them don’t care. You can’t be a billionaire and be a good person.”In a bid to understand more, he attempts to meet a billionaire in person. Ali approaches the Kardashians, Beckhams, Trumps, Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos and Beyoncé for an interview. “Kim K definitely saw my message. She didn’t reply, though. A couple of people hearted it. That was it.”double quotation markI grew up working class and heard ‘povvo’ so much. The community we’ve built is warm, honest and self-deprecatingInstead he meets a range of people who are both pro and anti-billionaire. These include the Green party’s Zack Polanski. “He’s the only person on the party political spectrum who’s speaking out about billionaires,” says Ali. “Everybody else is too busy trying to protect their donors. Many of his arguments resonated. I’m a lifelong Labour voter but might I vote Green now? Possibly.”He spends time with Apprentice winner turned “eight-figure entrepreneur” Joseph Valente (“fascinating character but he uses a lot of hairspray”). He flies to the Colorado ski resort of Vail to stay with Phones 4U founder John Caudwell. “I hate billionaires but I liked John, which was hard,” admits Ali. “I just think he’s unaware of his own power and privilege. He told me, ‘You can’t whisper in the ears of governments. I had a meeting with Keir Starmer and he didn’t take my advice.’ The fact that he even said that sentence! You got into that room because of money. The prime minister might not have taken your advice but there’s someone whose advice he did take. I admire John’s philanthropy but fundamentally we still disagree. Your rich friends are still wankers, mate!”View image in fullscreen‘Fascinating character but he uses a lot of hairspray’ … with Apprentice winner Joseph Valente.Photograph: BBC/Hello Mary LimitedAli takes the opportunity to poke around inside Caudwell’s kitchen, hoping to find one of the vast “curated” fridges he satirises online. “I genuinely thought I was going to get the internet lifestyle with everything neat and labelled but it was like a normal person’s fridge. It was dirtier than mine!” Did making the film challenge Ali’s views? “It didn’t alter my beliefs, it cemented them. I still think we don’t need billionaires and they should be doing more for the rest of us.”He’s told that if we make billionaires pay more tax, they will leave the UK and take their wealth with him. “Let them go!” he says. “Let’s look over the hill and see if the grass is greener. It’s just scare tactics. They ain’t going anywhere. They’ve benefited too much from this country and have too much of a stake here to suddenly leave. Tax them, I say. Tax diamonds. Tax yachts.” He laughs. “If they don’t pay up, move them all to the Isle of Wight and turn it into a giant prison for rich people.”This future star’s debut documentary ends with a shameless appeal for a full series. “We’ve already started talking about the next film,” he says. “Skincare, cosmetics and plastic surgery is the route I’m keen to go down. Shabaz asks: can I turn back time? That’s the question I want to pose. Watch this space.”In the meantime, he believes society is approaching tipping point. “I think we’re on the brink of something big,” says Ali with a twinkle of zeal in his eyes. “People are so sick of wealth inequality. We’re French Revolution era, ready to overthrow Marie Antoinette.”Shabaz Asks: Are Billionaires Bad? airs on BBC Three at 9pm on Tuesday 28 Julyand will be available on iPlayerExplore more on these topicsTelevisionfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Football, golf … behold the future of world sport: Donald Trump as referee-in-chief | Marina Hyde | The Guardian
description: After his dubious intervention at the World Cup, the president is now an arbiter of high-level fairway disputes. There’s one word for that: SAD! says Guardian columnist Marina Hyde
Author: Marina Hyde
Headline: After his dubious intervention at the World Cup, the president is now an arbiter of high-level fairway disputes. There’s one word for that: SAD! says Guardian columnist Marina Hyde
Content: Donald Trump playing golf in Doral, Florida, 27 October 2022.Photograph: Miami Herald/TNSView image in fullscreenDonald Trump playing golf in Doral, Florida, 27 October 2022.Photograph: Miami Herald/TNSOpinionDonald TrumpFootball, golf … behold the future of world sport: Donald Trump as referee-in-chiefMarina HydeAfter his dubious intervention at the World Cup, the president is now an arbiter of high-level fairway disputes. There’s one word for that: SAD!Tue 21 Jul 2026 08.15 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.14 EDTShareFor so many of us around the world, the final scenes of the World Cup were a chance to switch off the TV with an eye-roll and observe mildly: “Well, I think that’s quite enough America for us all for a while now.” And yet: there can and must always be more. It is simply the way of things. If you thought the World Cup being carried on to the pitch ahead of Sunday’s final in some kind ofLouis Vuitton sex trunkwas the nadir of hysterical trashiness in sport, then simply recall the lesson of the past few years and expand your mind. It can always get trashier.To the parched fairways of Royal Birkdale, then, the host ofthis year’s Open Championship, which – yesssssss! – was won by someone other than Bryson DeChambeau or Scottie Scheffler or any of the other radioactively ghastly US golfers you’d cheerfully head-flush in a Mar-a-Lago loo just as long as you could clear the classified documents out of it first. It has now been revealed that, during his heated row over a two-shot penalty he was handed for improving the area for his backswing, DeChambeauhas been accusedof threatening to call the US president unless it was all resolved in his favour. To repeat: threatening to call Donald Trump.View image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau on the 18th hole during the final round of the Open Championship at Royal Birkdale golf club, Southport, 19 July 2026.Photograph: Russell Cheyne/ReutersIf you missed this, DeChambeau picked up the penalty for improving the line of his swing, apparently having trodden down some long grass close to his ball on the fifth hole during the second round last Friday. He publicly lost his mind about it, demanded to be driven back to the scene with officials, then returned to the tournament scoring area and reportedly threatened to call Trump. Has a new portal to the dark side opened in sport? Given Trump’s known intervention in theWorld Cup(of which more shortly), who among us would bet against it?At time of writing, I had read everything about this sensational escalation, but I still don’t know nearly enough about it. I need every detail. I need everyone involved to reconstruct the entire conversation. I want to know exactly how quickly you might go from “no way did I do any gardening” to “fuck you – patch in the president”. This is a new era of American pant-wetting and I need to understand absolutely everything about its conventions.Certainly, all I already thought about Bryson DeChambeau has been magnified. If you’re unfamiliar with his work, as well as being a two-time US Open champion, Bryson is a hardcore Mar-a-Lago steak-gobbler whogot a shout-outin Trump’s 2024 election victory address, though that was delivered at the Palm Beach Convention Center and not at the latter’s Floridian golf-club residence. (Even so, every time we see those words, the hilarity should strike us anew. The president of the United States of America lives in a golf club. Honestly: I just CAN’T.) Anyway, in the small hours after his victory over Kamala Harris, Trump was very keen to pluck DeChambeau from the sliders queue or the ormolu urinals or wherever and get him up on stage. “Bryson DeChambeau is up here somewhere,” Trump told the cameras. “What happened to Bryson? Where is he? Bryson! He’s hitting balls. He’s on the way?” Eventually, DeChambeau realised there was nothing for it but to climb awkwardly on to the stage.View image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Fifa president Gianni Infantino at the World Cup final in New Jersey, 19 July 2026.Photograph: Andrew Harnik/Getty ImagesAnd yet, where is your orange god now, Bryson? Where was he on Friday, as you seemingly failed to summon him to a faraway British links course having been observed doing a bit of landscaping?There seems, at present, to be no evidence that Bryson put the call in, and since he flaked out of all media appearances for the tournament, no one has been able to ask if he contacted the president in any other way. We understand, however, that he asked for Trump to be called. Sad. Perhaps the only thing worse than demanding that the president be called over you dicking around with the grass is not having the president called over you dicking around with the grass. And then realising that, in many ways, you got less off Trump than some guy calledFolarin Balogunwho plays a sport called … hang on, let me get my lorgnette … “soccer”. That would be a lot of perspective for Bryson to absorb on a Friday lunchtime.The maddest thing about the entire revelation is what it says about how comically debased and politicised sport has been in such short order. Because the thing is, you can totally see it. You can genuinely imagine that the only situation room Trump wouldn’t lose focus in after about 10 minutes is one where he was required to cheat remotely at golf. You can just picture him assembling Pete Hegseth and Marco Rubio and all the other guys with the too-big shoes in the White House situation room, and them huddling round a feed like the photo of theObama administration watchingthe Seal team raid that got Osama bin Laden. Only this one is coming from a grass-level camera at Royal Birkdale.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAs indicated, we already know the US president cheats at sport for people. Precisely two weeks ago, this column covered the business of him making three phone calls to the Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, about the red card andmatch suspensionhanded to US striker Balogun. On Sunday, the Uefa president, Aleksander Ceferin, went so far as toboycott the World Cup finalover, among other things, Infantino allowing this. The only question, surely, is when the next incident or threat will occur. The floodgates of Whitehousery have been opened, and only the US has the power to shut them.Marina Hyde is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionGolfWorld Cup 2026Bryson DeChambeauWorld CupcommentShareReuse this content

title: Tick bites are on the rise in the US and UK. Here’s how to protect yourself | Well actually | The Guardian
description: Experts share tips to avoid the blood-sucking pests that can transmit Lyme disease and other illnesses on to humans
Author: Adrienne Matei
Headline: Experts share tips to avoid the blood-sucking pests that can transmit Lyme disease and other illnesses on to humans
Content: Thirty-one million Americans are bitten by ticks annually – and the number has been trending up for over a decade.Photograph: KPixMining/AlamyView image in fullscreenThirty-one million Americans are bitten by ticks annually – and the number has been trending up for over a decade.Photograph: KPixMining/AlamyWell actuallyTick bites are on the rise in the US and UK. Here’s how to protect yourselfExperts share tips to avoid the blood-sucking pests that can transmit Lyme disease and other illnesses on to humansAdrienne MateiTue 21 Jul 2026 12.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 17.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleI won’t go into gory detail about the tick I found latched behind my ear approximately 35 hours after a hike in early June. Suffice it to say, the parasite was well fed, and I was horrified.I am far from alone in making such a disgusting discovery. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), emergency room visits for tick bites have beenunusually highin the US this year. Thirty-one million Americans are bitten by ticks annually – and the number has been trending up for over a decade. In the UK, ticks appear to berisingin number and species variety.“Tick populations are expanding geographically because we’re changing the climate,” explains Vett Lloyd, a professor of biology at Mount Allison University in New Brunswick who studies ticks and the pathogens they transmit.Milder winters and warmer summers raise ticks’ survival rates. Additionally, human expansion – especially “mowing down forests and living in suburbs on previously forested land” – has expanded tick habitat dramatically, explains Lloyd.Lyme disease cases in England rise by more than 20% in a yearRead moreExperts say that suburbs mean more prime habitat for mice, who thrive eating our garbage and harbor dangerous pathogens in their blood, like Lyme disease. More mice support more ticks, who feast on rodents as well as booming deer populations, lizards, birds and other creatures. Ticks can transmit diseases they pick up to human hosts. US rates of Lyme disease were69%higher in 2022 compared with the 2017-2019 annual average. In England, cases of Lyme rose over20%between 2024 and 2025.“The pathogens are expanding almost exclusively because of human changes in how we live in the environment,” says Lloyd.Here’s what you need to know to protect yourself from ticks.How do you check for ticks?“Ticks have a relatively boring life,” says Lloyd. They hang out in moist, dark and shaded areas like leaf litter. When hungry, they crawl up blades of grass, where they wait to brush on to anything tasty that walks past, she says.If a tick reaches your body, it typically climbs upward, seeking a dark, damp area, like the backs of knees, navel, groin, armpits or behind the ears, says Lloyd. There, they bite, injecting an anesthetic that prevents you from feeling pain or itchiness as they dig in.The best time to find a tick is before it bites. “It can’t give you a disease if it hasn’t started drinking your blood,” says Lloyd. Showering immediately after spending time outdoors may wash a tick off before it has a chance to latch.Perform a tick check after being outdoors. This can be done alone with a mirror, and by lightly running your hands all over your body. Unfed ticks will feel like bumps the size of a poppy seed or a sesame seed. A fed tick can swell to the size of a blueberry. If you’re comfortable with it, another person can help with the search.The longer a tick feeds, the more likely it is to spread disease. Removing a tick before it’s been on you for 24 hours greatlyreducesthe risk of disease transmission, per the CDC.What should you do if you’ve been bitten by a tick?“You want to try to get the whole tick out,” says Dr Charlotte Mao, a pediatric infectious disease physician who works with the Bay Area Lyme Foundation. Mao recommends using “fine tip tweezers with firm gentle pressure” to grasp the tick at its head and pull it up. “You want to try to not have the mouth parts break off and stay stuck in the skin.”Wash the bite site and your hands thoroughly with soap and water or rubbing alcohol, she says.Trying to remove it is better than leaving it in, even if you don’t get the tick out perfectly. “While the tick is on you, it can be transmitting pathogens,” notes Lloyd. “The mouth parts are organic; they will eventually dissolve, and the part of the tick that contains the pathogens is all in [its] body,” she says. If you accidentally “pop” the tick, wash your hands well and disinfect the bite site, as these fluids can spread disease.Both Mao and Lloyd suggest saving the tick in a Ziploc bag in your freezer, noting the date and geographic location it was found, if possible.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toWell ActuallyPractical advice, expert insights and answers to your questions about how to live a good lifePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“It’s not your ideal souvenir,” says Lloyd, but if you begin to exhibit flu-like symptoms, facial drooping, severe headaches or any other worrisome new health issues in the month after your bite, you can get it tested. The Bay Area Lyme Foundation’s website offers alistof laboratories that perform tick testing, some by mail. In the UK, you can mail your tick to the government’sTick Surveillance Schemeto help researchers map tick habitat, or tointernationaltick test labs.Consult your primary care provider after a bite, Mao says. They may give you an antibiotic prophylaxis – typically a single dose of doxycycline – to protect against Lyme, as well as other diseases commonly caused by ticks, like anaplasmosis, ehrlichiosis and Rocky Mountain spotted fever. However, there are some tick-borne illnesses that cannot be treated with antibiotics, says Lloyd. These includealpha-gal, an allergy to red meat and other animal products, and babesiosis, a parasitic infection that causes flu-like symptoms and jaundice. If you notice any strange symptoms in the weeks after your tick bite, talk to a doctor right away.If you identify an expanding solid red or bullseye-shaped rash near the site of your bite, this may be a sign of earlyLyme disease. Your doctor may give you a longer course of antibiotics: one to two weeks, depending on the advice of your regional health authority.Is there any way to prevent getting bitten by a tick?To reduce your risk of tick bites, the CDC recommends wearing Environmental Protection Agency-approved insect repellants such as Deet or picaridin, long-sleeved shirts, long pants and closed shoes, and tumble-drying clothing on high after returning home to kill any lingering ticks. Stick to the open middle of trails while hiking rather than getting too close to brush or leaf litter.Ticks aren’t only a threat to hikers and hunters; depending on where you live, you can be bitten by a tick in your own back yard, notes Erika Machtinger, a medical and veterinary entomologist.Machtinger recommends a few strategies for making your outdoor space safer.Cutting back vegetation to prevent big shady areas and mowing your lawn frequently to reduce grassy habitat are the simplest interventions, she says. Stone walls are attractive to rodents, and have beenshownto harbor high concentrations of ticks, says Machtinger. Seal up any nooks or replace them altogether.“The single best way to manage ticks is with an acaricide,” a tick-killing pesticide, Machtinger says.Some homeowners are wary of the effect of acaricide on pollinators and other beneficial insects. A gentler, more targeted option is tick tubes: cardboard rolls stuffed with permethrin-treated cotton that mice discover and transport back to pad their nests, which basically treats them against ticks, Machtinger says.Pet owners are athigher riskfor tick bites, Machtinger says, because “they spend a lot of time with their pets outdoors”. Pets can also transport ticks on their bodies. While Machtinger says cats are pretty good at grooming ticks off their bodies, she recommends checking dogs for ticks and combing their fur out before they come indoors.Explore more on these topicsWell actuallyHealth & wellbeingInsectsClimate crisisAnimalsLyme diseasefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham urges cabinet to examine ‘all possible ways’ to tackle cost of living crisis | Politics | The Guardian
description: As new prime minister briefs cabinet on ‘cost of living government’, critics ask where money will come from
Author: Kiran Stacey
Headline: As new prime minister briefs cabinet on ‘cost of living government’, critics ask where money will come from
Content: Andy Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for rolling out a national care service.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/ReutersView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for rolling out a national care service.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/ReutersPoliticsAndy Burnham urges cabinet to examine ‘all possible ways’ to tackle cost of living crisisAs new prime minister briefs cabinet on ‘cost of living government’, critics ask where money will come fromKiran StaceyandPeter WalkerTue 21 Jul 2026 15.53 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham is under mounting pressure to explain how he will pay for his policy pledges as he implored his new cabinet to examine “all possible ways” to tackle the cost of living crisis.On his first full day in power, the prime minister faced questions about how he would fund the removal of VAT from electricity bills and an expected uplift in defence spending – an issue that led his new chancellor, John Healey, to resign as Keir Starmer’s defence secretary last month.Sources say measures to ease the cost of living will include reducing the national bus fare cap by a third, from £3 to £2, with the prime minister set to announce the move on Wednesday.The Guardian has also learned Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for implementing his promise to roll out a national care service, a move that could cost further money before the election.Gathering his cabinet in Downing Street for the first time, the prime minister told ministers his would be a “cost of living government”, adding that his priority would be to make life cheaper for voters – albeit within existing borrowing rules.“What can we do just to take that little bit of pressure off people’s shoulders, just to give them that little extra sense that help is coming so that they can have that bit of hope that things are getting better?” he asked colleagues. “And that’s the mission that we’ve got to set ourselves together.”He asked his colleagues to look into what their department could do to ease cost of living pressures, both “big and small”. “We need to be a cost of living government, getting that cost of living down, looking at all possible ways of doing that.”Burnham began the process on Tuesday morning with an announcement that VAT would be removed from electricity bills from 1 October, reducing the electricity price cap by £45 at a total cost of £850m next year.No 10 has ruled out using defence bonds to pay for higher military spending.The move came alongside major changes to Whitehall, which will see the creation of a powerful new Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet and hand responsibility for the UK’s growth strategy to the new No 10 North, which will be overseen by Louise Haigh, Burnham’s de facto deputy prime minister.View image in fullscreenDarren Jones, who was in charge of the now scrapped ID policy, said money for that scheme had not yet been identified.Photograph: Thomas Krych/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockBurnham’s promise to pay for the VAT cut by scrapping the government’s digital ID scheme was immediately called into question, however, by Darren Jones, the minister in charge of the policy until recently, who said the money for that scheme had not yet been identified.“The DigitalID program was unfunded,” he posted on X on Tuesday. “The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget.”Downing Street said on Tuesday that the tax cut was funded for the next financial year, but money would have to be found in future years from existing departmental budgets, suggesting spending cuts to come. No 10 has also ruled out using defence bonds to pay for higher military spending.The intervention from Jones came amid wider disquiet among allies of Starmer over Burnham’s pledge to end factional infighting, after more than half of the outgoing prime minister’s cabinet were removed or demoted in Monday’s reshuffle.One outgoing official said: “It’s less a purge of Starmerites than getting rid of people who were loyal to aLabourleader, while promoting people who were serially disloyal. That’s a dangerous message to send your MPs.”Burnham is set to continue making ministerial appointments this week, including giving the Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar a seat in the Lords and a job in the business department. Sarwar was the first major Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer to resign as prime minister earlier this year.Healey is looking at longer-term plans to cut the cost of living as part of a budget to be delivered later this year. A spokesperson for the prime minister added he had also ruled out using higher borrowing to pay for defence spending – something previously advocated within government by Healey. “It is not something we’re looking at,” they said.Sources have told the Guardian Burnham is now working on a major social care announcement in the coming weeks as he looks to begin laying the groundwork for a social care service before the election.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThis would involve bringing forward the review of social care by Louise Casey, who is due to report by 2028, but is now likely to do so as soon as next year. Those consulted on the policy say Burnham will give Casey scope to work up a range of options, including a new national service funded by taxes or a social insurance model that could see social care costs capped for a limited period of time.They admit, however, that any funding model is likely to trigger a political backlash with previous proposals having been abandoned amid a storm of controversy about the prospects of a new “death tax” being used to pay for them.View image in fullscreenJohn Healey is looking at longer-term plans to cut the cost of living as part of a budget to be delivered later this year.Photograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockBurnham told reporters this week: “I am ready to use some of my political capital on this issue … I would not want to leave office having done what Westminster has done, not just for 15 years – actually, I think you’re getting close to 30 years.“It’s not defensible. How many people have lost their homes and their savings in that time? We need to face it and fix it.”A spokesperson said the announcement would come “in due course”.Burnham admitted on Tuesday that he would face financial constraints as he looks to both ease the cost of living for voters and implement lasting social reforms. “We have got to show fiscal discipline, we have got to show our commitment to the fiscal rules is real,” he told his cabinet.As a result, allies say he has abandoned a nascent plan to raise the threshold at which workers start paying income taxe, which has been frozen for several years.The prime minister told the Times this week the issue had come up more than any other during the Makerfield byelection, but he is now understood to have dropped expensive plans to reverse the policy.This could have involved increasing the top rate of income tax to pay for it, but Burnham’s advisers believe this could be seen as a violation of Labour’s manifesto pledge not to raise income tax, national insurance or VAT.Burnham will spend the end of the week in Manchester, with the government encouraging civil servants working on growth to move to the city. A spokesperson said there would be relocation packages on offer for anyone choosing to do so.Explore more on these topicsPoliticsAndy BurnhamLabournewsShareReuse this content

title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’ | Ukraine | The Guardian
description: After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to government
Author: Luke Harding
Headline: After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to government
Content: Oleksandr Syrskyi was credited with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024.Photograph: Vitalii Nosach/EPAView image in fullscreenOleksandr Syrskyi was credited with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024.Photograph: Vitalii Nosach/EPAUkraineZelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to governmentLuke Hardingin DniproTue 21 Jul 2026 17.34 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVolodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparkednationwide protests.In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said.Zelenskyy said he was bringing Mykhailo Fedorov back into government after hisremoval as defence ministerprompted thousands of people to demonstrate in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, in one of the biggest political crises since Russia’s 2022 invasion.View image in fullscreenVolodymyr Zelenskyy with Oleksandr Syrskyi at the handover of drone missiles to the Ukrainian army in Kyiv in December 2024.Photograph: Sergey Dolzhenko/EPAUkraine’s president paid tribute to Syrskyi and credited him with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024, in which foreign tanks rolled on to Russian territory for the first time since the second world war.“We have come a long way. The defence of Ukraine continues. And every soldier deserves to be treated with respect,” Zelenskyy said, adding that he was grateful to Syrskyi and to “every one of our soldiers” fighting on the frontline.Zelenskyy will be hoping that his radical reshuffle of the army’s leadership will assuage protesters who have taken to the streets demanding Syrskyi’s removal. They had given a deadline of Friday for Fedorov to be reinstated and promised “indefinite protests” if their request was ignored.1:32Why Ukrainians are protesting against Zelenskyy’s dismissal of defence minister – videoFedorov welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment. “This brings a breath of fresh air and new hope to the struggle of free people for freedom and justice. It is a voice for change that could not be ignored,” he said.He added: “The high expectations of the Ukrainian people must be met with a clear vision for ending the war for independence, a strong team and decisive actions aimed at saving the lives of our soldiers, automating the frontline, delivering asymmetric strikes and crippling the Russian economy.”The Guardian view on Ukraine’s challenges, inside and out: tensions at home arise from the struggle against Russia | EditorialRead moreDrapatyi, the new commander-in-chief, is well-regarded within Ukraine’s army and is liked by the public. He is viewed as an ally of Fedorov, whose attempts to reform the military and force it to adopt modern technology and data led to a bitter clash with the old-school Syrskyi.A major general, Drapatyi is commander of Ukraine’s joint forces group. He has been fighting Russia since its covert attempt in 2014 to take over the eastern Donbas region. He became famous nationally when hedrove an infantry fighting vehicle over a barricadeset up by pro-Russian militants in the city of Mariupol.Ukraine war briefing: Russia behind deadly strike on cargo ship, says Ukraine, as Putin thanks North KoreaRead moreThe Ukrainian media outlet Babel described Drapatyi as one of Ukraine’s most capable battlefield commanders. It said his new job would be fundamentally about reforming a large institution rather than commanding troops in combat.Zelenskyy said he and Drapatyi agreed that Ukraine’s long-range drone attacks on targets in Russia such as oil refineries would continue. “Our programme of precision strikes will be implemented with absolute precision,” he said, promising a smooth and coordinated handover following Syrskyi’s departure.He also said the new commander-in-chief would be tasked with updating the country’s defence strategy and reforming its structure. Another pressing issue is mobilisation, at a time when after nearly five years of full-scale war few Ukrainians are ready to volunteer.It is unclear what Fedorov’s new role will be, amid reports that he was insisting on being given his old job back as defence minister. This has now been filled by Yevhen Khmara, a former special operations commander.View image in fullscreenYevhen Khmara replaced Mykhailo Fedeorov as Ukraine’s defence minister.Photograph: Ukrainian presidential press service/AFP/Getty ImagesZelenskyy said he met Fedorov on Tuesday. “I offered Mykhailo a prominent position within the government that would enable him to unify the technological sector of our state and ensure its development,” he said, without giving further details.Zelenskyy added: “Our shared wish is one – victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace.” He said “a significant path has been travelled, the defence of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment”.Explore more on these topicsUkraineVolodymyr ZelenskyyRussiaEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC | Ebola | The Guardian
description: UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease
Author: Nadeem Badshah
Headline: UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease
Content: A patient with Ebola receives medical care at the Rwampara Ebola treatment centre in Bunia, Ituri, in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, earlier in July.Photograph: Benediction Murhabazi/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA patient with Ebola receives medical care at the Rwampara Ebola treatment centre in Bunia, Ituri, in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, earlier in July.Photograph: Benediction Murhabazi/AFP/Getty ImagesEbolaHealth worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRCUK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the diseaseNadeem BadshahTue 21 Jul 2026 18.39 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 18.53 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital inLondonafter potential exposure to the Ebola virus.The UK resident was medically evacuated from theDemocratic Republic of the Congo(DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.UKHSA said the person was evacuated as a “highly precautionary measure”, adding: “The individual is not currently displaying any symptoms and remains well, but as a precautionary measure they have been evacuated on a flight chartered solely for the evacuation.“They are now being assessed and monitored in isolation by infectious disease specialists at a London hospital.”The agency said the incubation period ofEbolaranges from two to 21 days and the individual will be closely monitored and is required to isolate throughout this period.There are no confirmed cases of Ebola in the UK and the risk to the general public remains low.Richard Pebody, the director of epidemic and emerging infections at the UKHSA, said: “This individual has been transferred out of an abundance of caution and we’re pleased they remain well.”Last month, a patient suspected ofhaving the Ebola virus at a Glasgow hospitaltested negative.Precautionary measures were taken after the patient was admitted to the Queen Elizabeth University hospital.Anoutbreak of the diseasein the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda in May was declared a public health emergency of international concern by the World Health Organization (WHO).The virus is transmitted to people from wild animals and then spreads in the human population through direct contact with the blood, secretions, organs or other bodily fluids of infected people and with contaminated surfaces and materials such as bedding or clothing.France confirms first Ebola case in doctor who had worked in DRCRead moreAccording to WHO, symptoms can include fever, fatigue, malaise, muscle, pain, headache and sore throat. These are followed by vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, rash, and symptoms of impaired kidney and liver functions.Explore more on these topicsEbolaLondonDemocratic Republic of the CongoHealthAfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: France becomes first EU country to ban social media access for under-15s | France | The Guardian
description: Parliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel Macron
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Parliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel Macron
Content: Emmanuel Macron visits a children's health centre in Paris on Tuesday, the same day the French parliament voted to ban children from social media.Photograph: Michel Euler/APView image in fullscreenEmmanuel Macron visits a children's health centre in Paris on Tuesday, the same day the French parliament voted to ban children from social media.Photograph: Michel Euler/APFranceFrance becomes first EU country to ban social media access for under-15sParliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel MacronAFPTue 21 Jul 2026 15.14 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFrance’s parliament has approved a bill banning social media access for children under 15, making it the first EU country to bar children from apps such as TikTok.The president, Emmanuel Macron, has championed the ban as a key reform of his final term in office and pledged to enforce it by September.“France is leading the way inEuropewhen it comes to protecting our children and teenagers,” Macron said in a video posted on social media, hailing “a major step forward”.He thanked members of parliament for backing the legislation on Tuesday.“The Constitutional Council must now rule on it, and then it will be time to take action to make this measure a reality and protect our children online,” he added on X.The social media ban sceptic: are we getting it wrong on kids, tech and mental health?Read moreAfter approval by the Senate earlier on Tuesday, members of the National Assembly passed the bill by 279 votes to 81.A growing number of countries are taking steps to restrict social media access amid multiplying warnings over its harmful effects on children.The ban was to be introduced in two stages, with under-15s blocked from creating new accounts from 1 September. The ban would apply to existing accounts from January 2027, according to the legislation.The digital minister, Anne Le Hénanff, said before the vote that the timeline was realistic, “because age-verification tools already exist” and others are still in the works, and the onus was on the platforms to impose the rule.“For four months, all of us in France will have to prove our age,” she told journalists. “If someone is under 15, the account will be closed.”The minister also gave assurances that users’ personal data would be protected.Enforcing the measure by September would ensure that one provision of the bill – a ban on mobile phones in high schools – takes effect at the beginning of the French school year.France’s public health watchdog last year said platforms such as TikTok, Snapchat and Instagram were harmful to adolescents, particularly girls, though this was not the sole reason for their declining mental health.Lawmakers agreed on the need for regulation but the two chambers had disagreed on the approach.French senators had opted for a two-tier system distinguishing between blacklisted platforms flagged as harmful to a child’s development, and those that could still be accessed with parental consent.But the lower house’s blanket ban prevailed, despite criticism from some on the left over the age verification process, the speed of its implementation and risks of circumvention as well as privacy concerns.Exceptions to the ban are provided for sites such as online encyclopedias and educational platforms, and the text does not provide for any penalties for children or parents.“The major platforms are, in my view, largely ready,” assembly member Laure Miller told AFP.The blacklisting system would have taken more time, including fresh consultations with the European Commission on the criteria, and carried a “small risk” of non-compliance with European law, said centrist senator Catherine Morin-Desailly.The European Commission has been considering a social media ban after a push by member states, including France, Greece and Spain, for limiting access.Last week, Ursula von der Leyen, the president of the commission, said children should have “phased and gradual access” to social media.The European Commission has also unveiled an age verification app that EU member states, including France, have begun testing in recent months.After Macron’s government suspended pensions reforms last year, a social media ban could be his last major domestic change before he steps down next year.France will be closely watched by other European states – it is one of 20 countries worldwide to haveproposed or introduced such measures.Last December, Australia became the first country in the world to require TikTok, YouTube, Snapchat and other top sites toremove accounts held by under-16s or face heavy fines.Explore more on these topicsFranceSocial media bansChildrenEuropeSocial mediaDigital medianewsShareReuse this content

title: Ann Widdecombe was hit on head repeatedly with hammer, court told | UK news | The Guardian
description: Joshua Kerry appears at Westminster magistrates court charged with murder of former MP earlier this month
Author: Geraldine McKelvie
Headline: Joshua Kerry appears at Westminster magistrates court charged with murder of former MP earlier this month
Content: Court artist sketch of Joshua Kerry appearing at Westminster magistrates court in London.Photograph: Julia Quenzler/ReutersView image in fullscreenCourt artist sketch of Joshua Kerry appearing at Westminster magistrates court in London.Photograph: Julia Quenzler/ReutersUK newsAnn Widdecombe was hit on head repeatedly with hammer, court toldJoshua Kerry appears at Westminster magistrates court charged with murder of former MP earlier this monthGeraldine McKelvieandJamie GriersonTue 21 Jul 2026 11.39 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 09.39 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA man has appeared in court charged with the murder of the former MP and Reform UK spokesperson Ann Widdecombe.Joshua Kerry, 28, is accused of killing the 78-year-old former Conservative minister at her home in Haytor, Devon, earlier this month, by hitting her on the head with a hammer 21 times.During a brief hearing at Westminster magistrates court on Tuesday afternoon, Kerry, who was dressed in a grey sweatshirt and tracksuit bottoms, spoke only to confirm his name, date of birth and address.The court heard that security camera footage from the property indicated Widdecombe had been fatally attacked on 8 July.Kerry is alleged to have entered her bungalow through the front door while she was eating her lunch, after driving there from his home in Rotherham, South Yorkshire.The prosecutor, Kashif Malik, claimed Kerry was in the house for about two minutes before driving off, having allegedly taken Widdecombe’s wallet from her handbag.Malik said the alarm was raised after Widdecombe failed to attend a pre-arranged online interview for Channel 5’s Matt Allwright show, shortly before 1pm. Producers from the show are said to have tried to contact Widdecombe and her personal assistant several times.She was found lying face down on her kitchen floor by her gardener on the morning of 9 July and declared dead by emergency services shortly afterwards. Malik said a provisional cause of death was given by a pathologist as blunt force trauma to the head.Kerry was arrested on suspicion of murder on 11 July by officers from Devon and Cornwall police after he was linked to a red Vauxhall Corsa car seen outside Widdecombe’s bungalow shortly before her death.The case was later taken over by counter-terrorism officers and Kerry was rearrested on suspicion of the commission, preparation or instigation of acts of terrorism. The investigation into whether there was an alleged political or terrorism motivation for Widdecombe’s death is continuing.Later on Tuesday, Kerry appeared at the Old Bailey before Mrs Justice Cheema-Grubb, where he was denied bail and remanded in custody. A case management hearing was scheduled for 9 October, with a provisional trial date set for 8 June 2027.On Tuesday morning, an inquest opened in Exeter into Widdecombe’s death. Philip Spinney, the senior coroner for the county of Devon, Plymouth and Torbay, confirmed the time and date of the former MP’s death and read a short statement before suspending the inquest until the conclusion of the criminal proceedings.He said: “The circumstances of the death are as follows: concerns were raised that Miss Widdecombe was uncontactable. So, an employee who was due at the home address was asked to conduct a welfare check. Miss Widdecombe was located within her property. She was found to be unresponsive, and with visible injuries.“The emergency services were called, and she was sadly declared deceased at the scene. A postmortem examination has taken place, and at this stage the precise medical cause of death has yet to be established.”Spinney paid tribute to Widdecombe, who was the Conservative MP for the seat of Maidstone and the Weald, previously Maidstone, between 1987 and 2010. In 2019, she defected to the Brexit party to serve as an MEP; latterly, she was an immigration and justice spokesperson for its successor party, Reform UK.“It is plain that Miss Widdecombe was much loved and will be greatly missed, and it’s important that we all understand the impact the loss of life has had on those who mourn,” Spinney said.“A court could appear a harsh and clinical setting for what is an intensely private matter, the death of a loved one. I would like to offer my deepest condolences to the family of Miss Widdecombe.”Explore more on these topicsUK newsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Madeleine McCann’s younger brother Sean to swim for Scotland at Glasgow 2026 | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: Madeleine McCann’s younger brother, Sean, has been selected to represent Scotland in swimming at the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: Madeleine McCann’s younger brother, Sean, has been selected to represent Scotland in swimming at the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow
Content: Sean McCann has swum for Scotland at youth level and hopes to compete at the 2028 LA Olympics.Photograph: Jamie Squire/Getty Images for Commonwealth SportView image in fullscreenSean McCann has swum for Scotland at youth level and hopes to compete at the 2028 LA Olympics.Photograph: Jamie Squire/Getty Images for Commonwealth SportCommonwealth Games 2026Madeleine McCann’s younger brother Sean to swim for Scotland at Glasgow 2026Freestyle swimmer eligible through father GerryMadeleine’s disappearance still under investigationYara El-ShabouryTue 21 Jul 2026 15.15 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 17.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMadeleine McCann’s younger brother, Sean, has been selected to represent Scotland in swimming at theCommonwealth Gamesin Glasgow.McCann, a student at Loughborough University, is expected to compete in a number of longer-distance freestyle events when competition begins at the Tollcross International Swimming Centre on Thursday, and hopes a strong performance in Glasgow will strengthen his bid for selection to Team Great Britain for the 2028 Olympic Games in Los Angeles. McCann is eligible to represent Scotland through his father, Gerry, who was born in Glasgow.Mollie O’Callaghan reveals she has spinal fractures ahead of Commonwealth GamesRead moreThe 21-year-old and his twin sister were two years oldwhen their sister Madeleine disappearedshortly before her fourth birthday during a family holiday in Praia da Luz, Portugal, in 2007. Her disappearance has become one of the world’s most high-profile missing person cases and subject to ongoing media attention, while aMetropolitan police investigationinto her disappearance has been open since 2011.McCann previously represented Scotland at the 2023 Commonwealth Youth Games in Trinidad and Tobago, where he finished fifth in the 400m freestyle and seventh in the 1500m freestyle. He also competed for Great Britain in the 10km at the 2025 European Open Water Championships in Croatia.In an Instagram videoposted by Loughborough Swimming last year, McCann said his ambition was to compete at the Commonwealth Games and the Olympics and explained how he got into swimming, saying: “When I was eight, my mum [Kate McCann] took me to Loughborough Town [swimming club] and I hated it at first, didn’t want to go, but after a while, it grew on me and I’m still here now.”Speaking to the Joe Humphries Memorial Trust, which in 2021-22 awarded him a grant, McCann said: “I train nine times a week in the pool, as well as three gym sessions, totalling over 20 hours of training per week. I’ve had to remain extremely dedicated, getting up at 4am multiple mornings each week to train, since the age of 11.”Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth GamesnewsShareReuse this content

title: Fiscal hawk or big spender – what kind of chancellor will John Healey be? | John Healey | The Guardian
description: Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promises
Author: Kiran Stacey
Headline: Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promises
Content: John Healey strides out for his first cabinet meeting as chancellor on Tuesday.  He has largely avoided being aligned with any faction.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/ReutersView image in fullscreenJohn Healey strides out for his first cabinet meeting as chancellor on Tuesday.  He has largely avoided being aligned with any faction.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/ReutersJohn HealeyProfileFiscal hawk or big spender – what kind of chancellor will John Healey be?Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promisesKiran StaceyPolicy editorTue 21 Jul 2026 13.58 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOn the frequent occasions thatJohn Healeywould travel abroad as defence secretary, he was so determined to keep working he would get the crew to tether a desk to the inside of his military aircraft so he could read his papers.His diligence is one reason why Andy Burnham, the new prime minister, made Healeyhis unexpected pickfor chancellor on Monday. Allies say others include their shared commitment to re-industrialisation and devolution.After an occasionallyvicious briefing warbetween allies of other potential candidates, the hardworking Healey is seen as a safe choice who can unite a fractured party while not scaring the markets.But many who know him say the new chancellor is more ideologically driven than he may seem, and that Burnham will come unstuck if he treats his most senior minister as little more than a factotum.“This is being billed as equivalent to Gordon Brown appointing Alistair Darling,” said oneLabourofficial, referring to the former prime minister’s choice of chancellor, who was initially seen as someone who would simply do the bidding of No 10.“But it turned out Darling had views of his own whichdid not always chimewith Brown’s – Andy has to avoid making the same mistake.”The decision to appoint Healey was arguably the most important Burnham made on his first day in Downing Street. The new chancellor will be tasked with finding money for the prime minister’s various policy promises, including higher defence spending, taking utilities into public control and finding ways to cut voters’ living costs.Burnham has promised not to raise income tax, VAT or national insurance, and to stick to the last government’s borrowing rules, placing his chancellor in the same straitjacket that hampered his predecessor, Rachel Reeves.View image in fullscreenAs defence secretary, Healey earned a reputation as a strong ally of the armed forces.Photograph: Leon Neal/AFP/Getty ImagesHealey is in an almost unique position to take the tough decisions required while keeping Labour MPs onboard. Since entering parliament in 1997, he has largely avoided becoming attached to one faction or another.Although he served in the Treasury for five years while Brown was chancellor, he was always respected by the opposing Blairite camp. More recently, he has successfully bridged the widening gap between allies of the former prime minister Keir Starmer and those on the soft left agitating for his removal.Part of Healey’s ability to appeal to different wings of the party comes from his personal warmth.
