He will often involve relatively junior members of staff in policy and political discussions and, like his new boss in No 10, has an ability to remember names and details about people’s lives.David Walker, the bishop of Manchester, was one of the local Labour members who voted for him to be the Labour candidate for the seat of Wentworth in 1997 – over other rising stars such as Yvette Cooper – now health secretary.“One of the things that impressed us at the time was that John was committed to living in the community and putting down roots here,” said Walker. “He always made sure he turned up to community events and showed his face, even when he was busy with national politics.”Luke Pollard, his former ministerial colleague at the Ministry of Defence, called Healey “genuinely the finest and most decent man I’ve worked with since being elected”.View image in fullscreenJohn Healey addressing new Treasury colleagues on Tuesday. He is known for remembering details about people and their lives.Photograph: Kirsty O’Connor/TreasuryAs defence secretary, Healey earned a reputation as a strong ally of the armed forces, putting the repair of dilapidated military housing at the top of his list of priorities. To some in government however, this closeness to the military was a weakness.When he asked the Treasury for an extra £28bn to fund the defence investment plan (Dip), just months after negotiating a multi-year budget, Reeves was infuriated.Her allies said Healey had been hoodwinked by service chiefs into believing that last year’s strategic defence review was fully funded, only to have to go back and ask for more when it came to thrashing out the detail.Those close to the chancellor, however, say he needed more money for his budget because of the additional geopolitical commitments Starmer had made in the months after the defence review, including playing peacekeeping roles in Ukraine and the Gulf.Healey’s shock resignation over defence plan pushes Starmer to brinkRead moreOne former government official said the row showed that far from being a fiscal hawk, as some have claimed, Healey would come down on the side of higher spending when presented with a policy dilemma.“He is definitely not a fiscal hawk or even a firm hand in the tiller as he’s being portrayed by some,” they said. “He is a big spender.”In the end,Healey resignedin protest just days before the Dip, which some of Starmer’s allies believe was part of a deal with Burnham to become his chancellor.Wes Streeting, Healey’s replacement as defence secretary, suggested additional money was also likely to be found from cuts elsewhere. “We understand the sacrifices that involves from other government departments,” he told a press conference during his first day in the job.The chancellor will now have to do the kind of adjudicating between departments that could, for the first time, make him unpopular with large parts of the parliamentary Labour party.The question is whether, when faced with a minister in sudden need of extra cash, as he himself once was, he loosens the purse strings or sticks to the discipline of his predecessor.Explore more on these topicsJohn HealeyEconomic policyAndy BurnhamLabourTax and spendingMilitarynewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘A big job ahead of him’: world leaders congratulate Andy Burnham as he becomes Britain’s PM | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: Beyond the diplomatic platitudes global media focuses on the ‘immense’ scale of the challenge he faces in the job
Author: Jon Henley
Headline: Beyond the diplomatic platitudes global media focuses on the ‘immense’ scale of the challenge he faces in the job
Content: Andy Burnham makes his first speech as British prime minister in Downing Street on Monday.Photograph: Tejas Sandhu/SOPA Images/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham makes his first speech as British prime minister in Downing Street on Monday.Photograph: Tejas Sandhu/SOPA Images/ShutterstockAndy Burnham‘A big job ahead of him’: world leaders congratulate Andy Burnham as he becomes Britain’s PMBeyond the diplomatic platitudes global media focuses on the ‘immense’ scale of the challenge he faces in the jobUK politics live – latest updatesJon HenleyEurope correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 05.48 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWorld leaders have warmly congratulatedAndy Burnhamon his entry into 10 Downing Street as Britain’s seventh prime minister in a decade, but international media largely focused on the scale of the challenges awaiting him.Emmanuel Macron, France’s president,saidhe hoped for a deeper relationship “in the service of our peoples, and by giving new momentum to the partnership between the UK and the EU” rekindled by Burnham’s predecessor, Keir Starmer.The German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, whospoke with Burnhamon Monday evening including about continued support for Ukraine, said Germany and the UK were close partners and he looked forward to working together.The president of the European Commission,Ursula von der Leyen, said she looked forward to “strengthening the EU-UK partnership”. A “very good first discussion” had revealed a common commitment to “bringing the EU and UK closer and closer”, she later added.Andy Burnham vows to ‘bring back hope’ as he unveils his cabinetRead moreBeyond the diplomatic platitudes, however, media coverage focused on Burnham’s regional persona – and the huge task awaiting him.France’s Public Sénat saidthe new prime minister came from “the working class of the north of England”.He had managed to “build an image as an ‘everyman’, appearing without a tie, very often in a T-shirt”, the political TV channel said on its website, standing out as “a politician on the ground, close to everyday concerns”.Italy’sCorriere della Sera saidthe UK was experiencing “profound unease, instability and social resentment” and “needs a push” forward, but Burnham’s nationalisation, state intervention and reindustrialisation plans suggested he saw the past as a model.The UK “appears incapable of imagining and planning for the future and prefers to take refuge in a backward-looking Eden”, it argued, even though the pre-Thatcher years were marked by “energy rationing, rubbish in the streets and IMF bailouts”.8:31Andy Burnham becomes Britain’s new prime minister - The LatestTheIrish Times notedthat Burnham may be “a breath of fresh air” but the challenges that brought down Starmer – “a budget unable to meet desperately needed demand in defence, housing, welfare, care for the aged, and training for the young” – remained.The new prime minister, the paper said, would be “hamstrung by a commitment to abide by strict fiscal rules. His instinct may be to tack to the left, but he is already trying to prove that … he will govern from the centre” and the job was “immense”.In anNew York Times essaytitled The King of the North Won’t Save Britain, Tom Hazeldine said that with his “northern accent, casual attire and everyman persona”, Burnham “trades on his outsider status, a precious commodity in antipolitical times”.But, Hazeldine added, a UK that had wanted “a bounce” after Starmer had actually ended up with – in the absence of anything better – “a career Blairite with a northern soul and a Whitehall brain. For the country, it looks a lot like more of the same.”Italy’s prime minister,Giorgia Meloni, wished Burnham “a successful tenure”, adding that Italy and the UK were “bound by a deep friendship and a collaboration that, over the years, has grown ever stronger” and she was sure would continue.Outside the EU,Donald Trump, who had previously described Burnham as “the mayor of a town” and “extremely liberal”, said on Monday night the two had had a “very good conversation” on topics including North Sea oil.“The Prime Minister has got a big job ahead of him, but he will be able to do it and, of course, the U.S.A. will be there to help!” Trump posted on Truth Social. “The call was interesting, and went very well. I wished Prime Minister Burnham, GOOD LUCK AND GODSPEED!”Ukraine’s president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, described the UK as “one of the global leaders”, adding that the two countries’ relationship was “stronger than ever and Kyiv stood “ready to work together in all possible formats”.Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, said Australia and the UK were “longstanding friends” bound by “deep ties”. He looked forward to strengthening the partnership and promoting “security, resilience and prosperity in the years ahead”.Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamEmmanuel MacronFriedrich MerzUrsula von der LeyenGiorgia MeloniDonald TrumpAnthony AlbanesenewsShareReuse this content

title: What the EU wants from Andy Burnham | Brexit | The Guardian
description: European leaders have made positive noises about the new UK PM, but he is likely to face the same restrictions as Starmer
Author: Jennifer Rankin
Headline: European leaders have made positive noises about the new UK PM, but he is likely to face the same restrictions as Starmer
Content: A young woman looking at the Bundestag building in Berlin. Talks about the youth mobility agreement have been deadlocked.Photograph: Halyna Kavun/AlamyView image in fullscreenA young woman looking at the Bundestag building in Berlin. Talks about the youth mobility agreement have been deadlocked.Photograph: Halyna Kavun/AlamyBrexitAnalysisWhat the EU wants from Andy BurnhamJennifer Rankinin Brussels andLisa O’Carrollin LondonEuropean leaders have made positive noises about the new UK PM, but he is likely to face the same restrictions as StarmerUK politics live – latest updatesTue 21 Jul 2026 07.31 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTShareAndy Burnham is unknown in Brussels, but that will soon change. In his schedule for the autumn is an EU-UK summit that may underscore the same dilemmas that have confronted previous British prime ministers in the relationship with Europe.EU leaders were quick to offer congratulations to Britain’s prime minister, the seventh since the 2016 Brexit referendum. The European Commission president, Ursula von der Leyen, said she looked forward to strengthening the EU-UK partnership “for the security of our shared continent” and “the prosperity of people on both sides of the Channel”. The German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, invited Burnham to visit Berlin “very soon”, noting that the UK and Germany’s “deep connection is particularly evident in these times”.Georg Riekeles, a former adviser on the commission’sBrexittaskforce, told the Guardian: “People don’t know what to expect. But he also starts with a blank sheet of paper … and that can be an advantage.”The main concern, said Riekeles, who is now an associate director at the European Policy Centre, was whether Burnham would be “on the same trajectory” in terms of defence and security cooperation, energy and the “very positive agenda” on economic issues.One EU diplomat observed: “For a British prime minister it is not necessarily a bad thing to be little known. If you are well known it is probably because you were prominent on one side or the other of the Brexit debate.”Burnham’s most notable statement on the EU was hiswish to see Britain rejoin in his lifetime, whilespeaking to the Guardianat the Labour party conference in September 2025. But during campaigning in the leave-voting constituency of Makerfield, he made it clearreversing Brexit was not a priority, because “Britain would be stuck in a permanent rut if we are just constantly arguing”.Michael Jacobs, who advised the former prime minister Gordon Brown, said Burnham’s statement on rejoining “felt like it was from the heart” but was also politically astute. “It tells pro-Europeans that he is one of them. But it tells leave voters that it’s not his political priority for now, and he is not going to relitigate the Brexit arguments.”Among the first calls Burnham had after entering No 10 were with von der Leyen, Merz and the French leader, Emmanuel Macron.He has appointed Hamish Falconer as the Europe minister, replacing Nick Thomas-Symonds. The latter’s departure is a blow to the UK-EU reset talks started by Starmer, but some claimed they had lost momentum and Falconer would bring fresh energy. One of his first calls after being appointed was to the European trade commissioner, Maroš Šefčovič.Elected as an MP in 2024, Falconer brings a wealth of international experience, with a decade spent in the Foreign and Commonwealth Office, a stint in the National Crime Agency, and a career dealing with international terrorism, hostage recovery and countering human trafficking.He served as Middle East minister under Starmer.The return of Johnny Reynolds, as business secretary, will help in talks aimed at protecting European trade from China, and tricky negotiations to scrap an upcoming 10% tariff on exports ofBritish cars to the EU and vice versa.Under Starmer, the UK and EUsigned a security and defence pactand cooperated closely on Ukraine. Despite failing to agree British participation in a €150bn EU defence loans scheme, the two sides moved on. In Starmer’s final week in officethe UK signed an agreementto participate in the EU’s €90bn (£77bn) loan for Ukraine, meaning British companies can provide more weapons funded through the scheme.Now Burnham inherits the reset agenda launched by Starmer. The two sides are due to sign a trio of agreements at the summit: a deal to reduce paperwork and certification for fresh food in an effort to ease trade barriers for British exporters; linking emissions trading systems; and a youth exchange scheme enabling 18- to 30-year-olds to work, study, travel and volunteer in the EU and UK.Ireland’s Europe minister, Thomas Byrne, told reporters this month that he expected “positive continuity, at the very least”.Ireland, which holds the EU Council’s rotating presidency until the end of the year, will be involved in the diplomatic preparations for the summit, the date of which is yet to be decided. “There are tricky issues there, and I think we can get them over the line,” Byrne said.Talks have beendeadlocked over the youth mobility agreement. EU member states think the UK’s proposed cap of 50,000 youth visas is too low and they oppose their nationals paying the NHS surcharge and higher rate of tuition fees for foreign students. But Some diplomats see a middle ground whereby EU students get a discount on foreign fees but pay above the domestic rate.Jacobs suggested Burnham may raise the cap on numbers. “The idea that leave voters regard such a scheme as a form of immigration was always slightly absurd, and I think Burnham will see that as a relatively easy win to offer pro-Europeans.”But when it comes to the UK-EU economic relationship, Burnham will face the same constraints as Starmer.Earlier this year the EUrejected an attempt by Starmer to negotiate a single market for goods, minus the free movement of people. EU member states suggested the UK could join the European Economic Area, but that would require accepting the free movement of people andmaking payments to the EU budgetwith no voting rights in shaping new EU rules.Jacobs said: “Given Burnham’s sensitivity to the immigration concerns of his own Makerfield voters, and of many others, the idea that he would seek to re-enter the single market by allowing free movement and making a massive financial payment to the EU budget (which is what the EU would demand) seems implausible.“On the substantive issues, I think he will do pretty much what Starmer has been trying to do with the so-called ‘reset’, and he will come up against the same barriers in Brussels.”Explore more on these topicsBrexitAndy BurnhamLabourForeign policyEuropean UnionEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: What will Andy Burnham’s VAT cut on electricity bills mean for you? | Household bills | The Guardian
description: New prime minister is implementing immediate tax cut to help ease cost of living pressures for British households
Author: Hilary Osborne
Headline: New prime minister is implementing immediate tax cut to help ease cost of living pressures for British households
Content: Households with high electricity usage will benefit the most, but the measure is less useful for those heating their homes with gas.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenHouseholds with high electricity usage will benefit the most, but the measure is less useful for those heating their homes with gas.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesHousehold billsExplainerWhat will Andy Burnham’s VAT cut on electricity bills mean for you?New prime minister is implementing immediate tax cut to help ease cost of living pressures for British householdsAndy Burnham to cut VAT on electricity bills as first cost of living moveHilary OsborneandJillian AmbroseTue 21 Jul 2026 09.24 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 09.20 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHousehold electricity bills for people in Great Britainwill not attract VAT for six months, under the first of a series of cost of living measures announced by the new prime minister, Andy Burnham.How much will I save?Normally households pay 5% VAT on their electricity. This is applied once the daily standing charge and cost of the units you have used have been added up, so the actual sum you pay will vary from bill to bill.From 1 October – the day the regulator Ofgem’s new price cap on energy comes in – there will be no VAT on the electricity you use if you live in Great Britain. This will cover the winter months, which typically bring the highest bills of the year.Using figures based on a typical household’s energy use, the government says the saving will in effect reduce the annual price cap by £45. Your actual savings will depend on your usage.In Northern Ireland VAT will remain at 5% because post-Brexit rules mean that EU rates apply. However, the Stormont government will be given money to fund cost of living measures for people living there.Graph showing new annual dual-fuel bill estimatesI’m on a fixed-rate energy tariff – will I save?Yes. On a fixed-rate tariff the cost of your standing charge and units are set for a certain period, but your bill still varies according to how much you use, and VAT is still applied at the end. Your provider will treat you as it does customers on a standard tariff.Does this mean my bill will go down?Not necessarily. If you are not on a fixed-rate tariff then the amount you pay per unit of electricity moves in line with Ofgem’s price cap, and this may rise in October. The latest prediction from the experts at Cornwall Insight is that the cap will increase by 2%, with the war in the Middle East the main reason.If that is right, you will be paying less than without the VAT cut, but will still end up paying more overall.The Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS) thinktank says that since the start of the Iran war the price of gas has risen much more sharply than the cost of electricity – 24% compared with 5%. “If the goal of today’s policy is to help households that have lost out as a result of the war, it is not well targeted at achieving that aim,” it says.Who benefits most?This is a saving for everyone, but because VAT is charged on how much you use, as well as the standing charge, households with high energy usage will benefit most.The cut will be good news for people who rely solely on electricity for heating, and those who have other reasons to use a lot of power. It will also be a boost for households who have adopted some green technologies: electric cars will be cheaper to charge and heat pumps cheaper to run.Adam Scorer, the chief executive of the charity National Energy Action, says: “It will help everyone, but be less useful for the huge majority of low-income households who heat their homes with gas and cannot afford the upfront cost of shifting to solar, batteries and heat pumps. It is not a trivial distinction.”View image in fullscreenThe decision will be a boost for households who have adopted green technology such as heat pumps.Photograph: David McGill/AlamyCamilla Born, the head of the industry-backed campaign group Electrify Britain, said: “By choosing to take VAT off electricity only – rather than gas – it appears that Burnham has finally gotten the memo that the priority is to get Britain using our homegrown clean power. Until we shift to electric heating, driving and manufacturing we will remain exposed to fossil fuel prices.”The IFS says that relative to household spending, the cut will be most meaningful to the lowest-income households. “On average, the 10% of households with the lowest disposable incomes allocate 5% of their spending to electricity bills, compared with just 2% for the 10% of households with the highest disposable incomes,” it says.“In cash terms, however, a disproportionate share of the cost of today’s tax cut will go to higher-income households who use more electricity overall despite it making up a smaller share of their spending.”Graph of typical levels of consumption with dual-fuel direct debit accountsShould the government do more?Charities say that while the VAT cut is helpful, the government should be looking at the problems faced by people who are already behind with their bills. Energy debt is at a record high, according to the industry trade association Energy UK, which says households owe a total of £5.5bn to providers.National Debtline says energy is the second most common debt its advisers hear about, and that on average people contacting it owe £2,575 to their providers. A debt relief scheme that would allow suppliers to write off some outstanding bills has been proposed and consulted on by Ofgem, but is not in place.Steve Vaid, the chief executive of the Money Advice Trust, the charity that runs National Debtline, says: “Ministers should move ahead with the scheme now to provide urgent respite for people who’ve built up energy debts through no fault of their own.“With nearly half (46%) of the people we support at National Debtline not having enough money to even cover their essential bills, it is clear households desperately need support with the cost of living, support to build financial resilience, and increased access to advice to help them find a way forward.”View image in fullscreenEnergy UK says households owe a total of £5.5bn to providers.Photograph: MBI/AlamyAndrew Sissons, a director at the thinktank Nesta, which has advised the new government on energy policy, said that while the VAT relief would help during the colder months, November’s budget “would be the right opportunity for the new chancellor to set out a wider and more long-term set of reforms to reduce electricity costs”.Nesta has put forwarda more detailed plan, which would change the way household gas is charged and remove some policy levies from bills – at a cost of £3.2bn a year to the taxpayer – and would shave £130 off average bills.Industry has warned that businesses will need help, too. The VAT relief will not apply to most businesses, aside from small companies and charities, so will do little to stemthe economic contagion of higher energy costs, which has inflated the price of everyday goods.Explore more on these topicsHousehold billsEnergy billsUK cost of living crisisTax and spendingConsumer affairsEnergy industryEnglandexplainersShareReuse this content

title: ‘No electricity, no water, no fuel’: Ukrainian drone strikes bring darkness to Crimea’s summer | Crimea | The Guardian
description: Interviews with residents of Russian-held region point to frustration with Moscow as Ukrainian campaign to isolate it takes toll on daily life
Author: Pjotr Sauer
Headline: Interviews with residents of Russian-held region point to frustration with Moscow as Ukrainian campaign to isolate it takes toll on daily life
Content: A bicycle light cuts through the darkness during a power outage in Yevpatoria, Crimea. Photograph: Alexey Pavlishak/ReutersInterviews with residents of Russian-held region point to frustration with Moscow as Ukrainian campaign to isolate it takes toll on daily lifeByPjotr SauerTue 21 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFirst, the guests stopped coming. As Ukrainian drones and missiles pounded Crimea throughout the spring and into the summer, Russian tourists began cancelling their bookings at the small guesthouse Marina runs in Yevpatoria, a resort town on the peninsula’s Black Sea coast.Then petrol became scarce, making it harder to stock the business with food and other essentials. Now, electricity and running water are cut off for much of the day, just as the summer heat sets in.“For 12 years they told us Crimea was 100% protected,” Marina said, referring to the promises Russian officials have made since seizing it fromUkrainein 2014. “And then, within a month, we’ve been thrown back into the Stone Age – no electricity, no water, no fuel.”View image in fullscreenA pedestrian in Yevpatoria uses a torch after authorities limited electricity supplies.Photograph: Alexey Pavlishak/ReutersSince the start of this year, Kyiv has launched its largest sustainedair campaign against Crimea, targeting both the peninsula and the land and sea transport links connecting it to Russia. Residents are facing the most severe disruption to daily life since its annexation in 2014.It was the first Russian-controlled region to experience the acute motor fuel shortages that have since spread to Russia. The Kremlin-installed authorities last month declared a state of emergency and temporarily banned sales to private motorists in an effort to preserve supplies for the military and emergency services.Ukraine releases drone footage showing attacks on energy sites in Russian‑held CrimeaBut as Ukrainian drones increasingly targeted the peninsula’s energy infrastructure, the crisis has quickly moved beyond petrol. More than 70% of Crimea’s roughly 2.5 million residents have experienced some form of power outage, according to a Guardian analysis, while many have now gone several consecutive days without electricity or running water.Interviews with more than a dozen residents across Crimea pointed to a growing sense of abandonment and frustration with both the Russian-installed authorities and Moscow, as Kyiv seeks to turn the peninsula “into an island”.Crimea mapThe festivals, summer camps and beach events that once filled Crimea’s season have been cancelled. In their place, long queues for petrol and a daily search for somewhere to shower and charge a phone. “We’re surviving, not living,” one resident said.Aleksei, a Crimean native who supported Russia’s annexation of the peninsula in 2014, says his restaurant has been largely empty this summer as tourists stayed away, while shortages and disrupted supply routes have made it increasingly difficult to source fresh produce from outside the peninsula.“The authorities kept insisting Crimea was ready for anything. Literally anything. It turns out the authorities failed to prepare for the worst,” he says. “People are hunting for fuel like it’s The Hunger Games.”View image in fullscreenCars queuing for fuel in Yevpatoria.Photograph: Alexey Pavlishak/ReutersLike others interviewed by the Guardian, Aleksei asked for his surname to be withheld for security reasons. Crimea is home to one of Russia’s most pervasive and repressive security apparatuses that has left many people fearful of speaking openly. Public expressions of dissent are rare.Comments beneath officials’ social media posts have, however, become flooded with pleas for help from people who say they cannot contact relatives, buy food or cope with the prolonged outages.“Belogorsk has been without electricity for more than 70 hours, and all we keep getting are promises,” read one of dozens of comments addressed to Crimea’s Russian-installed head, Sergei Aksyonov.View image in fullscreenA dark street during a power outage in Yevpatoria.Photograph: Alexey Pavlishak/ReutersLocal Telegram chats have turned into improvised survival guides, with residents swapping tips on where fuel has just arrived, setting alarms for late-night voucher releases, sharing maps of the shortest queues and informing each other which neighbourhoods still have electricity or running water.Since it was seized by Russia in 2014 amid the turmoil of Ukraine’sMaidan revolution,which ousted the country’s pro-Russian president, Crimea has been transformed into one of the Kremlin’s most strategically important territories.In 2022, it became the main staging ground for the invasion of southern Ukraine, where Russian forces advanced far more rapidly than in their failed assault on Kyiv. Since the full-scale invasion, its military infrastructure has been used for air attacks and battlefield support. Moscow has also encouraged hundreds of thousands of Russians to settle on the peninsula.The 2014 annexation – of what for many Russians is seen as a desirable region of warm weather and beaches – sent Vladimir Putin’s approval ratings soaring and, for years, annual celebrations marking Crimea’s “return” served as choreographed displays of Russian military power and national resurgence.View image in fullscreenThe beach in Yevpatoria.Photograph: Alexey Pavlishak/ReutersBy targeting Crimea – long held up by the Kremlin as the crown jewel of Putin’s rule – Ukraine hopes to strengthen its hand in any future peace negotiations, says Rob Lee, a military analyst and senior fellow at the Foreign Policy Research Institute.“Ukraine has more leverage in future negotiations than it did before because it can now reliably strike almost any target in Crimea.”Ukraine has focused its strikes on the main transport routes supplying Crimea, particularly the Novorossiya highway, a key logistics corridor linking the peninsula to Russia’s Rostov region through the occupied cities of Melitopol and Mariupol.Ukrainian forces struck more than 240 Russian military trucks during the first week of July alone, according to an analysis of verified strike footage by the French open-source intelligence analyst Clément Molin.More recently, Ukraine has escalated its campaign against Russian shipping in the Sea of Azov. Over the last week, Kyiv’s military said it hadstruck90 vessels used by Russia to supply the occupied peninsula.In a recent interview with the independent Russian journalist Dmitry Kolezev, an unnamed Russian general said Ukraine’s recent successes were partly the result of Moscow diverting resources away from defending Crimea and towards its offensive in the Donbas.View image in fullscreenFuel storage tanks burning after an attack in Kerch, Crimea, last month.Photograph: Vantor/ReutersWith the military leadership fixated on capturing the remaining parts of the Donbas, the general said, Crimea had slipped to “third or fourth” on Russia’s list of priorities.Lee says Ukraine has succeeded in producing large numbers of medium- and long-range drones, making it increasingly difficult for Russia to defend against them all. “The cost of these drones is low enough that it’s not prohibitively expensive for Ukraine to keep launching these attacks,” he said.Map of Russian oil refineries struck by UkraineMilitary analysts on both sides believe Ukraine is likely to intensify its campaign in the coming weeks against the Kerch Bridge, which opened in 2018 to link Crimea to Russia, in an effort to deepen the peninsula’s mounting shortages.Successes in isolating Crimea have provided Ukraine with a much-needed morale boost at a time when Russian forces continue to make slow but steady gains on the battlefield, most recently advancing to the brink of capturing the strategically important city of Kostiantynivka in eastern Ukraine.For Moscow, the crisis on the peninsula is an embarrassment it can neither hide nor easily fix. The Kremlin instead has sought to play down the crisis.During a meeting with senior officials last week, Putin instructed the government to urgently provide subsidies to Crimea and Sevastopol to finance fuel purchases and lower petrol prices on the peninsula. “These are temporary problems,” he said.But in Crimea, many residents said the Kremlin’s reassurances only deepened their frustration. “They keep telling us these are only temporary problems,” said Dmitry, a university student. “We’ve been living like this for weeks. This isn’t normal life, and it can’t go on much longer.”Explore more on these topicsCrimeaUkraineRussiaEuropefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilers | The Odyssey | The Guardian
description: Inside all the blink-and-you-miss-it moments, subtle easter eggs and poignant takeaways from Christopher Nolan’s ancient epic
Author: Owen Myers
Headline: Inside all the blink-and-you-miss-it moments, subtle easter eggs and poignant takeaways from Christopher Nolan’s ancient epic
Content: Matt Damon as Odysseus. The Odyssey grossed $264m on its opening weekend.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreenMatt Damon as Odysseus. The Odyssey grossed $264m on its opening weekend.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesNow you've seen itThe OdysseyThe Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilersInside all the blink-and-you-miss-it moments, subtle easter eggs and poignant takeaways from Christopher Nolan’s ancient epicThis article contains spoilers for The OdysseyOwen MyersTue 21 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Odysseylooks set to be the blockbuster hit of the summer, withglobal opening weekend grossesof $264m, critical rave reviews includingfive starsfrom the Guardian’s Peter Bradshaw, and reports of fanstraversing continentsto experienceChristopher Nolan’s epic tale of gods and monsters in one of the few dozen screens worldwide with the capability to screen Imax in 70mm. It also has the potential to rule the box office for the coming weeks, with few other true blockbusters to be released this summer, with the exception of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (hence the cast’shumiliation ritualof a press tour). Read on for a spoiler-packed breakdown of the epic battles, narrative twists and out-of-nowhere cameos that bring richness to Nolan’s idiosyncratic vision of ancient life.Sex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left outRead moreA monumental spectacleAfterOppenheimerwon best picture at the 2024 Oscars, Nolan knew he had a once-in-a-lifetime chance to realize a decades-long dream of bringing Homer to the big screen. “OK, I’ve now got an opportunity to make a film I wouldn’t be able to make otherwise,” he recentlytoldthe Guardian. You can see every penny of The Odyssey’s reported $250m budget on screen. The landscapes of Ithaca are breathtakingly beautiful, and there’s a creepy pall to our hero’s journey to the underworld that Nolan achieved by shooting on the black sands of Iceland’s beaches under the hazy light of the midnight sun. (Fun fact: Nolan also used Icelandic glaciers as the backdrop for the alien planet ofInterstellar.)Nolan filmed in six countries to achieve the range of landscapes in the film, which also included Scotland, Greece, Italy and Malta, and, more controversially, Western Sahara. Controlled by Morocco, the region is often referred to as Africa’s last colony, with the UN classifying it as a non-decolonized territory. “Audiences coming to see The Odyssey deserve better,” the artist and writer Mohamed Sleiman Labat wrote in anessayfor the Guardian. “Those cinematic shots sold to them as the places and moments where historical epics took place were captured at the expense of the Sahrawi people’s suffering.”The film’s epic scenes in the Mediterranean were filmed in the ocean, with Nolan eschewing CGI in favor of real roiling seas and Odysseus and his crew aboard the only functionalViking shipin the world (the delightfully named Draken Harald Hårfagre). A replica of the boat was built for scenes shot in a colossal water tank on the Universal lot. “There were these two jet engines, like 737 engines, blowing water on us,” Damon hassaid. “It was definitely about as hard as you can make water work in a controlled environment.”Technical wizardryView image in fullscreenNolan on set with cinematographer Hoyte van Hoytema.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesNolan has been a champion of Imax for two decades: in 2008, The Dark Knight was the first major Hollywood film to use the cameras for its action sequences. The camera set-up has been used increasingly prominently in his films since then. Nolan used half a dozen Imax cameras to shoot the film, all enclosed in soundproof “blimp” casing to mask their loud operational whirr.The team also worked with an LED company to create lighting rigs they called “pyrohedrons” (fire pyramids) to give the illusion that warm firelight was casting a glow over interior and night-time scenes. “I wanted the camera to see what the human eye would see,” Nolan told theNew York Times.Bringing the divine down to earthIn Homer’s epic poem, gods are “self-interested beings, whose interventions in human lives are motivated primarily by their own desires, whims, and preferences”, as classicist Emily Wilsonput itin her translation. Yet Nolan’s vision of how gods, demi-gods and mortals interact is somewhat earthier than in Homer, with Zendaya’s Athena less of a fearsome divine force than a pal and a confidante to Odysseus.Acting the fool: why have Hollywood press tours become so gimmicky?Read moreCharlize Theronis always a treat (even in her ropey Netflix fare), but Nolan’s interpretation of nymph Calypso may not feel strong enough for some viewers to work as a framing device for the bulk of the film. In Homer, the nymph is a cruel enslaver who holds Odysseus captive on her island; Nolan sees the character as lonely and somewhat pitiable. It’s worth wondering what Theron would have done with a spikier version of the role – she excels as acartoon villain.Like classicist Emily Hauser, who recentlywrotefor the Guardian about Nolan’s omissions, some viewers may also miss the Homeric princess Nausicaa. While interacting with Odysseus, she sniffily pooh-poohs him as “an unpromising man” – her barbs would have added a little light relief to the picture.Nolan’s womenWhenLupita Nyong’owas announced as Helen of Troy – the face that launched a thousand ships – rightwing commentators denounced the casting choice for predictably small-minded reasons. It is ironic that the character is afforded little screen time: when Tom Holland’s Telemachus goes to visit Helen and her husband, Menelaus (Jon Bernthal) in Sparta midway through the film, the famed beauty is near mute, with a grisly scar covering the left side of her face. It’s a bold choice from Nolan, but a little odd that we never find out the cause of her injury.View image in fullscreenAnne Hathaway and Tom Holland in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal Pictures/APOdysseus’s loyal wife Penelope (Anne Hathaway) is a steely and cunning presence despite the suitors’ invasion of her palace. “We wanted to make sure that she did not look like she was mourning her husband,”saidcostume designer Ellen Mirojnick, who dressed Hathaway in the red and blue colors of Ithaca. “She was always viewed as the queen.” The actor has likened her character to a slowly simmering volcano that eventually erupts in scenes where she curses the suitors and pushes her double-crossing servant Melantho (Mia Goth) to certain death while the palace is at war.One of the film’s most memorable scenes sees Odysseus and his men face the sirens, whose alluring song can lure sailors to their death. As their boat approaches their island, Odysseus is tethered to a mast to hear their beautiful yet terrible song while it is muffled to the audience’s ears, as if we, like Odysseus’s first mate Eurylochus (Himesh Patel), have our ears plugged with wax. The sound is “all the things you want it to be, then all the things you wish you never wished for”, says Odysseus, as Nolan powerfully leaves it to the audience’s imagination to fill in the blank.Surprise cameosView image in fullscreenJohn Leguizamo as Eumaeus.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesSome of the film’s most touching moments come from John Leguizamo, barely recognisable with white hair and a dirty smock in the role of Eumaeus, the nearly-blind swineherd that remains loyal to Odysseus to the very end. As Greek military leader Agamemnon, Benny Safdie strikes a fearsome presence in a smart bit of against-type casting from Nolan. Meanwhile, not all cameos were planned: a pod ofdolphinswho made a surprise appearance on a day of shooting ended up making the film’s final cut.Chastity, nodding and enormous pores: will women also love Nolan’s Odyssey?Read moreYet perhaps most surprising of all is the casting of rapperTravis Scottas a bard storyteller who opens the movie with a story of Odysseus’s heroic exploits at Troy. As he orates, the character pounds his staff on the floor in the tempo of dactylic hexameter (which as every good classicist knows, is the original meter of Homer’s poem). “I cast him because I wanted to nod towards the idea that this story has been handed down as oral poetry, which is analogous to rap,” Nolan said in an interview withTime.Oscar chancesView image in fullscreenTom Holland as Telemachus.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesIt’s likely far too early to make many accurate predictions about next year’s Oscars, given that many of the awardsiest-seeming films won’t premiere until fall film festivals. But Nolan is one of the Academy’s favorite directors, winning seven out of Oppenheimer’s 13 nominations in 2024 and a respectable three out of eight for Dunkirk a few years prior.The Odysseyseems like a lock for nominations in best picture, best director and most of the craft categories, leaving the acting categories as a toss-up.There’s no debate about who the male lead of the film is, but there’s all to play for in the supporting categories, withHollandandRobert Pattinsoneligible for supporting actor andZendaya,Nyong’oand Theron potential competitors in the supporting actress category – and Hathaway, if Universal decides not to run her in lead. But I’d personally lean towards Leguizamo for his turn as Eumaeus, andSamantha Morton’s staggering, scene-stealing role as Circe, a witch who turns Odysseus’s men into pigs.Being something of an outsider to the Hollywood star system hasn’t stopped Morton getting two Oscar nominations to date (for Sweet and Lowdown and In America). With the weight of Nolan’s film behind her, I’ll be gunning for her to go all the way this time.Examining the true cost of warView image in fullscreenRobert Pattinson in the film.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesThere’s a good case to be made that The Odyssey’s best sequence is its final 30 minutes, where Odysseus finally makes it back home with the help of Athena and sneaks into his Ithaca palace disguised as a beggar man. After Penelope challenges the men to string his bow – and Odysseus is the only one able to – he throws off his rags and reveals his true identity.His face-off with the suitors isn’t the merciless slaughter thatHomerdescribed – it’s a fair fight with both sides armed – but the suitors, led by Pattinson’s slimy Antinous, are no match for the master of the house. His recapturing of Ithaca is bittersweet, interspersed with flashbacks to the “fires of anarchy and pain” that blazed through Troy as the Greeks sacked the city. There’s no glory in war for Nolan, echoing the sentiments of Dunkirk and Oppenheimer. As Odysseus puts it: “We have broken the fragile bond between men.”There may be few true winners here, but Nolan’s cap to the story is somewhat more uplifting than Homer’s. Whereas the great poet ended The Odyssey with Odysseus planning “raids” to recoup his lost wealth, Nolan sees a rosier and less grimly mercenary future for Odysseus and Penelope. In a beautiful coast-side scene, the reunited couple embark on a journey west to honor the souls of his fallen men while Telemachus – who may be anywhere between 16 and 22 at this point; Holland certainlydoesn’t seem to know– stays to rule over Ithaca.Explore more on these topicsThe OdysseyNow you've seen itMatt DamonLupita Nyong'oSamantha MortonCharlize TheronAnne HathawayRobert PattinsonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: I was struggling after eight rounds of IVF – then a midwife became my surrogate | Surrogacy | The Guardian
description: Narzny McKinney and her surrogate, Sarah, discuss their experience – and the misconceptions that surround the practice
Author: Jessica Murray
Headline: Narzny McKinney and her surrogate, Sarah, discuss their experience – and the misconceptions that surround the practice
Content: Narzy McKinney holding her baby, Zak, alongside the surrogate Sarah Turner.Photograph: suppliedView image in fullscreenNarzy McKinney holding her baby, Zak, alongside the surrogate Sarah Turner.Photograph: suppliedSurrogacyI was struggling after eight rounds of IVF – then a midwife became my surrogateNarzny McKinney and her surrogate, Sarah, discuss their experience – and the misconceptions that surround the practiceUK surrogacy law ‘creaking at seams’ as demand rises sharplyJessica MurraySocial affairs correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter struggling with infertility and eight unsuccessful rounds of IVF, Narzny McKinney had two precious embryos left – her last chance to have biological children. After considering her options, she made the decision to find a surrogate to carry her child for her.“We were eight years down the line at this point and I just didn’t want to risk anything going wrong,” she said. “It was a tough decision but it was absolutely the right thing to do.”She and her husband, Roy, signed up to two UK surrogacy agencies, and began attending social events, where prospective parents get the chance to meet potential surrogates.But soaring demand – due to a rise in infertility as well as older people and gay couples seeking to be parents – meant McKinney and her partner had little luck meeting anyone other than parents in the same position as themselves.“It’s quite emotionally draining, because obviously you go with high hopes each time.You can probably go to a social most weekends; it takes up a big part of your life,” she said. They also considered going abroad – as a growing number of people in the UK are doing, with agencies reporting bumper demand – but knew it would be logistically difficult.View image in fullscreenNarzny McKinney with her son, Zak.Photograph: suppliedAfter two years of waiting, they got a call from the agency, Nappy Endings, to say a surrogate had come up – Sarah Turner, a mother of three and midwife in London, who had always wanted to be a surrogate.Turner said: “I’d always felt very lucky that I was able to have low-risk pregnancies and have babies without any issue. So I thought I would always really like to help somebody that couldn’t do it as easily as I could. I was very up for it. I did a lot of research. I know myself and I was quite relaxed about it.”The two couples hit it off instantly, and a short while later, Turner, then aged 41, became pregnant with one of the last two embryos.From the beginning, Turner tried hard to ensure that McKinney was as involved in the pregnancy as she could be – she bought her a bracelet that would vibrate every time the baby kicked.“Having had three of my own babies, I can’t even imagine how difficult it must have been for Narzny, trusting another woman to carry her baby for her and not being able to do it herself,” Turner said. “That really got me in the guts, and I just used to sit and think, how must she feel? She has to trust me to do everything right for her.“So I thought it would be so nice if she could feel the baby move and have that reassurance.”McKinney said the strong relationship between mother and surrogate was crucial in ensuring the process went well and was comfortable for everyone involved: “We went to all the appointments together. I was really connected.”View image in fullscreenThe McKinneys with Zak. ‘They were really mindful of how I was feeling,’ says Turner.Photograph: suppliedAfter meticulous planning for the birth itself, their best-laid plans went out the window when Sarah gave birth at home so quickly that McKinney and her husband – who lived a 40-minute drive away – weren’t able to make it in time.“People would say, ‘Oh my God, how do you feel about missing it?’ ButI imagine we would have been so stressed and anxious, so it was nice turning up and being told, ‘it’s all done, he’s safe and he’s perfect’,” she said.Turner admitted the immediate aftermath of the birth was the most difficult part of the whole process for her. “The hormones come into play and you can’t do anything about that. So it was challenging,” she said.“Your mind knows there shouldn’t be a baby but your body doesn’t. But Narzny McKinney and Roy were perfect; they didn’t just abandon me as soon as they had their baby. They checked in, they sent photos, we visited them. They were really mindful of how I was feeling.”Now McKinney is enjoying motherhood with one-year-old Zak, and is still regularly meeting up with Turner and her family for catch-ups.They both agree that some legal reform – along with better education and awareness around how surrogacy works – would have helped their experience run more smoothly. Under current UK laws, the surrogate and their spouse must be listed as the baby’s legal parents until a parental order is completed after birth.McKinney said she had to visit three different courts to get the parental order to transfer legal parenthood to her and Roy, something which campaigners say should be the case from birth. They also said it took months to get Zak a passport.“It’sa shame because he’s obviously been ours since day one, but the legal documentation to show that is quite far behind,” she said.McKinney also said she had come across people who assumed she had deliberately chosen surrogacy due to a reluctance to give birth, or questioned whether she would have the “same bond” with her child.View image in fullscreenNarzny and Roy McKinney and their son, Zak.Photograph: supplied“You don’t say that to fathers, do you? You wouldn’t say that if we’d adopted our child. You would not find that an acceptable thing to say,” she said.Turner agreed that more education and acceptance was needed. “Because then people wouldn’t feel like it has to be done behind closed doors or that it’s something to be ashamed of,” she said. “Families can be made in all different ways, and actually it’s lovely that we can all help each other.”The pair said they didn’t face any negativity about the surrogacy, but they’re aware of some people’s preconceptions and criticisms of the practice.“I know there are a lot more celebrities doing it now, but for us, it was down to her fertility. It wasn’t just like, ‘Oh I can’t be bothered to carry a pregnancy, I’m going to get someone else to do it’. It was a purely medical thing,” said Sarah. “So nobody had anything negative to say to us about it.”Turner added that while people have hailed her decision to be a surrogate as an incredible selfless act, she doesn’t think of it in such grandiose terms.“I’m quite chill about it,” she said. “Everyone says it’s this amazing thing you’ve done. I don’t necessarily even think like that. I’m glad I did it, I’m glad they’ve got their baby and I’m glad they’re a happy family – it’s really lovely to watch them grow.”Explore more on these topicsSurrogacyParents and parentingPregnancyFamilyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The Undeclared War review – Simon Pegg’s cyber-attack thriller fails to push any of the right buttons | Television | The Guardian
description: The rebooted GCHQ digital warfare series has more zip than its predecessor – but people bashing away on computers in a bunker near Cheltenham is inherently unsexy
Author: Jack Seale
Headline: The rebooted GCHQ digital warfare series has more zip than its predecessor – but people bashing away on computers in a bunker near Cheltenham is inherently unsexy
Content: Reanimated … Simon Pegg as Danny Patrick in The Undeclared War.Photograph: Simon Ridgway/Channel 4View image in fullscreenReanimated … Simon Pegg as Danny Patrick in The Undeclared War.Photograph: Simon Ridgway/Channel 4TV reviewTelevisionReviewThe Undeclared War review – Simon Pegg’s cyber-attack thriller fails to push any of the right buttonsThe rebooted GCHQ digital warfare series has more zip than its predecessor – but people bashing away on computers in a bunker near Cheltenham is inherently unsexyJack SealeTue 21 Jul 2026 17.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIf the espionage battles of the future are going to be fought online, TV thrillers will have to reflect that fact, much as they might not want to. Cyber-attacks, cross-border malware and digital misinformation just aren’t especially televisual: whatever you do, you always end up with a speed-typing nerd muttering “Shit!” as gobbledegook scrolls across a monitor and red lights flash in the distance.In 2022, Channel 4’s missable drama The Undeclared War tried to crack the problem by throwing heavyweight film-making pedigree at it. Peter Kosminsky, the esteemed director behind Wolf Hall and The Government Inspector, had evidently researched the back end out of the subject, presenting the details of an imagined campaign of Russian interference in Britain’s daily digital functionality – and, from there, the democratic process of an election – in a way that real-life sleepless nerds said was credible enough.The problem was the drama itself, which in Kosminsky’s hands was slow, quiet and dingy, as if the topic of cyberwarfare were too important to be sullied with cheap tricks like storytelling momentum. Scene after scene featured tech whizzes staring listlessly at each other in the oddly silent environs of GCHQ. The contrivance that Kosminsky and his writers did add was not an improvement: a young adult fantasy that saw computer science undergraduate Saara (Hannah Khalique-Brown) embarrass Britain’s best pro hackers within minutes of arriving on a student work placement, by spotting a crucial detail in Russian code that everyone else had overlooked. With her glum college buddy Vadim (German Segal) in St Petersburg, working on Russian cyber-attacks while secretly being more loyal to Saara than to Vladimir Putin, the commencement or avoidance of a third world war depended on two dreamers just out of their teens.Surprisingly recommissioned for a second season four years later, The Undeclared War returns without Kosminsky as director and without the frisson of a near-future what-if: the original was set two years in the future, in 2024, but the new run carries the timeline straight on, so we’re now two years in the past.Big changes have been made, however, and new director Paul McGuigan is keen to let us know: the kinetic opening scene is shot in one take, all the way up to the first ad break. It follows the newly animated head of GCHQ operations, Danny Patrick (Simon Pegg), as he strides down corridors and bursts into conference rooms, raising his voice at people and dealing instantly with nuggets of bad news handed to him on scraps of paper by quivering underlings. It’s a thriller!View image in fullscreenA bunker on the outskirts of Cheltenham … Hannah Khalique-Brown as Saara in The Undeclared War.Photograph: Simon Ridgway/Channel 4Danny’s new status as the unquestioned lead character – Saara is still there, implausibly bypassing British and Russian surveillance by direct-messaging Vadim in a gaming server’s chatroom, but she is no longer the story’s main driver – is confirmed when we follow him out of the office and into his hitherto unexplored home life. His wife, Barbara (Siân Brooke), seems like a rock, ready with a cuddle and some witty mockery when a frazzled Danny once again returns from work and stumbles into bed fully clothed, only minutes before it’s time to get up for the school run. Then we discover Barbara heads up the China team at GCHQ! What with that and his best mate being Charlie (Danny Sapani), GCHQ’s head of security and the man in charge of deducing which of Danny’s team is a traitor feeding intel to the enemy, Danny faces a stressful intersection of the personal and the professional.This has more zip than the previous effort in contrasting Saara’s GCHQ work with her life as a young British Muslim, which was another element of season one that never got off the ground. But The Undeclared War is still scrabbling around for something that will compensate for the digital age’s cold wars being essentially action-free. In the old days your top people would be sneaking into buildings to steal files, garrotting their enemies and living elaborate double lives in chic foreign cities. Now they’re pressing buttons in a bunker on the outskirts of Cheltenham.The politics of the show, meanwhile, have not evolved. New writer Colin Teevan simply repeats the one-dimensional dynamic between idealistic Danny, his more pragmatic boss David (Alex Jennings) and infuriatingly gung-ho cabinet minister Richard Marston (a satisfyingly villainous Ed Stoppard), who always wants to performatively retaliate against Russia in a way that Danny thinks would be reckless. “A symmetric attack would be …” says Danny – and at this point hardcore Undeclared War sufferers can join in with the rest of the line, because it felt like Pegg said it 100 times in season one – “highly escalatory!” Danny is desperate to stop it, but the show could use a bit of escalation: The Undeclared War’s threat level is still much too low.The Undeclared War is on Channel 4 nowExplore more on these topicsTelevisionTV reviewSimon PeggreviewsShareReuse this content

title: The Cherry Orchard review – the RSC’s starry cast brings a disappointing lack of sparkle | Royal Shakespeare Company | The Guardian
description: Tamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and the soft-edged tone mutes its force
Author: Arifa Akbar
Headline: Tamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and the soft-edged tone mutes its force
Content: Helen Hunt plays Madame Ranyevskaya and Kenneth Branagh Lopakhin in The Cherry Orchard.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenHelen Hunt plays Madame Ranyevskaya and Kenneth Branagh Lopakhin in The Cherry Orchard.Photograph: Johan PerssonRoyal Shakespeare CompanyReviewThe Cherry Orchard review – the RSC’s starry cast brings a disappointing lack of sparkleSwan Theatre, Stratford-Upon-AvonTamara Harvey’s production of Chekhov’s final play can seem woolly in meaning and the soft-edged tone mutes its forceArifa AkbarTue 21 Jul 2026 19.19 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 20.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLaura Wade’s reworking of Anton Chekhov’s final, plaintive play brings a dazzle of big names. Helen Hunt is aristocratic matriarch Madame Ranyevskaya, Bill Pullman her brother, andKenneth Branagh(so soon after his lead role in the Royal Shakespeare’s production of The Tempest) their nemesis, Lopakhin, a shopkeeper’s son here who takes over the reins of the family estate and represents the rising forces against Russia’s old order of ossified elites.But for such a starry cast Tamara Harvey’s production has a disappointing lack of sparkle. You do not feel the story’s charge, its complications around love and its stinging losses. The tone is too soft-edged, almost endearing, and the cast such an ensemble that there is no magnetic centre to hold you.Wade’s new version, based on a translation by Helen Rappaport, seems less like a revisionist reimagining than an updating of language, comically benign and stranded in a pre-revolutionary Russia in which the two aristocratic siblings, Ranyevskaya and Gaev, speak in American accents, while Lopakhin and servant Yasha (Julian Moore-Cook) are Northern Irish. The point about the accents is unclear, and the production as a whole seems woolly in meaning.View image in fullscreenBill Pullman as Gaev.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenHelen Hunt, Guy Henry, Kenneth Branagh and Michael Elwyn.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenEsther Smith and Amber Gadd.Photograph: Johan PerssonThe work was regarded by Chekhov as a comedy with farcical elements and Harvey brings out the farce in places, while ancient manservant Firs (Michael Elwyn) is a highlight with his doddering comedy. This does not make up for the lack of emotional and dramatic impact, although the storyline around Ranyevskaya’s adopted daughter, Varya (Esther Smith), and her yearning for a marriage proposal from Lopakhin does develop some emotional dimensions.Like Branagh’s good-natured, sometimes joshing Prospero, his Lopakhin is amenable, lacking the sharp-pronged anger of Adeel Akhtar’s inBenedict Andrews’ astonishing version of the play at the Donmar Warehouse two years ago. When his class rage comes, after the auctioning of the estate, when he reveals that he is its owner, it is the most arresting moment of the night. But the drunken outburst melts away as abruptly after this scene, back to benign neutrality.Hunt seems featureless and uncomplex as the entitled, passionate and grieving Ranyevskaya. She lack personality in the early scenes and emanates generic concern in the later ones, when she feels the estate slipping away. Pullman, at least, brings charm and comic charisma, and you wish his role were not quite so minor.Anna Fleischle’s set design is full of faded grandeur, with chandelier and lace coverings across the furniture. There is a dash of expressionism in the bare branches twined around the room, which gestured at the cherry orchard beyond it. The sound design is subtle and effective, with birdsong and the grinding sound of trees being felled.Written in 1903, when the Russian aristocracy was on the cusp of crumbling, points about social discontent and a rising peasant class are made, often through the play’s eternal student and socialist, Trofimov (Alfred Enoch plays him as a likable prig rather than a straightforward firebrand). But they do not resonate in any significant way, either politically or dramatically.There is so much bustling on stage too and not enough languor. No one seems to feel any Chekhovian longueurs or ennui – not on that side of the stage anyway.Plays until 29 AugustExplore more on these topicsRoyal Shakespeare CompanyKenneth BranaghHelen HuntAnton ChekhovTheatrereviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Pasta windows’: a charming Italian restaurant tradition – or the sign of a terrible tourist trap? | Restaurants | The Guardian
description: A woman ostentatiously making her own pasta in an eaterie in Florence or Rome might seem the height of authenticity. But travellers, beware ...
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: A woman ostentatiously making her own pasta in an eaterie in Florence or Rome might seem the height of authenticity. But travellers, beware ...
Content: Instagrammable …Photograph: JulyProkopiv/Getty Images/iStockphotoView image in fullscreenInstagrammable …Photograph: JulyProkopiv/Getty Images/iStockphotoPass notesRestaurants‘Pasta windows’: a charming Italian restaurant tradition – or the sign of a terrible tourist trap?A woman ostentatiously making her own pasta in an eaterie in Florence or Rome might seem the height of authenticity. But travellers, beware ...Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.51 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleName:Pastawindows.Age:Pretty new.Pasta windows? That’s never going to work; I mean as soon as it rains they’ll go soggy and collapse …Duh! Not actual windows made of pasta.Oh, so is it a new pasta shape – like alphabetti spaghetti (remember?), but little windows?That’s so cute …Nope, not that either.Igive up then, what is a pasta window?So picture a lovely cobbled Italian square, in Rome or Florence.Got it, I’m there. Though let’s make that a piazza, in Roma or Firenze, for the genuine Italian vibes.OK, and there’s a restaurant – sorry, trattoria – with tables outside, and stripy parasols.Perfect. I’ll have an Aperol spritz and imagine I’m in A Room Witha View while I study the menu and decide what to eat.Now look up, and sitting in the window of the restaurant is a person, perhaps an old woman, rolling and shaping the dough, then hanging it: strands of linguine, strips of tagliatelle, sheets of lasagne …Oh myGod, a proper authentic Italian nonna, working with the love and knowledge that has been passed down through the generations of the family. I have discovered the real Italy!Except maybe not.Excuse me?It’s a tourist trap.Who says?Actual Italians. Such as Sophie Minchilli, who does food tours of Rome andtold the Washington Postthat this trend for pasta windows is “wrong”. And Neapolitan chef Michele Pascarella,who told Metrothat no proper Italian restaurant would display someone making pasta in the window, and one that does “is usually more of a kitschy tourist trap than an authentic restaurant”.But, look, they’re all over Instagram!Exactly! And that nonna you can see is an Insta-gran.Hmm, maybe there is something a bit weird about having someone on display, working in the window.Zoo-like even. Imagine a chippy with a fishwife in the window, gutting and skinning haddock.Ew, no thanks. Any other tips for spotting a tourist trap restaurant?So you’re on the main street, and a smiling but slightly aggressive and annoyingly persistent person is waving a multi-translated and heavily laminated menu – with photos – in your face while attempting to shepherd you into their establishment …I’m guessing that’s a no?On so many counts. See also huge places with a huge menu and a huge number of tourists in there already,but very few local people.So where should I go?Take a stroll a few streets back – from the main drag or from the beach. Ask a local resident for their favourites.Do say,after consulting alanguage app:“Mi scusi, può consigliarmi un buon ristorante?” [Excuse me, can you recommend a good restaurant?]Don’t say:“Look, she’s doing pasta shells now. Go on, get in there with her, for a TikTok …”Explore more on these topicsRestaurantsPass notesPastaItalian food and drinkFoodItaly holidaysItalyEuropefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘A guy did a runner in a Jaguar’: the petrol theft scandal – and the garage owner struggling to stop it | Crime | The Guardian
description: Since the start of the Iran war in February, there has been a 23% rise in fuel theft, with more than £1.2m stolen from British forecourts each week. Why aren’t the police doing more to apprehend the culprits?
Author: Sammy Gecsoyler
Headline: Since the start of the Iran war in February, there has been a 23% rise in fuel theft, with more than £1.2m stolen from British forecourts each week. Why aren’t the police doing more to apprehend the culprits?
Content: ‘They have no fear’ … petrol-station owner Shailesh Parekh at one of his garages in Erdington, Birmingham.Photograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘They have no fear’ … petrol-station owner Shailesh Parekh at one of his garages in Erdington, Birmingham.Photograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianCrime‘A guy did a runner in a Jaguar’: the petrol theft scandal – and the garage owner struggling to stop itSince the start of the Iran war in February, there has been a 23% rise in fuel theft, with more than £1.2m stolen from British forecourts each week. Why aren’t the police doing more to apprehend the culprits?Sammy GecsoylerTue 21 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 00.44 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleApetrol station tucked away in the suburbs of Birmingham seems an unlikely place for a crime spree. But this garage has become a prime target for thieves. Some fuel runners are easy to spot, approaching the petrol pumps in face masks, but after a recent spate of fill-and-dashes by less conspicuous drivers, no motorist is beyond suspicion. “It could be anyone,” says Shailesh Parekh, who is on high alert, as always.The 62-year-old owns six forecourts across Birmingham, and today I’m visiting his station in Erdington, a residential area about five miles from the city centre. Parekh remembers a time when a motorist speeding off with an unpaid-for tank was rare. So much so, it would be the talk of the station for months. Those days are long gone. Now, it’s a weekly and sometimes daily occurrence. “In the last three years, it’s been very bad,” Parekh tells me from the back office, where a monitor beams CCTV images from every inch of the forecourt.Thefts have grown worse since the start of the war in Iran in February. In the first week of July, the station saw three fuel runners, and in June, 12 people drove off without paying. Across his six garages, says Parekh, fuel theft cost him more than £40,000 last year, and predicting who will drive off is becoming impossible.In his experience, fuel theft used to be carried out by organised thieves or criminals on the lam, who might have stolen a car. Not now. “I had a guy all suited and booted do a runner in a Jaguar,” he says. Sometimes, thieves will wear hi-vis jackets to impersonate workmen. The day before my visit, at another of his forecourts, a man who “looked like a farmer” filled up his tank with £140 worth of petrol and “buggered off”. Parekh says thieves are becoming casually audacious. “They come right in front of you, because they have no fear.”View image in fullscreen‘In the last three years, it’s been very bad’ … petrol theft has risen dramatically in Birmingham. Photograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianPhotograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianThe issue isn’t confined to Parekh’s garages. Petrol stations have seen a 23% rise in fuel theft since the start of the Iran war, with incidents soaring from 9,089 in January and February to 11,170 in March and April, according to Forecourt Eye, a company that collects payments on behalf of about 1,000 garages around the UK. The Timesestimatesthat more than £1.2m of fuel is being stolen from forecourts each week. Gordon Balmer, executive director of the Petrol Retailers Association, a trade body representing forecourts, says “first-time offenders” are becoming more common, with many “trying it on” due to a “combination of rising household costs and a bit of opportunism”.The soaring levels of theft come amid rising fuel prices, which have shot up after the successive crises of Covid-19, Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and the Iran war.Accordingto the RAC, prices reached a high of 199.09p for a litre of diesel in 2022, before subsiding again. From mid-February to June 2026, the cost of petrol rose from 131p to 155p per litre, while the cost of diesel rose from 141p to 176p per litre. This means that filling a 60L tank with petrol went from £79 to £93, while filling it with diesel rose from £85 to £106.Parekh’s forecourts are fitted with “more CCTV cameras than Birmingham airport,” he jokes, but pursuing a fuel thief is usually pointless. “Half of the runners, we don’t even report them. It’s such a mission,” he says.With CCTV footage and a licence plate number, catching the thieves should be easy, but in reality failing to pay for fuel ends up in a legal grey zone. While intentionally driving off without paying for fuel is a criminal offence, convictions are rare, especially for one-off offenders. If a motorist drives off by mistake or discovers after filling up that they cannot pay, the unpaid amount is generally treated as a civil debt rather than a criminal offence. Unless it’s pretty blatant, it’s difficult to prove that a motorist didn’t drive off in error. And if a driver has filled up and then can’t make the payment, it’s up to the forecourt owner to do what they can to recover the amount. Still, bilkers who are caught and prosecuted face up to two years in prison and a fine. One prolific fuel thief inKent was jailed for 16 monthsafter pleading guilty to stealing fuel from nine forecourts in 2024.View image in fullscreen‘Half of the runners, we don’t even report them’ … fuel theft cost petrol-station owner Shailesh Parekh £40,000 last year.Photograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianNot all the thieves Parekh encounters dash off after filling their tanks. Some use the pump, then come in and declare themselves penniless, leaving him with few options, because “we can’t retrieve our fuel from their tank”. Balmer says a lot of first-time offenders are doing this, because many “see it like a payday loan”. Regardless of how unpaid fuel is taken from his forecourt, Parekh says when he has reported incidents to the police, they often tell him “it’s a civil thing”. This is something Balmer has encountered too. Another station he is in contact with reported an intentional drive-off to police, who incorrectly told them it was a civil offence and there was nothing they could do.In November 2024, Supt Fran Harrod, a senior Lincolnshire police officer,told the BBCthat somebody who has driven off without paying for fuel might not be “having a great day” and the incidents don’t always constitute a crime. She recommended that forecourt owners try to recover money through a civil route instead of “using huge amounts of officer hours to go round and ask people to pay up, because the police’s time isn’t free either”. Parekh says this approach adds insult to injury: “You can’t go into Asda and say, ‘I’m leaving with this trolley full of items but I’ve got no money to pay for it.’”In March this year,the Times reported freedom of information datafirst revealed by the Forecourt Trader website. It found that in 2024, five police forces across the UK closed a fuel theft investigation without identifying a suspect in nine out of 10 cases.View image in fullscreen‘We have more CCTV cameras than Birmingham airport’ … cars suspected of fuel theft at a petrol station in Erdington.Photograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianWest Midlands police, the force Parekh deals with, did not respond to the Times, but a2022 freedom of information requestrevealed that, of the 1,150 reports of “people driving off without paying for fuel” between January and March 2022, 1,126 (98%) had a recorded outcome indicating that no further action was taken. Of these, 1,106 involved no identified suspect and 20 involved an identified suspect; the outcomes of the remaining 24 reports were left “blank”. Parekh says officers “take the view that this is a totally preventable crime”, telling him he should install prepaid pumps to stop thefts. This, he says, is a non-starter because “the whole trade has to move to that system, otherwise people will get annoyed and go elsewhere”.West Midlands police say it investigates reports of “bilking, and people need to be clear that driving off without paying for fuel is theft and the consequences are very serious”, adding it would “encourage businesses to take steps to minimise the likelihood of them becoming a victim – such as operating a prepay policy and installing good-quality CCTV”.Balmer says he has also faced police indifference. He recalls one incident in which “two guys broke into a forecourt, filled up with fuel and filmed it all for TikTok”. Despite this incident being raised with the relevant police force, the culprits weren’t brought to court because police “didn’t think it was something that they should criminalise them for”, says Balmer. “Even though they had committed a crime.”Parekh has faced similar challenges with getting police to take this crime seriously, including an instance on his forecourt that turned violent. One of his cashiers approached a van driver who he suspected of stealing fuel and stood in front of the vehicle to stop it driving off. The van began advancing towards the cashier, and a tyre went over his foot; luckily he managed to escape with only bruises. A customer helped track down the perpetrator, and the incident was reported to the police. There was also CCTV footage of the vehicular assault. “I pursued the police for 14 months,” says Parekh. “They didn’t go and arrest that man – they didn’t do anything. In the end, I left it.” The West Midlands force says it “won’t tolerate any type of theft” and will “always seek to identify, detain and pursue prosecution against anybody involved in theft offences”.View image in fullscreen‘Police take the view that this is a preventable crime’ … petrol stations are advised to install prepaid pumps.Photograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianParekh recalls one time when police did act. Officers were grabbing a coffee from the garage shop and telling Parekh about a notorious fuel thief they were after. On the forecourt, a van parked next to a pump caught their attention. Officers went to inspect it. Inside the van, they found a man surrounded by containers, in the process of filling them with Parekh’s fuel. After being caught in the act, the thief was arrested.Can-fillers such as this, who sell containers filled with stolen fuel, are becoming more common. Christmas and Easter used to be the worst seasons for fuel theft, but recently it has become a year-round phenomenon, especially since fuel has “become a more sellable item,” says Parekh.The losses to Parekh are having an even greater impact given rising national insurance contributions and business rates, the latter of which, he says, rose from £16,000 to £43,000 last year. And it isn’t just fuel theft he is contending with: he says his forecourt shop is often ransacked by thieves. It’s so bad that at one point there were 20 pictures of shoplifters in the window. He blames this, in part, on people with addiction issues and prison leavers being placed in houses in multiple occupation (HMOs) in the area.Parekh says this has led to a number of violent encounters at the station. Garry Backhouse was manning the till when a regular customer “snapped” and “flashed a massive blade” at him, before being apprehended by a police officer who was in the shop at the same time. On another occasion, the 51-year-old cashier was attacked on the forecourt, while off duty, when trying to stop a shoplifter. Backhouse, who started working at the forecourt 11 years ago, quit after the first incident, but returned after a stint in a tile shop. Now, as a protective measure, none of Parekh’s staff work alone, which further adds to costs, and the door to the shop is kept locked after 8pm.View image in fullscreenAttacked … Garry Backhouse, 51, has been the subject of violent crime at a petrol station in Birmingham.Photograph: Fabio De Paola/The GuardianAnother tool for the garage is VARS Technology, an automatic number plate recognition system and debt recovery service, used at one in eight petrol stations around the UK. It flags up “blacklisted vehicles” that have previously driven off without paying for fuel at a member forecourt. “If the registration flashes up, we don’t start the pump,” says Backhouse. But this technology, and other similar products, have been accused of issuing “false” fuel debts. In one case, this meant that a family who had been accused of driving out of a petrol station in Manchester without paying for £20 of fuel waseffectively bannedfrom more than 1,300 forecourts. Any visit they made set off an alarm.Some fuel thieves steal or clone licence plates to hide their tracks, which can pose further difficulties for those falsely accused of fuel theft.
