The new PM now has to choose what he really wants. This cannot go on much longer.Explore more on these topicsThames WaterNils Pratley on financeWater industryNationalisationcommentShareReuse this content

title: Healey as chancellor? Streeting as defence secretary? This isn’t a cabinet to change the UK like Burnham promised | Owen Jones | The Guardian
description: Putting Ed Miliband in charge of the Treasury would have signalled a genuine new era. Instead, the new PM has played it safe, says Guardian columnist Owen Jones
Author: Owen Jones
Headline: Putting Ed Miliband in charge of the Treasury would have signalled a genuine new era. Instead, the new PM has played it safe, says Guardian columnist Owen Jones
Content: Ed Miliband and John Healey at the first meeting of Andy Burnham’s new cabinet at Downing Street, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/Daily Telegraph/PAView image in fullscreenEd Miliband and John Healey at the first meeting of Andy Burnham’s new cabinet at Downing Street, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/Daily Telegraph/PAOpinionPoliticsHealey as chancellor? Streeting as defence secretary? This isn’t a cabinet to change the UK like Burnham promisedOwen JonesPutting Ed Miliband in charge of the Treasury would have signalled a genuine new era. Instead, the new PM has played it safeTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.33 EDTShareAndy Burnham has promised to bring “four decades of neoliberalism” to an end. The first serious test of that pledge was always going to be thecabinet he appointed. The red flags are impossible to ignore.Ending neoliberalism would mean putting privatised services back into public hands, unleashing public investment and using the power of the state to reshape the economy. Yet Burnham’s support for the government’s suffocating fiscal rules – and his preference for the vaguer language of “public control” over public ownership – had already raised doubts about how far he was prepared to go.Had he truly meant it, Ed Miliband would have been the obvious choice for chancellor. The former Labour leader has been on a political journey since concluding that his defeat in the 2015 general election was partly the consequence of insufficient radicalism. “I felt my analysis was big,”he later said, “but my response was not big enough, not bold enough.”As energy secretary, Miliband was the one cabinet minister who combined dynamism with a genuine determination to challenge the status quo. His record included the establishment of the publicly owned Great British Energy, the return of energy-system planning to public hands and an interventionist industrial policy.He would also have been prepared to confront Treasury resistance to transformative economic policies. This is, after all, a government bureaucracy with much to answer for in Britain’s long economic decline. Its record stretches back to the 1970s, when excessively pessimistic projections of the country’s borrowing requirements helped precipitate thenotorious International Monetary Fund bailoutand the avoidable public sector cuts that followed.Yet Burnham seems to have folded in the face of a loud and very public campaign against Miliband’s appointment, centred on warnings of an impending backlash from the markets. Some of Burnham’s allies briefed against Miliband on the grounds ofhis supportfor net zero policies – an alarming position for a supposedly progressive party amid rolling heatwaves. It is therefore reassuring that Miatta Fahnbulleh has been appointed as his successor. A bright progressive economist, she at least represents continuity with Miliband’s approach.But instead, the former defence secretary John Healey has been appointed chancellor. There is one potential bright spot: the choice may bode well for Burnham’spromise of a mass council-housebuilding programme. Healey served as Labour’s shadow housing secretary under Jeremy Corbyn, when the party was committed to building 100,000 council homes a year. As defence secretary, he alsooversawthe state’s repurchase of more than 36,000 military family homes. Angela Rayner’s appointment as secretary of state for housing, communities and local government offers further reassurance.But there is no evidence that Healey is committed to the transformative economic programme required to end four decades of neoliberalism. He served as a minister in Gordon Brown’s Treasury when the government remained wedded to an economic model founded on financial deregulation and dependence on the City, falling corporation-tax rates, the private finance initiative and the continued restriction of trade-union power.Hisresignation from Keir Starmer’s cabinetin support of still higher defence spending offers further cause for alarm. The Ministry of Defence (MoD) habitually demands funding for projects thatend up billions of pounds over budget. Thedefence reviewwas supposedly fully funded; it then transpired that an £18bn black hole remained. The department has repeatedlyfrittered awaymoney. Billions were spent on aircraft carriers derided by the former chief of the defence staff David Richards as “unaffordable vulnerable metal cans”, while the Ajax armoured vehicles arrivedeight years lateafter a catalogue of problems.As one defence analystputs it: “We already spend 30% more on defence than France for a smaller, less ready, less-capable military.” Britain’s defence market is unusually concentrated in the hands of a few companies: in 2024-25, the10 largest suppliersreceived nearly 40% of all MoD spending, while almost half of contracts were awarded without competition. As defence secretary, Healey could have challenged the MoD over its inefficiency, overspending and dysfunctional procurement. Instead, he amplified its demands for yet more money.That is likely to mean a renewed assault on welfare spending to fund additional weapons. With the veteran rightwinger Pat McFadden remaining at the Department for Work and Pensions, it is difficult to imagine otherwise.The defence of Healey’s appointment is that No 10 will exercise an unusually tight grip over economic policy. Burnham has his own economic advisers, including the former Goldman Sachs chief economist Jim O’Neill, who favours applying greater flexibility to the fiscal rules that constrain Britain’s ability to invest. Leaving aside the question of whether a former investment banker is the ideal figure to help lead a rupture with neoliberalism, it remains unclear how coherent Burnham’s economic vision really is. Only Miliband’s appointment would have made his promise of fundamental change remotely plausible.Miliband has instead been appointed foreign secretary. That is at least an improvement on Yvette Cooper, who has now been moved to health secretary. Miliband won the Labour leadership partly by repudiating the Iraq war and later instructed his MPs to vote in favour of recognising a Palestinian state. In 2012, hewarnedthat an Israeli ground invasion of Gaza would be “a disaster”, and condemned Israel’ssubsequentonslaught in 2014 as “wrong” and “unjustifiable”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAlas, Miliband toed the line under Starmer, and did not vote for a ceasefire in November 2023. His first statement as foreign secretarycorrectly describesRussia’s invasion as “illegal” but refers only to “conflict in the Middle East”. He speaks of “the terrible suffering of the people of Gaza” without naming who has inflicted it. He should notenew pollingthat says 50% of Britons believe Israel has committed genocide – only 17% disagree – with mass public support for ending all arms sales and imposing sanctions.Notably, Wes Streeting has been appointed defence secretary. The Blairite former health secretaryreleased correspondence with Peter Mandelsonin which he wrote that Israel was “committing war crimes before our eyes”. He must now be asked whether he stands by that judgment – and whether he will abide by the legal obligations that logically follow from it.For progressive voters already angered by the failure of the Starmer project, the retention of Shabana Mahmood as home secretary will be another source of antagonism. Her plans for one of Europe’s harshest immigration and asylum regimes speak to the broader problem in British politics. Migrants and minorities have been scapegoated for injustices caused by a broken economic system.Burnham’s cabinet is certainly an improvement on what came before. But on the choices that will decide whether his promised break with neoliberalism is real, he has flunked the first test.Owen Jones is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsPoliticsOpinionAndy BurnhamLabourJohn HealeycommentShareReuse this content

title: Hunter Biden for president? Stranger things have happened  | Arwa Mahdawi | The Guardian
description: Once Republicans’ favourite punchline, Joe Biden’s son is now building an online following by trolling JD Vance, taunting Trump and embracing the absurdity of US politics, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Author: Arwa Mahdawi
Headline: Once Republicans’ favourite punchline, Joe Biden’s son is now building an online following by trolling JD Vance, taunting Trump and embracing the absurdity of US politics, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Content: ‘If you’re going to milk your famous name, at least make sure you’re not boring.’Photograph: Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘If you’re going to milk your famous name, at least make sure you’re not boring.’Photograph: Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call/Getty ImagesOpinionHunter BidenHunter Biden for president? Stranger things have happenedArwa MahdawiOnce Republicans’ favourite punchline, Joe Biden’s son is now building an online following by trolling JD Vance, taunting Trump and embracing the absurdity of US politicsTue 21 Jul 2026 11.06 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.29 EDTShareWhat sort of longshot candidate do we reckon is going to run for US president in 2028? TheRock? Kim Kardashian? Kermit the Frog? US politics has become so debased that being rich, famous and a natural-born citizen (sorry, Zohran Mamdani) now seem to be the only qualifications required for a job in the White House.Which is why you shouldn’tentirelywrite off murmurings that Hunter Biden might be interested in following in his daddy’s presidential footsteps. When asked by a Fox News reporter about Hunter’s 2028 odds last month, Donald Trump suggested Joe Biden’s prodigal son might “do well”. If other “basket case[s]” in the Democratic party could do well,Trump added, then Hunter probably had a shot. Various analysts appear to agree. “Could Hunter Biden run for office? Don’t rule it out,” a recent Timesheadlineblared. The article quotes a Biden biographer saying: “With a character like Hunter Biden in a populist, social media-driven political environment, you have to never say never.”You would definitely have said “never” a couple of years ago: Hunter wasn’t exactly the US’s favourite person during his dad’s presidency. His various scandals made him a target for Maga and a headache for Democrats. But in recent months, Hunter has reinvented himself as a social-media star. Since reactivating his X account in May, the former first son has posted anaverage of 23 timesa day – Elon Musk-levels of posting. Except while Elon is tweeting about how “whitesare a rapidly dying minority”, Hunter is cracking self-deprecating jokes about drugs and gaining fans across thepolitical spectrum, with some calling him the “Maga whisperer”. Writingon X in June, Biden said he’d gladly take the title. “Left, right, D or R we all want the same things,” Hunter added. “We’re being divided on purpose by the Epstein Elite Oligarch class because as long as we’re at each other’s throats, they get fat and rich off of our misery.”Amen to that. Still, despite his refreshing political commentary, I’m not sure we should be treating the man as a folk hero or a Maga whisperer quite yet. It seems to me Hunter’s comeback isn’t so much motivated by political ambitions as by his millions of dollars ofdebt. He’s always been good at capitalising on his last name, and I suspect he’s realised he can parlay being provocative online into monetisable opportunities. To that end, Hunter has been going on every podcast that will have him, including sitting down with the far-right conspiracy theorist Candace Owens and the white nationalist Nick Fuentes. He’s also launched a paid Substack calledWhere’s Hunter?(a reference to one of Trump’sfavourite catchphrases) and joked aboutstarting an OnlyFans.Pete Hegseth wants testosterone for his soldiers. Finally, some gender-affirming care | Arwa MahdawiRead moreLook, if you’re going to milk your famous name, at least make sure you’re not boring. (Trump’s spawn have failed spectacularly in this regard.) And Hunter’s new Extremely Online persona may be occasionally unhinged, but it’s not boring. On Saturday, for example, Hunter went on the progressive commentator Jennifer Welch’s podcast and joked that America’s biggest problem wasn’t “oligarchy”, but “closetocracy”, or the angry “closeted gay mafia” of Republicans in DC. This is relevant, Hunter said, because he believes the “internal conflict creates so much vitriol inside a person that they take it out on the rest of the world”. Hunter added that while he didn’t get a “gaydar ping” from Senator Ted Cruz, the man definitely gave him a “really kinky weird” vibe. I don’t know that this particular conversation was big or clever, but I’m sure it annoyed Cruz, so there’s that.Speaking of things that aren’t big or clever, Hunter’s also been trolling JD Vance following the vice-president’s creepy comments about Biden’s “suggestive” ice-cream licking. (I’ll spare you JD’s thoughts on corn dogs.) On Sunday, which was National Ice Cream Day for those who celebrate, Hunter posted a photo of himself licking an ice-creamwith the caption: “Hey @JDVance. I wish I knew how to quit you.” And Hunter hasn’t minced his words when it comes to Vance’s boss. In a recent interview, he said that if he were sitting next to Trump,he’d tell him to“go fuck his fucking self”. He then noted that he’d challenged Donald Trump Jr to a UFC fight “three times” but hadn’t heard back. He added: “That’s how I’m going to pay all my debt.”Good luck with that, mate. Hunter has zero chance of ever winning a presidential nomination, but I’d bet good money he’d beat Don Jr in a cage fight. The US may be divided, but that’s a Biden-Trump rematch I think everyone in the country could get behind.Arwa Mahdawi is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsHunter BidenOpinionUS politicsDemocratscommentShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham promised urgent action on the cost of living – the heat is now on chancellor John Healey to deliver it | Alfie Stirling | The Guardian
description: The PM and the new boss at the Treasury face an even trickier task than their predecessors. They need a good plan, and we have one,  says Alfie Sirling of the Joseph Rowntree Foundation
Author: Alfie Stirling
Headline: The PM and the new boss at the Treasury face an even trickier task than their predecessors. They need a good plan, and we have one, says Alfie Sirling of the Joseph Rowntree Foundation
Content: The new UK chancellor John Healey.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAView image in fullscreenThe new UK chancellor John Healey.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAOpinionJohn HealeyAndy Burnham promised urgent action on the cost of living – the heat is now on chancellor John Healey to deliver itAlfie StirlingThe PM and the new boss at the Treasury face an even trickier task than their predecessors. They need a good plan, and we have oneAlfie Stirling is an economist and director of policy at the Joseph Rowntree FoundationTue 21 Jul 2026 05.04 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.13 EDTShareAs Keir Starmer found, it takes more than words alone to warm a tepid bath. Voters reserve thegreatest punishmentfor the politicians they think acted too little, or too late, to prevent them getting poorer. For Andy Burnham, much will now depend on whether he has got the right ideas, and inJohn Healeythe right chancellor, to address Britain’s growing affordability crisis: not least as the new prime ministersets out new measures todayto give people “some breathing space”.Before Rachel Reeves took office, the average family was set to beworse off in 2029than they had been in 2019. That’s not supposed to happen. The norm is for household incomes to rise byabout £5,000(2026 prices) every five years. Never before on modern record have they fallen across an entire decade.Given the scale of the challenge, the last chancellor ultimately failed to move the dial either far enough or fast enough. That means Healey will inherit essentiallythe same outlookfor living standards as Reeves did. Only this time, there is next to no time to prepare, and he will have barely half as much time to act – before having to defend his record at the ballot box.To make matters worse, the economic situation is even more complicated now too. Household and business spending isdrying up, and crying out for replenishment through interest rate cuts or government support. But at the same time, inflation and borrowing costs are already elevated, just as fresh price pressures from the conflict in Iran start to penetrate supply chains.So if anything, the Bank of England is more likely to raise interest rates higher – and reduce economy-wide spending further – than it is to cut them. Any government that intervenes too clumsily in support of living standards now risks even higher borrowing costs for itself, families and firms.It’s hard to overstate the seriousness of this triple bind faced by the new chancellor: a politically non-survivable outlook for household incomes, little time to respond and a “natural” macroeconomic trap, where a wrong move could only make things worse.The key problem to solve is how to provide the right support for families now, without provoking higher interest rates. The answer: lower inflation directly by pushing down on essential prices, especially for low- and middle-income families; and pay for it with slightly higher tax contributions, or a small amount of foregone profit from investment income such as capital gains, dividends and rents.Take two examples: energy and renting a home. On the first, today’s temporaryVAT cut on electricityis a good signal of intent, but the government will need to upgrade again soon. The Joseph Rowntree Foundation has recommended an “affordable energy guarantee” that reforms the Ofgem pricing system to provide a basic portion of cheaper energy for everyone – with larger portions for those with lower incomes or greater energy needs.Energy support of this kind could replace the less well-targeted VAT cut in future years, but retain a focus on electricity to support net zero transition. It can be designed to fit a range of price tags for government, and the revenue could be raised byclosing the gapin tax treatment between capital gains (and other forms of investment income) and earnings from work – thus ending the current unfairness whereby a quarter of people with incomes above £10m yearpay a lowereffective rate of tax than the average employee.On the second – renting a home – government should intervene to slow the growth in rents nationally, while allowing mayors to opt out or go further so measures can be tailored for local housing markets. This should be accompanied by a rebalancing of landlord taxation that allows mortgaged landlords to fully tax-deduct their interest costs, paid for by charging national insurance on any remaining income instead.Because mortgaged landlords typically have far lower profits than those who own outright, thelatest evidenceshows that the combination of rent controls and tax reform of this kind could actually reduce the number of landlords in financial distress overall. This is important for maintaining house prices and the supply of new development into the market, which has been the traditional concern with rent controls.New modellingshows how a package built around proposals such as those above (on energy, rents and tax) could return real incomes to growth for a majority of households – more than making up for both the fall in incomes from the last parliament and the anticipated effects from conflict in the Middle East as well. These measures could also reduce inflation by half a percentage point, easing pressure for higher interest rates.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThere is also good – albeit often overlooked – evidence that improving household economic security in this way helps to drive longer-term economic growth as well. When people are more financially secure, they are bothmore productive at worktoday and more likely torisk the move to a better jobtomorrow.Of course, considerable challenges remain. Healey will need to sort the wheat from the chaff from a plethora of competing demands, and the noise from those losing out from tax reform will need to be managed too. Ultimately Burnham is right to make “breathing space” from the cost of living his early priority. The question is whether, and by when, Healey can deliver it. Voters were promised a “change” chancellor once already – both they, and the new prime minister, really need one now.Alfie Stirling is an economist and director of policy at the Joseph Rowntree FoundationExplore more on these topicsJohn HealeyOpinionAndy BurnhamLabourcommentShareReuse this content

title: My stepmother compliments everything I do – and my ego is getting out of control | Zoe Williams | The Guardian
description: She praises me whether I’m heaving a plant pot into the air or pairing a phone to a Bluetooth speaker. It’s enough to make me start believing I am, in fact, quite magical, writes Zoe Williams
Author: Zoe Williams
Headline: She praises me whether I’m heaving a plant pot into the air or pairing a phone to a Bluetooth speaker. It’s enough to make me start believing I am, in fact, quite magical, writes Zoe Williams
Content: ‘There’s a lot of general observation about the condition of being me, and how fabulous that must be.’Photograph: Posed by model; AleksandarNakic/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘There’s a lot of general observation about the condition of being me, and how fabulous that must be.’Photograph: Posed by model; AleksandarNakic/Getty ImagesOpinionFamilyMy stepmother compliments everything I do – and my ego is getting out of controlZoe WilliamsShe praises me whether I’m heaving a plant pot into the air or pairing a phone to a Bluetooth speaker. It’s enough to make me start believing I am, in fact, quite magicalTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTShareAfter about five minutes of my stepmother’s company, I’m always in a good mood. It’s not because of our beautiful harmony, though please think that too if it makes me sound good. In her 80s, and feeling the ravages of time ever so slightly, she makes a constant litany of benevolently envious remarks. There’s a lot of general observation about the condition of being me, and how fabulous that must be: “Oh to be 50 again”; “It must be wonderful to have an appetite”; “I wish I could still sleep in”. Ordinarily I don’t give much thought to the singular pleasures of human maintenance activities – eating, sleeping, having a shower – and when you really look at it, it isn’t a bad life, to be 52.Then she’ll move on to admire my particular skills: “Look at that!”; “What?”; “Look how fast you got out of the car.” It’s a fact, I’m incredibly good at this: I don’t grunt while I’m doing it; it doesn’t take 17 minutes. I’m pretty good at all sitting to standing manoeuvres. “That’s nothing,” I think. “You should see me get out of a squat.”Pairing a phone to a Bluetooth speaker; getting a printer to work; rewinding live radio; for these acts and more, I am basically Clive Sinclair. Strength-based activities, mainly unscrewing lids, picking things up and putting them somewhere else, unleash a torrent of enjoyable plaudits. Sometimes I’ll heave a plant pot into the air and set it down again nicely, just showing off. Today I opened her front door, which is a bit stiff, by kicking it and she simply said: “Marvellous”. I can keep my ego in check for as long as a lunch, but if we spend a couple of days together, I basically think I’m magic.Sometimes my stepmother will think I can do something that I cannot – read a menu without glasses, remember the name of a person we were just talking about – and because she’s only human and schadenfreude never gets old, that putsherin a good mood. So that makes two of us.Zoe Williams is a Guardian columnistDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? title: We’ve been desperate for someone to take on Donald Trump. Who knew it would be Coldplay’s Chris Martin? | Simon Price | The Guardian
description: The World Cup left us with some surprising heroes. Kudos to pop’s leader of the bland and his very public rebuke to hatred and division, says music journalist Simon Price
Author: Simon Price
Headline: The World Cup left us with some surprising heroes. Kudos to pop’s leader of the bland and his very public rebuke to hatred and division, says music journalist Simon Price
Content: Chris Martin performs in the World Cup final half-time show.Photograph: Tullio Puglia/FIFA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenChris Martin performs in the World Cup final half-time show.Photograph: Tullio Puglia/FIFA/Getty ImagesOpinionWorld Cup 2026We’ve been desperate for someone to take on Donald Trump. Who knew it would be Coldplay’s Chris Martin?Simon PriceThe World Cup left us with some surprising heroes. Kudos to pop’s leader of the bland and his very public rebuke to hatred and divisionTue 21 Jul 2026 09.25 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTShareLong before the United States turned theWorld Cupfinal into an orgy of pop music and advertising, the signs were already there that this year’s co-hosts should never have been allowed within a thousand miles of the tournament. The garish half-time show curated by Chris Martin of Coldplay seemed to reinforce that – but then came a moment of improbable genius.I was surprised. The last time America hosted, the problem wasn’t commercial overkill so much as sheer shrugging apathy. I was in New York during USA ’94. Apart from a few flapping banners outside the Nike store, you wouldn’t have known one of the world’s biggest sporting events was taking place. This, in hindsight, was a blessing. Football was allowed to football.In our rampantly hypercapitalist age, we can only dream of such innocence. In the run-up to its 2026 iteration, co-hosted with Mexico and Canada, the cost to supporters of handing the lion’s share of World Cup games to America’s supersize-everything culture became horribly clear, fromobscene ticket pricesto price-gouging by local authorities over travel to games, with a $13 train ticket to the MetLife Stadium, venue for the final, hiked to $105. Then came the announcement of so-called “hydration breaks” midway through each half. This was in no way just an excuse for American broadcasters to cram in more advertising. Never again.View image in fullscreenA hydration break during the World Cup round of 32 game between Australia and Egypt in Dallas Stadium, Texas, 3 July 2026.Photograph: Jerome Miron/Imagn Images/ReutersOn the eve of the tournament, things only got worse. Africa’s top referee, Omar Artan,was refused entry to the Statesfor having the temerity to be Somali. Iraqi striker Aymen Husseinwas detained and questioned for seven hourson arrival at O’Hare airport. In a better, saner universe, all 48 competing nations would have boycotted the whole thing in solidarity at this point. But the reaction of the Fifa president Gianni Infantino to any concerns was to tell us to “chill” and “relax”.Then Fifa announced that this year’s half-time would last 25 minutes –breaking the organisation’s own rules– due to a pop concert along Super Bowl lines. On the day, it began bizarrely: Madonna singing Music on some weird sci-fi Jeep deep in the bowels of the stadium, then emerging from the tunnel flanked, randomly, by Brazil legends Ronaldinho and Ronaldo. The rest of the show was a surreal jumble. Two men exchanging wisecracks, their presence baffling to anyone who doesn’t watch Ted Lasso. The Muppets doing Seven Nation Army. The uncanny valley faces of K-pop megastars BTS looking more like AI creations than flesh and blood. Shakira being Shakira. Wandering minstrel Justin Bieber doing a mournful acoustic ballad called Everything Hallelujah that’s the very antithesis of a bop, planting the crowd’s arses firmly back in their seats.But then Chris Martin pulled out his Trump card. After footage of Pelé saying the word “love” on the big screens,Coldplayperformed a song called Believe in Love, with lines including “Be kind to each other / We’re in this together”, surrounded by dozens of children of multiple ethnicities – the sort of kids Trump’s ICE squads would doubtless deport in a heartbeat – wearing T-shirts with slogans such as “all of us a family”, “I am you and you and you”, and “stars that make a galaxy”. They’re sentiments that would feel trite in more privileged times, but feel radical and essential in 2026. Finally, the sky above the stadium exploded in rainbow smoke above the bleak Sopranos hinterland around the MetLife, an unmistakeable statement of LGBTQ+ solidarity. One can only imagine what the president made of it, behind the bulletproof glass. Martin was, effectively, trolling him.The US isn’t the only ethically questionable host of a World Cup in history: think of Qatar in 2022 and Russia in 2018, and some of us have memories going all the way back to Argentina in 1978 (and we’ve got Saudi Arabia coming up in 2034). But it’s the first to mess with the fabric of the actual sport, and to relegate football to a sideshow, subservient to the gods of commerce and celebrity.Ultimately, the Super Bowl istheirs. Let them do what they want with that. Football isours, everyone’s. Here’s hoping, naively, that some future incarnation of Fifa will consign Trump and Infantino’s venal and vulgar innovations to the dustbin. In his own way, Chris Martin has shown us how.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSimon Price is a music journalist and authorDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026OpinionWorld CupColdplayDonald TrumpGianni InfantinocommentShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on social care reform: Andy Burnham is right – this issue must be gripped | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: Ending the pattern of broken pledges and ditched proposals could help to rebuild trust in politics
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: Ending the pattern of broken pledges and ditched proposals could help to rebuild trust in politics
Content: Andy Burnham talks to staff and residents during a visit to a care home in Prestwich in April 2021.Photograph: Christopher Furlong/GettyView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham talks to staff and residents during a visit to a care home in Prestwich in April 2021.Photograph: Christopher Furlong/GettyOpinionSocial careThe Guardian view on social care reform: Andy Burnham is right – this issue must be grippedEditorialEnding the pattern of broken pledges and ditched proposals could help to rebuild trust in politicsTue 21 Jul 2026 14.04 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 22.14 EDTShareAndy Burnham chose not to make social care the centrepiece of his first speech as prime minister. Instead,rough sleepingwas named as his top priority upon entering 10 Downing Street. Given that earlier attempts at overhauling social care have flopped, he was wise not to over-promise on day one.Louise Casey, who is leading the independent commission on the issue, says that “an honest conversation with the nation” is needed. If Mr Burnham agrees, he will have to wait before making any big announcements. The public outreach phase of her work has not even begun.View image in fullscreenLouise Casey.Photograph: Antonio Olmos/The ObserverSocial care has personal significance for Mr Burnham. In recent days he revealed that his father’s Alzheimer’s means that he does not understand that his son is the prime minister. But he was right to name social care as an area on which“to expend quite a lot of political capital”. If public trust in politics is to be lifted from its current low base, and faith in the welfare state restored, politicians need to show that they are capable of gripping these seemingly intractable problems. For relatives of people with unmet needs, and others who see the system’s flaws closeup, feelings of helplessness and injustice can undermine confidence in society and the state overall.Mr Burnham’s previous effort at solving the social care crisis ended when the Conservatives won the 2010 election. Plans for a“National Care Service”, drawn up when he was health secretary, were scrapped. Since then, the issue has grown more complicated and challenging. While the increased demands on councils and families from an ageing population were predicted, the rise in working-age people receiving social care due to illness or disability is only partially understood. In theyear to 2024, councils in England spent £10.4bn on working-age adults, and £11bn on older people.Funding is where previous plans have come unstuck. The most popular option in the pre‑2010 consultation was for people to receive free care in return for a compulsory contribution, but hopes of cross-party cooperation collapsed when theTories labelled it a “death tax”. Mr Burnham’s most recent remarks suggest that this is still his preferred solution. On Monday, he told journalists that social care should be delivered “on an NHS basis” – implying a public service free at the point of delivery and funded by some form of taxation (though not income tax, as this was judged in 2010 to be unfair on working-age people).Other options could include a new form of social insurance or a combination of private and state payments. In his 2011 report, Andrew Dilnot recommended acap on lifetime care coststo protect people from the enormous expense of – for example – an elderly parent who lives for a decade with Alzheimer’s. This proposal was also dropped.One thing that has not changed is the need to stagger reform. Overhauling such complex arrangements will take time. Most social care is supplied by private companies. At some point Mr Burnham will have to decide whether this should continue, and whether the current friction between health and care systems can be smoothed as part of his devolution drive. Lady Casey has a strong track record as a troubleshooter. But Mr Burnham is right to foresee that he will need to expend political capital. The past 15 years have shown how difficult reform will be, but also how worthwhile.Explore more on these topicsSocial careOpinionAndy BurnhamLabourLouise CaseyeditorialsShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on Ukraine’s challenges, inside and out: tensions at home arise from the struggle against Russia  | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: The row over the sacking of the popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, highlights questions about the country’s military and political direction
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: The row over the sacking of the popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, highlights questions about the country’s military and political direction
Content: A protest against the dismissal of Mykhailo Fedorov in Kharkiv, Ukraine, on 20 July 2026.Photograph: ShutterstockView image in fullscreenA protest against the dismissal of Mykhailo Fedorov in Kharkiv, Ukraine, on 20 July 2026.Photograph: ShutterstockOpinionUkraineThe Guardian view on Ukraine’s challenges, inside and out: tensions at home arise from the struggle against RussiaEditorialThe row over the sacking of the popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, highlights questions about the country’s military and political directionTue 21 Jul 2026 14.04 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 18.55 EDTShareEven as Ukraine’s civilians endurebrutal strikes by Russia, thousands have taken to the streets to protest against Volodymyr Zelenskyy’ssacking of Mykhailo Fedorov, the popular young defence minister, last week after he clashed with the commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi. On Tuesday night, demonstrators’ calls were answered as theUkrainian president fired the generaland offered Mr Fedorov a “prominent” role and promised an overhaul of the army command.Mr Fedorov, widely credited for significant improvements in the country’s military performance and reducing corruption, had made a scathingattack on Gen Syrskyiafter his dismissal – accusing him of blocking reforms and refusing to cooperate. The general seems to have seen the minister asexceeding his remitand effectively taking control of units. The pair also butted heads over Mr Fedorov’s plans to improve recruitment and training to reduce thedeep anger created by the conscription system. A Soviet-schooled military officer deeply embedded within institutions tussled with a young civilian disruptor taking a more agile, improvisational approach.View image in fullscreenMykhailo Fedorov.Photograph: Fredrik Sandberg/TT/ShutterstockThe danger is that the row imperils Ukraine’s regained momentum in Vladimir Putin’s war, after last summer’s nadir. Moscow hasfailed to translate a massive expansion of ground forcesinto a sustained and decisive advantage. Its spring offensive fell well short of ambitions. Kyiv is taking the fight to the aggressor. Civilian casualties on both sides rose 37% in the first half of this year, the UN said, reflecting increasing strikes on heavily populated cities far from the frontline. Impressive attacks on logistics and critical infrastructure deepinside Russiahave led to widespreadfuel rationing, diminishing the public appetite for war, though Moscow may now be stabilising supply. Crimea, annexed in 2014 and now strategically critical, is no longer a tourist idyllbut is strugglingwith petrol, water and electricity shortages.But in an article this week, Valerii Zaluzhnyi, ambassador to London and former commander-in-chief,warnedagainst believing that Russia had lost. It is still counting on its size and the gradual weakening of international support to exhaust Ukraine “economically, militarily and psychologically”.The US president, who likes winners, was surprisingly positive after meeting Mr Zelenskyy at the Nato summit this month,sayingthat there was “a lot of love in that room”. The longer his amicability can be sustained the better, but no one is counting on it. European enthusiasm for enhanced sanctionsis wavering. Western leaders are watching the domestic crisis with concern. They want to see stability.The most important issue, however, is the judgment of Ukrainians. They have understood that wartime can require different leadership, even if they had existing concerns about Mr Zelenskyy’s centralising tendencies, populism and suspicion of potential political rivals. But they have not handed him a blank cheque. There were concerns when hefired Mr Zaluzhnyias military chief in 2024. A year ago, protests forced him to U-turn after hestripped anti-corruption agencies of their independence.This crisis is at best an unwelcome distraction from Ukraine’s military and diplomatic successes. But it also demonstrates that while war has muted criticism and taken elections off the table, Mr Zelenskyy remains answerable to the public. That accountability, unimaginable in Russia, is another powerful reminder of whyUkraineis fighting, as well as a way to ensure that it does so effectively.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsUkraineOpinionVolodymyr ZelenskyyRussiaCrimeaeditorialsShareReuse this content

title: Yet another wake-up call on declining child health | Children's health | The Guardian
description: Letters: Readers respond to a warning from the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health on a ‘national embarrassment’
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Readers respond to a warning from the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health on a ‘national embarrassment’
Content: ‘Children’s health is shaped by whether families can afford nutritious food, live in warm homes and access secure housing.’Photograph: Anna Solovei/GettyView image in fullscreen‘Children’s health is shaped by whether families can afford nutritious food, live in warm homes and access secure housing.’Photograph: Anna Solovei/GettyChildren's healthLettersYet another wake-up call on declining child healthReaders respond to a warning from the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health on a ‘national embarrassment’Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.58 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTShareWe were pleased to see your report (UK children will be one of unhealthiest generations in decades, doctors say, 14 July) on the deteriorating health of UK children, based on a study by the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health. However, it was disappointing to see no mention of the shrinking health visitor workforce and the impact that this has on the health and wellbeing of young children and their families.Health visitors aredescribedby Unicef UK as the “backbone of the early years … the safety net around all families”. A long-established and trusted profession, health visitors are specialist public health nurses who work with families to improve health and reduce inequalities. They provide the only universal service that sees babies and children in their own homes.All of the big-ticket health concerns that have been flagged in this damning report fall within the remit of health visitors, who can contribute significantly to improving birth outcomes and child health, tackling falling immunisation rates, and supporting strong foundations for health and development across the life course – alongside their work in safeguarding, parenting support and addressing the wider social determinants of health. They must continue to be at the forefront of community-based efforts to improve child health and wellbeing.Yet the profession is in crisis. With 45% fewer health visitors than in 2015, preventive support for families has been eroded over years of underinvestment. Rebuilding the workforce must be a priority. If we are serious about improving child health and reducing inequalities, investment in health visiting is key.Barbara StilwellPresident,International Collaboration on CommunityHealthNursing Research,Alison MortonCEO,Institute of Health Visiting,Sally KendallProfessor of community nursing and public health,University of Kent,Gina HigginbothamEmeritus professorof ethnicity and community health,University of Nottingham,Pam SmithProfessor of nursing,University of EdinburghThe Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health is right to describe the state of children’s health in the UK as a “national embarrassment”. While greater investment in health services is essential, the roots of poor child health lie far beyond the NHS. The most striking finding of the RCPCH study is not worsening child health alone, but the deep inequalities that drive it.Childrenfrom poorer communities face higher rates of infant mortality, obesity, poor mental health and other adverse health outcomes.Children’s health is shaped long before they enter a clinic or hospital. It is shaped by whether families can afford nutritious food, live in warm homes, access secure housing, enjoy green spaces, attend well-funded schools and grow up free from the chronic stress of poverty.After years of austerity, local government cuts, reductions in preventive services, the cost of living crisis and rising child poverty, it should surprise no one that health outcomes have stagnated. The consequences extend far beyond childhood: as healthy life expectancy stalls, more people face lives shaped by ill health.Povertydoes not simply affect children’s health; it drives inequalities that persist throughout life.The widening gap between rich and poor children is the result of political choices. Tackling poverty, inequality, insecure housing and the erosion of public services would do more to improve children’s health than any number of new clinical targets. The healthiest societies are those that create the conditions in which good health can flourish.As Sarah Woolnough of the King’s Fund said, this report is a wake-up call. The question is: how many wake-up calls do we need before governments act?DrLouise LawsonLecturer in social policy, University of GlasgowHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsChildren's healthChildrenHealth policyPublic services policyInequalityPovertyHealthlettersShareReuse this content

title: Broaden newborn screening programmes | Health | The Guardian
description: Letter: Screening for spinal muscular atrophy is a step forward, says Dr Janet Hoskin, but what about other serious genetic conditions such as Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: Screening for spinal muscular atrophy is a step forward, says Dr Janet Hoskin, but what about other serious genetic conditions such as Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
Content: ‘Every child deserves the best possible start in life.’Photograph: Oscar Wong/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Every child deserves the best possible start in life.’Photograph: Oscar Wong/Getty ImagesHealthLettersBroaden newborn screening programmesScreening for spinal muscular atrophy is a step forward, saysDr Janet Hoskin,but what about other serious genetic conditions such as Duchenne muscular dystrophy?Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.58 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTShareThe decision to introduce newborn screening for spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) is a major breakthrough for families and campaigners (All newborns in England to be screened for spinal muscular atrophy from 2027, 16 July). Early diagnosis means children can access treatment sooner, improving outcomes and giving families clarity at a crucial time.However, this success raises an important question: why are other serious genetic conditions, such as Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), still excluded from newborn screening programmes? About 100 boys are born with DMD each year. It causes progressive muscle weakness and is often diagnosed only after years of uncertainty. Although new treatments such as Givinostat are beginning to offer hope for some young people, early diagnosis remains vital. It can help families access information, specialist care and support much sooner, and may allow more children to benefit from emerging treatments.Through my research with families affected by DMD and organisations such asDuchenne UK, I have heard repeated accounts of delayed diagnoses, multiple GP visits and years spent searching for answers. Some parents only discover the condition after having additional children who are also affected. For many, the diagnosis comes after a long and exhausting journey.Newborn screening is not only about access to treatment. It also enables families to plan for the future and secure the support their children need. Too often, families of disabled children face lengthy battles with services before receiving appropriate help.Campaigners deserve enormous credit for achieving newborn screening for SMA. I hope this milestone marks the beginning of a broader conversation about other rare genetic conditions and the barriers families continue to face. Every child deserves the best possible start in life, and every family deserves timely diagnosis, meaningful support and the opportunity to thrive.Dr Janet HoskinAssociate professor, University of East LondonHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? title: Ann Widdecombe was a Westminster one-off with a fine sense of humour | Ann Widdecombe | The Guardian
description: Letter: The former Labour MP David Hinchliffe recalls working with the former Conservative MP, saying their disagreements were always without rancour
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: The former Labour MP David Hinchliffe recalls working with the former Conservative MP, saying their disagreements were always without rancour
Content: ‘Ann had, as a key quality, a fine sense of humour.’Photograph: Sipa US/AlamyView image in fullscreen‘Ann had, as a key quality, a fine sense of humour.’Photograph: Sipa US/AlamyAnn WiddecombeLettersAnn Widdecombe was a Westminster one-off with a fine sense of humourThe former Labour MPDavid Hinchlifferecalls working with the former Conservative MP, saying their disagreements were always without rancourTue 21 Jul 2026 12.57 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTShareOf all the many articles I have read following the death of Ann Widdecombe, I think Gaby Hinsliff’s piece got closest to portraying her unique character (Ann Widdecombe’s death should make Britain ask itself: what sort of political culture do we want?, 17 July). I entered the Commons in 1987, at the same time as Ann, and got to know her well as a formidable opponent through doing media interviews and committee work together. We fundamentally disagreed on most issues, but always without rancour.Our most heated clash was over my amendment to adoption legislation to allow unmarried partners to apply to adopt. It was a strange situation where we had Ann, who as Hinsliff recalled, publicly professed to being “a middle-aged virgin”, passionately arguing the case for marriage, while I, married with children, put forward the numerous merits of single people.Ann had, as a key quality, a fine sense of humour and shared colleagues’ amusement over the absurdity of our contrasting positions.David HinchliffeLabour MP for Wakefield, 1987–2005Have an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsAnn WiddecombeConservativesHouse of CommonslettersShareReuse this content

title: Yoga for scoliosis can be life-changing | Health | The Guardian
description: Letters: I’ve worked to cope with the chronic pain through ashtanga yoga, says Louise Zamparutti. Plus letters from Kirie Pedersen and Alice Devon
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: I’ve worked to cope with the chronic pain through ashtanga yoga, says Louise Zamparutti. Plus letters from Kirie Pedersen and Alice Devon
Content: ‘Consistency over the years will keep you in the best possible shape.’Photograph: AlamyView image in fullscreen‘Consistency over the years will keep you in the best possible shape.’Photograph: AlamyHealthLettersYoga for scoliosis can be life-changingI’ve worked to cope with the chronic pain through ashtanga yoga, saysLouise Zamparutti.Plus letters fromKirie PedersenandAlice DevonTue 21 Jul 2026 12.57 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.12 EDTShareThank you for publishing Natasha Livingstone’s article (A moment that changed me: I started yoga – and saw my scoliosis in a surprising new light, 15 July). I have been ashamed of my scoliosis my entire life. It wasn’t diagnosed until my early 20s, and by that time I had begun a career as a dancer and choreographer. My dance teachers were always criticising my off-kilter posture and I constantly felt that I didn’t deserve to pursue a career in dance due to my disfigured spine. My curvature is both thoracic and lumbar, and runs both sideways and forward-backward. I, too, have an uneven waistline, a hip curve on one side and straight rib-to-hip line on the other. Pants fit unevenly on me, and for years I would never wear an open-backed top.Reading this piece made me recognise how hard I’ve worked to cope with the chronic pain, through ashtanga yoga, which I taught for over 10 years, and now through lap swimming. But I haven’t come to terms with the shame. This piece gave me the sense that I am not alone – that there are others of us who have scoliosis and deal with it in a myriad of ways. As the author of this article shows, there should be no shame in having the strength to cope with it and do whatever we want to do in our lives.Louise ZamparuttiSun Prairie, Wisconsin, USNatasha Livingstone’s story is also mine. I had a crooked body and lower back pain where scoliosis caused a pinched nerve. By my 50s, the pain was so bad I sometimes walked with an ice pack tucked into my jeans. Sitting was the worst. And I’m a writer. Then, while we were hiking, a yoga-teaching and licensed physical therapist friend asked politely: “Can I tell you something about your back?” She said: “If you don’t do something now, you’ll be twisted into the shape of a pretzel before too long.”She taught me basic yoga poses, and I cried because they were so hard. But I persisted. Later, when I went to another physical therapist, I asked her to incorporate the exercises into my daily yoga routine. As Livingstone says, physical therapy routines are boring as hell, which is why most people don’t continue and then say it doesn’t work.At 75, I’ve now been pain-free for years. I practice my yoga/physical therapy routine six out of seven days. It works for my scoliosis – and for my heart and mind as well.Kirie PedersenBrinnon, Washington, USHow wonderful that Natasha Livingstone discovered yoga for her scoliosis! I had the same diagnosis at age 14. That was 1969, and there were two choices: a cement cast from my hips to my neck until I turned 19, or expect to be in a wheelchair by 19. I rejected both, and found my way to yoga because my best friend was doing it and I wanted to be like her.Fifty-six years later, I’m still doing it – no pain, normal life, and shockingly flexible for a 70-year-old woman. Some days I’ve done it for hours, other days not at all – totally by whim. But consistency over the years will keep you in the best possible shape. Keep it up.Alice DevonMetairie, Louisiana, USHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsHealthYogaHealth & wellbeingFitnesslettersShareReuse this content

title: Here are 48 reasons to cherish the World Cup (minus the Geopolitics) | Soccer | The Guardian
description: In today’s Football Daily: Some love for the GWC
Author: Niall McVeigh
Headline: In today’s Football Daily: Some love for the GWC
Content: Lamine Yamal’s brother Keyne next to a giant replica of the World Cup.Photograph: Mauro Pimentel/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenLamine Yamal’s brother Keyne next to a giant replica of the World Cup.Photograph: Mauro Pimentel/AFP/Getty ImagesFootball Daily newsletterSoccerHere are 48 reasons to cherish the World Cup (minus the Geopolitics)Sign up now! Sign up now! Sign up now? Sign up now!Niall McVeighTue 21 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.57 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMISTY, WATER-COLOURED MEMORIESThe GeopoliticsWorld Cupis already fading from view, replaced by a thin July gruel of pre-season friendlies and transfer tales. But there’s still time to cast another look back over the 48-team, 104-game, 308-goal leviathan that has dominated the last six weeks of our lives. Without further ado, here are 48 reasons to cherish this year’s World Cup (minus the Geopolitics).The very first game between Mexico and South Africa sawthree red cards dished out, ITVshowing offtheir studio’sManhattan backdrop, anda duckanointed as a national hero. Germany looked ominous, putting seven past Curaçao, whosesolitary goalsent Telemundo commentators wild. Vozinha and Cape Verde had us wondering about Spain, andthe giant keeper’s heroicshelpedhis mumget over to watch him play.View image in fullscreenOne more Vozinha pic for the road.Photograph: Daniel Zuchnik/Getty Images for FanaticsAnother classy veteran,Lionel Messi, banged in six group-stage goals, (briefly) becoming the World Cup’s all-time top scorer. Cristiano Ronaldo was bad,then (briefly) “back”, and then gone again.Scotland fansleft a far greater impression on the tournament than their team, making Bostontheir spiritual home.England put away Croatiawith a dazzling displayof attacking intent that was not to be repeated, whileAnthony Barryexpertly channeled a frustrated lower-league manager in his half-time interviews.It’s probably time for a hydration break, but Mauricio Pochettinojust kicked over the Powerade. Poch did a great turn as a middle-aged actor playing a World Cup coach, andthe US (briefly) looked like a really good team, before being derailed by something or other, we forget. Fellow co-hosts Canada earned their first-ever World Cup win in style,smashing sixpast Sunday League’s Qatar,who earned two red cards. Also sent off: discretion’sMiguel Almirón, for covering his mouthwhile talking– not that it helped Turkey,whocrashed out after two gamesdespite taking 1,057 shots on goal.View image in fullscreenMiguel Almirón makes sure to let the ref see his lips.Photograph: Darren Yamashita/Imagn Images/ReutersGroup E sawJapanbeing tidyon and off the field, an inconsistentSwedenwinningandlosing5-1, andHerve Rénardshowing upfor some reason. Ecuador’s “touchline dreamboat”Sebastián Beccaceceturned heads, while Marcelo Bielsa could onlyshake his dome sorrowfullyasUruguay fell apart in real time. Riyad Mahrez confused everyone by puttingAlgeria 3-2 up against Austria, who immediately equalised anddoubled the torment for Iran, alreadydenied a dramatic winner against Egyptby VAR.Time for another hydro-break, brought to you by the sweet elixir of goals. Cape Verde’sSidny Lopes Cabraldeliveredthe goal and celebration of the tournamentagainst Argentina, who brought extra-time magic of their ownthanks to Julián Alvarez. Haiti’sWilson Isidorand Uzbekistan strikerEldor Shomurodovjoined the pantheon of great forgotten World Cup goals, whileEgypt’s brilliant breakaway against Argentinawas cruelly disallowed. Their manager, combustible hardman Hossam Hassan,responded accordingly.View image in fullscreenStrike!Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersThe knockouts brought a sharper edge, withGermanybundled out by ‘housery’s Paraguayand the Dutch denied by aMorocco teamwho lived up to their billing.Portugal v Croatiawas absolute psychodrama, and Belgium looked more likely tofight among themselvesthanfind a way backagainst Senegal. TheBattle of the Aztecawas a belter. England players threw their heads at everything,Javier Aguirredelivered the GWC’s best touchline moment, andHarry Kane lost his voice. Brazil’s fragile, vibes-based journey was shattered by Erling Haaland’s merciless precision asNorwayrowed onin (almost) perfect unison, with Haalanddominating defencesandSocial Media Disgracesalike.Neymar, meanwhile, won the Mark Corrigan award for winningin the most minor way possible.On to the quarter-finals, whereKylian Mbappédictated the play as France(briefly) looked unstoppable, andSpain conceded a goal(Charles De Ketelaere becoming an eternalpub quiz answer).Breel Embolo’s needless divewas exposed in 4K, Englandgot a little helpfrom on highagainst Norway,Gabriel Clarke stirred the pot, and Danny Murphy continued the sobering tale ofBob the Cat. The semis can be summed up in two key moments – Lamine Yamalturning Lucas Digne’s brain inside-out, and seven defenders failing to close downEnzo Fernández.Oh, England!View image in fullscreenLucas Digne having his blood twisted.Photograph: Franck Fife/AFP/Getty ImagesThegame of the tournamentcame in the third-place playoff,a ridiculous knockabout affairwon 6-4 by England, with Mbappé stat-padding his way to the Golden Boot. The final was not nearly as much fun, withWayne Rooneyneatly summarisingthe chaotic half-time show, and Fernández’s bone-headed second yellow card summing up Argentina’s reductive efforts. There’s no bigger moment than the one that wins it all, though, sotake a bow, Ferran Torres.Finally, after a tournament of tedious celebrity-spotting, it was nice to see Javier Bardem and Carlos Alcaraz genuinely celebrating Spain’s success, beforeKeyne Yamalmade the pitch his playground. Ominously, Luis de la Fuente’s red-and-gold army are co-hosts next time, when we may have to rattle off 64 memorable moments. We’ll get to work on that,you can start making your predictionsand we’ll do it all again in 2030.KEVIN KEEGAN (1951-2026)Tributes have poured in over the last 24 hours to Kevin Keegan afterhis death at the age of 75. His playing career was remarkable – he remains (and will surely remain for a long time) the only British player to be twice named European Footballer of the Year, he won the title three times with Liverpool (and the European Cup), the Bundesliga with Hamburg and was an England stalwart for a decade. But through TV appearances, adverts, even a top 40 hit, his impact as one of football’s early celebrity breakout stars can’t be measured by medals alone. And that’s before we get to his wild managerial career, remembered mostly – for good or ill – by his blow-up at Alex Ferguson in 1996. “Kevin carried the love of the game on his sleeve, there’s no question about that, and that carried right on to the fans, to the players,”said Ferguson on Tuesday. “That period at Newcastle was of emotion, it was excitement. They brought these great players to the club too and they added to it, and it gave Newcastle – I think – their best ever team.” Amid a deluge of other tributes, Alan Shearer perhaps summed up his feelings most succinctly: “My hero. My manager. My friend. Sleep easy Boss.”For more on Keegan’s remarkable career as a player and manager, here’s Rob Draper’swonderful tribute, Richard Williams’sobituary, Louise Taylor onhis three eras at Newcastleandhis life in pictures.View image in fullscreenKevin Keegan runs out at Anfield in August 1971 after signing for Liverpool.Photograph: Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesQUOTE OF THE DAY“As time goes by, you realise there is something much bigger than just a result. For years, this group has represented the team in the best possible way. It teaches that competing isn’t just about winning; it’s about giving your all for the jersey and never giving up. It has been a privilege to be part of a group that always stepped up, competed at the highest level, and defended these colours with pride, humility, and commitment” – despitetheir showing in the GWC final, Enzo Fernández hypes up Argentina’s performance without fully grasping the meaning of the words “pride” and “humility” as they return home.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: Juan Ignacio Roncoroni/EPARECOMMENDED FOLLOWINGBig Website: onBlueSpace,TikBook,Instachat, you name it.FOOTBALL DAILY LETTERSdouble quotation markPresident Trump confused (yesterday’s Football Daily)? Maybe that was down to someone telling him that Spain, who he has relentlessly criticised for not spending enough on defence, actually had the best one of all the GWC participants” – John Myles.double quotation markDespite your rather mixed review (yesterday’s Football Daily), I actually thought the GWC was quite good. My main focus wasthe Golden Boot race. Forget match quality or results. With so many elite scorers doing loads of goals it was quite intriguing to see who would come out on top. And for a wee while one of my countrymen, Elijah Just, had the impertinence to be in the mix with his three goals for New Zealand. Exciting” – Rod de Lisle.double quotation markSo the third-place game was 90 minutes of pointless action and the final was a shot-for-shot remake of the 2010 version? How fittingly American” – Rowan Sweeney.If you have any, please send letters tothe.boss@theguardian.com.Today’s prizeless letter o’ the day goes to … John Myles. Terms and conditions for our competitions, when we run them, arehere.This is an extract from our daily football email … Football Daily. To get the full version,just visit this page and follow the instructions.Explore more on these topicsFootballFootball Daily newsletterWorld Cup 2026World CupfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘Reclaim football’: Sepp Blatter joins La Liga chief in call for Gianni Infantino to resign | Gianni Infantino | The Guardian
description: Sepp Blatter has joined the attack on Gianni Infantino, his successor as the Fifa president, while Javier Tebas has criticised the lack of an opposition candidate next year
Author: Louise Taylor
Headline: Sepp Blatter has joined the attack on Gianni Infantino, his successor as the Fifa president, while Javier Tebas has criticised the lack of an opposition candidate next year
Content: Gianni Infantino has not had an opponent in a Fifa presidential election since 2016.Photograph: Emilee Chinn/Fifa/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenGianni Infantino has not had an opponent in a Fifa presidential election since 2016.Photograph: Emilee Chinn/Fifa/Getty ImagesGianni Infantino‘Reclaim football’: Sepp Blatter joins La Liga chief in call for Gianni Infantino to resignBlatter attacks successor as Fifa presidentJavier Tebas says system is ‘rotten at its core’Louise TaylorTue 21 Jul 2026 08.44 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Fifa president,Gianni Infantino, has come in for heavy criticism from his predecessor, Sepp Blatter, and the La Liga president, Javier Tebas, with the pair united in the belief that he should stand down.As Spain celebrated theirWorld Cupwin on Sunday, Tebas accused Infantino of “destroying the football industry” by prioritising the World Cup over national competitions. He claimed “his time is up”. Blatter has called for “fans, players and national associations to reclaim football and bring it back to its roots under new leadership”.David Squires on … Spain winning the World Cup final in the shadow of TrumpRead moreTebas is particularly opposed to the Fifa president’s enthusiasm forexpanding the 2030 tournamentto 64 teams from 48. “Increasing the number of national teams makes no sense,”Tebas told Italy’s La Gazzetta dello Sport.“The football industry is not just the World Cup. Not everything can revolve around the World Cup. It is national competitions that support this sport.“They [Fifa] are destroying the football industry, which generates tens of thousands of jobs, for an event that lasts 40 days and involves a minority of players. We need fewer national teams and more protection for national football, at all levels. They don’t realise they are making irresponsible decisions.”Tebas believes Infantino should resign, although he admits he finds it hard to envisage such a scenario. “He has the support of the system, of the federations. There is no opposition candidate, nobody wants to stand just to lose. It is a system rotten at its core.”Scandal-proof: why Infantino’s Fifa power can’t be touched – videoAfter 10 years at Fifa’s helm, Infantino is poised to be re‑elected for a fourth term in March,potentially standing unopposedfor the third time in a row, but Tebas believed the Swiss was a World Cup win away from being forced out. He argues that had the USA defeated Belgiumin their last‑16 gameInfantino might been mired in an unrecoverable scandal.The USA striker Folarin Balogun hada one-match ban suspendedby the Fifa disciplinary committee, facilitating his involvement against Belgium. The decision was taken after a phone call madeto Infantino by Donald Trump. Infantino said the decision to overturn Balogun’s ban was made independently by its disciplinary committee “based on the applicable regulations and the specific facts”.Tebas said: “The suspension of the American player’s ban was absolutely serious. They were lucky Belgium eliminated the USA because a case could have arisen that might have cost Infantino his job. Since Belgium won they were able to bury the matter. But these things are just the tip of the iceberg.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBlatter,a controversial figure during his tenure as Fifa president, appears to regard the Balogun case as a tipping point. “The longest World Cup has come to an end with the right champions,” he wrote on X. “But on the way to the final whistle, the tournament lost its credibility. Politics must not be allowed to overshadow the game.”Tebas feels much the same abouthydration breaksandextended half‑time intervals. “Fifa arranges things as it wants, for what it wants, for its own interests, certainly not football,” he said. “So if they need a 27-minute half-time break, they’ll have it.“Hydration breaks are a lie. We have them inLa Liga, but when it’s genuinely hot. The pitches in Dallas, Los Angeles, Atlanta had air conditioning; I had to put a jumper on in the stands. It was a lie, a break for advertising.”Fifa has been approached for comment.Explore more on these topicsGianni InfantinoFifaLa LigaWorld Cup 2026Football politicsWorld CupSepp BlatternewsShareReuse this content

title: Only four years to the next men’s World Cup – what can we expect from the 2030 edition?  | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: If you thought the 2026 tournament was sprawling, think again with six host countries over three continents getting involved in four years’ time
Author: Natalie Tan
Headline: If you thought the 2026 tournament was sprawling, think again with six host countries over three continents getting involved in four years’ time
Content: The Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, announces Morocco, Portugal and Spain as the hosts of the 2030 World Cup.Photograph: Harold Cunningham/FIFA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, announces Morocco, Portugal and Spain as the hosts of the 2030 World Cup.Photograph: Harold Cunningham/FIFA/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Only four years to the next men’s World Cup – what can we expect from the 2030 edition?If you thought the 2026 tournament was sprawling, think again with six host countries over three continents getting involved in four years’ timeNatalie TanTue 21 Jul 2026 06.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIf the2026 World Cupwas the tournament’s largest edition yet – featuring 48 teams for the first time in its 96-year history, being played in three different countries – the 2030 World Cup is going to have an even bigger footprint.How many countries will host the 2030 World Cup?Technically three. The tournament is set to be jointly hosted by Morocco, Portugal and Spain, marking the first time theWorld Cupwill be held in more than one confederation and the first time it will visit north Africa.In addition, though, and in honour of the World Cup’s 100-year anniversary, a special match is scheduled to be held at Estadio Centenario in Montevideo, Uruguay – the stadium that hosted the 1930 World Cup final. There will also be one match each in Argentina and Paraguay.How did this come about?The bidding process for the 2030 World Cup began in earnest in 2022, with the host to be officially selected at the 2024 Fifa congress. The governing body’s rules for rotating the tournament between continents meant that football federations from Asia (AFC) and North America (Concacaf) were ineligible to host following the Qatar 2022 and North America 2026 bids respectively.Uefa federations Spain and Portugal – unsuccessful in 2018 and 2022 and ineligible in 2026 – added Morocco to their joint bid in 2023. That same year, Conmebol federations Uruguay, Argentina, Paraguay and Chile launched their joint bid, with a proposed tournament name of “Centenary World Cup”.In October 2023, the Fifa council unanimously selected the Morocco-Portugal-Spain bid over the South American bid.And why are South America still hosting some games?The Fifa president, Gianni Infantino,announcedthat Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay would host one match each to celebrate the centenary of the World Cup “in the most appropriate way”. Uruguay hosted – and won – the tournament’s inaugural edition with Argentina beaten 4-2 in the final. A game will also be played in Paraguay in recognition of the country’s role as the home of Conmebol, the only confederation in existence in 1930.Fifa has alsoannounced“a unique centenary celebration ceremony”, scheduled to take place in Montevideo the week before the tournament begins on 13 June 2030.How many teams will take part?Initially the idea was for it to be 48 teams again, but during this World Cup Infantino raised to prospect of it being expanded with a further 16 nations.“That’s definitely ​an issue that will be examined and discussed in the relevant committees after this World Cup,”Infantino said. “When organising ⁠a World Cup, it’s important to organise it for the whole world – not just Europe and South America – but effectively the ⁠entire world.An expansion had already been suggested in 2025 when the Conmebol president, Alejandro Domínguez, put forth an official proposal to expand the number of participating teams to 64 in honour of the tournament’s centennial. “[It] will be unique because 100 years are celebrated only once,” Domínguezsaid.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhat will it look like?Spain last hosted the World Cup in 1982, the final of which involved Italy defeating West Germany in the Bernabéu. Co-hosts Portugal and Morocco have hosted various major sporting events, among them the 2004 Euros and the 2025 Afcon.The 2030 World Cup will take place across 17 host cities – one more than the 2026 edition – with 11 of the proposed 20 stadiums located in Spain. Among the 17 host cities are Marrakech and Tangier in Morocco, Porto and Lisbon in Portugal and Barcelona, Madrid and San Sebastián in Spain.The main talking point so far?Without a doubt the inclusion of the centenary match hosts. By rejecting the South American host bid but declaring that Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay would host one match each Fifa ensured that Conmebol would be ineligible to bid for the 2034 World Cup with Asia and Oceania the only two continents able to host. (The 2034 edition has since been awarded to Saudi Arabia, a decision that has been heavily criticised by human rights groups).And beyond that?Well, who knows? After theshenanigans of Donald Trump’s interventionand Folarin Balogun’s red card being suspended, could we see the Spanish king, Felipe VI, get involved after an over-zealous Lamine Yamal tackle in the last 64? With these guys in charge, nothing is off the table.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Liverpool owner FSG in talks to sell 30% stake to Amit Bhatia consortium for £1.35bn | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Liverpool’s owner, Fenway Sports Group, is negotiating a deal to sell a 30% stake in the club to a consortium led by Amit Bhatia for £1.35bn
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: Liverpool’s owner, Fenway Sports Group, is negotiating a deal to sell a 30% stake in the club to a consortium led by Amit Bhatia for £1.35bn
Content: Fenway Sports Group bought Liverpool in 2010.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PAView image in fullscreenFenway Sports Group bought Liverpool in 2010.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PALiverpoolLiverpool owner FSG in talks to sell 30% stake to Amit Bhatia consortium for £1.35bnBhatia resigns as a director of QPRMinority stake deal values the club at £4.5bnMatt HughesandWill UnwinTue 21 Jul 2026 16.13 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.20 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLiverpool’s owner is in talks over ­selling a minority stake to a consortium led by the former Queens Park Rangers co‑owner Amit ­Bhatia and backed by his father-in-law ­Lakshmi Mittal, the billionaire Indian steel magnate. The Guardian understands a sale of 30% of the club is being discussed, with an offer of around £1.35bn on the table.Fenway Sports Group confirmed in a statementto the Financial Timesthat it had begun negotiations with a group led by Bhatia over a ­potential minority share sale that would value ­Liverpool at about £4.5bn.Manchester United supporters groups in uproar over suspension of season ticketsRead more“An investment consortium led and ­managed by Amit Bhatia has expressed ­interest in ­making a strategic ­minority ­investment in Liverpool Football Club,” the statement said.It is believed that talks over the deal have been going on for three months.FSG paid £300m to take ­control of Liverpool in 2010 and is poised to secure a spectacular return on its investment. The ­Boston‑based group, led by John W Henry, which also owns the ­Boston Red Sox and Pittsburgh ­Penguins, has pre­viously held discussions over a minority sale without reaching agreement but, with the Mittal family ­possessing an estimated net worth of £23bn, there is no doubt the buyers have the resources to execute the transaction.Industry sources expressed surprise at the purported £4.5bn valuation but it is in line with recent deals.Chelsea were bought four years ago by the Todd Boehly/Clearlake Capital group for an overall £4.25bn, although £2.5bn was to purchase the shares from Roman Abramovich. These proceeds were deposited into a frozen UK bank account while £1.75bn was a legally binding commitment for future investment directly into the club. Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s purchase of 25% of Manchester United in 2024 valued the club at £4.3bn.In an indication of the ­seriousness of the consortium’s bid for ­Liverpool, Bhatia resigned as a director ofQPRon Tuesday after 19 years. The ­London-born entrepreneur has held numerous leadership roles at QPR since investing in the club in 2007. He said: “QPR has been a deeply important part of my life, and of my family’s life … I step back from my formal responsibilities with pride, gratitude and affection.”Liverpool are in the US under their new head coach, Andoni Iraola, for a pre-season tour which begins with a game against Sunderland in ­Nashville on Saturday.Explore more on these topicsLiverpoolQPRFenway Sports GroupBusinessnewsShareReuse this content

title: Morgan Rogers ‘excited to join biggest club in London’ after making £117m Chelsea move | Chelsea | The Guardian
description: Morgan Rogers has joined Chelsea from Aston Villa for a fee of £117m, signing a seven-year deal to become the Blues’ record signing
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Morgan Rogers has joined Chelsea from Aston Villa for a fee of £117m, signing a seven-year deal to become the Blues’ record signing
