Content: Morgan Rogers was enthused by the chance to work with Xabi Alonso and said Chelsea was a club he always admired from a young age.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenMorgan Rogers was enthused by the chance to work with Xabi Alonso and said Chelsea was a club he always admired from a young age.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesChelseaMorgan Rogers ‘excited to join biggest club in London’ after making £117m Chelsea moveEngland international signs seven-year contractXabi Alonso was key factor in Chelsea’s record signingPA MediaTue 21 Jul 2026 17.16 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMorgan Rogers has joined Chelsea fromAston Villafor a fee of £117m, signing a seven-year deal to become the Blues’ record signing.A deal was agreed on Saturday for the 23-year-old to move to Stamford Bridge as the club stole in ahead of Arsenal, who had been in talks to sign the England international and had appeared to be favourites to secure his signature.Men’s transfer window summer 2026: all deals from Europe’s top five leaguesRead moreIt is understood that the chance to work with the new Chelsea managerXabi Alonsowas a key factor in persuading Rogers, who has just returned from helping England to a third-place finish at the World Cup, to choose west London.He started in the2-1 semi-final defeat to Argentinain Atlanta last Wednesday as well as the6-4 win over France on Saturdaythat earned the bronze medal. He told his new club’s website: “I’m so excited. For me, Chelsea are the biggest club in London and a club I’ve always admired since I was a kid. I’m really excited about the project with the new manager, the players we’ve got and where the club is heading. That’s why I’m here and I can’t wait to get started.”A versatile forward who satisfies Chelsea’s need for a player that can play multiple attacking roles, he has enjoyed a rapid rise at Villa Park following his move from Middlesbrough in January 2024 and helped fire Villa to a top-four finish and the Europa League last season.He leaves Villa having scored 31 times in 125 appearances under Unai Emery.The deal represents one of the most significant buys of Chelsea owners BlueCo’s four-year reign. Co-owner Behdad Eghbali indicated towards the end of last season that the club had learned the lessons of an underwhelming campaign in which the team finished 10th and were thrashed in the Champions League last 16 by Paris Saint-Germain, and that they would be targeting players in the market that would be in a position to help them challenge for the title.View image in fullscreenRogers walks on the pitch at Stamford Bridge.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesThe former Liverpool, Real Madrid and Bayern Munich midfielder Alonso, a two-time Champions League winner as a player, was hired partly in order to convince the right calibre of player to move to Chelsea.The deal for Rogers is arguably the biggest the club have pulled off since Cole Palmer moved from Manchester City in 2023. Rogers becomes the fourth signing of the summer as the Blues overhaul their squad, following the arrivals of defender Marco Palestra, striker Emmanuel Emegha and winger Geovany Quenda.In June, the club failed in an attempt to reunite Alonso with Sunderland’s Granit Xhaka, with Chelsea unwilling to meet the Black Cats’ valuation of a player who will turn 34 in September.Explore more on these topicsChelseaXabi AlonsoTransfer windowAston VillanewsShareReuse this content

title: The Hundred returns with the hits and hope that fans feel the love again | The Hundred | The Guardian
description: Revamped tournament begins with pyrotechnics and plenty more fireworks from Nicholas Pooran’s bat
Author: Simon Burnton
Headline: Revamped tournament begins with pyrotechnics and plenty more fireworks from Nicholas Pooran’s bat
Content: MI London's Nicholas Pooran picks up runs on the leg side during the Hundred game against SunRisers Leeds at the Kia Oval.Photograph: Steven Paston/PAView image in fullscreenMI London's Nicholas Pooran picks up runs on the leg side during the Hundred game against SunRisers Leeds at the Kia Oval.Photograph: Steven Paston/PAThe HundredThe Hundred returns with the hits and hope that fans feel the love againRevamped tournament begins with pyrotechnics and plenty more fireworks from Nicholas Pooran’s batSimon Burntonat the Kia OvalTue 21 Jul 2026 17.09 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 17.05 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn 2012 John Newman’s first hit, as featured vocalist on Rudimental’sFeel the Love, went straight into the charts at No 1. The following year his first solo release,Love Me Again, did it too, also earning him a Brit nomination for single of the year and one for the Ivor Novellos as best song musically and lyrically.In 2022, as he prepared to relaunch a career that had reached such an extraordinarily early and ludicrously high peak, Newman was interviewed by the NME. “I’m crazy nervous but really excited,” he said. “I could become the biggest artist in the world or it might all go wrong, who knows?”New owners, team names and much bigger budgets: the 2026 Hundred explainedRead moreFour years later Newman took to a small, temporary stage built on the back few rows of SSE Energy Solutions Stand at a half-full Oval to provide the entertainment between the women’s and men’s opening games of the Hundred.“I love you so much!” he shouted as his set – very much relying on those early hits, but undoubtedly entertaining – built towards a climax. Literally on the first row of seats in front of the stage was a group of six people who didn’t even turn around.A gentle underarm throw away the SunRisers Leeds men’s squad warmed up while managing to completely ignore not only Newman’s best efforts but the fireworks launching sporadically just a matter of feet from them.Cricketcan be a cruel game, and this was it at its most vicious. Thanks for asking, Mr Newman but no, we will not love you again. Or, indeed, at all.Newman was, to be fair, performing to a niche audience, one for which few singers could possibly be more interesting than, say, Jason Roy, a man who a little later – while being forced to respond to fans’ questions by the BBC, communicating through an earpiece as he was prowling the outfield for MI London – was asked to identify his favourite childhood hobbies and reacted as if a hobby was an entirely foreign concept.View image in fullscreenPhoebe Litchfield goes on the attack during the women’s game at the Kia Oval.Photograph: Saud Ahmed/PPAUK/Shutterstock“What did I do? What did I used to love doing?” he gabbled, flummoxed, as if he had been asked to identify the great cities of ancient Mesopotamia. It is fair to say his mind was elsewhere. Eventually, after a bit of time and some gentle prompting, he identified “those model aeroplanes, those plastic ones. A bit like Lego but more nerdy.”Never mind Airfix kits, here it was a struggle to put together a decent partnership. After SunRisers reached an underpowered 143 MI London’s chase started badly: Roy, on his 36th birthday, demonstrated the batting struggles when nudging his second delivery to slip to depart for a duck, his team at that point two for four, and gradually their target started to slip out of reach. But the game was transformed when Nicholas Pooran joined Sam Curran; both went on to reach half-centuries – the Trinidadian off just 19 balls, Curran off 34 – and soon it was a stroll, their partnership yielding 93 runs off 40 balls of carnage.A little earlier, with unexpected time to fill after SunRisers Leeds cantered to victory in the women’s game – 108 genuinely was a feeble target – competition winners were rewarded with stuffed toys by two people in meerkat costumes representing the Hundred’s main sponsor, a popular market comparison website, while another in a wombat suit, without an obvious role in proceedings, compensated with some surprisingly grooved dance moves.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe SpinSubscribe to our cricket newsletter for our writers' thoughts on the biggest stories and a review of the week’s actionPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAs they did so ground staff pushed the boundary back to a more male-challenging distance, a somewhat condescending switch given that MI London’s Hayley Matthews and, even more eye-catchingly, SunRisers’ Phoebe Litchfield had just demonstrated their ability to deposit the ball deep into the stands (boundaries in the Hundred are permitted to be a maximum of 70 yards from the centre of the pitch in the women’s competition, and 90 yards in the men’s; for it to be a Pooran-challenging distance it would have had to be cradled by the crowd). Given the branded boundary padding that had to be detached, loaded on to carts, moved and reattached, rope relocation was a surprisingly complicated business, which must have been made even more irritating by said padding endlessly repeating the word “SIMPLES” in large capital letters.View image in fullscreenFireworks at the start of the men’s game.Photograph: Saud Ahmed/PPAUK/ShutterstockHundred teams are given a budget for their mid-game entertainment, which they can choose to supplement should they wish to sign up big names. MI London chose instead to bust their fireworks budget, and Nita Ambani, the Indian businesswoman whose Reliance Group owns 49% of the team, watched while a tiny fraction of her fortune went up in smoke as the men took to the field. The new-look Hundred may be a game owned by the wealthy, but this one belonged to Pooran.Explore more on these topicsThe HundredCricketfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Glasgow Games – and unicorn mascot – ready to emerge from shadow cast by 2014 | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: As the Commonwealth Games return to Scotland’s biggest city, the mood is markedly different from the last time Glasgow hosted the event
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: As the Commonwealth Games return to Scotland’s biggest city, the mood is markedly different from the last time Glasgow hosted the event
Content: Finnie the official mascot for Glasgow 2026 outside the Armadillo venue ahead of the Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PAView image in fullscreenFinnie the official mascot for Glasgow 2026 outside the Armadillo venue ahead of the Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PACommonwealth Games 2026Glasgow Games – and unicorn mascot – ready to emerge from shadow cast by 2014As the Commonwealth Games return to Scotland’s biggest city, the mood is markedly different from the last time Glasgow hosted the eventYara El-Shabouryin GlasgowTue 21 Jul 2026 15.25 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 22.17 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe first person to stop on Glasgow’s streets does not recognise the Commonwealth Games mascot, Finnie the unicorn. “I genuinely didn’t realise that the unicorn was the mascot,” Karl, a 33-year-old Glaswegian native, says with a laugh. “I caught a glimpse of one of the posters from the bus and thought: ‘Are the bronies making a comeback?’”It is a telling response. As the Commonwealth Games return to Scotland’s biggest city, the mood is markedly different from the last time Glasgow hosted the event in 2014,when anticipation built for months. This time, the Games have arrived with considerably less fanfare.How to watch the 2026 Commonwealth Games from Australia – explainerRead moreWhile there are signs that an international sporting event is imminent – lamppost banners, purple merchandise and the occasional volunteer – the loudest soundtrack comes from the relentless hum of construction machinery. George Square, the city’s principal plaza, remains one of several major building sites in central Glasgow. Work to transform its historic heart will not be completed until October, making the unfinished square an apt metaphor for aCommonwealth Gamesthat was never meant to resemble the spectacle of 2014.“I think the city’s missed a trick,” another passerby, Claudia, says. “If George Square had been finished in time, it could’ve been a proper meeting point for fans and visitors. Even if the Games are smaller, you still want something in the city centre that makes it feel like there’s a major event on. There is too much construction around the city.”Whenthe Australian state of Victoria withdrewfrom hosting in 2023 because of spiralling costs, the future of the Games looked uncertain.Glasgow stepped inwith an entirely different proposition: a compact, low-cost event using almost exclusively existing venues and expertise built for 2014. The programme has been reduced from 17 sports to 10, with events including hockey, badminton, diving and triathlon absent, though the Games will feature the largest number of Para sport events in history, with 47 finals across six sports. About 500,000 tickets have been made available compared with the 1.2 million sold in 2014, and tickets have still not sold out two days out. The organising budget sits at £150m-£160m, a fraction of the estimated £550m spent 12 years ago.Not everyone sees the quieter buildup as a problem. “People keep saying it’s not like 2014, but it was never meant to be,” says George, who was a volunteer 12 years ago. “Glasgow’s basically stepped in at the last minute and used the venues we’ve already got. Considering the circumstances, I think the organisers deserve a bit of credit.”View image in fullscreenScottish distance runner Eilish McColgan is one of the faces of the Games.Photograph: François Nel/Getty ImagesKarl, a volunteer this time around, says: “I don’t really get all the doom and gloom around it. It’s a smaller Games than 2014 and everybody knew that from the start. If a few thousand people come to Glasgow, the venues are busy and folk enjoy themselves, that’s a good outcome. It doesn’t have to be the biggest thing that’s ever happened to the city to be worth hosting.”Organisers also reject the idea that a stripped-back Games should be viewed as a diminished one. George Black, chair of the Games, says: “Years ago, the Commonwealth Games was a genuine topic of conversation across sport, across governments and across headlines. Glasgow, and Scotland, took the brave decision to step up and show that these Games matter and that there is no other city than Glasgow that could step forward and make these Games a success.”Madeleine McCann’s younger brother Sean to swim for Scotland at Glasgow 2026Read moreRather than George Square, organisers are directing local supporters without tickets towards Kelvingrove Park, which will screen up to 12 hours of sport each day alongside music and family entertainment. Whether that recreates the festival atmosphere of 2014 remains to be seen but there are hints that Glasgow’s affection for the Games still runs deeper than the subdued buildup suggests, with the Scottish distance runner Eilish McColgan admitting she might not have considered returning from marathon training for any other Commonwealth Games. “It’s hard to turn down a Commonwealth Games. It’s in Glasgow. If it was anywhere else in the world I probably wouldn’t bother.”Since 2014, Glasgow has welcomed Cop26, the inaugural Cycling World Championships and the World IndoorAthleticsChampionships. Has hosting major events become so routine in this city that another global sporting competition no longer feels extraordinary? “Once the competition begins, the city will do what it always does: just get on with it,” George says.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth GamesAthleticsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Iraola determined to keep Mac Allister and Alisson at Liverpool this season | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Andoni Iraola said Liverpool must strengthen a squad that will be without Hugo Ekitiké and Conor Bradley for several more months
Author: Andy Hunter
Headline: Andoni Iraola said Liverpool must strengthen a squad that will be without Hugo Ekitiké and Conor Bradley for several more months
Content: Alexis Mac Allister (left) and Alisson, shown enjoying Liverpool’s title celebrations 2025, are both part of next season’s plans.Photograph: Adam Vaughan/EPAView image in fullscreenAlexis Mac Allister (left) and Alisson, shown enjoying Liverpool’s title celebrations 2025, are both part of next season’s plans.Photograph: Adam Vaughan/EPALiverpoolIraola determined to keep Mac Allister and Alisson at Liverpool this seasonCoach aims to strengthen with Ekitiké and Bradley outMac Allister has been linked with move to Real MadridAndy Hunterin ChicagoTue 21 Jul 2026 17.42 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndoni Iraola has said he is determined to keep Alexis Mac Allister and Alisson for his first season as Liverpool head coach and must strengthen a squad that will be without Hugo Ekitiké and Conor Bradley for several more months.The dust had barely settled on Argentina’sWorld Cup final defeat by Spainwhen Mac Allister was linked with a move to Real Madrid. Iraola, in Chicago for the start of Liverpool’s pre-season tour of the US, accepted the midfielder is unlikely to be 100% ready for the Premier League opener at Newcastle due to a post-World Cup holiday. But he allayed doubts over Mac Allister’s long-term future at Liverpool, where he is under contract until 2028, by insisting the 27-year-old is part of next season’s plans.Liverpool owner FSG in talks to sell 30% stake to Amit Bhatia consortium for £1.35bnRead moreIraola said: “I think Alexis has been one of the best players at the club for the last few years, and he has had a very good World Cup, so it is normal that other clubs want our best players. It always happens in other markets. But I want to keep my good players. I’m looking forward more to signing new players rather than losing the ones we have here.”Alisson is another established figure whoseLiverpoolfuture has been questioned, with the Brazil international strongly linked with Juventus at the end of last season. Iraola has not met Alisson or Mac Allister in person due to their World Cup commitments but has spoken with both, and guaranteed the former will remain a Liverpool player next season.“I am very happy with the situation we have right now in the goalkeeping area,” said Iraola. “We have Alisson, we have Giorgi [Mamardashvili]. Now we are going to send Armin [Pecsi] on loan but we have the guarantee of the three keepers here. Freddie [Woodman] played some games last season, I think it is a guarantee for us.“Alisson is also someone in a changing room that has lots of references, in terms of experience. He is someone that definitely will help us in this process of adaptation of the new signings and the young players. We need the references like Virgil [van Dijk], Alisson, Joe Gomez; players that have been here for a long time, and I am sure we will have this help.”Liverpool have brought a young squad on their US tour, which currently involves a training camp at Chicago Fire before the first game against Sunderland in Nashville on Saturday. Florian Wirtz, Alexander Isak and Ryan Gravenberch will join the tour next week when Van Dijk, Cody Gakpo and Alisson will begin their pre-season training regimes back on Merseyside.Iraola admitted Liverpool are “very thin” in terms of defensive numbers and while a fit Bradley would be considered first choice at right-back, his recovery from a serious knee injury sustained in January means the club could be forced into the market.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Stefan [Bajcetic] and Wataru [Endo] are close to training with the group,” he said. “With the others [injured players], we’ll have to wait more. The closest one to returning after that is [Giovanni] Leoni. Conor and Hugo are way off. We are talking about months.”Explore more on these topicsLiverpoolAndoni IraolanewsShareReuse this content

title: Evenepoel pips Pogacar again as Tour de France night-time doping test row deepens | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: Remco Evenepoel took back-to-back stage wins in the Tour de France, and eroded some of Tadej Pogacar’s overall lead, with victory in the stage 16 time trial from Evian to Thonon-les-Bains
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: Remco Evenepoel took back-to-back stage wins in the Tour de France, and eroded some of Tadej Pogacar’s overall lead, with victory in the stage 16 time trial from Evian to Thonon-les-Bains
Content: Remco Evenepoel enjoys the view from the podium after his stage 16 Tour win.Photograph: Pete Goding/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenRemco Evenepoel enjoys the view from the podium after his stage 16 Tour win.Photograph: Pete Goding/ShutterstockTour de France 2026Evenepoel pips Pogacar again as Tour de France night-time doping test row deepensBelgian wins time trial, says chase of leader ‘not over yet’Florian Lipowitz, third last year, quits race after crashingJeremy Whittlein Thonon-les-BainsTue 21 Jul 2026 15.19 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRemco Evenepoel took back-to-back stage wins in theTour de France, and eroded some of Tadej Pogacar’s overall lead, with victory in the stage 16 time trial from Evian to Thonon-les-Bains.The Olympic road and time trial champion, now second overall in the Tour, won on the Belgian national day from Pogacar by 28 seconds, to reduce the Slovenian’s advantage to just over four and a half minutes. It was the third Tour time trial win of his career.Tour de France 2026: Evenepoel wins stage 16 time trial but Lipowitz crashes out – liveRead more“It’s been a while since I’ve been on this level so it feels amazing to be in this good shape in the Tour,” Evene­poel said. The Belgian, who described Pogacar as a “superstar”, added: “It’s not over yet. There are still some hard stages to come.”Pogacar’s UAE Emirates XRG teammate, Isaac del Toro, remains in third place, but Paul Seixas is now just 20 seconds away from a podium position after taking time on the Mexican rider in the race against the clock.Evenepoel’s success was tempered by the loss of his Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe teammate, Florian Lipowitz, third in the Tour last year, who quit the race after crashing.Lipowitz fell on a tight right-hand bend as he exited a downhill section. He was carried away from the scene on a stretcher and it was later confirmed he suffered a broken collarbone. He was the second 2025 podium finisher – after Jonas Vingegaard on Sunday – to leave the race in 72 hours.While the race continued, the debate over the ITA drug testing agency’s nocturnal activities, which involved 2am and 5am visits to Vingegaard and Pogacar respectively last Sunday, continued.View image in fullscreenTadej Pogacar in action during the time trial.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersThe decision to test both riders outside of the usual window of 6am to 11pm has been heavily criticised by some, despite the tests being authorised by the Paris prosecutor’s office. “With a French judge it’s not going to be hard to get that warrant,” Jonathan Vaughters, manager of the EF Education EasyPost team, said.“They’re going to look at the history of the sport. To a French judge that is not a sports scientist, or a haematologist, I do not think it would be too difficult to convince them. I doubt if they had to fight that hard for it.”Vaughters insisted that he is “very supportive” of the ITA. “I think this is an example of them doing a good job. I just don’t think you should read into the fact they got a warrant from a judge. My guess is the bar is not that high.”Pascal Chanteur, vice-president of the CPA, the professional riders association that has been critical of nocturnal testing, also questioned the Paris prosecutor’s action, saying “the judge knew nothing of cycling”.“The CPA supports night-time testing if there’s enough evidence,” Chanteur said, suggesting also that there was no significance in Vingegaard being tested at 2am and Pogacar at 5am. “This is because of the ITA’s limited resources.”The French sports code specifies that testing procedures “must be ­carried out under conditions that ensure strict proportionality between the infringement of the athlete’s rights and the objectives of the fight against doping”.View image in fullscreenAn artist paints as Fred Wright rides past on the way to Thonon-les-Bains.Photograph: Thibault Camus/APSpeaking in May this year to Reuters, Olivier Banuls, the ITA’s director of testing, said: “We do a lot of testing but we don’t do mass testing. To do mass testing is to throw money out the window.”The outcome of the tests on both riders, however, remains unknown, leaving the Tour in a state of limbo, carrying on regardless, despite the cloud of suspicion that hangs over Pogacar who is closing in on what would be a record-equalling fifth Tour win.Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tadej PogacarTour de FranceCyclingnewsShareReuse this content

title: French Open becomes first grand slam to offer players share of tournament revenue | Tennis | The Guardian
description: The French Open is to offer players a share of the tournament’s revenue as prize money, the first grand slam to do so
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: The French Open is to offer players a share of the tournament’s revenue as prize money, the first grand slam to do so
Content: The French Open’s willingness to commit to a profit-share model is a major development.Photograph: James Fearn/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe French Open’s willingness to commit to a profit-share model is a major development.Photograph: James Fearn/Getty ImagesTennisFrench Open becomes first grand slam to offer players share of tournament revenueMove is significant step in row over prize moneyUS Open will come under pressure to follow suitMatt HughesTue 21 Jul 2026 11.34 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheFrench Openhas become the first grand slam tournament to offer players a share of the event’s revenue in a significant step forward in the row over prize money. Officials from Roland Garros made the offer to the players’ representative, Larry Scott, in talks at Wimbledon a fortnight ago in a move that will increase pressure on the US Open, which is due to announce its prize fund for this year’s tournament at the start of next month.While an agreement has yet to be reached, the French Open’s willingness to commit to a revenue-share model to determine prize money is a major development, and sets Roland Garros apart from the three other grand slam tournaments.The summer of the underdog: why outsiders are the most compelling sights in sport | Natalie TanRead moreDebbie Jevans, chair of the All England Club, angered the players last month by saying that it made “no sense” to use tournament revenues to determine prize money, which led them tothreaten a media boycottfor the first week of Wimbledon, althoughthat protest was cancelled.The French Open appears to have taken a different approach, with the commitment to revenue sharing being accompanied by a willingness to contribute to player pensions and healthcare and give the players a greater say in the running of the tournament.The players are seeking guarantees that all the grand slam tournaments will pay 16% of their revenue in prize money with immediate effect, rising to 22% by 2030. The grand slam tournaments have offered significant increases in prize money in recent years, but the players want an agreed formula based on revenue sharing rather than having to wait each year for notification of the annual award.The US Open is under particular pressure as it has had longer to reach an agreement with the players and the tournament next month coincides with the arrival of a new chief executive of the USTennisAssociation, Craig Tiley.A number of players – including the men’s world No 1, Jannik Sinner – have threatened not to play in the US Open’s mixed doubles that precedes the singles at Flushing Meadows, if significant progress is not made.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |FortnightlySign up toThe HotspotA new fortnightly newsletter covering the intersection of sport and the climate crisisPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast year the US Open increased its prize money by 20% to $90m, compared with $75m the year before. A similar rise this year would mean the players receive a total prize pot of more than $100m for the first time.Explore more on these topicsTennisFrench OpenUS Open tennisWimbledonUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: UK military ‘eyes innovative electric flying taxi’ as 2029 launch planned | Aerospace industry | The Guardian
description: Vertical Aerospace says government has given £10m backing, taking its total investment in the project to £48m
Author: Jasper Jolly
Headline: Vertical Aerospace says government has given £10m backing, taking its total investment in the project to £48m
Content: Vertical Aerospace made the first public demonstration of its Valo electric VTOL aircraft Valo transitioning from flight using aeroplane wings to vertical descent.Photograph: Toby Shepheard/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenVertical Aerospace made the first public demonstration of its Valo electric VTOL aircraft Valo transitioning from flight using aeroplane wings to vertical descent.Photograph: Toby Shepheard/AFP/Getty ImagesAerospace industryUK military ‘eyes innovative electric flying taxi’ as 2029 launch plannedVertical Aerospace says government has given £10m backing, taking its total investment in the project to £48mJasper Jollyin FarnboroughTue 21 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBritish flying taxi startup Vertical Aerospace has said the UK government has expressed interest in buying a military variant of its aircraft as it races to gain approval for flights with paying passengers.Vertical also welcomed another £10m grant from the government, taking itstotal UK state funding to £48m.The company demonstrated its prototype model of the flying taxi on Monday at the Farnborough international airshow, an industry event at an airfield west of London.The aircraft is one of several electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft which manufacturers are hoping will revolutionise urban transport by landing quietly – relative to a helicopter – in built-up areas with no direct emissions.Vertical Aerospace's electric plane can take off and land vertically - loopHowever, those companies have found it much harder than initially expected to start flying passengers, and several have collapsed after an investment bubble during the coronavirus pandemic burst.Vertical itself faced a cash crisis in 2024which led to the eventual exit of founderStephen Fitzpatrick, the owner of energy company Ovo. It was taken over by the US distressed debt investor Jason Mudrick.The company survived and is now going through the complex process of gaining certification for a totally new aircraft from the UK’s Civil Aviation Authority.It is racing against itsUS rival Joby Aviation, which has also switched from conventional to vertical flight in public tests from New York’s JFK airport to Manhattan. Other rivals that are further behind are Eve, owned by Brazilian jet maker Embraer, and the US startup Archer Aviation – as well as several Chinese companies.Monday’s flight was the first public demonstration of Vertical’s aircraft transitioning from conventional flight using its aeroplane wings to vertical flight – when four of its propellers tilt upwards.The aircraft flew in to land on the runway at Farnborough, before hovering in the air, helicopter-like, and dropping slowly to the tarmac.View image in fullscreenVertical Aerospace’s Valo electric VTOL aircraft at the Farnborough international airshow.Photograph: Toby Shepheard/AFP/Getty ImagesVertical’s focus remains on its electric model, which offers the prospect of flight with no direct carbon dioxide pollution, but it is also working on a hybrid system that will add a gas turbine.While burning fossil fuels to power the motors would cause significant emissions, it could also allow greater range and quicker refuelling than recharging batteries.Stuart Simpson, the Vertical chief executive, said in an interview that the hybrid option opens up “dual use” – civilian and military.He said: “It leverages the commercial airframe, but with a different powertrain, which the military are very interested in.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Because silent takeoff and landing, you can cruise up to 1,000 miles in it, low noise signature, low heat signature – [it is] really interesting.”Vertical said the UK government had “confirmed interest from the Ministry of Defence in the defence applications for Vertical’s eVTOL, hybrid-electric and autonomous capabilities”.The company plans for similar flights for four passengers in its Valo production aircraft in 2029, after announcing a further delay to certification earlier this month. The company had originally aimed to be in service by 2025.Simpson said “we’re really confident” the company would meet the new target timeline after communications with the regulator.“I set 2028 hand in hand with them,” Simpson said. The CAA and EU Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) “both signed up to work towards 2028, and they’re both comfortable with 2029 because they knew they could do 2028”.Vertical said the government money was offered “in connection with Vertical finalising the down-selection of UK sites for its aircraft and battery facilities”.It intends to build about 150 aircraft a year at its existing facility in Kemble, Gloucestershire, but it eventually wants a larger site to build 1,000 a year, as well as to assemble its battery packs.The company said building in the UK would make it easier to certify the aircraft.Vertical says flights should cost about the same as a taxi ride, because the same aircraft will be able to do several return trips in the time a cab could do a journey such as from London’s Heathrow airport to the centre of the city.Explore more on these topicsAerospace industryManufacturing sectorDefence policynewsShareReuse this content

title: Thames Water joins hosepipe ban as England and Wales approach drought status | Water | The Guardian
description: About 23 million people across country affected by water-saving measures with ‘prolonged dry weather’ in eight areas
Author: Damien Gayle
Headline: About 23 million people across country affected by water-saving measures with ‘prolonged dry weather’ in eight areas
Content: Thames Water’s hosepipe ban will cover more than 10 million customers across London and the Thames valley.Photograph: Yui Mok/PAView image in fullscreenThames Water’s hosepipe ban will cover more than 10 million customers across London and the Thames valley.Photograph: Yui Mok/PAWaterThames Water joins hosepipe ban as England and Wales approach drought statusAbout 23 million people across country affected by water-saving measures with ‘prolonged dry weather’ in eight areasHow can I water my garden in a hosepipe ban and will there be drought this summer?Thames Water investors offer ‘golden share’ in bid to head off nationalisationDamien GayleTue 21 Jul 2026 10.05 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAbout 23 million people will be banned from using hosepipes by this weekend, asEnglandand Wales moves closer to drought status.On TuesdayThames Waterbecame the latest water company to announce ahosepipe ban, covering the more than 10 million customers it supplies water to across London and the Thames valley.It came afterSouth East Wateron Monday extended its ban – already covering Kent – to cover 2.4 million customers in Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire. Five other water companies have already imposed bans.Months of dry, hot weather, including an unprecedentedthree heatwavesand the driest spring on record for England and Wales, have left water supplies severely depleted, particularly in the south and south-east.Despite an exceptionally wet winter, drought now seems inevitable. On Tuesday the NationalDroughtGroup (NDG), an official body that brings the government together with water companies, weather forecasters, farmers and conservationists, said eight areas in England were in “prolonged dry weather” – the stage before drought.These areas are East Anglia; the West Midlands; Devon, Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly; Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire; Hertfordshire and London; Wessex; Thames; and Solent and the South Downs.Thames Water said its region had just 40% of the usual rainfall during spring, while demand for water was 10% higher than normal in the Thames valley and home counties, and 7% higher in London.The 100m litre-a-day (22m gallons-a-day) increase in demand was equivalent to adding the entire city of Manchester to the network, the company said. “We would not be taking this step unless it was necessary to protect water supplies for everyone and the environment,” said Nevil Muncaster, its director of strategic resources.Thames Water’s ban officially begins on Thursday, but the company is asking customers to cut their use straight away. South EastWateralso asked customers to observe restrictions, although the extension to its ban does not come into force until Saturday. Customers of Affinity Water, Anglian Water, Cambridge Water, Southern Water and South West Water are already banned from using hoses.Only 13% of rivers were classed as at normal levels for the time of the year, while national reservoir storage was at 78.8%, about 5% lower than the long-term average.View image in fullscreenA combine harvester cuts through a wheat field at Plastow Green, Hampshire. Crops have ripened early, said Paul Tompkins of the National Farmers’ Union.Photograph: David Hartley/ShutterstockThis montha report foundthe UK’s climate extremes are becoming increasingly normal, as temperatures are pushed higher by carbon emissions in the atmosphere.The extreme weather was not just affecting water companies, NDG members said, but also having a severe impact on the environment and food production.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every daySign up toHeadlines UKGet the day’s headlines and highlights emailed direct to you every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Farmers and growers across the country are working around the clock to harvest crops which have come early, keep fruit and vegetables watered, and care for their livestock,” said Paul Tompkins, the deputy president of the National Farmers’ Union. “For many arable farmers, this could be another difficult and expensive harvest after extreme weather impacts in both 2024 and 2025, which saw both drought and flood.”Meanwhile, 53 wildfires have hit across the country, including on prime agricultural land and delicate protected sites.“Our soils and vegetation have lost so much moisture in this heat that any natural resistance to a fire has significantly reduced,” said Rob Gazzard, who advises the Forestry Commission on wildfires. “Not only does it make vegetation more likely to burn, but it increases the intensity of fires once they start. This means fires take hold and spread quickly and are difficult to suppress.”Temperatures have cooled in recent days from the record-breaking spell of hot weather the country endured over several weeks at the start of summer. But no little respite from the dry weather is expected.“Much of England has experienced a notably warm and dry first half of summer, with July particularly dry and some locations recording little or no rainfall so far this month,” said Will Lang, the chief meteorologist at the Met Office.“There are some signs of more changeable weather at times into early August, which may bring rain to some areas. However, confidence in the detail remains low, and any rainfall, particularly across southern parts of the UK, is unlikely to be widespread or substantial.”Explore more on these topicsWaterWater industryDroughtThames WaterSouth East WaterWildfiresExtreme heatnewsShareReuse this content

title: Trump’s HIV funding cuts having ‘severe and devastating’ impact around the world | Global health | The Guardian
description: The President’s Emergency Plan for Aids Relief has saved 26m lives globally, but slashing of funds and dismantling of USAID have forced clinics to shut in dozens of countries
Author: Kat Lay
Headline: The President’s Emergency Plan for Aids Relief has saved 26m lives globally, but slashing of funds and dismantling of USAID have forced clinics to shut in dozens of countries
Content: Nairobi’s Nyumbani Children's Home, which cares for orphans with HIV, in February last year.Photograph: T Mukoya/ReutersView image in fullscreenNairobi’s Nyumbani Children's Home, which cares for orphans with HIV, in February last year.Photograph: T Mukoya/ReutersGlobal healthTrump’s HIV funding cuts having ‘severe and devastating’ impact around the worldThe President’s Emergency Plan for Aids Relief has saved 26m lives globally, but slashing of funds and dismantling of USAID have forced clinics to shut in dozens of countriesSupported byAbout this contentKat Lay, Global health correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNo part of the United States’ anti-HIV programme has escaped unscathed from Donald Trump’s funding cuts, despite pledges from the administration to protect care that saved lives around the world, a new report has found.Funding cuts to the President’s Emergency Plan for Aids Relief (Pepfar) have caused “severe and devastating” disruption globally, with thousands of staff laid off and many clinics closed, according to research being presented next week at the26th International Aids Conferencein Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. It warns that changes in US policy risk reversing progress in the fight against HIV/Aids.Organisations still in receipt of US funding are also changing what they do to comply with the Trump administration’s policiestargeting diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) initiatives, reproductive rights and the provision of gender-affirming care, researchers found.Elise Lankiewicz, from the Aids research foundation Amfar and co-lead author ofits Pepfar Pulse report, said: “Disruption was incredibly widespread. No part of the programme really escaped unscathed – and that includes areas of the programme that this administration intended to protect.”Pepfar iscredited with saving more than 26 million livesand changing the trajectory of the HIV epidemic since it was set up in 2003 during George W Bush’s presidency.It works through partner organisations around the world, who find people at risk and offer them testing for HIV, protection against the virus, such as condoms or medicines, and treatment if needed.However, many of its grants were abruptly withdrawn by the Trump administration in 2025 – more than half had been managed via USAID, theagency dissolved by Trump in July 2025. A waiver that was issued months before, in February 2025, theoretically allowed programmes providing life-saving treatment or preventing mother-to-child HIV transmission to continue.“The reality is it’s incredibly hard to cut one award without there being ripple effects throughout the system,” said Lankiewicz.View image in fullscreenA protest in New York calling for the closure of Delaney Hall immigrant detention centre and denouncing the Trump administration’s cuts to HIV/Aids funding.Photograph: G Randazzo/ZumaAmfar surveyed 166 organisations across 46 countries that had been due to receive Pepfar funding in 2025. More than half of the respondents had at least one terminated award, resulting in more than 1,700 closed clinics, drop-in centres or other sites. Prevention services were particularly hard hit. More than 16,000 full-time staff lost their jobs.Almost two-thirds of organisations providing services to prevent mother-to-child HIV transmission reported disruption, with a fifth ending services entirely. A fifth of organisations providing HIV care and treatment stopped at least one service, the researchers found. Many said they were unable to obtain supplies of laboratory commodities, condoms or drugs.Five surveyed organisations had closed; three as a direct result of losing Pepfar funding. However, the researchers said: “This is likely a significant under-count, since closed organisations would no longer have active email accounts by which to receive the survey.”Services for the key populations of people most vulnerable to HIV – such as gay or transgender people, injectable drug users or sex workers – were most likely to have ended.Almost four out of five respondents said they had been asked to restrict their work to comply with an additional US policy – typically anti-DEI executive orders, but also anexpanded “global gag rule”, said Lankiewicz.“They made pretty dramatic shifts to their programming to align with these new policies,” she said, adding that this included ending services tailored to specific people such as sex workers, as well as support for victims of gender-based violence.Greg Millett, a member of the study team, said: “Until now, much of the discussion about the impact of recent Pepfar disruptions has relied on projections and modelling. This study provides some of the first real-world data.”The results mirror earlieranalysis of US government data, which showed a severe drop in numbers of frontline health workers, HIV diagnoses and prevention programmes.“These disruptions threaten to reverse progress toward controlling the HIV epidemic,” Millett told a press briefing ahead of the Aids 2026 conference, which is organised by the International Aids Society.Explore more on these topicsGlobal healthAids and HIVUSAIDDonald TrumpTrump administrationUS foreign policyReproductive rightsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Abandoned monkey found in laundry bag on London bus | Animal welfare | The Guardian
description: Animal welfare charities say marmoset could be ‘first of many’ since new law on primate licenses and microchipping
Author: Rachel Hall
Headline: Animal welfare charities say marmoset could be ‘first of many’ since new law on primate licenses and microchipping
Content: The female common marmoset has been named Oyster, after the London transport payment system, and is said to have settled in ‘really well’ at a sanctuary.Photograph: RSPCA/PAView image in fullscreenThe female common marmoset has been named Oyster, after the London transport payment system, and is said to have settled in ‘really well’ at a sanctuary.Photograph: RSPCA/PAAnimal welfareAbandoned monkey found in laundry bag on London busAnimal welfare charities say marmoset could be ‘first of many’ since new law on primate licenses and microchippingRachel HallTue 21 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA “stressed and dehydrated” monkey has been found in a laundry bag on aLondonbus in what animal welfare charities warn could be the “first of many” abandonments now owners must obtain primate licenses.The female common marmoset was discovered on a 302 bus waiting in Kensal Rise, north-west London, on Friday by a passenger whose buggy was obstructed by the bag containing the monkey in a cage. The monkey has since been named Oyster after the London transport payment system.The bus driver contacted the RSPCA, which handled 238 reports of primate neglect and cruelty between 2021 and 2025.New legislation passed in April mandated that all primates kept inEnglandare licensed and microchipped. Owners who failed to obtain a license by 6 April risk an unlimited fine or jail.View image in fullscreenOyster was abandoned on a 302 bus in Kensal Rise, north-west London, in a cage inside a laundry bag.Photograph: RSPCA/PAAlison Cronin, the director of Monkey World and a contributor to the new legislation, said over the past decade “a lot of well-meaning people … have fallen victim to people on social media promoting these wild animals as good domestic pets”.She said UK breeders were “making a killing” out of this misconception, though she welcomed the law change’s requirement for them to ensure they were only selling monkeys into homes with suitable environments.Although a Born Free survey in January suggested there had been limited uptake of the new licensing system, Cronin said she had observed that many owners have since obtained licenses.She said abandoned monkeys were “nothing new” and not necessarily a reflection of the law change. “These animals have been out there and subject to abuse and neglect for all of the years running up to this,” she said.The driver and Metroline staff are aiding RSPCA inquiries to trace the person responsible for dumping the marmoset, which had not been microchipped.Clare Dew, RSPCA London’s chief investigator, said Oyster’s abandonment “was clearly intentional”, and she suspected it was “a direct result of the new legislation”.The “frightened marmoset” was well concealed in the bag, Dew said. “She’d been left with some apple but was, understandably, very stressed and dehydrated.”Oyster is now healthy and living in a sanctuary with specialist primate facilities, where she “settled in really well over the weekend” and will be introduced to a male companion shortly.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every daySign up toHeadlines UKGet the day’s headlines and highlights emailed direct to you every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionEvie Button, a RSPCA exotic animals expert, described primates as “highly intelligent and social wild animals who need a lot of space, mental stimulation and appropriate companions – needs that can’t be met by human company alone”.She added: “There is a real concern that keepers who cannot or do not want to license their pet may now instead abandon them.”The government estimates that 5,000 primates are kept as pets in England.Chris Lewis, the captivity research and policy manager at Born Free, called on local authorities and the government to raise greater awareness of the law change.“Primates are complex, intelligent animals whose needs cannot be met when kept as a pet,” he said. “Although not the complete ban which experts and primatologists advocated for, the introduction of the Animal Welfare (Primate Licences) (England) Regulations represented an important step towards protecting privately kept primates, but their success depends on effective implementation and strict enforcement.”Explore more on these topicsAnimal welfareAnimalsLondonEnglandPetsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Milky Way flipped in collision with dwarf galaxy 10bn years ago, astronomers say | Astronomy | The Guardian
description: Durham University researchers find cataclysmic direct hit with Gaia Sausage sent Milky Way into its current position
Author: Ian Sample
Headline: Durham University researchers find cataclysmic direct hit with Gaia Sausage sent Milky Way into its current position
Content: The Milky Way galaxy and stars over Emerald Lake in the Lassen Volcanic national park, California.Photograph: John Sirlin/AlamyView image in fullscreenThe Milky Way galaxy and stars over Emerald Lake in the Lassen Volcanic national park, California.Photograph: John Sirlin/AlamyAstronomyMilky Way flipped in collision with dwarf galaxy 10bn years ago, astronomers sayDurham University researchers find cataclysmic direct hit with Gaia Sausage sent Milky Way into its current positionIan SampleScience editorTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAstronomers have found evidence that the Milky Way was flipped on its side following a direct hit from a wayward galaxy long before the solar system was born.The cataclysmic collision took place about 10bn years ago when the Milky Way was struck head-on by an incoming dwarf galaxy known – for purely astronomical reasons – as the Gaia Sausage.The Milky Way tore the interloper to shreds and absorbed its stars, but the chaos unleashed in the event probably flipped the entire disc of the Milky Way by more than 90 degrees into the position seen today, researchers said.Astronomers atDurham Universityused supercomputer simulations to show that Milky Way-like galaxies involved in head-on collisions with the Gaia Sausage were likely to experience a “disc flip” that dramatically, if slowly, reoriented the galaxy. “It probably takes at least a few hundred million years,” said Kirill Batrakov, the lead researcher on the project.Computer model shows Milky Way colliding with neighbouring galaxyThe researchers made the discovery while investigating the curiously slow rotation of stars in the Milky Way’s halo. The halo is the sparse, roughly ball-shaped cloud of stars that surrounds the galaxy. Most of the halo’s stars originally formed in smaller galaxies that strayed too close to the Milky Way and merged over time.Previous observations from the European Space Agency’s Gaia mission show that stars in the Milky Way’s disc orbit the heart of the galaxy at about 220km (137 miles) a second. But a longstanding mystery has surrounded those in the stellar halo, which rotate far more slowly, circling the galactic centre at about 25km a second.In search of an explanation for the disparity, the Durham team turned to computer simulations of evolving Milky Way-like galaxies. The simulations showed that galaxies ended up with slow-moving stars in their haloes if they were struck head-on by nearby galaxies the size of the Gaia Sausage and subsequently experienced a disc flip.Details are being presented this week at the Royal Astronomical Society’s NationalAstronomyMeeting in Birmingham.The ancient collision first came to light in 2018, when an international team of astronomers used further observations from the Gaia mission to map the passage of stars through the Milky Way.Some were found to be on radical, sausage-shaped orbits, which the researchers traced back to a dwarf galaxy breaking up as it ploughed straight into the heart of the Milky Way. The stars’ extreme orbits are a smoking gun for the colossal collision and led to the dwarf galaxy being named the Gaia Sausage.View image in fullscreenObservations from the European Space Agency’s Gaia satellite have helped map the Milky Way and neighbouring galaxies.Photograph: European Space Agency/AFP/Getty ImagesAstronomers now recognise the collision as the galaxy’s last major merger and a defining event in the Milky Way’s history. It fundamentally reshaped the galaxy, creating the bulge at the centre and dominating the stellar halo.Though classed as a dwarf galaxy, the Gaia Sausage contained stars, gas and dark matter amounting to more than 10bn times the mass of the sun. The next major upheaval might not happen for a few billion years when a nearby dwarf galaxy called the Large Magellanic Cloud merges with the Milky Way.Another merger is already under way with the far smaller Sagittarius dwarf galaxy. That is expected to have a much less dramatic impact on our cosmic neighbourhood. “It’s not as massive as the Gaia Sausage was, so it is not going to influence the Milky Way as much, and the Milky Way itself is far more massive now than it was back then,” said Batrakov.Explore more on these topicsAstronomySpaceDurham UniversityEuropean Space AgencynewsShareReuse this content

title: Stand still, make eye contact: Denmark tells tourists how to behave in a wolf encounter | Denmark | The Guardian
description: Government urges visitors not to approach or feed wolves as it manages safety amid growing population
Author: Miranda Bryant
Headline: Government urges visitors not to approach or feed wolves as it manages safety amid growing population
Content: Wolves started returning to Denmark in 2012 after they were eradicated 200 years earlier.Photograph: Rolf Larsen/Getty Images/500px PlusView image in fullscreenWolves started returning to Denmark in 2012 after they were eradicated 200 years earlier.Photograph: Rolf Larsen/Getty Images/500px PlusDenmarkStand still, make eye contact: Denmark tells tourists how to behave in a wolf encounterGovernment urges visitors not to approach or feed wolves as it manages safety amid growing populationMiranda BryantNordic correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 09.45 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDanish authorities have advised tourists to stand still, make eye contact and put up “maximum resistance” in the event of attack if they encounter a wolf while on holiday.After a 200-year absence, wolvesstarted returning to Denmarkin 2012. There are now estimated to be 49 wolves on the Danish peninsula of Jutland.Generally, it is rare for wolves to even approach humans, but in the area around the popular tourism destination of Oksbøl, young ones were spotted doing so earlier this year.The municipality of Varde and Visit Vesterhavet have distributed flyers containing the government advice around holiday homes, campsites and tourist offices in Danish, English and German.Tourists are told to keep calm and still in the presence of a wolf, hold eye contact, take a picture of the animal and, if it will not go away of its own accord, shout, clap hands and wave arms. They are advised not to follow or to turn their backs on an approaching wolf. The tip is to “stare directly at it” to “show dominance”.While there have been no wolf attacks on humans inDenmark, in the event of it happening, tourists are urged: “You must offer maximum resistance: hit and kick and use any blunt weapon.”The government advice also states that children under the age of 10 should be accompanied by an adult in the Oksbøl area.Wolves have long had a presence inDenmark– the oldest wolf bones in the country are 13,000 years old – but after the last wolf was shot in 1813 they only started to make a comeback in the last two decades.Their presence has received a mixed response, often dividing people along political lines. In April, two ponies were killed by wolves in a fortnight. Soon after, four MPs from the far-right Danish People’s party werereported to policefor sharing a Facebook post calling on people to “Shoot the wolf”.Wolves are protected by EU law.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenThe return of wolves has divided opinions in Denmark.Photograph: Ricochet64/Getty Images/iStockphotoThe wolf expert Peter Sunde, a wildlife ecology professor at Aarhus University, said: “During the winter and spring of 2026, at least two young wolves were observed approaching humans and exhibiting markedly reduced flight distances in the area around Oksbøl that is a tourist hotspot. One of these wolves is still alive.”This type of behaviour, he said, usually happens when young wolves are repeatedly exposed to humans. “It has been discussed whether tourists, who may be unaware both of the presence of wolves in the area and of the basic recommendations regarding wolf encounters, may have contributed to the development of this behaviour,” he added.“To prevent similar behaviour from developing in future generations of young wolves, it is important that people who live in or visit wolf habitats – including tourists – understand that they should neither approach wolves nor leave food that may attract them.”Varde’s second deputy mayor, Sandie Eis Ravn, said the campaign was aimed at informing tourists at the request of local businesses. “If you are visiting somewhere and you know there is a wolf in the nearby forest, to make sure you have the right code of conduct when you meet this creature,” she said.Explore more on these topicsDenmarkEuropeWildlifenewsShareReuse this content

title: Miliband has been relentlessly attacked for net zero; what will happen now? | Ed Miliband | The Guardian
description: Tories, Reform, fossil fuel industry and some in his party lined up against decarbonisation target as energy secretary
Author: Fiona Harvey
Headline: Tories, Reform, fossil fuel industry and some in his party lined up against decarbonisation target as energy secretary
Content: ‘Miliband has been the most successful minister under the Starmer administration,’ says a climate thinktank.Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Miliband has been the most successful minister under the Starmer administration,’ says a climate thinktank.Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesEd MilibandAnalysisMiliband has been relentlessly attacked for net zero; what will happen now?Fiona HarveyEnvironment editorTories, Reform, fossil fuel industry and some in his party lined up against decarbonisation target as energy secretaryTue 21 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.34 EDTShare“Whenever the Labour party feels a bit sad,” according to one close observer of the current government, speaking some months ago, “they wheel out Ed Miliband to talk aboutrooftop solar.”It might not have been literally true, but it often felt during Keir Starmer’s premiership as if energy secretary Miliband brought the sun. Unfailingly upbeat, with a simple message that was understandable to voters, he had a clear mission – todecarbonise the UK’s power sector by 2030– that was seen to be progressing fast.While the Treasury struggled with feeble growth and political gaffes such as the winter fuel payments, the NHS was disrupted by doctors’ strikes, and education laboured under dilapidated schools and discouraged teachers, Miliband’s department seemed to get stuff done at an unusual rate.“His detractors won’t like to admit it, but Miliband has been the most successful minister under the Starmer administration,” says Ed Matthew, the UK director at the E3G thinktank. “Perhaps that is why they haveattacked him so viciously.”Rightwing media publications have publishedfrequent editorials attacking Miliband, labelling him a “net-zero zealot” with a “messianic” devotion to climate action. Even as three intense heatwaves struck this summer and firefighters battled wildfires across the country, the calls for more drilling for the fossil fuels that are supercharging extreme weather got louder.The fossil fuel industry, along with the Conservatives, Reform UK and sections of the Labour party, has beenlobbying Andy Burnham, the new prime minister, for the Jackdaw gasfield, east of Aberdeen, and Rosebank, an oilfield west of Shetland, to be given the go-ahead. Media reports suggested Burnham was preparing to announce new drilling in the North Sea shortly after entering No 10.View image in fullscreenEd Miliband, seen here in 2009 at the Little Cheyne Court windfarm in Kent, was also energy secretary under Gordon Brown.Photograph: Graeme Robertson/The GuardianMiliband may be personally disappointed if Burnham does this, having previously described Rosebank as “climate vandalism”. Miliband was also behind the push to have no new licences as a manifesto commitment.But giving the go-ahead to Jackdaw or Rosebank would not technically breach the manifesto ban, which left a loophole for the issue of licences that were already within the planning system.For some months, it has been clear that some such compromise might be demanded and the ground has been prepared. One close civil society observer, who did not wish to be named, said: “It is not what we wanted, but we have to get on with the rest of what’s needed.”That bigger picture has been described as “an energy revolution”, the trajectory of which is unstoppable, whatever the changing political weather.Matthew said: “Setting the 2030 clean power target was less about meeting the target than setting turbo boosters under the clean economy to get lift-off – which [Miliband] has achieved with record levels of renewables contracts and reforms to massively speed up deployment. This is critical to economic growth, reindustrialisation, energy security and, yes, bringing down bills. This is an energy revolution which is now inevitable and no amount of grumbling by the politicians and media barons funded by fossil fuel interests can reverse it.”Now Miliband has moved beyond the energy department to the Foreign Office. He has always had an eye on the global aspects of this transition, actingas a powerful voiceat climate summits and arguing for change. He will bring his climate knowledge and undoubted energies to bear on this role.Bar chart of rising planing approvals under LabourAfter all, within 72 hours of taking office in the energy department in July 2024, Miliband hadlifted the ban on onshore windfarmsand begun the process of consenting offshore windfarms and solar farms, while reforming a previously chaotic queue for grid connections.Auctions for low-carbon generationsecured enough to supply about 23m homes, while the warm homes plan to install insulation, heat pumps and clean energy should lift about 1 million families out of fuel poverty. A new nuclear programme for small modular reactors is also under way.Last month, the government announced that the private sector hadcommitted about £100bn to the UK’s clean technology sector. Alongside that, there is a plan to support 400,000 people into low-carbon jobs – more than double the number employed in the rapidly declining North Sea basin.View image in fullscreenEd Miliband and Bridget Phillipson take a look at a solar installation on the roof of a school in Sheffield.Photograph: Zara Farrar/DESNZIt is curious why Miliband, a formerLabourleader who lost to the Conservatives in 2015, has also been the target of unrelenting internal negative briefing. Some of it comes from his long history, stretching back to his role as one of former prime minister Gordon Brown’s proteges in the early 2000s. But other factions also pitched in.He ispopular with Labour members, which may irk some. “At a time when lots of people want to say ‘a plague on all your houses’ about politicians, Miliband stands out as a rare example of someone who’s actually made a difference,” says Craig Bennett, the chief executive of the Wildlife Trusts. “So much of that has down been down to his genuine leadership and having a realsense of purpose and vision behind it, and determination to get on with it, even when you have extraordinarily ridiculous attacks from some parts of the political spectrum.”Attacks on Miliband from other political parties have been equally vituperative: he has been behind only Starmer, and perhaps the chancellor, Rachel Reeves, as one of thekey targets of Kemi Badenoch, the Tory leader. Nigel Farage and his Reform party have vowed tomake net zero “the next Brexit”. And two large unions, Unite and the GMB,launched a fierce campaignagainst his proposed elevation to chancellor of the exchequer, thoughother unions backed him.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAmi McCarthy, the head of politics at Greenpeace UK, points to the deep pockets of vested interests and the might of the fossil fuel industry. “It’s no surprise that an agenda which challenges the profits of big oil has made him the target of coordinated smears by the industry and its allies, including the latest attacks we’ve seen this month,” they say.Businesses, led by the CBI, pointed out that the UK’s high energy prices – about 45% above the G7 median – were damaging businesses and dampening growth, as well as raising the cost of living.But as the CBI has made clear, net zero is not the culprit. In fact, energy prices would have been much higher without Miliband’s actions. His reforms add up to about £150 off bills for the average household, while about 6 million families on low incomes will gain about £150 more through the warm homes discount.The problem is that people arenot seeing the result in their pockets, because fossil fuel prices have risen even faster, through the compound shocks of the Ukraine invasion and the Iran war, which have sentgas and oil prices soaring. Added to this are the rules Labour inherited governing the UK’s privatised power market, which mean that theprice of electricity is set by gas-fired power stations, which can be highly profitable for them but disastrous for consumers.Miliband has been trying to wean the UK off fossil fuels, to cheap renewable energy. This will require more investment,particularly in the grid. And the government is intent on decoupling gas and electricity prices, but this is proving a tough and slow process.Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRead moreIf he had been made chancellor, as was rumoured at one point, Miliband could have been expected to tackle this. ButJohn Healey, previously the defence secretary, may have less enthusiasm for that fight.His move to the Foreign Office will prevent Miliband from following through on this project, and it remains to be seen whether his replacement, Miatta Fahnbulleh, will continue it with such enthusiasm. But it will cheer many of his international allies; the fight to limit the climate crisisis a global one, and the US departure from the field, coupled with uncertainty in Europe, and China and India’s flirtations with Russia, haveleft a leadership vacuum.Mohamed Adow, the director of the Power Shift Africa thinktank, says: “On the international stage, Miliband is seen as someone who understands that ambitious climate action and economic renewal can go hand in hand. Climate diplomacy is built on relationships, trust and persistence, and countries notice when senior ministers are prepared to engage directly. Putting [him] at the heart of government would send a positive signal to the rest of the world.”Teresa Ribera, a vice-president of the European Commission responsible for green transition, has been a close ally since Miliband began his first stint as energy secretary in 2008. “He is, by some distance, one of the most skilful and respected negotiators I have ever worked with, and one of the most honest and principled politicians I know. He commands respect in international institutions and among the world’s most influential governments, but equally among developing countries, workers, scientists and business and civil society leaders,” she told the Guardian.This will help Britain, and the world, in his new role. “I have absolutely no doubt that he has all the qualities needed to become an outstanding foreign secretary,” says Ribera. “His experience ininternational climate negotiations, his understanding of the economic and social dimensions of the energy transition, and his unwavering commitment to fairness and social justice make him one of the most valuable figures not only in British politics, but in global public leadership.”Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsEd MilibandLabourJohn HealeyClimate crisisRenewable energyEnergyanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Not enough water for UK’s datacentre plans, trade body says | Water | The Guardian
description: Industry says government’s failure to address cooling demands means AI growth plans are ‘fatally flawed’
Author: Pippa Neill
Headline: Industry says government’s failure to address cooling demands means AI growth plans are ‘fatally flawed’
Content: Cooling towers in Slough, Berkshire, which has the UK’s highest concentration of datacentres.Photograph: Martin Godwin/The GuardianView image in fullscreenCooling towers in Slough, Berkshire, which has the UK’s highest concentration of datacentres.Photograph: Martin Godwin/The GuardianWaterNot enough water for UK’s datacentre plans, trade body saysIndustry says government’s failure to address cooling demands means AI growth plans are ‘fatally flawed’Pippa NeillEnvironment reporterTue 21 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 07.39 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe UK will not have enough water for future datacentres, the water industry has said in stark criticism of the government’s AI growth plans.Datacentres rely on large amounts of water to manage the heat generated by densely packed servers. Cooling towers, chillers and humidification systems make direct use of this water, with large volumes also consumed indirectly through high electrical power needs.Water UK, the trade body that represents water companies in Britain and Northern Ireland, said ina publicly available written briefing to MPsthat the government’s water forecasts were “fatally flawed” and “explicitly exclude” datacentres.It wrote that the government “makes not a single mention of water” in its policy for delivering AI growth zones, its AI for science strategy or its AI opportunities action plan.It added that the Environment Agency’s most recentnational framework for water resources, published in June 2025, also failed to include an estimate of the water demands of datacentres.“There appears to be an assumption that the country will always have enough water for its economic needs,” the briefing said. “Nothing could be further from the truth.”In May,a House of Lords reportsaidEngland faced a 5bn litre a day shortfallfor public water supplies by 2055.Water UK said datacentres were already being built in water-stressed areas, with more than three-quarters located in the south and east of England. Millions of people in these regions have been placed under a hosepipe ban by water companies afterprolonged extreme heatand lack of rainfall.View image in fullscreenWater UK said it could take up to 15 years to plan and build a new reservoir.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesThe trade body said any new datacentre growth in these areas “may be impossible” without policy change because of existing pressure on the network.“As leakage and demand targets are already very ambitious, the only solution to deliver new supply is to increase the amount of water taken from the environment – which may be harmful – or to build new supplies, and major reservoirs can take 15 years to plan and build,”WaterUK wrote.It added: “Water companies have cut leakage by around 40% since privatisation and plan to reduce it by a further 17% by 2030.”However, Greenpeace said last week that water companies werewasting five times more water through leaky pipesthan even a national hosepipe ban would save.The UK’s highest concentration of datacentres is in Slough, which is served by Affinity Water. About 125 datacentres are proposed or under construction in Affinity’s region, much of which is subject to a hosepipe ban.In May,the company told MPsthat some of these datacentres were requesting up to 3m litres of water a day, equivalent to the peak water demand of 3,500 homes.“The planning framework is built around household growth and per-capita consumption, and has not adapted to commercial users whose water needs can match those of a market town,” Affinity said in a written submission to the environmental audit select committee.Because of Environment Agency permitting, datacentres are currently required to make use of drinking water only. Water UK estimated that datacentres in England use 6.6m litres of drinking water every day. With the government’s ambition to triple datacentre capacity by 2030, this could increase to 19.8m litres a day.Jon Chappel, Water UK’s deputy director, said this was “unforgivable” and a “genuine failing of government”.England’s current water shortfall is 220m litres more a day than was assumed in water forecasts. Water UK said this deficit was currently being met by “accepting a greater risk of environmental harm, and restrictions on water use in the event of drought”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toDown to EarthThe planet's most important stories. Get all the week's environment news - the good, the bad and the essentialPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSimilar concerns have been raised in Ireland, where hosepipe bans have ignited criticism of water companies and the government over a lack of clarity about datacentres’ water usage.The government has designated datacentres as critical national infrastructure, on the basis that their loss or compromise could result in “major detrimental impact on the availability, integrity or delivery of essential services” and “significant impact on national security, national defence, or the functioning of the state”.Oliver Hayes, head of big tech and campaigns at the campaign groupGlobal Action Plan, said it was not clear why datacentres had been given this status, but that water companies may be powerless to restrict their water use as a result. This could risk datacentres being prioritised over households at times of peak water demand.“I think it is highly likely that we’re going to end up seeing datacentres that are actually running chatbots, or enabling people to do benign and very wasteful, pointless things, protected and given favourable treatment when it comes to resources,” Hayes said.Chappel said Water UK had asked the government to publish a drought hierarchy to set out which sectors are prioritised in times of water shortages, but that ministers had yet to do so.“Without this there will be a trade-off across society” he said.View image in fullscreenHosepipe bans have come under scrutiny in the light of datacentres’ vast water usage.Photograph: Hugh R Hastings/Getty ImagesAt the end of June, water companies, the water regulator Ofwat, the Environment Agency, the Met Office and the Welsh government-sponsored body Natural Resources Wales announceda £75m campaign to encourage people to use less water.
