Water use per person in England and Wales is among the highest in Europe.The government was expected to publish a national policy statement for AI growth in February, providing an overarching strategy and defining how cumulative impacts are considered, but this has been delayed.A government spokesperson said: “The environment and sustainable development must go hand-in-hand and we will protect our water supply while unlocking growth, including looking at alternatives or ways to minimise impact on supplies and output of wastewater from datacentres.“We are also taking decisive measures to secure water supplies and meet higher demand, including record levels of infrastructure investment and building nine new reservoirs.”Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsWaterDatacentresWater industryAI (artificial intelligence)newsShareReuse this content

title: Former Met police chief admits surveillance of Stephen Lawrence’s family was institutionally racist | Undercover police and policing | The Guardian
description: Paul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abuses
Author: Rob Evans
Headline: Paul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abuses
Content: Paul Condon, pictured in 2008, said officers in the unit ‘were told daily as their job to lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life and what they’re asked to do’.Photograph: Luke MacGregor/ReutersView image in fullscreenPaul Condon, pictured in 2008, said officers in the unit ‘were told daily as their job to lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life and what they’re asked to do’.Photograph: Luke MacGregor/ReutersSpycops scandalUndercover police and policingFormer Met police chief admits surveillance of Stephen Lawrence’s family was institutionally racistPaul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abusesRob EvansTue 21 Jul 2026 15.02 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA formerMetropolitan policecommissioner has admitted that the surveillance of the family of Stephen Lawrence by undercover officers was institutionally racist, the spycops inquiry has heard.While Paul Condon was in command of the Metropolitan police in the 1990s, a controversial undercover unit spied on the family as they sought to persuade Scotland Yard to properly investigate Lawrence’s racist murder.The officers reported information about the separation of Lawrence’s parents, Doreen and Neville, to their superiors.Giving evidence on Tuesday, Condon said the covert monitoring of the Lawrences and their campaign “meets very well the parameters of institutional racism”.The failure to rigorously investigate the 1993 murder was one of the biggest and most contentious issues he had to handle, and caused enormous damage to the reputation of the UK’s biggest police force.Speaking more widely about the undercover unit, Condon also said the Metropolitan police had failed over decades to control the special demonstration squad (SDS), which committed “horrendous” abuses.He said he was appalled by the wrongdoing of the unit, which spent 40 years spying on tens of thousands of political activists. He told the inquiry there were “rogue officers who’ve done some bad things” but that the greater fault lay with the Met’s systemic failure to supervise the unit rigorously.He said the undercover officers were told daily to “lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life”, but added that the Met should have realised the covert work “can be corrupting” if strong rules were not in place.Condon, who became a member of the House of Lords after his police career, is the highest-ranked officer to have given evidence to the long-running public inquiry, which is headed by the retired judge Sir John Mitting.View image in fullscreenDoreen and Neville Lawrence, seen here in 1995, were covertly monitored as they campaigned for Scotland Yard to properly investigate their son’s murder.Photograph: PA Images/AlamyThe inquiry was set up after the Guardian revealed how the SDS spied on the Lawrences and their supporters in the 1990s.Mitting is examining wide-ranging misconduct by the undercover officers. This includes how they monitored grieving parents while theysought justice for relativesor campaigned to expose police malpractice,regularly deceived women into long-term relationshipsand even fathered children, stolethe identities of dead childrenandlied in court.The SDS undercover officers adopted false identities to infiltrate mainly leftwing groups between 1968 and 2008 in deployments that typically lasted four years.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWho were the undercover cops who deceived women into sexual relations?Read moreAt the outset of his testimony, Condon said he wanted to “personally apologise for every single person who has been damaged by the actions of officers in the special demonstration squad over the decades, and I am appalled by so much of what I have learned throughout this inquiry and I sincerely apologise to every single person who has been damaged in this process”.He said there were “horrendous things that went wrong” in the SDS and “appalling behaviour by individual officers”. He added: “Equally, I think the greater fault lies with the Metropolitan police as a whole over decades, that these officers were told daily as their job to lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life and what they’re asked to do.”“The Metropolitan police should have realised that without limits, without ethical guidance, that this work in those circumstances can be corrupting and even someone with a strong moral compass will give in to baser instincts.”However, Condon said he knew nothing of the abuses when he was in charge of the Met between 1993 and 2000. He said it was “unrealistic” to suggest that, as the head of the Met, he would have been aware of the day-to-day running of a small and secretive unit.Condon said he “never at any time commissioned, encouraged, authorised, acquiesced or was aware of any” of the surveillance that was carried out on the Lawrences and their supporters.Asked by David Barr, the inquiry’s chief lawyer, if he would accept that the surveillance was “an expression” of institutional racism, Condon replied: “Yes.”An inquiry by Sir William Macpherson into the killing said in 1999 that institutional racism within the Met was one of the reasons thatallowed Lawrence’s murderers to escape justice.Police leaders accepted the findings, then laterclaimed to have reformed the serviceto the extent that it no longer applied.Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsUndercover police and policingSpycops scandalMetropolitan policeLondonPoliceRacenewsShareReuse this content

title: Russian warship carries out rare live-fire exercise 45 miles off UK coast  | UK news | The Guardian
description: New defence secretary Wes Streeting denounces drill by Neustrashimy as ‘performative and irresponsible’
Author: Dan Sabbagh
Headline: New defence secretary Wes Streeting denounces drill by Neustrashimy as ‘performative and irresponsible’
Content: The Russian frigate Neustrashimy off Simonstown, South Africa. If arrived off the British coast earlier this month.Photograph: Gallo Images/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe Russian frigate Neustrashimy off Simonstown, South Africa. If arrived off the British coast earlier this month.Photograph: Gallo Images/Getty ImagesUK newsRussian warship carries out rare live-fire exercise 45 miles off UK coastNew defence secretary Wes Streeting denounces drill by Neustrashimy as ‘performative and irresponsible’Dan SabbaghDefence and security editorTue 21 Jul 2026 11.23 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 04.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA Russian warship fired its main cannon in a short notice test 45 miles south of Plymouth on Monday, the day Andy Burnham was appointed as prime minister.The Neustrashimy suddenly announced it intended to fire its guns in the morning and requested that a British warship, which had been tracking it, retreat a couple of miles to a safe distance.The patrol vessel HMS Tyne repositioned five miles away, allowing the Russian frigate to conduct a 30-minute firing drill at about 9.30am in international waters to the south of the UK.Wes Streeting, on his first full day as UK defence secretary, described the incident as “performative and irresponsible”. “It’s the tip of the iceberg of the daily threats that this country faces and our allies face.”Russian warships have been sailing around British waters for much of 2026. Last month another frigate, theAdmiral Grigorovich, fired warning shotswithin a few hundred metres of a couple on a British pleasure yacht, south of the Isle of Wight.Conducting a live-fire exercise at short notice in international waters close to another country is a rare event and usually conducted with due notice. However, if proper safety warnings are given it is legal.The 130-metre-long Neustrashimy is armed with a 100mm cannon capable of firing 50 rounds a minute at a range of 20km. It is also armed with anti-ship cruise missiles and has Gatling rotary machine guns as part of its air defence systems.British defence sources said they could not remember the last time a Russian warship had test-fired so close to the UK. Firing exercises are usually conducted with particular care in the Channel, one of the world’s busiest commercial waterways.The Neustrashimy arrived off the British coast earlier this month, replacing theAdmiral Grigorovichwhich had been on an extended three-month deployment, typically in the Channel or the North Sea.On Tuesday morning the Neustrashimy was sailing east of the Isle of Wight, and thought likely to be heading into the North Sea, where it may refuel. It is now being tracked by HMS Mersey, which has taken over shadowing responsibility from HMS Tyne.Maritime tensions between the UK and Russia are heightened. A Russia-linked oil tanker wasseized in the Channel by the Royal Marines last month, prompting warnings that there could bea Russian retaliation.Russian warships have been used to escort some oil tankers, though at other times their presence is designed to be psychological and tie up UK naval resources in monitoring them.Dmitry Peskov, the Kremlin’s spokesperson, said Russian warships always acted “in strict accordance” with international and maritime law. Asked whether the Russian government felt relations with the UK would improve under the new prime minister, Peskov said it had “no such hopes”.A Ministry of Defence spokesperson said: “A Russian naval vessel shadowed by the Royal Navy conducted a live-fire weapons exercise in international waters approximately 40 nautical miles south of Plymouth yesterday.“The Royal Navy monitored that exercise throughout, continues to track the vessel’s activity closely, and stands ready to protect UK national security.”Explore more on these topicsUK newsRussiaMilitaryEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: UK borrows less than expected as Healey vows to ‘buffer against uncertainty’ | Government borrowing | The Guardian
description: Figure of £16bn from ONS comes after Andy Burnham revealed plans to cut VAT on electricity bills
Author: Richard Partington
Headline: Figure of £16bn from ONS comes after Andy Burnham revealed plans to cut VAT on electricity bills
Content: City workers during a lunch breach. The UK economy has shown signs of resilience in recent months.Photograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenCity workers during a lunch breach. The UK economy has shown signs of resilience in recent months.Photograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockGovernment borrowingUK borrows less than expected as Healey vows to ‘buffer against uncertainty’Figure of £16bn from ONS comes after Andy Burnham revealed plans to cut VAT on electricity billsBusiness live – latest updatesRichard PartingtonSenior economics correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 05.49 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 02.46 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe UK government borrowed less than expected in June, in a boost for Andy Burnham as he set out plans tocut VAT on household electricity billsand promised a new direction for the British economy.TheOffice for National Statistics(ONS) said public sector net borrowing – the difference between government spending and income – was £16bn last month, £7.9bn less than in June 2025.The figure came in below the predictions of City economists and was £300m less than the forecast from the Office for Budget Responsibility, largely because of lower inflation-linked debt interest costs.Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRead moreBritain’s economy has shown signs of resilience in recent months despite concerns over the impact of higher energy prices linked to the Iran war. The UK government’s borrowing costs have risen amid jitters in global bond markets, and as investors fret over the new prime minister’s tax and spending plans.The latest snapshot comes as Burnham announced a fresh tax cut to support households with the cost of living by removing VAT from domestic electricity bills from 1 October.The newly appointed chancellor,John Healey, said the cut would be funded this year from cancelling the digital ID programme.Healey said he and Burnham had agreed to “work in lockstep to meet the fiscal rules with a buffer against uncertainty” alongside making plans to help cut the cost of living.“Fiscal control is the first duty of any chancellor. It is mine. And fiscal credibility is the bedrock for economic stability and for national security,” he said.The prime minister has said he will stick to Labour’s fiscal rules and the party’s manifesto tax promises, making it tougher for him to pursue a radically different path to Keir Starmer.Retaining the self-imposed borrowing and debt constraintsdrawn up by Rachel Reeveshas been seen as vital to avoiding a backlash in the markets. However, with spending pressures looming, Healey could be forced to consider tax increases or other measures to help finance the new prime minister’s agenda for the economy.It comes with bond markets finely poised amid expectations Burnham could run a looser approach to public finances than Starmer and Reeves. The new prime minister told reporters in the Downing Street garden on Monday that he could consider utilising “flexibility” in the fiscal rules to bolster public investment, in comments read by some investors as a signal for higher borrowing.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe latest figures from the ONS showed debt interest payments reached £11.8bn in June, a figure £5.3bn lower than the same month a year earlier, but still the fourth highest June on record.Borrowing was £57.6bn in the financial year to date. While this was £3.7bn less than in the same period last year, it is £2.7bn above the OBR forecast.Nabil Taleb, an economist at PwC UK, said the strain on public finances could leave Burnham and Healey facing difficult trade-offs.“With borrowing costs still sensitive and fiscal headroom limited, even modest commitments can carry significant consequences. What matters is whether ambition is matched by credible funding and a convincing grip on borrowing,” he said.Ruth Gregory, the deputy chief UK economist at CapitalEconomics, said: “June’s public finances were a rare piece of good news for the new PM Burnham and chancellor Healey, but with the UK’s debt burden still rising, there is limited scope for extra public borrowing.”Explore more on these topicsGovernment borrowingEconomicsAndy BurnhamnewsShareReuse this content

title: UK surrogacy law ‘creaking at seams’ as demand rises sharply | Surrogacy | The Guardian
description: Experts urge ministers to update legislation to prevent intended parents resorting to riskier arrangements abroad
Author: Rachel Hall
Headline: Experts urge ministers to update legislation to prevent intended parents resorting to riskier arrangements abroad
Content: Just under 1,000 children were born to surrogates in 2025, double the figure for 2015.Composite: Guardian Design/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenJust under 1,000 children were born to surrogates in 2025, double the figure for 2015.Composite: Guardian Design/Getty ImagesSurrogacyUK surrogacy law ‘creaking at seams’ as demand rises sharplyExperts urge ministers to update legislation to prevent intended parents resorting to riskier arrangements abroadI was struggling after eight rounds of IVF – then a former midwife became my surrogateRachel HallandJessica MurrayTue 21 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 08.08 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSurrogates, parents and children are being failed by the UK’s archaic laws, which are “creaking at the seams” as demand for surrogacy grows rapidly, experts have said.Figures obtained by the Guardian show there were just under 1,000 children born to surrogates in 2025, double the number in 2015. The rise has been driven by a rise in the number of gay couples seeking to be parents, increasing infertility and shifting cultural attitudes.Data shows that applications for parental orders, which are usually made shortly after birth to reassign legal parenthood from the surrogate to the intended parents, reached more than 980 in 2025.Of these, 150 children were born in the UK, where surrogates can only receive money for expenses. The remainder were born abroad, mostly in the US, Ukraine, Nigeria, Georgia, Colombia and Mexico, where surrogates can be paid a fee for having a child.Top 10 countriesUK surrogacy agencies told the Guardian they were busier than ever. The founder and director of Brilliant Beginnings, Helen Prosser, said they had been forced to turn away new intended parents as demand for surrogates had soared well above the level they were able to provide.A Law Commission review published in 2023 urged the government to change UK surrogacy law, including by giving legal parenthood to the intended parents from a child’s birth.At present, the surrogate must be listed on the baby’s birth certificate as its mother, while the intended parents must apply for a parental order after birth, which can take months. Experts said this can be distressing for the new family, and deters many prospective surrogates who do not wish to be a child’s legal mother.The lawyers also recommended greater regulatory oversight of surrogacy agencies, and pre-conception medical and safeguarding screening. The recommendations were accepted and informed a draft bill, but the government has said it is not a legislative priority.Total number of applications by yearThe review’s author, Prof Nicholas Hopkins, said the best way to prevent intended parents going abroad, where arrangements with surrogates can be riskier and less well regulated, was to ensure there was a properly regulated scheme in the UK.Its co-author, Prof Gillian Black, said there were also cases in which a child has needed hospital treatment, for which only the surrogate has been able to legally consent. “That doesn’t reflect the reality on the ground where the child’s being cared for and living with the intended parents. The surrogate doesn’t want that responsibility at all,” she said.Significant disagreement about the ethics of surrogacy exists globally. The UK permits an “altruistic” model, but countries such as the US, Mexico and Nigeria allow women to be surrogates for money. France, Germany, Italy and Spain are among the countries that heavily restrict or ban surrogacy.The UN’s special rapporteur on violence against women and girls, Reem Alsalem, has called for surrogacy to be abolished globally, calling it a “system of violence, exploitation and abuse against women”.Parental order applications by age groupShe has argued it leaves women and girls who are “impoverished, destitute and affected by crisis and conflict” open to exploitation by people from “western wealthier states”.Hopkins said that while the draft bill languishes in parliament, the “significant” problems identified in their report will continue, and more parents will opt for overseas surrogacy, where there may be a greater risk of exploitation or legal difficulties.Prosser said the “murkiness” in the current legal system was off-putting for surrogates, and this was pushing more prospective parents to go abroad.“In some places, it’s a bit of a wild west,” she said. “There are language barriers and sometimes difficulties having a relationship with the surrogate, to say nothing of the risks to the surrogate as well.“Our frustration is we could make it easier here in the UK for people to stay here, but the law reform has been parked.”Prof Kirsty Horsey, who researches surrogacy regulation at Loughborough University, fears that “culture wars” concerns may play into the government’s hesitation to push forward legislative change, given the issue’s growing controversy.Her recent report,Surrogacyin the UK: Myth busting and reform 10 years on, said the law was “creaking at the seams” and had unnecessary barriers that led to transnational surrogacy increasingly taking place in jurisdictions where neither surrogates nor parents had legal protections.She said people did not always realise that if they had a baby through surrogacy in a country where it was illegal, or entered into complex multi-jurisdictional arrangements, they could struggle to obtain legal parenthood on their return.New Zealand, Australia, Ireland and Canada are changing their approaches to better reflect who the intended parents are, make it easier for people to access trustworthy sources of information, and “send the message to citizens that there are good, well-regulated ways of doing surrogacy on home soil”, she said.A UK couple facing a lengthy court battle to become parents of their baby born via surrogacy recently launched apetitionto change the law to recognise intended parents from birth. It received more than 100,000 signatures, the threshold for a parliamentary debate, within days.Rachel Westbury, who is the founder of surrogacy agency Nappy Endings and has been a surrogate multiple times, said the current waiting time for intended parents using her service was about three years.“A lot of people have been through enough heartbreak and the waiting is difficult,” she said. “So we get couples looking at all different places [globally] just to try and quicken the process. People are so desperate.“We really do have a huge surrogate shortage in the UK, because of the restrictions.”Westbury said there needed to be more education about surrogacy in the UK, and the different reasons people turn to it, including a rise in people having cancer treatment at a young age which leaves them unable to have children.“We’ve had midwives look intended parents up and down and tell them to leave the hospital because they’re not the ones carrying the baby, even though it’s their baby being carried. I think that lack of education is probably scary to people,” she said.Explore more on these topicsSurrogacyParents and parentingFamilyPregnancyChildrennewsShareReuse this content

title: Trump must divulge financial details about businesses in $10bn BBC lawsuit | Donald Trump | The Guardian
description: US judge’s ruling could open up window into first family’s multiple ventures, in drawback of lawsuit on media
Author: Roque Planas
Headline: US judge’s ruling could open up window into first family’s multiple ventures, in drawback of lawsuit on media
Content: The British Broadcasting Corporation logo displayed above the main entrance to BBC's Broadcasting House in London, England.Photograph: Leon Neal/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe British Broadcasting Corporation logo displayed above the main entrance to BBC's Broadcasting House in London, England.Photograph: Leon Neal/Getty ImagesDonald TrumpTrump must divulge financial details about businesses in $10bn BBC lawsuitUS judge’s ruling could open up window into first family’s multiple ventures, in drawback of lawsuit on mediaRoque PlanasTue 21 Jul 2026 19.52 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 19.53 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump must divulge detailed financial information from his many businesses as part of his $10bn defamation lawsuit against the British Broadcasting Corporation, a federal judge ruled during a discovery hearing on Tuesday, according to news reports.The ruling by US magistrate judge Enjoliqué Lett could open a unique window into the hundreds of businesses owned by Trump’s family trust. It also highlights a potential drawback for Trump’s strategy of bludgeoning critical media with multibillion-dollar lawsuits.Trumpfiled a defamation lawsuitlate last year seeking $10bn in damages over a 2024 documentary called “Trump, A Second Chance,” which features a scene of the speech he made before his supporters attacked the US Capitol on 6 January 2021. Thescene splices lines togetherto make it appear that Trump directly urged his supporters to attack. The BBC apologized to Trump last year for the misleading edit, but has argued the president’s defamation claims have no merit.Lett is also allowing the BBC tosubpoena dozens of membersof Trump’s inner circle, according to Bloomberg News, in an effort to find evidence of the president’s thoughts and intentions on January 6. Those subpoenas will target Trump family members and key advisers, including Donald Trump Jr, Steve Bannon and Stephen Miller.Trump’s legal team is likely to appeal Tuesday’s decision.Trump’s lawyers are also trying to dodge the forced release of his financial records by limiting the scope of the lawsuit. Alejandro Brito, a Trump lawyer, told Lett on Tuesday that he intends toamend the current complaint, according to Politico. The amended complaint will say the documentary only caused reputational damage, rather than undermining his businesses, making the financial disclosures unnecessary.But for now, theBBCcan move forward with requests for Trump’s financial records. The BBC aims to argue that Trump’s wealth skyrocketed since the documentary aired, contradicting the claim that the documentary undermined his businesses.The White House did not immediately return the Guardian’s request for comment.Explore more on these topicsDonald TrumpBBCLaw (US)newsShareReuse this content

title: Bill Gates foundation met with Jeffrey Epstein about 30 times, external review finds | Bill Gates | The Guardian
description: Staff ‘raised on multiple occasions’ risks of tech mogul’s philanthropic body associating with disgraced financier
Author: Anna Betts
Headline: Staff ‘raised on multiple occasions’ risks of tech mogul’s philanthropic body associating with disgraced financier
Content: Bill Gates departs after a closed-door interview with the House oversight committee over his ties to Jeffrey Epstein on Capitol Hill last month.Photograph: Kent Nishimura/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenBill Gates departs after a closed-door interview with the House oversight committee over his ties to Jeffrey Epstein on Capitol Hill last month.Photograph: Kent Nishimura/AFP/Getty ImagesBill GatesBill Gates foundation met with Jeffrey Epstein about 30 times, external review findsStaff ‘raised on multiple occasions’ risks of tech mogul’s philanthropic body associating with disgraced financierAnna BettsTue 21 Jul 2026 16.37 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 17.36 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA new externalreviewcommissioned by the Gates Foundation has found that there were “roughly 30 meetings” betweenJeffrey Epsteinand foundation leaders and staff – includingBill Gates– between 2011 and 2014, even as foundation staff “raised on multiple occasions the risks of associating with Epstein because of his prior conviction”.The Gates foundation commissioned the review in March, following the justice department’s release earlier this year of millions of documents related to Epstein. The files included numerous references to Gates, several photographs of him, and records showing that he met withEpstein on multiple occasions, which prompted renewed scrutiny of Gates’spast tiesto the disgraced financier.The foundation also said earlier this year that it was aware of emails in the Epstein files “betweenJeffrey Epsteinand Gates Foundation staff”. The foundation said, at the time, that it “regrets having any employees interact with Epstein in any way” and retained the law firm WilmerHale to conduct a review of the foundation’s past engagements with Epstein.Bill Gates tells US House ‘I have never victimized anyone’ in Epstein testimonyRead moreThe report, of which a summary was released on Tuesday, took about five months. It was based on more than 50 interviews with current and former foundation personnel, including Gates, and reviewed extensive written materials relevant to the review, the law firm said.“WilmerHale found no evidence of participation in or knowledge of Epstein’s ongoing sex trafficking operations or criminal activities,” the summary of the report states. “WilmerHale found no evidence that the foundation was contemporaneously aware of Epstein’s use of the foundation’s name to help advance his conduct. WilmerHale also found no evidence that Epstein was paid by the foundation.”Gates, who has not been accused of any wrongdoing in connection with Epstein and has repeatedly denied having any knowledge of Epstein’s crimes, has publiclyexpressed regretover his past association with Epstein. He apologized to foundation employees during a February town-hall meeting for his past ties to Epstein, theWall Street Journalreported. In June, hetestifiedin front of the House committee on oversight and reform and told lawmakers that he “never witnessed nor had any indication” that Epstein was “engaged in ongoing criminal conduct”.The Microsoft co-founder said that he was introduced to Epstein in 2011 “through people I trusted in my professional and philanthropic work” and that Epstein had “claimed he could raise billions of dollars for global health from people for whom he provided tax and estate services”.“I recall being aware that Epstein had faced prior legal issues, but I did not fully understand the extent of the crimes he committed,” Gates said, referring to Epstein having pleaded guilty in 2008 to state charges in Florida of solicitation of prostitution and of solicitation of prostitution with a minor. “I accepted the introduction without applying the scrutiny I should have.”Gates said that their interactions ended in December 2014.According to the new report, Epstein was introduced to Gates and other foundation personnel in 2011 “by an adviser who held a dual role at the foundation and in Mr Gates’s personal office”.The review found that the foundation’s interactions with Epstein involved two matters – discussions about the “potential creation of a donor-advised fund entity” and Gates’s “engagement with a nonprofit organization that later became a foundation grantee”.WilmerHale said that it identified “roughly 30 meetings between Epstein and ten foundation leaders and staff, including Mr Gates, from 2011 to 2014” including one meeting on the foundation’s campus and several meetings at Epstein’s Manhattan residence.Buffett omits gift to Bill Gates’ foundation after his Epstein ties disclosedRead more“The foundation’s meetings with Epstein appear to have focused on the DAF concept, donor engagement, the foundation’s polio strategy, and introductions to IPI,” the summary of the report states. “Based on interviews and a review of available foundation records, WilmerHale found no evidence that these meetings were unrelated to philanthropy or involved illegal conduct.”The report also concluded that Gates “was aware of reputational concerns related to Epstein’s prior conviction and sentencing for a sex-related offense when he began conversations with him in 2011” and that foundation staff had “raised on multiple occasions the risks of associating with Epstein because of his prior conviction, and those concerns were raised to senior leadership, including Mr. Gates”.In a news release on Tuesday, the Gates Foundationsaidthat its governing board had approved a series of recommendations based on findings in the report, including creating a centralized vetting process for brokers, donor advisers, co-funders and others who facilitate funding partnerships and who are connected to the foundation, as well as creating an “escalation process for organizational risks on projects” and more.Gates said in a statement on Tuesday that “completing this review is an important step in providing the clarity that partners, employees, and grantees deserve as well as strengthening our oversight with additional policies going forward.“The work the foundation does to save lives and unlock opportunities so people everywhere can lead healthier, more productive lives, is more important than ever,” he said. “We are committed to ensuring trust and transparency are never compromised.”Explore more on these topicsBill GatesJeffrey EpsteinBill and Melinda Gates FoundationnewsShareReuse this content

title: Spanish hotel chain ceases operations in Cuba under pressure from Washington  | Cuba | The Guardian
description: Meliá group says Donald Trump’s sanctions make it ‘impossible’ to keep any of its 34 sites open
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Meliá group says Donald Trump’s sanctions make it ‘impossible’ to keep any of its 34 sites open
Content: The Meliá Cohiba in Havana, one of 34 hotels where the Spanish hotel chain is to cease operations.Photograph: Adalberto Roque/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe Meliá Cohiba in Havana, one of 34 hotels where the Spanish hotel chain is to cease operations.Photograph: Adalberto Roque/AFP/Getty ImagesCubaSpanish hotel chain ceases operations in Cuba under pressure from WashingtonMeliá group says Donald Trump’s sanctions make it ‘impossible’ to keep any of its 34 sites openAgence France-PresseTue 21 Jul 2026 11.10 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA Spanish hotel chain is to cease all operations inCubaat the end of the week due to “major difficulties” in doing business on the island under growing pressure from Washington.The latest US sanctions make it “impossible, de facto and de jure, to maintain even minimal operational stability”, the Meliá group explained in a statement on Tuesday to the Spanish stock market regulator.The company said it would strive to ensure a smooth transition to reduce the impact as much as possible on staff, suppliers and clients.Meliá announced last month that it would cease operating 15 of its 34 hotels in Cuba, joining other foreign companies that have cut ties with the island’s military conglomerate, GAESA, under pressure from Washington.Cuba edges toward breakdown as power cuts and US meddling push society to brinkRead moreMeliá, one of the largest foreign hotel operators in Cuba with 14,000 rooms, did not refer in that statement to the other 19 hotels it operated in Cuba in partnership with the country’s tourism ministry.Donald Trump, the US president, ordered new sanctions in May against Cuba, targeting a broad set of individuals and threatening foreign banks and companies that worked with them.Meliá was the first Spanish hotel group to establish a presence in Cuba after theCaribbeannation opened its tourism sector to foreigners in the 1990s, hoping to ease the economic crisis triggered by the collapse of the Soviet Union.In addition to an economic embargo in place since 1962, Washington has blocked all oil shipments to the island since January, with the exception of a single Russian ship.Explore more on these topicsCubaAmericasHotelsCaribbeannewsShareReuse this content

title: Ozempic maker Novo Nordisk sues US rival over ‘materially misleading’ ads | Pharmaceuticals industry | The Guardian
description: Danish pharma company says Eli Lilly using outdated trial data to make its weight-loss drugs appear more effective
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Danish pharma company says Eli Lilly using outdated trial data to make its weight-loss drugs appear more effective
Content: Zepbound and Mounjaro, two GLP-1 weight-loss drugs produced by US company Eli Lilly.Composite: ReutersView image in fullscreenZepbound and Mounjaro, two GLP-1 weight-loss drugs produced by US company Eli Lilly.Composite: ReutersPharmaceuticals industryOzempic maker Novo Nordisk sues US rival over ‘materially misleading’ adsDanish pharma company says Eli Lilly using outdated trial data to make its weight-loss drugs appear more effectiveAgence France-Presse in CopenhagenTue 21 Jul 2026 13.21 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOzempic maker Novo Nordisk said on Tuesday it is suing arch US rival Eli Lilly over “false and materially misleading” adverts for its competing weight-loss and anti-diabetes treatments.The Danish pharmaceuticals heavyweight complained that the US group – which makes rival weight-loss treatments Zepbound and Mounjaro – “intentionally selected outdated studies” that compared Lilly’s highest doses against lower doses of Novo Nordisk’s medicines.Eli Lilly’s ads “deceptively (presented) those results as proof of broad, product-level superiority while burying or omitting critical clinical context”, Novo Nordisk said.Novo Nordisk has developed a molecule called semaglutide, while Eli Lilly developed rival molecule tirzepatide.The competing drugs are a financial windfall for each of the pharmaceutical companies.“As new and more effective treatment options become available, people deserve accurate information that reflects the latest scientific evidence and helps them make informed care decisions,” said John F Kuckelman, Novo Nordisk’s general counsel.“Healthcare companies have a responsibility to keep their public claims accurate and current – ineffective, fine-print disclaimers do not fix the misleading impression created by major national campaigns.”The target of the Danish group’s wrath is an advertising campaign that was “aired during recent, widely viewed global sporting broadcasts and on TikTok and Facebook, causing widespread confusion”, it said.Eli Lilly responded to its rival’s accusations with a statement saying that the trial which underpins its advertising, known as Surmount-5, is “the only direct, head-to-head randomised clinical trial comparing tirzepatide and semaglutide in the context of weight management”.An Eli Lilly spokesperson added that “rather than competing on the merits of its products, Novo is asking a court to prevent Lilly from communicating the results of this trial. We firmly stand by our advertising campaign.”Anti-obesity treatments mimic a hormone secreted by the intestines, GLP-1.They can be used for weight loss and/or to treat type 2 diabetes, the most common form of the disease.Explore more on these topicsPharmaceuticals industryUS healthcareWeight-loss drugsHealthLaw (US)newsShareReuse this content

title: Headaches for Silicon Valley as China chips away at the US’s lead in the AI race | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question
Author: Blake Montgomery
Headline: Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question
Content: As Google falters on AI, China is eating into the US’s lead.Photograph: Nikolas Kokovlis/NurPhoto/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAs Google falters on AI, China is eating into the US’s lead.Photograph: Nikolas Kokovlis/NurPhoto/ShutterstockTechScape newsletterAI (artificial intelligence)Headaches for Silicon Valley as China chips away at the US’s lead in the AI raceGoogle’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into questionBlake MontgomeryTue 21 Jul 2026 08.08 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.24 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHello, I’m Blake Montgomery, writing to you after a double-header feature of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey and the World Cup final. What a great Sunday. Today in tech, we’re discussing howChinais chipping away at the US’s lead in the AI race and how Silicon Valley’s workers are taking action to protect their jobs from AI.As Google falters on AI, China eats into the US’s leadGoogleis struggling with the latest version of its Gemini modelA Chinese company, Moonshot, put out a free, surprisingly capable model last weekChina’s new models yet again thrown the US dominance in AI into questionGoogle is months behind in its launch schedule for the new version of its flagship AI model, Gemini 3.5. According toBloomberg, Google is attempting to improve Gemini’s coding abilities to catch up to Claude, the Anthropic bot that is widely considered the best AI coding tool. OpenAI released a new model at the start of July; Google had been expected to do the same in May. News of the delay sent Google’s shares sliding as investors questioned whether it could maintain its top position in the AI race.A refreshed and newly capable Gemini might also have ameliorated the damage to the US’s reputation as the leader in AI this week – but didn’t.As Google’s internal struggles became public, the Chinese startup Moonshot debuted an unexpectedly strong model, sending shivers through Silicon Valley and Washington DC alike. Moonshot’s Kimi K3 model featured surprisingly capable coding, particularly in a niche called “front-end coding”. “This may be the single biggest release of the year,” Anastasios Angelopoulos, the CEO of AI testing company Arena,said.By Sunday night, Moonshot couldn’t keep up with the demand for its latest model. “Kimi K3 has received far more love than we expected, and our GPUs are feeling it. Over the past 48 hours, demand has pushed close to the limits of our current capacity,” the companypostedon X.Unlike ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini, Kimi K3 is free to use and open-weight, meaning anyone can download it and run it locally on their own computers, as its core components are released to the public.It’s been the general strategy of Chinese AI companies: open-source and open-weight models, beating US AI companies on price as Chinese manufacturers do, and mirroring the broader trade war of the US and China. DeepSeekinspired a major US market panic last year. Alibaba previewed its latest model, Qwen3.8 Max, on Sunday, showing off a similar number of parameters as Kimi K3. The company plans to publish the model’s weights soon, perBloomberg.The strategy has been made possible by Beijing, which subsidizescomputing resourcesandpower consumptionfor its domestic AI startups, allowing them to release their AI models free of charge. The basic logic goes that if these startups can’t compete on capability with US models, they can compete on cost and undercut the US parent companies, which need to earn a profit eventually.It’s all causing major headaches in Silicon Valley. There are no longtime incumbents in the AI business to whom customers will default. If those buyers, especially businesses who need lots of generated code, can turn to cheap or free alternatives, there might be little need to spend big on brand-name bots. The South China Morning Post, which is itself owned by the Alibaba Group, reported last week: “As US AI costs soar, global businesses pivot to China’s low-cost, open-weight models.”In the US, nerves are rattling. Hawkish Trump administration officials are rekindling their efforts to block any US access to Chinese AI models via de facto bans, commerce department rules or an executive order,Axiosreported on Monday.OpenAI’s policy head for advanced AI, former Donald Trump adviser Dean Ball, wrote that Beijing’s open-weight approach to AI would lead to “full AI communism” with government-run datacenters powering AI as a public good. He denounced that future. OpenAI has been sounding the alarm about Chinese models for years, which reads as part political disagreement and part business strategy, since Chinese open-weight models threaten OpenAI’s projected profits.“I would guess that the Trump Administration will at some point realize that their best strategy here would be to create large amounts of regulatory risk around the use of open-weight Chinese models,” Ball wrote.There is no alignment between the US’s leading AI companies and the government players in the same industry. Emil Michael, the US Pentagon’s chief technology officer, responded to Ball: “Every industry/ecosystem has its supreme village idiot. Dean Ball is that for AI.”As Beijing presents a unified front, the White House’s response is growing more scattered by the day. Trump himself had talked until recently of complete deregulation and never restraining any AI investment, research or releases. By contrast, the White House is restricting access – even allies’ access – to Anthropic and OpenAI’s advanced cybersecurity models. Even the personnel are changing. Chris Fall, the director of the Trump administration’s Center for AI Standards and Innovation, is resigning just three months into his tenure,Axiosreported on Monday.Not all of the AI crowd is against these models’ incursion on US dominance. Bill Gurley, a longtime venture capitalist best known for his early backing of Uber, wrote of China’s AI models in theWashington Post: “They aren’t a security threat – they’re what competition looks like.”China’s advanced AI does pose a risk to the global business of AI, which is, as yet, still a race without a clear winner. Cheap Chinese AI models may trump their expensive US counterparts. Theentire US economyis tied tight to the AI industry, raising the stakes of the Chinese models’ capabilities. Undercutting the US’s AI frontrunners’ business could unsettle the entire US stock market even more so than it already has.That competition is set to play out in the future. In the present, though, China’s recent releases have thrown the politics of AI in the US into chaos. That reads as the bigger win.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDatacenters face global political blowbackNew York becomes first state to impose one-year pause on new AI datacentersTrump rails against New York’s statewide datacenter moratoriumAlbanese’s AI blueprint sparks calls for datacentre moratorium until new regulations in placeSilicon Valley’s workers are worried about what AI is doing to their jobsView image in fullscreenThe Meta CEO, Mark Zuckerberg, attends the annual Allen and Co Sun Valley Media and Technology Conference in Sun Valley, Idaho, on 9 July.Photograph: Brendan McDermid/ReutersEmployees are taking some of the US’s first collective actions to protect their jobs from AILast week,Meta workers filed a lawsuit against their former employeralleging that it automated their firing via algorithmic decision-making. Meanwhile, thousands of Google’s workerspetitioned their CEO for layoff protectionsas the company shifts its spending from employees to AI infrastructure.“Make no mistake: this is a company that is enjoying massive, unprecedented success,” Parul Koul, a Google software engineer and the president of the Alphabet Workers Union, said outside the company’s California headquarters. “These layoffs and cuts are not difficult decisions, but simply profit being put over the people that make this company run.”These Silicon Valley employees are making these moves against the backdrop of the AI boom, which ischanging software engineering rapidlyandcausing unions to gain popularity.A Meta employee who was part of a recent union drive in the UK said: “It’s about us having a voice in all of the operations of the company, from products to employee experience.”Meta countered last week’s lawsuit with an unusually direct statement: “These claims lack merit and are not based on facts. Workforce management and organizational decisions were and are made by people, not AI.” Google did not issue a statement in response to last week’s petition.We’ve seen Silicon Valley luminaries warn of futuristic apocalyptic scenarios, but we’re seeing those worries come to life among the fearful rank-and-file employees, who are now taking action. We’re witnessing how those workforces, who have a front-row seat to AI’s development, respond to its effects on their employment.Read more fromReworked, a Guardian series about what’s at stake as AI disrupts our jobs.The wider TechScape‘Adversarial clothing’: are garments designed to confuse facial recognition systems about to go mainstream?IBM loses quarter of its value as tech giant’s shares plunge and profits falter‘What’s the point?’ Teenagers give their verdict on Britain’s social media curfewAmazon Web Services customers receive bills for up to $1.5tn after global glitchTrump made $1.4bn from crypto in one year. Is Justin Sun the man who helped him do it?Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)TechScape newsletterChinaGooglenewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Defence shares rise after Andy Burnham appoints John Healey as chancellor | Stock markets | The Guardian
description: Babcock and BAE Systems benefit as markets hope former defence secretary will increase spending on sector
Author: Lauren Almeida
Headline: Babcock and BAE Systems benefit as markets hope former defence secretary will increase spending on sector
Content: John Healey walks in Downing Street after being appointed chancellorPhotograph: WIktor Szymanowicz/NurPhoto/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenJohn Healey walks in Downing Street after being appointed chancellorPhotograph: WIktor Szymanowicz/NurPhoto/ShutterstockStock marketsDefence shares rise after Andy Burnham appoints John Healey as chancellorBabcock and BAE Systems benefit as markets hope former defence secretary will increase spending on sectorBusiness live – latest updatesLauren AlmeidaTue 21 Jul 2026 05.32 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleShares in defence companies have risen sharply afterAndy Burnhamappointed John Healey as chancellor, prompting hope among investors of increased spending on suppliers to the military.Defence stocks were among the biggest risers on the FTSE 100 on Tuesday morning after the former defence secretary was announced as chancellor after markets closed on Monday.Babcock Internationalrose 7%, BAE Systems was up 3% and Rolls-Royce increased nearly 2% on the FTSE 100. On the FTSE 250, QinetiQ rose nearly 4%.Investors hope Healey will use his new position to increase defence spending – possibly though issuing“war bonds”, a form of borrowing allocated only for the military that he has previously advocated for in government.Andy Burnham to cut VAT on electricity bills as first cost of living moveRead moreHealeyresignedlast month in a row over defence spending, accusing Keir Starmer and his chancellor, Rachel Reeves, of putting the country’s security at risk and arguing that a long-awaited investment plan fell well short of what was required.But Chris Beauchamp, the chief market analyst at the broker IG, said that Healey’s appointment would not necessarily lead to an immediate windfall for defence.He said: “As chancellor, he will have many competing demands, and won’t just be the MoD’s man in No 11. His experience made him an obvious candidate for the role, and he represents a middle way between[Ed] Miliband and [Shabana] Mahmood, but it will not be easy to find lots more cash for defence, especially when the new PM is so busy making broad spending commitments in other areas.”UK government bonds were relatively unmoved and sterling was up slightly against the dollar on Tuesday. The yield on the 10-year gilt is about one basis point lower, though crucially still above the 5% level at 5.03%.Bond market investors have been watching Burnham’s statements carefully amid expectations he could run a looser approach to public finances than Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves.The new prime minister told reporters in the Downing Street garden on Monday that he could consider utilising “flexibility” in the fiscal rules to bolster public investment, in comments read by some investors as a signal for higher borrowing.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionOfficial figuresreleasedon Tuesday showed that the UK government borrowed less than expected in June, easing pressure on the gilt market. Separately, the governmentannouncedplans to reduce household electricity bills by an average of £45 a year from October, a policy expected to cost £850m this financial year.Healey is expected to outline more long-term action to bring down living costs at the budget later this year. There has been speculation about reducing the cap on bus fares and temporarily freezing rents in the private sector.Explore more on these topicsStock marketsFTSEBAE SystemsBabcock InternationalBondsJohn HealeyDefence policynewsShareReuse this content

title: Mitie agrees £3.1bn takeover by OCS in blow to London stock market | Mitie | The Guardian
description: Board of UK government contractor recommends deal with private-equity owned rival
Author: Lauren Almeida
Headline: Board of UK government contractor recommends deal with private-equity owned rival
Content: Mitie was founded in 1987 and employs 84,000 staff.Photograph: Ceri Breeze/AlamyView image in fullscreenMitie was founded in 1987 and employs 84,000 staff.Photograph: Ceri Breeze/AlamyMitieMitie agrees £3.1bn takeover by OCS in blow to London stock marketBoard of UK government contractor recommends deal with private-equity owned rivalBusiness live – latest updatesLauren AlmeidaTue 21 Jul 2026 05.10 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 15.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe outsourcerMitiehas agreed to a £3.1bn takeover by its private-equity owned rival OCS Group, ending nearly four decades on the stock market and making it the latest London-listed company to be acquired this year.On Tuesday, the government contractor said its board has recommended that shareholders accept a cash offer of 221.6p a share, a 46.8% premium to Monday’s closing price.Mitie, which was founded in 1987 and employs 84,000 staff, specialises in facilities management, such as engineering maintenance and other services such as hygiene and security.Thames Water investors offer ‘golden share’ in bid to head off nationalisationRead moreIts longstanding chief executive, Phil Bentley, announced last month that he would leave in March 2027 after more than 10 years in the top job.OCS, which operates across the UK, Europe, Asia Pacific and the Middle East and has 135,000 staff, is owned by the private equity group ​Clayton, Dubilier & Rice, which owns the Motor Fuel Group petrol station company andacquired the supermarket chain Morrisons in 2021.Mitie has contracts across government and a string of industries including defence, health and immigration providing services such as security and cleaning.Rob Legge, the OCS chief executive, said: “We would build a British facilities management group that is better positioned to support the organisations that keep the country running. Together, we can better support existing and new customers, help more people into work and strengthen our contribution to getting Britain moving.“While there is a long process ahead, both businesses remain focused on supporting customers and delivering the high standards they expect every day. Together, we can build something remarkable for our colleagues, our customers and the country.”Bentley said: “As part of a larger group with a wider geographical footprint, Mitie would have an even stronger platform to invest in our people, technology and services, and to do even more for the customers and communities we support.”The deal, which is expected to complete in the first quarter of 2027, is the latest in a long string of takeovers on London’s stock market – Intertek, easyJet, Beazley and Schroders have agreed to takeovers this year, while the US property group Prologis has made repeated attempts to buy itsFTSE100 rival Segro.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMitie shares have shot up 41% this morning to a record high of 213.6p. Last month, shares in outsourcers fell as the Cabinet Officesaid the “age of outsourcing is over”. The since departed chancellor, Rachel Reeves, said the government is planning “the biggest wave of insourcing of public services for a generation”.Earlier this month, the GuardianrevealedMitie had launched an investigation into allegations of racism, antisemitism, Islamophobia and hate speech among staff working in immigration removal centres.A Mitie spokesperson said at the time it took the allegations seriously and planned to investigate them thoroughly.Get in touchContact the Guardian Business teamThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject you can contact the Business team confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don't already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Scroll down and click onSecure Messaging.When asked who you wish to contact please select the ‘Business (UK & Global)’ team.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsMitieOutsourcingMergers and acquisitionsFTSEStock marketsnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘His chords are like sheets of diamond ice’: the beguiling psych-folk of late cult songwriter Ed Askew | Folk music | The Guardian
description: The late songwriter released around 50 records, writing gay love songs and effortless folk songs from folk’s fringes. Fans including Bill Callahan explain his singular brilliance
Author: Kat Lister
Headline: The late songwriter released around 50 records, writing gay love songs and effortless folk songs from folk’s fringes. Fans including Bill Callahan explain his singular brilliance
Content: ‘There’s a holy aspect to it’ … Ed Askew.Photograph: Christian FilardoView image in fullscreen‘There’s a holy aspect to it’ … Ed Askew.Photograph: Christian FilardoFolk music‘His chords are like sheets of diamond ice’: the beguiling psych-folk of late cult songwriter Ed AskewThe late songwriter released around 50 records, writing gay love songs and effortless folk songs from folk’s fringes. Fans including Bill Callahan explain his singular brillianceKat ListerTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn a grainy television clip from 1986, a flame-haired and neckerchiefed troubadour plucks away at a lute-like instrument, harmonica under chin, the gentle ripples giving way to a voice full of longing. This performance, uploaded to YouTube in 2010, has generated only a handful of comments, but peruse any of them (“Ed the Great!”; “pure gold”) and you begin to understand the passion that Ed Askew – an under-the-radar US poet, artist and musician – has inspired over the years.“He was living in a world that was for people who didn’t fit in,” Askew’s collaborator, Jerry David DeCicca, says on the free-spiritedness of his friend, whose musical life began with discovering the 10-stringed tiple as a teenager in the 1950s and ended with his death a year ago, aged 84, on the verge of him releasing his album The Final Painting, now being released posthumously this month.Its producer, DeCicca, first fell for what he calls Askew’s “singularity” in the early 00s. But in recent years, he has captured the hearts of many more of music’s most characterful contemporary acts: from Sharon Van Etten and Bill Callahan, both of whom lend their vocals to The Final Painting, to Glasgow psych-folk band Trembling Bells who brought Askew to London’s Cafe Oto in 2017, and indie-Americana singer-songwriter Steve Gunn, who accompanied him at gigs in the early 2010s. “I got to tour with him in 2011 and felt his Jesus-y vibes,” Callahan says. “Jesus with a little scamp mixed in.” For Callahan, there is a “primordial” element in Askew’s music, that “makes it feel like the world is evolving right before your eyes when his song is playing. There’s a holy aspect to it – those piano chords constructed like sheets of diamond ice.”From the scent of a marigold to the death of a Kennedy, what stands out the most about Askew’s songwriting is his flâneur-like observations, evoking the quotidian and the tremendous. In 2011, Askew cited his early influences as Kurt Weill, John Cage and Erik Satie, while others have tried to compare Askew to the likes of Robert Wyatt, Gaslight-era Bob Dylan or the Incredible String Band. The truth is that his baroque songwriting bears more resemblance to the multiple-angled paintings of Cézanne – an artist he greatly admired – or the writing of Joe Brainard mixed with the poetry of Lorca, than it does any of its musical equivalents.View image in fullscreenUnconcerned with commercial success … Ed Askew, date unknown.Askew’s song Moon in the Mind appeared on the soundtrack of Netflix’s smash-hit series Baby Reindeer in 2024, an inclusion that was welcome and unexpected in the last year of his life, DeCicca says. But for the most part, Askew wasn’t concerned with acclaim or commerciality. “He just wasn’t wired that way,” DeCicca says; instead, Askew simply focused on writing his fine-spun songs on a day-to-day basis.He displayed that beatnik essence from the very beginning. Born in Connecticut in 1940, Askew studied painting at Yale School of Art in the early 1960s, before moving to New York for a brief spell, playing for tips in the “basket house” folk music venues of the East Village in 1967. It was here that he attracted the attention of New York-based record company ESP-Disk – usually more inclined towards free jazz giants such as Sun Ra and Pharoah Sanders – who put out his 1968 self-titled debut (later retitled Ask the Unicorn), but soon shelved his follow-up Little Eyes, which wasn’t released until 2002.View image in fullscreen‘I play music for you to feel’ … Performing in Köln in 2013.Photograph: Ullstein Bild/Getty ImagesOver the course of his life, Askew put out approximately 50 albums between labels, archival releases, live albums and self-releases, “with more still unreleased in his vault”, says DeCicca. This was mostly a home-recorded and self-published enterprise funded by house painting and art teaching, plus welfare assistance for a time – before he returned to the studio in 2011. Regarding a song on the new record titled Someone, with Callahan on vocals, DeCicca wonders if Askew is waiting in a social security office while they’re calling out numbers: “16”, “22” and “46” are delivered by Callahan with his usual deadpan delivery. “I think if most people wrote a song like that it would be about their agitation but Ed’s point of view is so different,” DeCicca says. But who knows if it’s really about social security at all, such is the impressionistic nature of Askew’s lyric writing.Jay Pluck, Askew’s close friend and collaborator, points out just how pioneering Askew’s psych-folk songwriting was in the 1960s. “It was quite brave for him to be writing openly gay love songs so early on,” he tells me. And yet: “Over the years, I noticed that no one really knew that he was gay and I don’t know why – therearegay love songs on Ask the Unicorn and Little Eyes.” Pluck mentions a later song called Lamar, recorded on cassette tape in the late-90s, in which Askew is “professing his love in a Greek paean kind of way”.View image in fullscreen‘A true artist’ … Ed Askew.Photograph: Christian FilardoWould Askew have described himself as a gay songwriter, I wonder? “I think he would,” Pluck answers – before pausing. “Althoughhecouldbe a contrarian. If you asked him if he thought he was a great gay artist, he’d say no. But if you left off ‘gay’ he’d be like, why?” Pluck laughs. For Askew, his sexuality was simply who he was, as much a part of his songs as anything else.Folk singer Michael Hurley at 80: ‘The way music comes to you, it’s like dreaming’Read moreYet for Askew, “like most obscure musicians, there was never that Searching for Sugar Man blast,” DeCicca says, citing the 2012 documentary that launched Mexican-American musician Rodríguez out of obscurity and into late-in-life fame. “What allowed Ed to gradually find a global audience was not one event shining a spotlight; it was that he lived, day in and day out, as a true artist, sharing meaningful breadcrumbs for more and more people to discover.”It’s a slow-burn tale in the age of the algorithm, and a fitting one for a musician who lived life on his own terms: “I play music for you to feel,” he once sang.“There is no trying in his music,” Callahan says. “It just is.”The Final Painting is released 31 July on Drag CityExplore more on these topicsFolk musicPop and rockfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘New Girl was so scary I had to stop smoking pot’: Jake Johnson on perseverance, pickleball and Prince | Movies | The Guardian
description: As Nick in the blockbuster TV comedy, the actor was adored by millions. So will his new comedy The Dink, which he jokes is a ‘sexier Challengers’, make him even more popular?
Author: Rebecca Nicholson
Headline: As Nick in the blockbuster TV comedy, the actor was adored by millions. So will his new comedy The Dink, which he jokes is a ‘sexier Challengers’, make him even more popular?
Content: ‘I was just trying to get a seat at the Hollywood table’ … Jake Johnson.Photograph: Robby Klein/Getty Images North AmericaView image in fullscreen‘I was just trying to get a seat at the Hollywood table’ … Jake Johnson.Photograph: Robby Klein/Getty Images North AmericaMoviesInterview‘New Girl was so scary I had to stop smoking pot’: Jake Johnson on perseverance, pickleball and PrinceRebecca NicholsonAs Nick in the blockbuster TV comedy, the actor was adored by millions. So will his new comedy The Dink, which he jokes is a ‘sexier Challengers’, make him even more popular?Tue 21 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 05.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleYou might assume that Jake Johnson would know a little something about pickleball. The actor is starring in The Dink, a sort of spiritual heir to Dodgeball – it has aBen Stillercameo – and he plays Dusty Boyd, a former tennis pro turned plucky pickleball upstart. Presumably Johnson took the gig because he has a deep love of the sport, a fondness for its pockmarked balls and weirdly smooth paddles? “No experience of it whatsoever,” says the actor, cheerfully, on a video call from Los Angeles.Yet this underdog of a racket sport, loved by retirees, found him anyway. At the climax of the movie, Dusty must play against an old foe, which meant Johnson had to undertake months of real-life training in pickleball and tennis, so it looked as if he could play. “It was like WWE. They call it fake wrestling. The outcome is there, but you still have to wrestle. That was probably the most stressful part of the film to me.”The Dink came to Johnson through its writer, Sean Clements, who had taken up the sport. “He started playing pickleball, and then became friends with this group of older women in Beverly Hills. He’s like, they talk so much shit, and get so competitive, it’s just so funny.” At the time, Johnson was searching for a specific kind of comedy. “I was looking to do something really fun and light, with big comedic set pieces. I wanted to make a movie that feels a little old school, there’s no messaging, it’s not trying to teach people anything.” It was just before the 2024 presidential election in the US. “I knew, however that goes, it’s going to get crazy for our country, and it’s going to be very divisive, and the internet is going to get all mad at each other about everything, and we’re all going to be fighting,” he says.The Dink was born from a desire to escape, if just for a moment. “I thought, if I’m going to make something, I don’t want to throw more wood on to that fire. You could say the world is burning, and you have so much great evidence. But can we just talk about this, for an hour and a half? Because while this is going on, there’s also life.”There is no denying that culture has taken on a new intensity in recent years. FromMarty SupremetoOne Battle After Another, movies and television are heightened, brittle and usually immensely stressful to watch. “You go: man, I know everybody is loving this, and it’s winning all the awards, but I can’t watch it. I’m already fucking stressed. Give me a break!”The Dink is that break. Dusty is “a goober”, according to Johnson. He works at his father’s country club and remains bitter about the injury that ended his promising tennis career, for which he blames Andy Roddick. When Dusty takes up pickleball, he joins a tough-talking, hard-hitting gang of older women, led by Mary Steenburgen’s Candace, who schools Dusty in sport and life. In a particularly lovely strand, Dusty and Candace develop a rock-solid intergenerational friendship. “I don’t agree with this idea that men and women can’t be friends, and that no matter what, it’s going to be screwed up with sex,” he says. “That feels so crazy old school and puritan to me.” Steenburgen is a legend of comedy, and Johnson was already a big fan. “Parenthood meant a ton to me when I first saw it. So did Step Brothers. I feel about her the way that Dusty feels about Candace. I don’t view her as myolderfriend. Mary’s my friend. I would hang out with Mary whenever.”View image in fullscreen‘I would hang out with her whenever’ … Johnson and Mary Steenburgen in The Dink.Photograph: Andrew Cooper/Courtesy of Apple TVHas Johnson seen that other great racket sports movie, the steamy, sweaty, Zendaya-starringChallengers? He has not, he says, but he defers to his co-star Aaron Chen’s response: “Chen refers to our movie as the sexier Challengers.”Johnson started acting in high school, in Chicago. He had dropped out at 15, unsure of the path his life was going to take, but went back to give it one last shot. “I couldn’t crack the code. I just knew that nothing was working. And then I did a play, and I was like, oh,thisis great.” It changed the course of his life. “I got credit for the first time from stuffy adults who did not like me,” he says.What was there to dislike? “I don’t know. Like, if there was a party at someone’s house and somebody found matches, all the parents assumed I brought the matches,” he says. “And a lot of the time, I had brought the matches.” Acting funnelled that restless energy into something positive. “I didn’t even realise it could be a real job. I didn’t realise that Cheers, which was my favourite show, was just a bunch of working actors who had a job. Once I started doing the math on it, I thought, I’ve got to go all in. That’s everything for me.”View image in fullscreen‘He called us by our character names’ … with Zooey Deschanel and Prince in New Girl.Photograph: Fox Television/REX/ShutterstockHe moved to Los Angeles and spent years as a jobbing actor, taking whatever he could, whenever he could. “I call it grinding,” he recalls. “If a commercial came, I’m taking it. If a co-star [role] comes, I’m taking it.” He appeared in Curb Your Enthusiasm as “Man on Cell Phone”, in Get Him to the Greek as “Jazz Man”. He describes Hollywood as “a really long dinner table with a bunch of seats, and the seats keep getting taken out, and then a new phase will come. You just want to stay at the table. At that point, I was just trying to get to the table.”Then came New Girl, the smash-hit sitcom that arrived in his life when he was 33. For seven years, he played the romantic lead, Nick, to Zooey Deschanel’s Jess. “New Girl was a very scary job, because all of them were really prepared. I had to stop smoking pot, because I was like, oh, these fools know their lines! I got to make some changes and do it fast, or else I’m going to lose this job,” he laughs.That job gave him his seat at the table. New Girl was such a smash that after a couple of years, Prince reached out and requested a cameo. He was a huge fan of the show, and would watch it on his tour bus. He appeared in 2014, in a post-Super Bowl episode that was watched by 26.3 million people. On set, Prince referred to Deschanel as Jess, and to Johnson as Nick. “I grew up loving Prince. We would take road trips from Chicago just to listen to his music in Minneapolis. So I thought, I’m going to show this man respect. I’m not going to be weird. And then I heard him say, ‘I’d like to meet Nick now.’ And he was so great.” They talked for hours about sport and TV. “Cheers, Roseanne and New Girl. Those were his three shows.”View image in fullscreenBox-office material … with Tom Cruise in The Mummy in 2017.Photograph: Collection Christophel/AlamyWhen New Girl blew up, Johnson’s life was transformed. “I think it exploded my brain for about six months. I spiralled out, and I didn’t know what it all meant. Bill Murray’s got a great quote that when you first get fame, you’ve got about a year where you can kind of be an asshole, and then if you stay an asshole after that, that just means you’re an asshole.” Johnson took a while to adjust to being recognised when out in the world, but it helped that he was already in his 30s. “Honestly, the dust just settled. I thought, this is the life that I wanted.”Johnson has been working solidly ever since, with a varied and busy career, ranging from blockbusters including Jurassic World and The Mummy, to the cruelly cancelled adult publishing comedy Minx (“that one makes me sad”) and, more recently, Apple’s sex-cam murder mystery Maximum Pleasure Guaranteed. He has also reunited with his old Drinking Buddies and Digging for Fire director Joe Swanberg, for The Sun Never Sets, with Dakota Fanning. When we speak, they have just closed a deal to take it to cinemas, with an expected release date later this year.double quotation markIf I didn't have kids I'd have a ponytail and would be doing this interview shirtlessStill, he says, there’s nothing like having kids to keep you grounded. In 2014, his wife, the artist Erin Payne, gave birth to their twin daughters. “Kids changed everything. I think if I didn’t have kids, I’d have a ponytail now, and I wouldn’t be wearing a shirt in this interview, because I wouldn’t believe in shirts,” he laughs. “Kids humble you, fast. They arenotfans of the old Jakester and his work.”Not even Spider-Man? (He voiced a version of Spider-Man in two animated films, Into the Spider-Verse and Across the Spider-Verse; a third is in production.) “They thought Hailee [Steinfeld]’s Spider-Gwen was cool. They thought my character was the most embarrassing, because he just ate pizza and was the old chubby one. The vibe was kind of like, why’d you say yes to that?” He had to tell them that’s not exactly how it works. “I was like, I don’t get to say, ‘Can I be the coolest one in there?’”It sounds as if Johnson has no problem staying humble. “Exactly right,” he says. “Unfortunately!”The Dink is out on Apple TV on 24 JulyExplore more on these topicsFilmBen StillerComedy filmsSport filmsComedyinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: The Dink review – Jake Johnson’s pickleball comedy serves up engagingly silly laughs | Movies | The Guardian
description: Johnson is delusional ex-tennis player The Hammer, who has to switch to the sport he loathes after injury
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: Johnson is delusional ex-tennis player The Hammer, who has to switch to the sport he loathes after injury
Content: Nice support … Aaron Chen as submissive wingman PJ and Jake Johnson as Dusty.Photograph: Andrew Cooper/Courtesy of AppleView image in fullscreenNice support … Aaron Chen as submissive wingman PJ and Jake Johnson as Dusty.Photograph: Andrew Cooper/Courtesy of AppleMoviesReviewThe Dink review – Jake Johnson’s pickleball comedy serves up engagingly silly laughsJohnson is delusional ex-tennis player The Hammer, who has to switch to the sport he loathes after injuryPeter BradshawTue 21 Jul 2026 12.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.25 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThere’s some funny stuff here in this engagingly silly sports comedy from producer-star Jake Johnson about the world of pickleball, and how this (allegedly) facile pseudo-tennis game for children and old people threatens to infantilise and dumb down the world of tennis itself.Dusty (Johnson) is a middle-aged loser, delusionally nicknamed The Hammer, who once showed early promise at tennis before getting crushed by a humiliating teen encounter with the youngAndy Roddick(playing himself in cameo). These days Dusty coaches at the tennis club owned by his dad (Ed Harris), who is scowlingly contemptuous of everything Dusty says and does.
