Only one thing allows poor Dusty to reclaim his self-respect as a man and a tennis player: his utter loathing for pickleball, whose followers are grudgingly permitted one of their toytown mini-courts at the club – and he duly gets a meet-cute quarrel with pickleball enthusiast Candace (Mary Steenburgen).But Dusty’s doctor (Ben Stiller) recommends pickleball as the best way to recover from a wrist injury, itself the pretext for a mountain of “jerking off” gags, and he discovers that he kind of likes the noble art, with its slower, more thoughtful pace and its prominent role for the humble “dink” drop shot. And he more than likes Candace as well … although the film flinches from the reality of an age-gap relationship.Inevitably for any comedy about tennis,John McEnroehas a funny walk-on, playing himself and commentating with baffling intensity on the climactic on-court confrontation between Dusty and his nemesis Roddick. There is nice support from Australian comic Aaron Chen as Dusty’s cringingly submissive beta-male wingman PJ, as well as from Patton Oswalt as the pickleball partisan Slip – although 30 Rock fans will consider the film has supplied a pretty meagre role for Chris Parnell, the great Dr Spaceman in Tina Fey’s TV comedy classic.The Dink is on Apple TV from 25 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmComedy filmsTennisAndy RoddickJohn McEnroeBen StillerEd HarrisreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Shin Kamen Rider: who wouldn’t want to watch an action flick about a grasshopper-man hybrid? | Movies | The Guardian
description: Based on a 70s series, this film by Neon Genesis Evangelion creator Hideaki Anno is a thrilling lesson in standing up to evil (and driving very fast)
Author: Paul F Verhoeven
Headline: Based on a 70s series, this film by Neon Genesis Evangelion creator Hideaki Anno is a thrilling lesson in standing up to evil (and driving very fast)
Content: ‘Both cynical about humanity’s tendency to make things worse for itself, and insistent that we need to fight the good fight anyway’ … Hideaki Anno’s 2023 film Shin Kamen Rider.Photograph: YoutubeView image in fullscreen‘Both cynical about humanity’s tendency to make things worse for itself, and insistent that we need to fight the good fight anyway’ … Hideaki Anno’s 2023 film Shin Kamen Rider.Photograph: YoutubeStream teamMoviesShin Kamen Rider: who wouldn’t want to watch an action flick about a grasshopper-man hybrid?Based on a 70s series, this film by Neon Genesis Evangelion creator Hideaki Anno is a thrilling lesson in standing up to evil (and driving very fast)Paul F VerhoevenTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 19.40 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWriter and director Hideaki Anno – the visionary behind cult anime hit Neon Genesis Evangelion – grew up watchingtokusatsu: Japanese live-action films and TV shows that lean heavily on practical special effects.One of his idols was Kamen Rider, the protagonist of the 1971 series of the same name, a genetically augmented man-grasshopper, riding around and fighting evil on the back of a souped-up motorcycle.Decades later, in 2023, Anno faithfully recreated Kamen Rider’s story as part of his Shin trilogy (shin means “new” in Japanese), three bold reimaginings of the stories and characters that captivated him as a child.His first two films in the trilogy, Shin Godzilla (2016) and Shin Ultraman (2022), were critically acclaimed and both carried clear messages. Shin Godzilla is an avant garde critique of bureaucratic idiocy in the face of an otherworldly threat, whereas Shin Ultraman is a feelgood, optimistic tale of a gigantic alien superhero determined to save humanity from itself.Shin Kamen Rider (sometimes called Shin Masked Rider), however, has a worldview that sits tantalisingly in the middle of this tonal gulf. It is both cynical about humanity’s tendency to make things worse for itself, and insistent that we need to fight the good fight anyway.The one doing the fighting is Kamen Rider, whose true name is Takeshi Hongo (Sosuke Ikematsu), a young man kidnapped by an evil organisation called Shocker. The organisation transforms him, The Island of Doctor Moreau-style, into a horrifically strong, fast and agile grasshopper-human hybrid. But right before they flip the figurative good-to-evil switch, a disgruntled Shocker employee, Ruriko Midorikawa (Minami Hamabe), sets him free. Ruriko and the newly liberated Hongo unite and resolve to take Shocker down, one bloody showdown at a time.As Hongo and Ruriko battle increasingly Lovecraftian abominations on their crusade to topple Shocker, they encounter another poor soul augmented against his will: Hayato Ichimonji (Tasuku Emoto), the organisation’s second swing at a compliant grasshopper-themed super soldier. But Anno’s optimism wins out. Rather than murdering Hayato, they decide to free him from his brainwashing and recruit him.The Three Musketeers: D’Artagnan brings genuine thrills – and a massive budget – to a well-trodden taleRead moreWhat makes Kamen Rider so compelling is that he regularly contends with the fact that he doesn’t want the powers he’s been saddled with. He’s a hero whocouldsave the day, if only he had the stomach for what that may entail.In spite of its bloodiness, Shin Kamen Rider is a profoundly hopeful film. Kamen Rider has been chopped up, reassembled and fused with insect DNA, but refuses to give up in the face of monstrous odds, regardless of whether he can succeed. And if Kamen Rider never gives up, why, even in a world rife with injustice, would we?It’s also a thrilling ride. The film has a gorgeously evocative original soundtrack by Shunsuke Kikuchi, and utilises the almost Grindhouse-level of unsubtlety of the 1971 series’ crash-zooms, typography and editing. In short, Anno evokes the energy and spirit of the old series with such brazenness and lack of irony that it feels utterly new again. Nothing will make you feel like a kid again quite as much as when the skittish, mild-mannered Hongo transforms into Kamen Rider, mask snapping shut, eyes lighting up, and crimson scarf billowing behind in his wake. It’s primal, magical stuff.Shin Kamen Rider is available worldwide on Amazon Prime under the title Shin Masked Rider. title: Brunello: The Gracious Visionary review – fawning portrait of the king of cashmere | Movies | The Guardian
description: Giuseppe Tornatore traces Italian fashion designer Brunello Cucinelli’s life from poor farming family to billion-dollar empire in a sentimental vanity project
Author: Cath Clarke
Headline: Giuseppe Tornatore traces Italian fashion designer Brunello Cucinelli’s life from poor farming family to billion-dollar empire in a sentimental vanity project
Content: The good guy billionaire … Brunello Cucinelli in Brunello: The Gracious Visionary.Photograph: Publicity imageView image in fullscreenThe good guy billionaire … Brunello Cucinelli in Brunello: The Gracious Visionary.Photograph: Publicity imageMoviesReviewBrunello: The Gracious Visionary review – fawning portrait of the king of cashmereGiuseppe Tornatore traces Italian fashion designer Brunello Cucinelli’s life from poor farming family to billion-dollar empire in a sentimental vanity projectCath ClarkeTue 21 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBrunello Cucinelli is the Italian fashion designer and entrepreneur who built a billion-dollar empire. He’s known as the “king of cashmere”, which would have been a good title for this two-hour-long film, commissioned by Cucinelli about himself, directed by Oscar winning film-maker Giuseppe Tornatore. Instead, “The Gracious Visionary” gives an inkling as to the kind of film we’re dealing with – a vanity project with a capital “V”, capital “A”, “N” and all the other letters.Not that you would begrudge Cucinelli a film about his extraordinary life. He was born into a poor hard-working farming family, shown here in scenes reconstructed with sugary sentimentalism as a simple time of innocent pleasures. Cucinelli as a boy is played as a cheeky lad who grows into a teenager more interested in playing cards at the local bar than studying. (Though his attempts to set the town’s prostitute on the right path are included to show his moral compass.) After Cucinelli meets his future wife, he knuckles down, does some modelling, has the brainwave to produce cashmere jumpers for the women’s market in eye-popping colours (they were previously only made in boring grey and beige). Today he is the owner of a brand synonymous with understated luxury, available to those with £2,500 to spend on a jumper.The film is a hybrid of documentary and drama, and in interview the man himself comes across as a good guy billionaire. He is charming and prides himself on being a good employer, inspired by the poor conditions in the factory where his father worked after the family moved to the city for a better life. He has also invested heavily to save the medieval buildings in the town where his business is based from disrepair and collapse. But the film overdoes it with the sycophancy and exceptionalism. At one point we even see archive footage of Cucinelli in his 20s appearing in an Easter Passion play, playing the actual Jesus Christ.In the end this feels less like a film for cinema than a lavish in-house promo for new staff, a mandatory induction-day viewing. I’m not sure it’s necessary for the rest of us.Brunello: The Gracious Visionary is in UK and Irish cinemas from 24 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmDocumentary filmsItalyBiopicsreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Social Darwinists to tech bros: tracing the long fight against equality in US | Books | The Guardian
description: Historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter Thiel
Author: Martin Pengelly
Headline: Historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter Thiel
Content: Peter Thiel and Elon Musk in 2000.Photograph: Paul Sakuma/Associated PressView image in fullscreenPeter Thiel and Elon Musk in 2000.Photograph: Paul Sakuma/Associated PressBooksSocial Darwinists to tech bros: tracing the long fight against equality in USHistorian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter ThielMartin PengellyTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.43 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s new book is published amid celebrations of the250th anniversaryof the declaration of independence and its truth “self-evident, that all men are created equal”. Country of Lords, however, is a history of Neo-Aristocrats, Social Darwinists, Tech Utopians, and the Long FightAgainstEquality in America.Concise and hard-hitting, the book arrives at a time of glaring social andeconomicinequality, Phillips-Fein seeingElon Musk“emerging as a trillionairevery much on people’s minds, with a deep level of discomfort, both fear and anger about what this portends”.‘People are picking the dumbest fights’: the tortured history of America’s culture warsRead more“It’s always hard to know what’s going to be happening when you have written a book,” she said. “But I think those questions about economic equality and the impact of extreme levels of economic hierarchy and concentration, and the questions, ‘Can you still have a democracy any longer? What are the ways that you can counter that? What alternate visions are there if you want to live in a world that’s not characterized that way?’, are present.”Phillips-Fein studies conservatism and “questions about capitalism and democracy”. Her first book,Invisible Hands, was a study of businessmen who fought the New Deal of the 1930s. Her second, Fear City, about the New York economic crisis of the 1970s, was afinalist for the Pulitzer prize. Hers is a major voice. It’s also unique. Not many books start with founder John Adams and end with Peter Thiel of Palantir, Musk’sfellow techno-fascist. Might such company seem unfair on Adams, the Boston lawyer who was part of a great revolution but feared what was unleashed?“I hope this comes through,” Phillips-Fein said. “Adams is very different from [steel magnate] Andrew Carnegie and [Yale professor]William Graham Sumnerand the late 19th-century social Darwinists, and the pro-slavery advocates. He’s also definitely very different from people who are saying extreme levels of economic inequality are good.” Adams, however, was “very ambivalent and skeptical of egalitarian claims … during and after the French Revolution: a deep sense of you can have too much egalitarianism.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: WW Norton & Company“In his writing, he does also give a strong kind of conservative temperament and foundational sense of the problems of arguing for greater equality and a deep wariness and sense of pessimism about a politics that claims to do that.”And so Adams takes his place in Country of Lords. Other subjects include preachers of race-based inequality from George Fitzhugh, a Confederate theorist of slavery as natural good, toTheodore Lothrop Stoddard, a hack journalist who had a huge bestseller in 1920 with The Rising Tide of Color Against White World-Supremacy, “part of a larger intellectual subculture of ‘scientific’ racism … in the aftermath of world war one”. There is also Carnegie, the Scottish-born tycoon who flourished in the Gilded Age of the late 19th century.“What makes Carnegie so interesting is that he at least allows himself to have some sense of ambivalence,” Phillips-Fein said. “He says that while the concentration of wealth is absolutely necessary and inevitable, and the people at the top … should have the ultimate say over resources, he is deeply aware that this is politically dangerous, that … it creates an unstable society in which the poor and the working class might actually hate the wealthy, and that’s not in the interest of the wealthy in the long run. Also, he has a sense that human brotherhood is lost, or some kind of community that had existed.”Carnegie’s father was a Chartist, seeking working-class rights. Carnegie’s major outlet for doubts about his wealth came through philanthropy, prominently the construction oflibrariesfor working people.“He had a very deep sense of paternalism,” Phillips-Fein said. “He was very invested in the idea of business as a romance and kind of a space of adventure and conquest and feudal benevolence. I think Carnegie’s answer was ultimately that the wealthy should have their money but they shouldn’t bequeath much to their descendants, they should invest it in massive philanthropic projects, and this was better than raising wages or other things like that … It is his level of guilt and ambivalence that I think does make him appealing, if also troubling. I think it raises a set of questions that he certainly was never really able to resolve.”The 1892 Carnegie Steel strike and its violent suppression devastated Homestead, Pennsylvania, and left Carnegie disillusioned. Now, in Washington, Carnegie Science is the Qatari embassy. At Mount Vernon, the Carnegie library hosts an Apple Store.Country of Lords is also a primer on modern inequality. In the early 1900s,Henry Fordmixed mass auto production with vicious racism. In mid-century,William Shockleywas a proto-tech mogul “who went completely off the rails”.View image in fullscreenAndrew Carnegie.Photograph: AP“He was a co-inventor of the transistor and he figures in different histories of Silicon Valley because he ran this company and wanted to maintain a tremendous amount of control, and as a result a bunch of the employees quit and founded their own firm [twoeventuallyfounding Intel] … So after Shockley Labs fell apart, Shockley started to become drawn to racist science, pseudoscience and IQ testing in particular. I don’t think anybody really knows exactly what triggered this, but he starts to talk a lot about it publicly in the mid-60s. He was at Stanford at the time. He was supposed to be teaching physics and engineering.”Shockley, Phillips-Fein says, was really “a uniquely horrible person, and probably quite unhappy as well.” If that sounds familiar, also like Musk, Shockley wasn’t unique. Linked to the meritocratic movement of the mid-century, he was “not isolated. He is actually part of what is a growing, troubling community of people who coalesce in the late 60s, early 70s, and who are really responding, not always directly, to the context of the civil rights movement.“It was obviously focused on overturning racist legislation that undergirded segregation, but it also made claims about the distribution of resources, saying the reasons for Black poverty are not innate, they reflect centuries of legal and political structures that have upheld white wealth and laid the foundation for exploitation of Black workers and working-class people, and the political disfranchisement has resulted in this distribution of resources. In other words, that’s nothing to do with merit at all.”In such soil, Phillips-Fein finds the roots of “the tech titans of today”, rejecting liberal ideals.“They are very skeptical and hostile towards elite institutions, or at least, have a very broad relationship to them,” she said. “So Peter Thiel embraces this set of ideas about competition drawn from theeconomist Joseph Schumpeter. He writes about how companies in a normal marketplace are competing at the margins … but every so often someone will come along and create a new invention, usually that winds up dominating the marketplace and changing it, and whoever does that first will completely change the marketplace.“Thiel has the same idea. He kind of writes about how, ‘I was growing up, I wanted to be the best, I wanted to go to these elite institutions. I went to law school, I had the straight and narrow path, but then I realized that all that was wrong, and actually you had to disrupt everything, create your own monopoly, a monopoly of one: nobody else can ever be you,’ and then the people who are kind of blessed with this genius to recognize how to create something completely new in the world, those are the people who will deserve to win in this race and deserve to command the resources of society.”The Country of Lords endures.Country of Lords, published by WW Norton & Company, isout nowExplore more on these topicsBooksHistory booksPeter ThielElon MuskUS politicsUS income inequalityfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: These Wild English by Nicola Wilding review – explaining a mother’s drift to the far right | Politics books | The Guardian
description: A documentary-maker probes family history and the darker corners of the internet to solve an urgent political puzzle
Author: Fiona Sturges
Headline: A documentary-maker probes family history and the darker corners of the internet to solve an urgent political puzzle
Content: Nicola Wilding at her home in Caldbeck, Cumbria.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianView image in fullscreenNicola Wilding at her home in Caldbeck, Cumbria.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianPolitics booksReviewThese Wild English by Nicola Wilding review – explaining a mother’s drift to the far rightA documentary-maker probes family history and the darker corners of the internet to solve an urgent political puzzleFiona SturgesTue 21 Jul 2026 05.30 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.58 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen Sandra Forsyth began marching in support of the English Defence League, her daughter Nicola Wilding hoped it was a phase. The Cumbria-bred, Dover-dwelling Sandra was about to turn 60 and had been dealing with a lot of stress. Not only had her son been sentenced to two years in prison for armed robbery, but she was working at a home for adults with complex needs. She liked the job, but it could be gruelling – especially when clients hurled furniture at her or ripped sinks off walls.Then, in May 2013, came the death of Lee Rigby, a drummer with the Second Battalion of the Royal Regiment of Fusiliers who was murdered by two Islamist terrorists using machetes. Sandra didn’t know Rigby, Wilding notes, but his death nonetheless brought her pent-up rage to the surface. She began watching videos on Facebook by a man named Tommy Robinson, who wanted to know why the country’s brave soldiers were being murdered. By September of that year, she was protesting on the streets of London.Inspired by the puzzle of her mother’s late-life activism, These Wild English is a heartfelt and evocative memoir of Sandra who, up until her death in 2024, endured poverty, upheaval and abusive partners, as well as a heart condition leading to two rounds of surgery. “How did my mum, this proud tough woman who had brought up four kids pretty much alone, end up marching alongside far-right extremists chanting ‘No more refugees’?” Wilding asks. “And how did my brother – a kind, smart, funny man who loved his kids – how did he end up in prison? … Was it us? Did we do it to ourselves? Or was it something else – something bigger than us?”The “us” in this story is important. Wilding, the eldest of Forsyth’s children and one of the few in her family to find stability through education and a career, is a TV documentary maker specialising in poverty and rural working-class communities, though here she is no dispassionate observer. She also has an eye on the bigger picture, noting how wider events and the social and economic decisions taken in Westminster can affect the lives of vulnerable individuals and communities. For the Forsyth family, these have included the winter of discontent, mass unemployment and Thatcher’s right-to-buy scheme in the 1970s and 80s; the foot-and-mouth outbreak and the financial crisis in the 2000s; and the Tory austerity programme, Brexit referendum and resurgence of the far right in the 2010s.Wilding isn’t the first author to examine the alienation of the British working classes and the ways they have been exploited, pilloried and misunderstood: Kerry Hudson’sLowborn, Joel Budd’sUnderdogsand Rebecca Smith’sRuralhave all offered their own perspectives. In our social media-fanned, post-Brexit discourse, the white working classes are often cast as aggressors waging vicious war on immigrant communities, having been manipulated by self-interested politicians deliberately stoking division. Wilding has little time for such one-dimensional depictions, despite her clear dismay at her mother’s flirtation with the far right.Instead, writing in thoughtful and elegantly understated prose, she digs into the roots of Sandra’s dissatisfaction, telling a story that spans three generations, starting out with her early life in the 1960s on her father’s farm in Eden Valley, Cumbria, where she was tasked with looking after her siblings during the recurring periods when her own mother did a runner. Exasperated by the drudgery of parenting and rural living, Nana Forsyth would leave the family for weeks on end before returning home clutching an olive branch in the form of sweets for the kids and cigarettes for her husband.My mother was an excellent care worker. Why did she end up marching with the EDL?Read moreAt 18, Sandra became pregnant and attempted to make a life with the baby’s father, moving to a cottage up the road from the farm. But within a couple of years, he had left and Sandra was a penniless single mother. And so began a peripatetic pattern that would continue throughout her life: a new man, a new home, an implosion followed by deeper debt and the quest for a fresh start.In the book’s acknowledgments, Wilding credits the writer and farmerJames Rebanks, a former neighbour in Cumbria, for “seeing this book before I could”. It seems Wilding took some convincing to turn her documentarian’s gaze on her own flesh and blood, though, happily, she did. Full of grit and compassion, These Wild English blends the personal and political to illustrate the ways families are shaped by circumstance and inherited trauma, and can be buffeted by forces beyond their control. At its heart is Sandra, a protagonist for the ages who is wayward, charismatic, fierce, caring and – wisely or not – always up for a fight.These Wild English: A Family, a Class, a Country on Fire by Nicola Wilding is published by Profile (£16.99). To support the Guardian order your copy atguardianbookshop.com. Delivery charges may apply.Explore more on these topicsPolitics booksThe far rightSociety booksreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Cool Machine by Colson Whitehead review – final part of the Harlem Trilogy is freewheeling fun | Fiction | The Guardian
description: The grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealer
Author: Xan Brooks
Headline: The grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealer
Content: Colson Whitehead.Photograph: PR/PR from little brownView image in fullscreenColson Whitehead.Photograph: PR/PR from little brownBook of the dayFictionReviewCool Machine by Colson Whitehead review – final part of the Harlem Trilogy is freewheeling funThe grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealerXan BrooksTue 21 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 02.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn the summer of 1977 a bolt of lightning struck a substation on the Hudson River, knocking out power across New York City. Two nights of darkness sparked a wave of looting as Harlem and Bronx teenagers swiped high-end items from the downtown stores. They took speaker systems and turntables to experiment with at home. They taught themselves how to scratch and mix, and sang self-styled scat lyrics into stolen microphones. Hip-hop, according to urban legend, was the child of a lightning strike and a robbing spree.It’s art from crime, thinks Ray Carney, the hardscrabble hero of Colson Whitehead’s rollicking Cool Machine, the final part of his Harlem Trilogy. Or possibly vice versa, given that the man dislikes rap music and nurses a sneaking affection for old-school smash-and-grab. For years Carney has led the sort of intrepid double life that would make Spider-Man proud, supplementing his furniture business on 125th Street with a lucrative sideline as an underworld fence. In his stealthy, dogged fashion, he helps facilitate a process he describes as “the churn”: the movement of stolen goods through New York. The churn fuels the economy and helps refresh the culture. It’s the city’s equivalent of an endocrine system.The Harlem Trilogy, like its hero, is a cool-headed trickster, more ambitious and far-reaching than its dimestore facade would suggest. Carney’s tale can be relished as a set of shaggy-dog crime capers that bound from the high-stakes heist to the mysterious bag of loot to the fraught getaway through the rat-infested back alley. But it is also – perhaps principally – a story of New York through the decades, and its semi-autonomous circles of black and white society.Harlem Shuffle, the first book in the series, hotwired the action to the 60s civil rights movement. The second, 2023’sCrook Manifesto, played out during the 70s “Fear City” era, when the infrastructure collapsed, the murder rate spiked and Carney’s store doubled as a blaxploitation film set.double quotation markWe're in the dirty, regenerative 1980s: hip-hop on the stereo, graffiti and a widening gulf between rich and poorNow along comes Cool Machine, the grandstanding final chapter, which pitches us into the dirty, regenerative 1980s. That means hip-hop on the stereo, graffiti on the subway cars and a widening gulf between rich and poor. It means, in this case, a plot that plays like pop-art Marxist noir, jam-packed with cultural references and thrumming with class and racial tensions.Searching for a secret route into the five-star Waldorf Astoria, Carney encounters a Morlock-style community in the train sheds beneath Manhattan, a subterranean tent city governed by the self-styled “Mayor Crotch”. The homeless people in the tunnels, Carney realises, are casualties of the prosperity above. “The richer those up top got, the more miserable and numerous those below became. And the more cast-offs and have-nots, the higher you have to build to get away from them.”Carney is a creature of the underworld too, but he’s also a middleman or a bridge, directing the traffic up and down to the point where the colour line becomes porous. Skipping through three interlinked tales from 1981 to 1986, Cool Machine uncovers the seeds of 21st-century cultural appropriation in the trade of African artefacts and sports memorabilia. White and black crooks squabble over the ownership of a Jesse Owens Olympic medal. A Ngil mask from Gabon electrifies the grungy East Village arts scene. A decade ago, Carney’s director friend Zippo was shooting blaxploitation pictures uptown. Now he’s hanging with the arthouse crowd, working on a redubbed version of Alfred Hitchcock’s 1963 classic in which the N-word is substituted for every mention of birds. Hitchcock’s original film was always a parable for racial desegregation, Zippo insists. So he’s simply taking the subtext and making it text.Whitehead initially envisioned the Harlem Trilogy as a jaunty entertainment, a break from the serious Pulitzer-winning business of The Underground Railroad and The Nickel Boys. Cool Machine specifically covers the New York of his youth, a scene he experienced first-hand, which only adds to the air of vivid, freewheeling fun. Whitehead maps the streets, labels the landmarks and dips in at intervals with handy background information. He takes a dark and dangerous corner of history and makes it feel illuminated, magnified, even borderline family-friendly.It’s the churn, paradoxically, that keeps the tale honest. It adds grit and grandeur to the sugar hit of nostalgia. The churn, Carney decides, applies to more than the flow of stolen goods. It is the great whip and roil of the city itself; the successive waves of immigrants washing through the individual neighbourhoods. “The enclaves and ghettoes rose and fell and were reborn. That was the churn, too – the underlying melody of death and rebirth in blocks and avenues. You wake up to discover the city has unveiled another version when you weren’t looking.” Carney is now in his 50s and has one eye on the exit door. He’s toiling to maintain his position, fighting the currents of a city that is as ageless and elemental as the Mississippi River. New York, he comes to realise, doesn’t care whether he lives or dies. It’s all-seeing and indifferent and just keeps rolling along.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toBookmarksDiscover new books and learn more about your favourite authors with our expert reviews, interviews and news stories. Literary delights delivered direct to youPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionCool Machine by Colson Whitehead is published by Fleet (£22). To support the Guardian, order your copy atguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsFictionBook of the dayreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Splatoon Raiders review – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey | Games | The Guardian
description: The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill
Content: Better with friends … Splatoon Raiders.Photograph: NintendoView image in fullscreenBetter with friends … Splatoon Raiders.Photograph: NintendoGamesReviewSplatoon Raiders review – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to greyNintendo Switch 2The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-millJoshua RiveraTue 21 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 09.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSplatoon is among Nintendo’s most ingenious inventions of the last decade: a series of shooting games whose paint-splattered turf war nature means being bad at shooting is just as valuable as – if not more so than – being good. It is also blessed with an abundance of personality, with vibrant characters, a richly imagined world, and a hipness that the competition can only dream of achieving. Unfortunately, Splatoon Raiders makes it difficult to remember all of this.The first spinoff in the ink-soaked franchise, Splatoon Raiders is advertised as a single player-focused entry in this primarily multiplayer series. While this is true enough – one can play through the entire game solo just fine – Raiders is clearly best enjoyed cooperatively with up to three others. Players start each session on a hideout ship where they can upgrade their equipment and tweak their play style before heading out on bite-sized repeatable missions, and this structure is tailor-made for teaming up with friends or helping out strangers (the game lets you do either), as each person works towards creating their ultimate squid-kid avatar of chaos.Playing Splatoon Raiders solo feels like a lesser experience for this reason. Its levels are straightforwardly constructed to keep groups together. There is little of the puzzly rush of finding hidden depths with creative use of your tools. Instead, the fun is in putting together your kit. Splatoon’s weapons have always been goofy and playfully designed, but even better is learning how this new entry complements them with a suite of augmentable gadgets. As your options accrete and the game escalates in challenge, Raiders comes to life.View image in fullscreenGadgets bring the game to life … Splatoon Raiders.Photograph: NintendoSome may not get that far. Splatoon Raiders takes a long time to make it clear how completely the experience can change with the right kit, and while it offers a decent variety of challenges to tackle, players are meant to replay them. The minimal story, in which you are an unnamed mechanic stranded on a mysterious archipelago with the band Deep Cut (returning from Splatoon 3), is not terribly compelling. Which is surprising for a series with a history of casually revealing outrageous new details about its post-climate apocalypse world and its human-cephalopod inhabitants.Splatoon is Nintendo at its most offbeat, venturing forth in a direction few other game studios travel. And, according to sales, audiences outside of the company’s native Japan seem to struggle with how to receive it. In this context, Splatoon Raiders feels like a concession: Nintendo taking aside one of its rangier, peculiar creations and asking it to be a bit more normal. This is the biggest surprise about Splatoon Raiders: it is pedestrian. If you’ve played one of dozens of modern cooperative action games, you’ve played this. You have upgraded your gear and compared weapon stats and divined synergies between abilities in order to throw your character into the meat grinder over and over again, watching overwhelming hordes eventually become trivial as your stats improve, your arsenal more varied. There is only so much that Splatoon’s ink-fuelled weaponry and charming DIY design sensibilities can do to elevate this experience. Like its archipelago setting, Splatoon Raiders is far removed from what makes the main series so charming, and what’s left here feels a bit too much like watching paint dry.Splatoon Raiders is out 23 July; £41.99Explore more on these topicsGamesNintendoNintendo Switch 2Shooting gamesreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Bub – living an artist’s dreams and dreads in New York | Games | The Guardian
description: An autobiographical game about the devastating effects of chronic illness on an artist’s creative life gives players the chance to slow the pace right down
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: An autobiographical game about the devastating effects of chronic illness on an artist’s creative life gives players the chance to slow the pace right down
Content: ‘We want the player to feel like an artist’ … Bub.Photograph: PaperfrogView image in fullscreen‘We want the player to feel like an artist’ … Bub.Photograph: PaperfrogSummer games previewsGamesBub – living an artist’s dreams and dreads in New YorkAn autobiographical game about the devastating effects of chronic illness on an artist’s creative life gives players the chance to slow the pace right downNicole CarpenterTue 21 Jul 2026 05.33 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 05.50 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBubdoes not look like a traditional video game. Artist and stop-motion animator Case Jernigan spends hours, days, months in his studio drawing, painting and delicately cutting out pieces of collected paper, while coder and gameplay designer Todd Anderson builds scripts and physics simulations to make them into a game.In Bub, you play through the life of an artist in New York City, making art but also diving into his early memories and imaginative dreams. “You go from present day New York City into his childhood, or different sort ofvisions, which are all rendered in stop motion,” says Anderson. “His story is told in the present day, but also how he’s making sense of his past.”Jernigan says Bub is based almost entirely on his own life, both as a child and an artist, and one of the biggest themes is health: “I realised that a lot of my anxiety I was experiencing, and other unusual symptoms like problems with my eyes and other senses weren’t just run of the mill panic attacks. When I got diagnosed with MS I had what’s called a relapse, a really bad one, and I got very sick and had a lot of problems with my hands and my ability to make art.”Thus thetagline: the anxiety of “turning your memories into art while you still can”.View image in fullscreenWistful memories … Bub.Photograph: PaperfrogA decade ago, Jernigan had been working on a graphic novel of childhood anxiety and how it’s manifested into adulthood. But the format didn’t feel right; he wanted a way to have a bit more control over how a person was involved with the work, the pacing, the feeling of space. “What if you could choose exactly how long you want to be here, and whatever sensation you’re taking from it, whether it’s invigorating or calming or just beautiful,” he says. “You could sit there as long as you wanted.”A core piece of the appeal of autobiography in games is in how the creator uses the mechanics to influence how the player feels. Anderson says he wanted to make sure that Bub’s controls and mechanics are intuitive but don’t always do exactly what you might expect: “That’s always the relationship between the player and the controls. It’s constantly changing throughout.”So too with the visual style. The “New York City overworld” feels grounded with its consistent pen-and-ink style, coloured in with the different colours and textures of different papers Jernigan has collected over the years. “But then, as you get pulled into memories or through the process of art making for the character, you get into some of these more papercraft puppets, or the smudgy marker animation, or liquid and paint.”The music works similarly, created by Adam Schatz of Landlady and Japanese Breakfast, and Old Feels’ Asher Kurtz. “These artists have contributed a great deal to the sensation that you feel when you play the game,” says Jernigan. “It’s wistful music, lots of drones, lots of layers and texture. We think the musical identity very much mimics the visual identity of the game.”Everything is handmade. Currently the team is working on scenes in which the player will be able to take space to make art themselves, the sort of art that was created to make the game. “We want the player to really feel like an artist a little bit,” says Anderson, “And have some kind of undirected creative output.”Bub is due for release in 2027Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsArtAutobiography and memoirPCMultiple sclerosisfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: BBCPhil/Noseda/Selaocoe review – a proms concert that was everything the festival does best | Proms 2026 | The Guardian
description: A programme – Bach, Beethoven and a Jessie Montgomery premiere – that looked odd on paper made perfect sense in the hall, with Abel Selaocoe a mesmerising soloist in Montgomery’s ‘These Righteous Paths’
Author: Alexandra Coghlan
Headline: A programme – Bach, Beethoven and a Jessie Montgomery premiere – that looked odd on paper made perfect sense in the hall, with Abel Selaocoe a mesmerising soloist in Montgomery’s ‘These Righteous Paths’
Content: A storyteller through sound … cellist Abel Selaocoe with the Philharmonia Orchestra and Chorus, at the Royal Albert Hall.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouView image in fullscreenA storyteller through sound … cellist Abel Selaocoe with the Philharmonia Orchestra and Chorus, at the Royal Albert Hall.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouProms 2026ReviewBBCPhil/Noseda/Selaocoe review – a proms concert that was everything the festival does bestRoyal Albert Hall, LondonA programme – Bach, Beethoven and a Jessie Montgomery premiere –  that looked odd on paper made perfect sense in the hall, with Abel Selaocoe a mesmerising soloist in Montgomery’s ‘These Righteous Paths’Alexandra CoghlanTue 21 Jul 2026 09.16 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 09.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Thy magic power reunites / All that custom has divided / All men become brothers / Under the sway of thy gentle wings.” The BBC can’t have known when they booked Manchester’s BBC Philharmonic to perform Beethoven’s Ninth Symphony – culminating in Schiller’s ode to fellowship and unity – that it would mark Andy Burnham’s appointment as prime minister. Our new leader’s “gentle wings” will need the span of a 747 to embrace every warring faction beneath them, but what a welcome from aPromsconcert that was everything the festival does best.A strange programme on paper – Bach, Beethoven and a brand new cello concerto – made absolute sense in the hall, where musical past and future were held suspended together in a rapt continuous present. Bach’s Fantasia and Fugue in G minor set the tone. Arranged for orchestra by Andrew Davis, it had a distinct twinkle in its eye. Dutiful, even dogmatic, string exegesis takes a sudden sideways turn as the wind bend and distort the theme. And is that a celesta doubling the flute – a shimmering ghost in the orchestral machine, a childish distortion of the original organ? It’s wittily done, and under former chief conductor Gianandrea Noseda, the orchestra relished this fusion of sonic worlds.It was a process only amplified by the UK premiere of Jessie Montgomery’s These Righteous Paths – a cello concerto “born through” (in the composer’s own words) its soloist Abel Selaocoe, whose unique skills both enabled and inspired its unusual form. A tribute to Montgomery’s mother – playwright Robbie McCauley – the concerto’s three movements each take poems by McCauley as their starting point.View image in fullscreenFusing sonic worlds … Gianandrea Noseda conducting.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouThe texts are not always delivered directly, instead they inform the cello’s searching lines, wordless vocalise, mouth percussion, throat singing and African song – all performed by Selaocoe, who is less a singer or instrumentalist here than a storyteller through sound. There are nods to Bach and Elgar, but this concerto is entirely its own creation: an iridescent fabric woven between orchestra and soloist that dares imagine McCauley’s “new song … about something more”. Selaocoe is the mesmerising shaman at its centre.And then there was the Beethoven, deliberately classical and fleetfooted under Noseda, chasing the original shock of that extraordinary chorale finale. Classily delivered by a strong quartet of soloists and the Philharmonia Chorus, concealing the considerable strain of the composer’s demands, it was an argument not just for peace but progress, risk-taking, visionary optimism.Over to you, Andy.Available onBBC Soundsuntil 12 October.The Proms continue until 12 September.Explore more on these topicsProms 2026Classical musicBBC Philharmonic OrchestraPromsreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Midnight at the Never Get review – Ben Platt soars in honey-hearted cabaret-musical of 60s sweethearts | Stage | The Guardian
description: Platt excels as a crooner looking back on falling for his songwriter-lover – dazzlingly played by Gregor Milne – in a show of beguiling, bittersweet nostalgia and pristine songs
Author: Kate Wyver
Headline: Platt excels as a crooner looking back on falling for his songwriter-lover – dazzlingly played by Gregor Milne – in a show of beguiling, bittersweet nostalgia and pristine songs
Content: Sky-gliding voice … Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get at the Menier Chocolate Factory, London.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenSky-gliding voice … Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get at the Menier Chocolate Factory, London.Photograph: Johan PerssonStageReviewMidnight at the Never Get review – Ben Platt soars in honey-hearted cabaret-musical of 60s sweetheartsMenier Chocolate Factory, LondonPlatt excels as a crooner looking back on falling for his songwriter-lover – dazzlingly played by Gregor Milne – in a show of beguiling, bittersweet nostalgia and pristine songsKate WyverTue 21 Jul 2026 06.30 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBen Platt’s sky-gliding voice was made for decades gone by. The Tony, Emmy and Grammy award-winning actor (Dear Evan Hansen, The Last Five Years) is beautifully cast in Mark Sonnenblick’s (KPop Demon Hunters) out-of-time love story, a honey-hearted cabaret-musical of two gay musicians trying to make it in 1960s New York.‘Being American feels helpless and scary’: actor Ben Platt on nerves, queer songs and why he couldn’t do it without his husbandRead moreWith theMenier Chocolate Factoryset up as a lamplit cabaret (set by Scott Pask), Platt is Trevor Copeland, a crooning bar-singer reliving the years he spent with songwriter Arthur Brightman, played with plate-spinning brilliance by Gregor Milne. The history of their love is told through stories shaped around American Songbook-style music, only occasionally syrupy, as the pair slip in and out of Sonnenblick’s inventions: now a jaunty knee-bouncing number; now a big-band ballad.While Platt is the show’s star, Milne is its anchor, at once believable as the troubled genius delivering his lover new songs like cups of tea, and the steady hand behind the piano, serving as the story’s pulse as well as the focal point for the flawless live band. There is a quiet power in the two men singing each other love songs, as they begin to do in public, with their clearly queer lyrics losing some audiences and gaining others. When politics comes knocking on their underground gay club’s doors, they face a fundamental choice: to protest, or assimilate. Central to their differences and their downfall, this dilemma gives grit to their otherwise largely jazz-smooth lives.View image in fullscreenDarkness seeps in … Gregor Milne and Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get.Photograph: Johan PerssonAs darkness seeps into the story, we start to make sense of our narrator’s gaps in time and memory. Director David Cromer (The Fear of 13) retains the show’s intimacy by keeping things simple, with a minor magic trick – worth keeping secret – wrapping up the story’s loose threads, though the gut-punch would hit even harder with a trim. Midnight at the Never Get sees music as both heartbreak and cure. With one eye on who’s about to walk in through the door, it reveals what love leaves you with when all else is lost: songs to sing at the top of your lungs to the memory of who you used to be.At Menier Chocolate Factory, London,until 12 SeptemberExplore more on these topicsStageTheatreMusicalsCabaretMenier Chocolate FactoryreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Zendaya faces backlash for wearing ancient Iranian jewellery at Odyssey event | Archaeology | The Guardian
description: Film star promotes Christopher Nolan film epic wearing gold artefacts dating back almost 3,000 years
Author: Ellie Violet Bramley
Headline: Film star promotes Christopher Nolan film epic wearing gold artefacts dating back almost 3,000 years
Content: Zendaya at a photocall in London for The Odyssey. The earrings are fashioned from the Ziwiye hoard, which was uncovered in Iran in 1947.Photograph: Scott A Garfitt/APView image in fullscreenZendaya at a photocall in London for The Odyssey. The earrings are fashioned from the Ziwiye hoard, which was uncovered in Iran in 1947.Photograph: Scott A Garfitt/APArchaeologyZendaya faces backlash for wearing ancient Iranian jewellery at Odyssey eventFilm star promotes Christopher Nolan film epic wearing gold artefacts dating back almost 3,000 yearsEllie Violet BramleyTue 21 Jul 2026 11.01 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Odyssey is a story about a Greek king’s long journey home. But a pair of earrings worn on the new film adaptation’s press tour – and their long separation from their homeland – is sparking controversy.At a photocall in London, Zendaya, who plays the goddess Athena in theChristopher Nolan film, wore a pair of earrings fashioned from Iranian artefacts dating back to 700BC.The backlash was swift, with archaeologists criticising the use of precious and culturally significant artefacts as accessories in the promotion of a Hollywood film, claiming it was a “hijacking” of Iran’s contributions to humanity.The earrings belong to Barron London, a Mayfair-based gallery and dealerwhose website states: “With a reputation for discretion, the gallery works privately with collectors, estates and institutions to uncover and place pieces of significance.”According to a formal statement from Barron the artefacts, which were bought from another London jeweller, Glenn Spiro, in 2025, “are attributed to the Ziwiye hoard, discovered in north-western Iran in the late 1940s”.The hoard was a trove of gold, silver, bronze and terracotta found in 1947 and looted by shepherds.According to a 2015 research paper, the surviving pieces were “sold in antique markets out of Iran in the 1960s” and “are now scattered in different museums and private collections”.Prof Sussan Babaie, an Iranian-born art historian and Courtauld Institute professor, said it was “shocking to see that no one thought maybe this is not a good idea”.Expressing “outrage” thatZendayawas wearing the ancient objects, she said: “There is not enough cultural knowledge and cultural sensibility among those who dress [celebrities], those who accessorise them, those who put them up as the symbols of beauty and power and an expression of the movie’s high-mindedness.” Representatives for Zendaya and her stylist have been contacted for comment.The row comes amid scrutiny of western institutions that hold ancient artefacts. The British Museum has faced criticism over itslooted Benin bronzes,sacred Ethiopian altar tabletsand the Parthenon marbles.Kim Kardashian wore a dress that belonged to Marilyn Monroe at the Met Gala in 2022. As Scott Fortner, who has a collection of Monroe artefacts,told the BBC at the time: “When you buy something of such significance, you really need to stick to your word to protect and preserve it … and clearly that didn’t happen.” The gown’s owners denied that any damage had been done to the dress.View image in fullscreenA gold plate from the Ziwiye hoard showing a character fighting a lion. The object, which dates back to 700BC, is in the Louvre in Paris.Photograph: Lanmas/AlamyDr Annelise Baer, an archaeologist and producer whose video about Zendaya’s earrings garnered criticism for what some perceived as condoning the use of the artefacts, said the main ethical issue was “that the earrings themselves are made out of looted antiquities”.She added: “Zendaya wearing them is kind of a secondary fact … but because she has worn them on a red carpet for a major international movie premiere, this has suddenly put all of that into the public eye in a way that we haven’t really seen before.”According to Barron’s statement, the earrings “are responsibly stewarded in accordance with their documented provenance and all applicable legal requirements”.Babaie said the jewellery probably had “extremely important spiritual connotations”, adding: “These are objects that are not meant to be displayed as accessories… It’s human heritage. It’s world heritage, not just accessories.”Christopher Nolan’s Odyssey used occupied land as a film set. That feels like a betrayal | Mohamed Sleiman LabatRead moreThe effect, she said, was to strip the earrings “of all meaning”. It is, she claims, “hijacking, flattening and transforming [ancient Iran’s] cultural contributions to humanity”.The timing is also causing further anger. Babaie highlighted a “maddening” situation in which the press tour for The Odyssey is “glamourising the antiquities of Iran … just as billions of American dollars are being used to bomb Iran”, destroying sites of cultural and historical interest.In its statement, Barron added: “The intention behind the loan was never commercial. It was to celebrate the remarkable artistry of ancient Persian goldsmiths and to encourage wider appreciation of one of the world’s great artistic traditions. At a time when Iran is so often represented through the lens of contemporary politics, we hope these earrings can also remind audiences of the country’s profound cultural, artistic and historical legacy.”The Odyssey has also been criticised over one of its filming locations.Sahrawi film-makers are calling for a boycottof the movie over the use of the city of Dakhla in occupied Western Sahara – a stretch of land under Moroccan control sometimes referred to as “Africa’s last colony”.Explore more on these topicsArchaeologyIranZendayaThe OdysseyHistoryMiddle East and north AfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: Board short summer: why everyone suddenly wants to look like a surfer | Fashion | The Guardian
description: Surfboards, knee-length shorts, wetsuits, flip-flops: all have been seen on the catwalks this year. But what do actual surfers think of the trend? We went to Newquay to find out
Author: Morwenna Ferrier
Headline: Surfboards, knee-length shorts, wetsuits, flip-flops: all have been seen on the catwalks this year. But what do actual surfers think of the trend? We went to Newquay to find out
Content: Laid-back look … Morwenna Ferrier in Newquay.Photograph: Luke Gartside/The GuardianView image in fullscreenLaid-back look … Morwenna Ferrier in Newquay.Photograph: Luke Gartside/The GuardianFashionBoard short summer: why everyone suddenly wants to look like a surferSurfboards, knee-length shorts, wetsuits, flip-flops: all have been seen on the catwalks this year. But what do actual surfers think of the trend? We went to Newquay to find outMorwenna FerrierTue 21 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSummer is here, and with it the annual scramble to decide what kind of season we’re supposed to be having. What will it be? On offer in 2026 are “freaky sandal summer”, which requires you to wear weird shoes, and “euromaxxing”, which is presumably about making the most of the easyJet sale without considering the environmental impact. Maybe “poet girl summer” is for you, whereby you listen to Gracie Abrams while wearing a silk camisole. Or simply “no mascara summer”, which I won’t spell out but sounds quite appealing.The most plausible contender is “board short summer”, aphrase coined by Marie Claire, which broadly involves dressing like you surf even if you’ve never set foot on a board.View image in fullscreenMiu Miu’s board shorts.Marie Claire’s declaration came after a Miu Miu show that featured satin board shorts. There have also been sparkly board shorts at Dries Van Noten and a flurry of flip-flops on the catwalks at Chloé, Balenciaga and Bottega Veneta. On the high street, the bestselling shorts this summer, according to Uniqlo, are long and board-like, while Asos is selling a blue baseball cap embroidered with the words “surf instructor”.The pièce de résistance, though, came from Louis Vuitton, the biggest luxury fashion house in the world, which built an artificial wave 8 metres (26ft) high in the centre of Paris as the backdrop to a collection of board shorts and wetsuits forits show in June. To avoid any confusion, there were even a fewsurfboardsthrown in (price on request).View image in fullscreenRide the logo … Louis Vuitton’s menswear show at Paris Fashion Week last month.Photograph: Stéphane Cardinale/Corbis/Getty ImagesIn practice, board short summer is rather more old school. It’s about wearing oversized knee-length shorts with sun-faded Quiksilver T-shirts and bleaching your hair. It’s baggy jean shorts from Old Navy instead of Daisy Dukes, and choosing flip-flops over Birkenstocks. Culturally, it borrows as much fromPoint Breakand Blue Juice as it does from William Finnegan’s Pulitzer-winning memoirBarbarian Daysand the brilliant (if problematic) 60s documentaryThe Endless Summer.Spiritually, it’s about feeling grounded with the sand between your toes, rather than what gen Z might call “touching grass”.And, unlike most internet summers, it’s based on a lifestyle that actually exists. In Newquay, a coastal town inCornwallbuilt on surf culture and tourism and framed by some of Europe’s most breathtaking beaches, it’s always board short summer.It’s a hot day and I’m at Newquay’s Box & Barber cafe, where waiting staff in board shorts serve flat whites. Outside on the high street, lean surfers the colour of oak trees walk past me, topless without reproach. Arrived on the train with no gear? No problem. At the Cornwall Air Ambulance charity shop you can pick up a perfectly serviceable shortboard for £50 and a pair of board shorts for under a fiver.It seems easy to get the look. Just throw on an old Rip Curl vest, borrow your boyfriend’s shorts and fail to brush your hair. But, as with any hyperspecific trend, you run the risk of cosplay.View image in fullscreen‘I just wear relaxed stuff’ … Delphine Gwilliam-O’Connor, with Jack DayPhotograph: Luke Gartside/The Guardian“People think they have to dress a certain way in order to be accepted,” says Delphine Gwilliam-O’Connor, 28, an artist and surfer from Newquay. “It’s not so much people copying the look that might bother surfers. It’s the attitude they think needs to come with it. I surf but don’t really dress like ‘a surfer’. I just wear relaxed stuff. I think most of us do.”If you grew up in the 90s and 00s, there’s every chance you took your styling cues from one subculture or another. Skatewear tended to dominate in urban areas, so in some ways “surfwear feels like a natural progression”, says Mia Jacobs, a youth fashion strategist at trend forecaster WGSN. “Surf and skate culture share a similar aesthetic language and mindset, rooted in freedom, creativity and community,” she continues.Skatewear has already been absorbed by advertisers and fashion types eager to flatten it into a marketable lifestyle. Brands that started out specifically for and by skateboarders – such as Vans, Supreme and Palace – have collaborated with luxury fashion houses, fromRalph LaurentoComme des GarçonstoValentino. Though equally irreverent (and baggy), surfwear has largely resisted the same level of commercialisation. Until now.Its resistance has been helped by the fact that you need to be by the sea to pull it off. In the early 00s, when I was a teenager in the West Country, all my friends wanted to be surfers. Summer after summer, we came to Newquay. It was here that my friend got her first tattoo, and another lost his virginity in a tent. Though I progressed from the humiliating yellow foam boards to mid-length fibreboard ones during the summer holidays, I never got any further than that.View image in fullscreenBen Skinner … ‘Certain brands want to be part of the surfing image.’Photograph: Luke Gartside/The Guardian“A lot of people come here to learn to surf, but only a few keep it going,” says Ben “Skindog” Skinner, 42, a renowned surfer and board carver who runs a surfboard shop 10 minutes outside Newquay town centre. Surfers here are broadly divided into types. There are cores (hardcores), carvers (surfers who make boards) – Skinner is both – and groms (under-16s). “You can see why it’s so popular, and why it’s growing as a culture for all ages. It’s the travelling, the community, a lot of things that people would like to be part of,” he says.Skinner moved to Cornwall when he was 10 and started competing, winning British surfing titles for under-12s and under-14s and making the finals in the European championships. Now he is up there with the British greats, though he says his son Lukas Skinner, who competes globally, has “passed me”. If you’re under any illusions about surfwear’s direction of travel, Lukas was invited to that Louis Vuitton show (he didn’t go because he was competing in Spain).“You see it a lot – certain brands wanting to be part of the surfing image, and I think it just goes in cycles of fashion, like a lot of clothing,” Skinner says. What does he make of board short summer? “It’s funny, because [in Britain] we don’t really get to surf in shorts very often. We tend to wear wetsuits all year round. I prefer to wear something that keeps me warm.”View image in fullscreenMaddie Meddings … ‘A lot of people that live here don’t want to have loads of branded stuff.’Photograph: Luke Gartside/The GuardianMaddie Meddings, 31, a photographer who has been surfing for 17 years, isn’t surprised the style has been co-opted. “As subcultures go, it’s a very aesthetic one,” she says. “If you’re a surfer, you’re outside, on the beach, getting a nice tan. Most people don’t live like that.”“Consumers aren’t just buying into surfing as a sport; they’re buying into what it represents,” says Jacobs. “As daily life becomes increasingly digital and fast-paced, any aesthetic associated with a more carefree way of living is highly aspirational.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toFashion StatementStyle, with substance: what's really trending this week, a roundup of the best fashion journalism and your wardrobe dilemmas solvedPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe thing is, says Skinner, “the reality of surfing is hard. It all looks nice on Instagram but most of the time it’s about putting on sandy wetsuits in cold weather and just getting on with it.”One of the few women here to surf all year round, Meddings usually wears wetsuits in the sea. On land, it’s practical clothes collected over the years – baggy T-shirts and shorts, yes, but ones she has found on Vinted. “A lot of people that live here don’t want to have loads of branded stuff,” she says, her eyes drifting towards my Rip Curl vest. “Anything new just feels a bit wrong.”Indeed, it soon becomes clear that most surfers wouldn’t be seen dead in what I’m wearing. Like some mysterious cult, the surfing life tends to resist sartorial definition. Wevs, 52, a local surfer and former lifeguard, reckons it “still has that punk rock ethic, and you can’t really fake it” – something you don’t see so much in other subcultures any more, he says. “A lot of these boys, they’re like penguins: awkward on land but in water they’re unbelievable. To them, [surfing] is a way of life.” His look? Denim shorts, bare feet “and Blu Tack in my ears – earplugs don’t go in well”.View image in fullscreenNostalgia trip … Morwenna Ferrier in Newquay.Photograph: Luke Gartside/The GuardianJosh Hughes, a surfer in his early 30s who also works in cybersecurity, is aware of the trend but credits it to the resurgence of all things 90s. “It’s got to be a byproduct of that nostalgia. That 90s baggy look is still what epitomises surfing in people’s eyes. Those brands, like Rip Curl, Billabong, Volcom, they were made in the 90s, they predate all this.”Hughes is right. While surfwear is growing in high fashion and on the high street, it has already taken off in spectacular form on the secondhand market. In the last month, searches for board shorts have increased by 132% on Vinted compared to July 2025. On eBay, searches for Quiksilver board shorts have gone up by 338% since January, while searches for O’Neill flip-flops have grown 686% since last July. Secondhand shop Rokit also reports a boom in sales of shorts and flip-flops from Quiksilver, Rusty, Billabong, O’Neill and Rip Curl.You can spot the real surfers because if they’re not wearing wetsuits, and certainly not box-fresh O’Neill T-shirts, theyarewearing board shorts. “Surfers aren’t worried about Louis Vuitton stealing their look,” says Jamie, a photographer and surfer in his early 30s. On the contrary, says Skinner: “Ultimately, it’s good news because it’s hard to make a living out of what we do. Surf tourism is obviously holding a lot of us up. So once it goes into fashion, its value goes up.”View image in fullscreenA family of surfers demonstrate how to carry off the look in style.Photograph: Luke Gartside/The GuardianThe internet’s fixation with categorising the seasonal cultural mood began in 2019 with #HotGirlSummer, a phrase borrowedfrom a Megan Thee Stallion lyric. The idea was less about the weather and more about self-possession: anyone could be a “hot girl” if they put their mind to it. But while we tend to think of such epithets as mere branding exercises, sometimes they speak to something greater.Sometimesthere’s something in it. And the longer I’m in Newquay, the more I realise the draw of board short summer is less about the clothes and more about what they represent.Think back to the US in the 50s and 60s, where surfing became a refuge from the expectations of white-collar life and a rejection of capitalism. From the indigenous spirituality of the surf culture in Hawaii, where the sport began, to exploring the great outdoors and even adopting the laid-back look (in the 50s board shorts were made of twill andknown as “drowners”), surfing has always been more than just a hobby.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: LMPC/Getty ImagesAn element of outsiderism survives today. Many surfers live in vans parked at either end of Newquay’s Fistral beach during the summer months. Jamie lives in a garden shed and only moves into a flat during winter. He describes work as a means to an end. Gwilliam-O’Connor, for her part, is not so much horrified by the idea of non-surfers wearing surf gear as baffled by its ascent into high fashion. “We just don’t have much money,” she says with a laugh.“For ages, surfing was associated with hippies and stoners, and it became a bit of a parody. But it’s not really like that now,” adds Meddings, who thinks it’s the meditative element that appeals. “Maybe people are seeing it’s not just a sport. There are some people who dedicate their lives to it.”Board short summer might be easy to buy on eBay, but its credibility is another matter. The foot-high waves rolling into Little Fistral beach on Wednesday reach 3ft by Friday. Come autumn, you could be looking at 10ft. The Cribbar, a notorious wave between Little Fistral and Towan, can reach 20-30ft. No amount of faded vests can stand in for the skill and patience that surfing requires.As Wevs puts it: “It’s the best thing in the world, but it’s dangerous and it’s hard. You need to have lived the life.”Explore more on these topicsFashionSurfingCornwallfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The best LED face masks in the UK, tested: 12 light therapy devices that are worth the hype | Skincare | The Guardian
description: They claim to fix fine lines, blemishes, and redness – but which stand up to scrutiny? After consulting dermatologists and putting them to the test, we have crowned a brand new number one
Author: Sarah Matthews
Headline: They claim to fix fine lines, blemishes, and redness – but which stand up to scrutiny? After consulting dermatologists and putting them to the test, we have crowned a brand new number one
Content: View image in fullscreenThe FilterSkincareReviewThe best LED face masks in the UK, tested: 12 light therapy devices that are worth the hypeThey claim to fix fine lines, blemishes, and redness – but which stand up to scrutiny? After consulting dermatologists and putting them to the test, we have crowned a brand new number oneThe best anti-ageing creams, serums and treatmentsSarah MatthewsTue 21 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.LED face masks are booming in popularity – despite being one of the most expensive at-home beauty products to hit the market. They claim to either reduce the appearance of fine lines, stop spots or calm redness, with some even combining different types of light to enhance the benefits.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.However, it’s wise to be sceptical about new treatments that are costly and non-invasive, and to do your research before you buy. With this in mind, I interviewed doctors and dermatologists to find out whether these light therapy devices work.They’re not a miracle cure for deep-set wrinkles, but experts say the best light therapy masks can promote collagen production, improve elasticity and help smooth skin with sustained use. And while it may seem like a new technology,research from the 1990sshows the efficacy of light therapy in wound healing, acne treatment and skin rejuvenation.Red and infrared light are safe to use on all skin types, but be warned that blue light can worsen hyperpigmentation on darker skin tones.I’ve tested some of the most popular devices on the market – including infrared masks – to narrow down my favourite LED face masks for treating a range of skin concerns.At a glanceBest LED mask overall:CurrentBody Multi Light Therapy LED mask£499.99 at CurrentBodyBest budgetLED face mask:Silk’n LED face mask 100£98.77 at LookfantasticBest LED mask forfine lines and blemishes:Shark CryoGlow£257.99 at SharkNinjaBest LED device for acne-prone skin:Lustre Solo£50 at LustreBest LED mask for quick treatments:Dr Dennis Gross SpectraLite FaceWare Pro£465 at Cult BeautyBest LED mask for travel:Silk’n LED face mask Pro£259 at LookfantasticWhy you should trust meI’ve been reviewing health and beauty tech for the past four years. During this time I’ve witnessed many beauty fads come and go. Naturally tight-fisted and a sceptic, I was initially struck by the cost of these masks. How on earth can an elaborate LED light strip set you back more than £300?But after testing multiple light therapy masks, I’ve come to understand the technology behind them – and, sadly, you won’t be able to engineer your own light therapy system with a costume mask and a set of LED string lights.I don’t have many fine lines or wrinkles to test on, but I can comment on how blue light treatment affects acne because I’ve suffered from it since I was a teenager – despite my mum’s assurances that it would clear up after puberty. The experts I spoke to agreed on the efficacy of red and infrared light for anti-ageing.Are LED face masks worth it?Light therapy masks are fantastic additions to a skincare routine, helping to smooth, firm and reduce blemishes. Since I started using one more than a year ago, my skin is calmer, less prone to redness, and has a more even tone.However, LED masks can be expensive, and if you’re not disciplined enough to use one regularly, you won’t see results. Before you splash up to £500, keep in mind that light therapy masks are most effective when paired with a good skincare routine. It’s crucial to make sure skin is properly cleansed before treatment and protected from sun damage afterwards.If you don’t already have askincareroutine nailed down, don’t buy a light therapy mask just yet. Consider starting out using a gentle cleanser and moisturiser, with anSPFfor daytime. You could also incorporate targeted skincare serums, such asretinolandvitamin C, to help firm and brighten the skin without the need for an expensive light therapy mask.How I testedView image in fullscreen‘I tested the fit, comfort levels and usability of 15 of the most popular light therapy masks.’Photograph: Sarah Matthews/The GuardianSince LED treatments take a while to show visible results, it’s difficult to test multiple light therapy masks at once (at least, without spending a few years testing them), so I enlisted the help of dermatologists who were familiar with light therapy treatments long before they were commercially available.I spoke to Dr Barbara Kubicka, an expert in aesthetic medicine; Dr Derrick Phillips, a consultant dermatologist and spokesperson for the British Skin Foundation; and aesthetic doctor Dr Priya Verma, an authority on the science of skin ageing.I asked these experts to identify which features of a light therapy mask – such as bulb quality and quantity, light wavelength and treatment time – matter the most, to inform my choices. They also outlined the suitability of light therapy for different skin types and its associated risks.Armed with their advice, I tested the fit, comfort levels and usability of 15 of the most popular light therapy masks and other targeted LED devices. Getting hands on with the masks allowed me to pinpoint any of the key features the experts recommended, as well as features I found helpful as a consumer.I’ve been using a light therapy mask regularly for the past 18 months, so I can identify a good mask from a bad one. My tests also included monitoring results after four nightly sessions, with the obvious caveat that only very minimal changes could be observed after such a short-term treatment. I then tested my favourite masks over several months, too, helping me to verify any unique treatment claims – as well as giving me plenty of opportunities to scare my boyfriend by sneaking up on him wearing each one.Only masks that use the brightest, flicker-free bulbs, fit comfortably and are easy to use made the final cut. After testing, I returned the masks sourced for this article to the manufacturers.
