Throw in fewer LED bulbs than average and dim lights, and this mask is impossible to recommend.A high price tag doesn’t guarantee how good the mask is either, as evidenced by the £719Foreo FAQ 202 LED mask. Its lightweight and comfort level, along with an array of treatment options via its app-based controls, meant it impressed initially. However, its silicone design felt incredibly flimsy, and after a week of use, it had already started peeling away from the central battery pack.This might be forgivable if Foreo offered a full warranty and repair service. There’s atwo-year warrantycovering defects and faulty workmanship, but this does not cover “cosmetic deterioration caused by fair wear and tear, or damage caused by accident, misuse or neglect”. To access accidental damage coverage, you have to pay Foreo’s £9.99/month subscription. Even if you’re happy to risk it, you still have to shell out £4.99/month to access the mask’s full suite of preset treatments. Add to this the scale of advertising you’re bombarded with in the app – some of it unskippable. When you consider its price, this brazen money-grabbing leaves a very bad taste.Through testing this family of devices, I had also been hoping to find an undereye brightening LED patch to usurp the Peep Club wand, which requires constant movement across the eye area. I had hoped that theLustre GoGlowbrightening and de-puffing patches would fit the bill. They come in a handy headphone-like charging case and have a clever wireless design. However, when positioned under the eyes as advertised, the light was incredibly bright, which made it tricky to see. The sticky pads, although small, were uncomfortable to remove, and my skin was left feeling irritated for a day afterwards. The search for the perfect undereye LED patch continues …What you need to knowView image in fullscreenRed light is just one of several LED wavelengths used to target different skin concerns.Photograph: Sarah Matthews/The GuardianWhat is LED therapy, and how does it work?“LED (light-emitting diode) therapy is a non-invasive treatment that uses specific wavelengths of light to stimulate various cellular processes in the skin,” says aesthetic medicine expert Kubicka. “Depending on the colour or wavelength, LED light can target inflammation, promote collagen production, accelerate healing and even kill bacteria.”Light therapy is a great addition to your skincare routine – for me, it’s helped reduce breakouts, improve healing and reduce the appearance of my acne scars. LED face masks are quick, gentle and non-invasive, and suitable for most skin types (although as mentioned above, blue light can worsen hyperpigmentation on darker skin tones, according to the experts I spoke with). The masks are effective and foolproof to use, and the daily treatments also force you to take a little time for yourself – perfect for a morning pick-me-up or an evening wind-down.Does red light help with anti-ageing?“Red-light therapy can reduce the appearance of fine lines and wrinkles through a process called photobiomodulation,” says consultant dermatologist Phillips. “It energises mitochondria, which are the batteries of the cells, powering the production of collagen and elastin by fibroblasts, the factories of the skin.”Elastin and collagen are “the building blocks of firm, youthful skin”, says Kubicka. Stimulating the production of these molecules can “improve skin density, elasticity and overall smoothness” as long as you complete treatments regularly.I have yet to develop fine lines or wrinkles, but I’m keen to avoid them by using red-light therapy for “preventive ageing”. However, “most of the clinical evidencefocuses on treating visible signs of ageing or repairing damaged skin, rather than long-term prevention in younger skin,” as aesthetic doctor Verma explains.Still, Verma reassured me my efforts wouldn’t be in vain. “Using LED light consistently and appropriately may help support overall skin health […] especially when combined with sun protection and a good skincare routine.”What different colours of light therapy are available?Several colours are available, with each wavelength claiming to target a different skin concern. These colours correlate to the visible light spectrum, and each can have a different effect on the skin.Red light has a wavelength of between 630 and 700nm and is the most widely known form of light therapy, loved for its non-invasive potential anti-ageing benefits. Near-infrared light above 700nm “penetrates the skin more deeply to support tissue repair and reduce inflammation”, according to Kubicka. Blue light therapy (415nm), on the other hand, has been proven to target acne-causing bacteria, leading to a reduction in breakouts over time. Kubicka also says blue light can help regulate oil production – another common problem for those with acne.The effects of some types of light on the skin are better researched than others.Near-infrared,red lightandblue lightare backed by scientific studies that identify the biological processes that each type of light triggers in the body.Meanwhile, research into green and yellow light is more limited. One study questions theefficacy of yellow lighttherapy and claims that a previous study may have been influenced by the placebo effect. Similarly, a study ongreen light therapysuggests that more research is needed to provide a solid recommendation for use as an anti-ageing treatment.How and when would you recommend using light therapy?The “how” is simple: always use light therapy masks on freshly washed, dry skin, before applying serums and creams. This will give the light the most direct access to the skin.Despite what some instruction manuals advise, don’t jump into daily treatments straight away – you may end up with slightly irritated skin. Start by introducing treatments two to three times a week, and monitoring the impact on your skin. If you don’t notice any sensitivity, you can incorporate more treatments, if permitted by the manufacturer’s guidelines.Once your skin is used to the treatment, “home devices should be used three to five times a week, for 10-20 minutes a session,” says Phillips. Don’t expect instant results, though. If you’re hoping to treat breakouts, you should “see a reduction in redness and the frequency of acne breakouts within two to four weeks”. Those using red light for anti-ageing benefits will need to wait a little longer: “It typically takes six to eight weeks to see visible changes.”Is light therapy safe for all skin tones?“LED light therapy is generally very safe when used correctly,” says Verma. This is because, unlike laser hair removal, it only uses low-level, non-thermal light. “The most common side effects are mild and temporary, such as slight redness, dryness or irritation.”“People with known photosensitive disorders or taking medications that make them more sensitive to light (eg certain antibiotics) should consult their doctor,” says Phillips. He also warns that those with darker skin tones should exercise caution when using blue light, since “some blue wavelengths have been associated with hyperpigmentation”. The Lustre Solo patches recommended above offer exclusively blue light, but the other masks on this roundup have blue-light-free settings if you do find that it causes sensitivity.How much should I spend on an LED therapy mask?There are hundreds of light therapy masks available at different prices, but the quality varies wildly. You may see results from a budget-friendly mask, such as the£119 Silk’n mask, but spending more will generally get you a larger quantity of bulbs that offer a greater range of wavelengths. You’re also more likely to get a mask with a better fit and coverage.The best mask for you will depend on your skin concerns and budget. Those hoping to invest in a mask with anti-acne properties will be able to spend less, since blue light is more widely available, but if you’re looking for the best anti-ageing deep near-infrared light therapy, you will need to part with more cash.High-end red light therapy masks can cost up to £500; if you spend that much, just make sure you use it regularly, or you won’t see results.How to clean an LED face maskCleaning instructions for LED masks will differ slightly from brand to brand, based on the different materials used. As a general rule, you want to steer clear of using chemical or alcohol-based cleansers since these can damage a mask’s delicate silicone and padded components. Always check the instructions for your specific mask before jumping into cleaning.The usual best practice is to use a non-abrasive cloth, such asmicrofibre, dampened with water to wipe down your mask after every use. Try not to use too much water and never submerge the mask, as doing so will damage the electrical components. Always make sure your mask is unplugged and switched off before cleaning, and wait for the mask to dry completely before storing, charging or switching it on.For more:The best concealers for camouflaging blemishesThe best eye creams for banishing bags, puffiness and fine linesShark FacialPro Glow + DePuffi reviewCurrentBody Multi Light Therapy LED mask reviewSarah Matthews is a consumer journalist who specialises in health and beauty reviews. In her four years of product testing, she’s worked her way through countless hair styling tools, hair removal, light therapy and skincare innovations. She’s seen beauty trends come and go, andcan sniff out a fad from a gamechanger (that doesn’t mean she’sdisciplined enough to always follow her own advice, though)This article was originally published on 19 September 2025. Reviews published in the Filter may be periodically updated to reflect new products and at the editor’s discretion. The date of an article’s most recent update can be found in the timestamp at the top of the page. This article was amended on 21 July 2026; the CurrentBody Multi Light Therapy mask and Silk’n LED face mask Pro were added after testing, one mask was removed, and prices were updated throughout.Explore more on these topicsSkincareThe FilterBeautyGadgetsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: 101 uses for an empty vanilla pod | Chefs | The Guardian
description: Those expensive pods will bring their heavenly aroma to syrups, compotes, baked goods, desserts – and even your underwear drawer
Author: Anna Berrill
Headline: Those expensive pods will bring their heavenly aroma to syrups, compotes, baked goods, desserts – and even your underwear drawer
Content: Vanilla pods: a great natural scent.Photograph: Johner Images/Getty Images/Johner RFView image in fullscreenVanilla pods: a great natural scent.Photograph: Johner Images/Getty Images/Johner RFKitchen aideChefs101 uses for an empty vanilla podThose expensive pods will bring their heavenly aroma to syrups, compotes, baked goods, desserts – and even your underwear drawerAnna BerrillTue 21 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCan you do anything withvanilla pods once you’ve scraped out the seeds? They’resoexpensive.Jo, by email“I put them in my knicker drawer,” says Sarah Lemanski, owner ofNova Bakehousein Leeds. “They scent it nicely, that’s for sure.” But, culinarily speaking, and beyond custards and the like, Lemanski often uses them in vanilla syrup: “Chop the pod quite small, then dry-toast it with some of the sugar for the syrup – I use a 2:1 sugar-to-water ratio – to give it body.” The Guardian’s ownBenjamina Ebuehiis very much in agreement, and keeps a stash of said vanilla syrup in the fridge to fuel her matcha habit: “I can’t be paying £6 for oneeveryday.” (It would work well in iced coffee, too.)“Even when you’ve scraped out as much as you can from a pod, there are always some seeds left inside,” Ebuehi says, so there’s still so much flavour to be had in there. Perhaps the easiest way to keep the love alive is by sticking a spent pod in a fruit compote: “Once that’s cooled, get it in the fridge, but leave the pod in there, so it continues to seep out that flavour.” You could apply a similar tactic to jam.For Adam Sellar, executive baker atToklas Bakeryin London, it’s pod’s law to dry them out and blitz with caster sugar: “That gives a really good vanilla sugar for the likes of brioche, shortbread or any other simple biscuit.” Alternatively, grind the pods and add to salt: “That’s especially good with smoked sea salt,” Lemanski says, to add something a bit different to your next batch of chocolate chip cookies, say. “Remember that you can also freeze empty pods, then just make a big batch of vanilla sugar or vanilla salt, and gift it.”If tangy fermented kefir is part of your breakfast routine, that’s another good moment to use up an empty vanilla pod or two. “Leave them in the mix while it ferments, and they’ll lend it a gentle vanilla flavour without making it too sweet or dessert-like,” Sellar says. At the other end of the spectrum, spent pods would also be right at home in a bottle of whisky: “Don’t use a nice bottle, mind,” says Jan Ostle, ofWilsonsin Bristol. Rather, you want something “mediocre”. “Then, when you do Christmas drinks, you can be one of those really annoying people who prepared months ago.” Otherwise, he says, dry out the pods, mix with orange peel and black tea, then get the kettle on: “That would be beautiful.”Of course, brown butter (AKAbeurre noisette) can enhance the flavour of all the good things – cookies, cakes, brownies – so Lemanski would put empty pods to work in there, too. “I’ll either use the brown butter to baste brioche buns when they come out of the oven, or chill it down to solidify, then scoop on to pancakes or porridge.” Admittedly, we’re a way off porridge season at the moment, so right now Jo could do a lot worse than getting that vanilla in some good olive oil, Lemanski says: “Use it to finish desserts such as chocolate mousse, or yoghurt with raspberries and honey – it’ll make breakfast that bit more special.”Got a culinary dilemma? Emailfeast@theguardian.comExplore more on these topicsChefsKitchen aideFoodBakingHerbs and spicesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: José Pizarro’s recipe for tomato and bread salad with boquerones | Food | The Guardian
description: Spanish white pickled anchovies top this full-flavoured dish for hot summer days that’s easy to make and made for sharing
Author: José Pizarro
Headline: Spanish white pickled anchovies top this full-flavoured dish for hot summer days that’s easy to make and made for sharing
Content: Jose Pizarro’s tomato and bread salad with boquerones.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.View image in fullscreenJose Pizarro’s tomato and bread salad with boquerones.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.FoodJosé Pizarro’s recipe for tomato and bread salad with boqueronesSpanish white pickled anchovies top this full-flavoured dish that’s easy to make and made for sharingJosé PizarroTue 21 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn Spain, some of our most loved dishes come from making something special out of very little. This salad is exactly that kind of food: it’s about using what is already in the house and turning it into something generous and full-flavoured. I have always loved how these dishes bring people together, because they’re designed to be placed in the middle of the table for everyone to share. They remind me of long, relaxed lunches that slowly turn into the afternoon and evening, and where conversation matters as much as the food. Simple, honest and full of character, this is the kind of cooking that never goes out of style.Tomato and bread salad with boqueronesPrep10 minCook15 minSteep10 min+Serves4-6400g day-old white bread– sourdough, ciabatta or pane pugliese4 tbsp olive oilFlaky sea salt and freshly ground black pepper2 garlic cloves, peeled and finely sliced500g ripe tomatoes, sliced1 small cucumber, deseeded and chopped2 tbsp sherry vinegar4 tbsp extra-virgin olive oil150g boqueronesTear the bread into pieces. Heat half the oil with the garlic in a large frying pan, and fry over a low heat for 30 seconds. Add half the bread, turn up the heat and fry until golden. Tip the fried bread into a serving bowl, then add the rest of the oil to the pan and fry the remaining bread until golden. Add this to the bowl, and season with a little flaky sea salt.Add the tomatoes and cucumber to the bowl and toss. Put the vinegar and extra-virgin olive oil in a blender, add sea salt and freshly ground black pepper, blitz and pour over the salad.Toss to coat, then leave the salad to sit and steep for 10-15 minutes, so the bread soaks up all the juices. Arrange the boquerones over the top and serve.Explore more on these topicsFoodSummer food and drinkSaladFishTomatoesBreadVegetablesrecipesShareReuse this content

title: British Gas mixed up our energy meter – but now I’m being penalised | Money | The Guardian
description: When the problem was finally sorted out in our new-build home, it increased my tariff by 19%
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: When the problem was finally sorted out in our new-build home, it increased my tariff by 19%
Content: British Gas meters in a new-build housing development were registered to the wrong addresses.Photograph: David Burton/AlamyView image in fullscreenBritish Gas meters in a new-build housing development were registered to the wrong addresses.Photograph: David Burton/AlamyConsumer championsMoneyBritish Gas mixed up our energy meter – but now I’m being penalisedWhen the problem was finally sorted out in our new-build home, it increased my tariff by 19%Anna TimsTue 21 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleResidents in our new housing development are being billed for each other’s energy after the meters were mixed up.I realised British Gas was sending bills for my energy to the developer of the estate instead of to me. I informed British Gas of my details when I moved in seven months ago.When I finally persuaded it to put the account in my name, and tried to set up a direct debit, it sent my bank details to the developer and continued to bill it. It then began billing me for a meter with a different reference number to my own.It turned out that my newly installed meter – and those of my neighbours – had been registered to different addresses.British Gas offered me compensation for the data breach involving my bank detailsbut closed my complaint about the meter mix-up.I informed it that I was cancelling my direct debit as I was not prepared to pay someone else’s bill. British Gas merely informed me that this would result in a higher tariff.CG,ShropshireYour estate is still under construction, and you reckon all of the half-dozen properties that are now occupied are affected.Given that the charges are estimates, and that the properties range from five-bedroom houses to two-bedroom social housing, some residents may be being significantly overcharged.British Gas confirmed to me that it has so far identified that the meters of all of the homes it supplies have been mixed up.“Energy meters for new-build properties can sometimes be registered incorrectly following construction when a plot number is assigned to the wrong address,” it said.It told me that your meter has now been correctly assigned, and it is correcting your neighbours’ meters on the national database.When it did get in touch with you, it was to say that your tariff had increased by a total of 19% because you had cancelled your direct debit and forfeited the associated discount. You had cancelled it so that you wouldn’t be paying for someone else’s energy.You are reluctant to reinstate it until you have been issued with a revised bill showing your household’s actual consumption. That’s lost on British Gas, too.Instead, it says your payment for energy that may, or may not, have been used by you, is overdue and will incur a £13 surcharge if you don’t stump up pronto.Three months after I first contacted it, you are still getting contradictory bills that may, or may not, be yours, and you are still waiting for the promised compensation.We welcome letters but cannot answer individually. Email us atconsumer.champions@theguardian.comor write to Consumer Champions, Money, the Guardian, 90 York Way, London N1 9GU. Please include a daytime phone number. Submission and publication of all letters is subject toour terms and conditions.Explore more on these topicsMoneyConsumer championsConsumer affairsConsumer rightsEnergy billsHousehold billsEnergy industryCentricafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Exploring Germany’s East Frisian islands: mudflats, marshes and millions of birds | Germany holidays | The Guardian
description: The seven inhabited isles off the North Sea coast, part of a Unesco world heritage site, are a sanctuary for wildlife – with great hotels and restaurants too
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The seven inhabited isles off the North Sea coast, part of a Unesco world heritage site, are a sanctuary for wildlife – with great hotels and restaurants too
Content: The wide beaches of Norderney.Photograph: Jacques Nerwaque/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe wide beaches of Norderney.Photograph: Jacques Nerwaque/Getty ImagesGermany holidaysExploring Germany’s East Frisian islands: mudflats, marshes and millions of birdsThe seven inhabited isles off the North Sea coast, part of a Unesco world heritage site, are a sanctuary for wildlife – with great hotels and restaurants tooKate MannTue 21 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 08.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Can you hear that?” my guide Andrea Wellmeyer asks as I sink a little deeper into the wet ground, cold mud seeping between my toes. When the wind drops, I detect a faint but distinct crackling, similar to the rustle of a sweet wrapper or the bubbling of sparkling water. “That’s theWattknistern,” she says, “a natural phenomenon caused by the tens of thousands of mud shrimp that live here.”We’re standing in the Wadden Sea mudflats on Norderney, an island just off Germany’s North Sea coast. It’s Friday evening at low tide. In the town centre, bars are pumping with music, but here, just 15 minutes along the southern edge of the island, it’s a completely different story.“A lot of the flora and fauna is found under the surface,” Andrea says, showing me the tree-like tops of sand mason worm casings and the tips of marsh samphire growing upwards like tiny asparagus. Flipping over a cluster of wild oysters, she startles a crab that raises its pincers in the air before wriggling out of sight once more. Orange-beaked oystercatchers scurry back and forth on the exposed seabed and a pair of spoonbills wade gracefully through the shallow water beyond.View image in fullscreenA beach in Osterhook on the island of Langeoog.Photograph: Tasfoto/AlamyNorderney is one of the seven inhabited islands that make up the East Frisian islands (Ostfriesische Inseln)in Germany. The others are Borkum, Juist, Baltrum, Langeoog, Spiekeroog and Wangerooge. Stretching more than 50 miles from west to east, they are a popular holiday destination, known for their unique coastal landscapes and diverse wildlife, from grey seals and migrating birds to the creatures found in the mud.Each island has its own identity, from upmarket Juist with its horse-drawn transport to the historic red brick houses of sleepy Spiekeroog; but all have vast sandy beaches, grassy dunes and tidal mudflats. The islands belong to the larger Wadden Sea Unesco world heritage site and have been part of theLower Saxon Wadden Sea national parksince its formation in 1986.double quotation markHistoric seaside villas and retreats founded on the health benefits of briny sea air sit next to modern hotels and restaurantsNorderney was one of the first German seaside resorts in the North Sea. Today, historic seaside villas and retreats founded on the health benefits of briny sea air sit next to modern hotels and restaurants, such asInselloft Norderneyand Michelin-recommendedOktopussy, where summer scallops come with a caramelised miso-cauliflower purée. My favourite spot quickly becomesMarienhöhe, a dune-top pavilion built more than 100 years ago, where I eat delicately battered fish with warm potato salad.Despite being one of only two East Frisian islands where cars are permitted, the best way to explore Norderney is by bike. Leaving the town in the west, I zoom along the bike lanes that run through the dunes, stopping at a popular stretch of beach called Weiße Düne, before heading south towards the island’s tallest building, a lighthouse constructed between 1872 and 1874.View image in fullscreenWild oysters in the Wadden Sea.Photograph: Ron Giling/AlamySoon after, roughly halfway across the island, I reach a sign that says I can’t go any further on my bike. This is aRuhezone(quiet/restricted zone), a highly protected area where you can only walk on a couple of marked footpaths. A rusty shipwreck lies on the sand and seals bask in the sun at the far end.double quotation markMore than 10 million birds make a pit stop in the Wadden Sea every year, with spring and autumn the best time to see themThere are strict rules governing access in several sections of the island, part of a quest to let nature be nature. Heading out with an official guide helps you see and learn more. AlongsideWattwanderungen(mudflat hikes), you can also book salt marsh walks, birding excursions and boat trips to see the seals. The newly renovatedvisitor centreat Norderney harbour also has extensive information about the local ecosystems.My next stops are Spiekeroog and Langeoog, two calmer, car-free islands to the east. On Langeoog, I join a tour to learn more about the birds I have spotted over the last few days. “Many species need the Wadden Sea as the focal point of their migration, as a place to rest,” national park guide Fiona Wettstein says. “When they first arrive, they have completely burned through their fat reserves and practically drop down here.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice a weekSign up toThe TravellerGet travel inspiration, featured trips and local tips for your next break, as well as the latest deals from Guardian HolidaysEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenGreylag geese with chicks on Langeoog.Photograph: Image Professionals/AlamySetting off with bikes and binoculars, we spot red knots, redshanks, avocets and sandpipers, as well as squeaking lapwings with long crests and a short-eared owl swooping through the grasses. Geese with fluffy chicks in tow strut around the island, hissing loudly if you get too close. More than10 million birdsmake a pit stop in the Wadden Sea every year, with spring and autumn the best time to see them. The shoulder season is also a good time to visit to avoid the summer holiday crowds.I stay atSilt & Sand, a bright and airy design hotel just behind the dunes that opened last year. The expansive (almost 9-mile-long) beach on the other side is dotted withStrandkörbe, traditional hooded wicker armchairs found on beaches along the German coast.Despite the chain layout of the islands and their close proximity to one another, it isn’t possible to “hop” between them. Instead, you have to return to the mainland and make your way along the coast to a different ferry terminal each time, the exception being Juist and Norderney, which have a shared departure point in Norddeich. It is possible to walk from the mainland to some of the islands at low tide, but onlywith a certified guide.View image in fullscreenA hotel room at Silt & Sand on Langeoog.After my first few hours on Norderney, I give up on the weather forecast and start operating on a cloud by cloud basis instead, dark moody skies moving on as quickly as they appear. More reliable is the wind, churning the sea, bending the trees and blowing a fine mist of sand over the compact layer below. Having biked eastwards with ease, I’m met by a roaring resistance on my return, a fierce gust wrapping around my hat and prising it off.“This connection to the elements is part of the experience,” says Andrea. “Your entire system is challenged and pushed out of its comfort zone, something that doesn’t happen so often these days,” she adds as we make our way back to the familiarity and ease of the town.The trip was provided byTourismus Marketing Niedersachsenand theGerman National Tourist Office. Doubles atInselloft Norderneyfrom €160 and atSilt & Sandfrom €141 room-only.Book ferries through individual websites:Norderney and Juist;Spiekeroog.Explore more on these topicsGermany holidaysEurope holidaysfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Houseplant hacks: does dividing supermarket basil help it survive? | Houseplants | The Guardian
description: Seeds are packed into shop-bought pots, meaning they compete and wear themselves out. The best bet is to divide and conquer
Author: Gynelle Leon
Headline: Seeds are packed into shop-bought pots, meaning they compete and wear themselves out. The best bet is to divide and conquer
Content: That bushy basil plant is actually dozens of seedlings.Photograph: Roman Tsubin/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThat bushy basil plant is actually dozens of seedlings.Photograph: Roman Tsubin/Getty ImagesHouseplant hacksHouseplantsHouseplant hacks: does dividing supermarket basil help it survive?Seeds are packed into shop-bought pots, meaning they compete and wear themselves out. The best bet is to divide and conquerGynelle LeonTue 21 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe problemWe’ve all done it: picked up a lush, generous-looking pot of basil from the supermarket – full of promise and pesto plans, and within a fortnight it has collapsed into a dried-out, leggy mess. You assume you did something wrong. In reality, the plant was set up to fail before it even reached your kitchen.The hackThat full, bushy pot is not actually one plant; it is dozens of basil seedlings crammed into a single container to look bushy on the shelf. Packed that tightly, they compete for light, water and root space, and exhaust themselves within days. The fix is to divide the overcrowded mass into smaller, separated clumps and give each one room to actually grow.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toInside SaturdayThe only way to get a look behind the scenes of the Saturday magazine. Sign up to get the inside story from our top writers as well as all the must-read articles and columns, delivered to your inbox every weekend.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionHouseplant hacks: will a temperature drop make my orchid bloom?Read moreThe methodTip the basil out of its pot and gently tease the root ball into three or four smaller clumps, keeping as many roots intact as possible. Pot each clump into its own container of fresh compost, water them in and place them somewhere bright and warm. Pinch out the growing tips regularly to encourage bushier growth, rather than letting them flower and set seed.The testI divided one supermarket basil pot into four. Three of the clumps survived and put out new growth; one sulked and died, which felt like a fair survival rate. A month later, I had three healthy basil plants rather than a single dying one.The verdictDivide and conquer is the only real route to keeping supermarket basil alive. Give those crammed-in seedlings the space they were denied on the shelf, and you turn a near-certain compost-bin casualty into a proper, lasting supply.Explore more on these topicsHouseplantsHouseplant hacksGardening adviceGardensHerbs and spicesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Gruel and graft: museum asks if you could survive a Victorian workhouse | Museums | The Guardian
description: Revamped Ripon attraction reopens as a more immersive experience telling ‘national story’ of poverty and welfare
Author: Mark Brown
Headline: Revamped Ripon attraction reopens as a more immersive experience telling ‘national story’ of poverty and welfare
Content: Visitors to the museum, one of Britain’s most complete workhouses, can take part in activities including tasting gruel.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianView image in fullscreenVisitors to the museum, one of Britain’s most complete workhouses, can take part in activities including tasting gruel.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianMuseumsGruel and graft: museum asks if you could survive a Victorian workhouseRevamped Ripon attraction reopens as a more immersive experience telling ‘national story’ of poverty and welfareMark BrownNorth of England correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVisitors to one of Britain’s most complete surviving Victorian workhouses won’t have to strip and bathe in someone else’s bath water. But they might get some gruel, they can sweep the yard and there are “schoolmaster” volunteers happy to tell children how to behave.“You are literally going to walk in the footsteps of people who have experienced this place,” said the director of the RiponMuseumsTrust, Alexa Vernon.“I’m not sure we can serve gruel every day, but children will be able to get stuck into sewing and hunt for mice and listen to the sounds, and they might realise how lucky they are compared to Victorian children.”Vernon was speaking before the reopening of theRipon Workhouse Museumafter a £2.8m redevelopment.A key part of the revamp has been to make the experience more immersive with volunteers who might be baking bread or putting clothes through a mangle.View image in fullscreenThe museum intends to give a realistic experience of what it was like inside a Victorian workhouse.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianVisitors are asked if they would survive the Victorian workhouse. As they enter the vagrants’ block, the sound of unpleasant coughing fills the air. There are baths, a one-size-fits-all coarse men’s nightshirt and a reproduction “de-infestor” used to fumigate paupers’ and vagrants’ clothes.“All their possessions would be taken from them,” said the curator, Laura Allan. “They would be stripped. They would be bathed. If you were lucky you would be the first person in the bath water. If you were unlucky, you were the third.“It’s interesting that the reasons people became homeless in the past are very similar as today – so alcoholism, mental health, family breakdown, veterans returning from war.”Elsewhere in the museum, visitors can shut themselves in a vagrants’ cell, pick oakum from old ropes or pose for a family portrait in replica workhouse uniforms.View image in fullscreenThe museum’s reception area for vagrants and paupers.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianThe museum reopens to the public on Tuesday after major conservation and restoration work supported by theNational Lottery heritage fund.It became a museum in 2017, telling the story of the Victorian solution to poverty and homelessness – give people a bed and food, but make it as grim, unpleasant and rules-bound as possible.A revamp of the museum building had been badly needed, said Vernon. “You could honestly stick a screwdriver in the rotting window frames and it would stand up independently,” she said.“The roof was leaking. We had water damage throughout. The wiring was like spaghetti junction. We had unsupported arches. Our structural engineer said that technically the building shouldn’t stand up.”View image in fullscreenThe museum’s schoolroom.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianOn top of that the museum experience was more like walking round a 1970s council offices with bits of interpretation, unsurprising given that it was once office space forNorth Yorkshiresocial services.“Some of our Tripadvisor comments were along the lines of … it looks like they’ve not finished it,” Vernon said. “It wasn’t finished.”The lottery money has been transformative, and not just in fixing the leaking roof and rotting windows and painting the walls in horrible – but original – taupes, pinks and greens.One particular joy, curators say, has been the opportunity to open the cellar, allowing visitors to see where meat was slaughtered and stored.“This space, we think, was probably last opened in the 1930s,” said Allan. “This is probably exactly how it would have looked when it was a workhouse. It’s the only space that really has never been touched.”View image in fullscreenThe museum’s dining room.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianThe museum tells a story about Ripon but much more than that, said Vernon. “This is a national story,” she said. “Workhouses have an important story to tell about poverty, welfare and justice and there were workhouses up and down the country.“This was the Victorian solution to poverty.”The revamped museum is one of three in Ripon that tell vivid stories of Victorian life. There is also a courthouse museum and a prison and police museum.They tell historical stories, but their curators are passionate in their belief that they have contemporary resonance.“Poverty still exists, doesn’t it,” said Vernon. “People can come here and think about poverty, think about how we treat people and think about whether we are treating people more fairly now.”She hopes visitors to the museum will leave feeling more informed, “but also more compassionate, thoughtful and connected to the stories of those who came before us”.Explore more on these topicsMuseumsHeritagePovertyNorth YorkshirenewsShareReuse this content

title: Parents: Tell us how are you balancing work and childcare this summer | Parents and parenting | The Guardian

Content: Are you taking annual leave, reducing your hours or working early mornings and late evenings to fit everything in?Photograph: Sally Anscombe/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAre you taking annual leave, reducing your hours or working early mornings and late evenings to fit everything in?Photograph: Sally Anscombe/Getty ImagesParents and parentingParents: Tell us how are you balancing work and childcare this summerWith the cost of holiday childcare rising and many parents juggling work with looking after children, we’d like to hear how you’re managing this summerGuardian community teamTue 21 Jul 2026 06.04 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe summer holidays have begun, bringing the annual challenge many parents face in balancing work and looking after school-aged children. New research suggests arranging six weeks of holiday childcarecan cost working parents up to £1,145 per child, while almost three-quarters of parents say the summer childcare juggledamages their career.Are you paying for holiday clubs or childminders, relying on family and friends or working from home while caring for children? Are you taking annual leave, reducing your hours or working early mornings and late evenings to fit everything in?Has the summer holiday childcare challenge affected your work, career or finances? title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC | Ebola | The Guardian
description: UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease
Author: Nadeem Badshah
Headline: UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease
Content: A patient with Ebola receives medical care at the Rwampara Ebola treatment centre in Bunia, Ituri, in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, earlier in July.Photograph: Benediction Murhabazi/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA patient with Ebola receives medical care at the Rwampara Ebola treatment centre in Bunia, Ituri, in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, earlier in July.Photograph: Benediction Murhabazi/AFP/Getty ImagesEbolaHealth worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRCUK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the diseaseNadeem BadshahTue 21 Jul 2026 18.39 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 18.53 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital inLondonafter potential exposure to the Ebola virus.The UK resident was medically evacuated from theDemocratic Republic of the Congo(DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.UKHSA said the person was evacuated as a “highly precautionary measure”, adding: “The individual is not currently displaying any symptoms and remains well, but as a precautionary measure they have been evacuated on a flight chartered solely for the evacuation.“They are now being assessed and monitored in isolation by infectious disease specialists at a London hospital.”The agency said the incubation period ofEbolaranges from two to 21 days and the individual will be closely monitored and is required to isolate throughout this period.There are no confirmed cases of Ebola in the UK and the risk to the general public remains low.Richard Pebody, the director of epidemic and emerging infections at the UKHSA, said: “This individual has been transferred out of an abundance of caution and we’re pleased they remain well.”Last month, a patient suspected ofhaving the Ebola virus at a Glasgow hospitaltested negative.Precautionary measures were taken after the patient was admitted to the Queen Elizabeth University hospital.Anoutbreak of the diseasein the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda in May was declared a public health emergency of international concern by the World Health Organization (WHO).The virus is transmitted to people from wild animals and then spreads in the human population through direct contact with the blood, secretions, organs or other bodily fluids of infected people and with contaminated surfaces and materials such as bedding or clothing.France confirms first Ebola case in doctor who had worked in DRCRead moreAccording to WHO, symptoms can include fever, fatigue, malaise, muscle, pain, headache and sore throat. These are followed by vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, rash, and symptoms of impaired kidney and liver functions.Explore more on these topicsEbolaLondonDemocratic Republic of the CongoHealthAfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: Healthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report finds | Global development | The Guardian
description: Global hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat well
Author: Kat Lay
Headline: Global hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat well
Content: In Africa, 66.6% of the population was unable to afford a healthy diet in 2025, more than double the levels in Asia or Latin America and the Caribbean.Photograph: Jasmin Merdan/AlamyView image in fullscreenIn Africa, 66.6% of the population was unable to afford a healthy diet in 2025, more than double the levels in Asia or Latin America and the Caribbean.Photograph: Jasmin Merdan/AlamyGlobal developmentHealthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report findsGlobal hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat wellSupported byAbout this contentKat LayGlobal health correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOne in three people cannot afford to eat a healthy diet, a new report has found, prompting calls for urgent action on food affordability.There are 2.69 billion people unable to afford a diverse diet that meets their energy and nutrient needs, and it will take “immense” amounts of work to achievethe global target of zero hungerby 2030, the annual report from five UN agencies warned.Global hunger declined for a third consecutive year in 2025, with 7.8% of people affected, down from 8.1% in 2024. There were also signs that a prolonged upward trend inAfricawas coming to a halt.However, the proportion of people who cannot afford a healthy diet remains “extremely high”, said Maximo Torero, chief economist of the Food and Agriculture Organization, one of the report’s authors.The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World 2026 report calculated the cost of a healthy diet is 4.28 purchasing power parity (PPP) dollars a day. This means that in any given country, a healthy diet costs the amount of local currency with the same buying power that $4.28 has in the US.“The extreme poverty line is $3PPP per person,” Torero said. “So it’s significantly higher.”View image in fullscreenA typical Moroccan dish – Africa is now home to the highest number of hungry people for the first time, with about 309 million.Photograph: Crevous/AlamyHe said government policies had typically focused on subsidising cereals and starchy staples, rather than meat, dairy, fruit and vegetables.“That means calories, but not a diversity of diet,” he said, highlighting the impact of this on rates of obesity and diseases such as cancer and diabetes.Figures from the World Health Organization, another of the UN agencies behind the report, showadult obesityrose from 12.1% in 2012 to 16.2% in 2024.“It’s extremely urgent,” Torero said. “Obesity [among adults] is growing at a very steep level, so we need to now work intensively on trying to minimise those risks and those hidden costs that that substantial increase in obesity will bring.”He called for investment in infrastructure, research and development. Modelling in the report suggests more efficient supply chains could bring down the costs of meat and dairy as well as fruit and vegetables. New policies to encourage greater consumption of fruit and vegetables could also pay dividends, he suggested, reducing the economic impact of health issues linked to poor diets.Hunger increasingly used as weapon of war as ‘food-related violence’ surges, analysis showsRead moreIn Africa, 66.6% of the population was unable to afford a healthy diet in 2025, more than double the levels observed in Asia or Latin America and the Caribbean.Africa is now home to the highest number of hungry people for the first time, with about 309 million, surpassing Asia’s 292 million, the report showed.The rise is largely driven by higher population growth on the continent, Torero said, with the proportion of the population going hungry actually starting to decrease “for the first time in years”.“I think this is extremely important, because the numbers are for 2025, where already we have seen a significant reduction in overseas development assistance in the region,” Torero said.Shipping restrictions in the strait of Hormuz linked to the conflict in the Middle East are likely to hit next year’s figures, he said.The report is authored by the FAO, WHO, Unicef, the UN World Food Programme and the International Fund for Agricultural Development.Explore more on these topicsGlobal developmentHungerPovertyUnited NationsAfricaAsia PacificAmericasnewsShareReuse this content

title: Spanish hotel chain ceases operations in Cuba under pressure from Washington  | Cuba | The Guardian
description: Meliá group says Donald Trump’s sanctions make it ‘impossible’ to keep any of its 34 sites open
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Meliá group says Donald Trump’s sanctions make it ‘impossible’ to keep any of its 34 sites open
Content: The Meliá Cohiba in Havana, one of 34 hotels where the Spanish hotel chain is to cease operations.Photograph: Adalberto Roque/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe Meliá Cohiba in Havana, one of 34 hotels where the Spanish hotel chain is to cease operations.Photograph: Adalberto Roque/AFP/Getty ImagesCubaSpanish hotel chain ceases operations in Cuba under pressure from WashingtonMeliá group says Donald Trump’s sanctions make it ‘impossible’ to keep any of its 34 sites openAgence France-PresseTue 21 Jul 2026 11.10 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA Spanish hotel chain is to cease all operations inCubaat the end of the week due to “major difficulties” in doing business on the island under growing pressure from Washington.The latest US sanctions make it “impossible, de facto and de jure, to maintain even minimal operational stability”, the Meliá group explained in a statement on Tuesday to the Spanish stock market regulator.The company said it would strive to ensure a smooth transition to reduce the impact as much as possible on staff, suppliers and clients.Meliá announced last month that it would cease operating 15 of its 34 hotels in Cuba, joining other foreign companies that have cut ties with the island’s military conglomerate, GAESA, under pressure from Washington.Cuba edges toward breakdown as power cuts and US meddling push society to brinkRead moreMeliá, one of the largest foreign hotel operators in Cuba with 14,000 rooms, did not refer in that statement to the other 19 hotels it operated in Cuba in partnership with the country’s tourism ministry.Donald Trump, the US president, ordered new sanctions in May against Cuba, targeting a broad set of individuals and threatening foreign banks and companies that worked with them.Meliá was the first Spanish hotel group to establish a presence in Cuba after theCaribbeannation opened its tourism sector to foreigners in the 1990s, hoping to ease the economic crisis triggered by the collapse of the Soviet Union.In addition to an economic embargo in place since 1962, Washington has blocked all oil shipments to the island since January, with the exception of a single Russian ship.Explore more on these topicsCubaAmericasHotelsCaribbeannewsShareReuse this content

title: US claims of Cuba’s ‘unique threat’ jar with reality of hunger and blackouts | Cuba | The Guardian
description: State department’s 100-page report is latest push in Trump administration campaign to bring down government
Author: Ruaridh Nicoll
Headline: State department’s 100-page report is latest push in Trump administration campaign to bring down government
Content: Uncollected rubbish during a blackout in Havana this month. The country has been under a US oil blockade since January.Photograph: Yamil Lage/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenUncollected rubbish during a blackout in Havana this month. The country has been under a US oil blockade since January.Photograph: Yamil Lage/AFP/Getty ImagesCubaAnalysisUS claims of Cuba’s ‘unique threat’ jar with reality of hunger and blackoutsRuaridh Nicollin HavanaState department’s 100-page report is latest push in Trump administration campaign to bring down governmentTue 21 Jul 2026 09.33 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTShareThe US government’s latest allegations thatCubais behind “many of the most significant upheavals in recent American political history” and that its leaders pose “a unique threat to American homeland security” have come as startling revelations to those living under the six-month-long US oil blockade in Havana, where desperate people search through piles of uncollected rubbish for food, or else swelter at home in 24-hour-plus blackouts.The unsigned 100-pagereport, Cuba: Capital of 21st-Century Communism, published by the US state department on Monday, blames Havana for everything from “the George Floyd riots to the rise of antifa to the explosion of pro-terrorist activism on American college campuses”.‘Front groups and fellow travelers’: state department attacks US left over CubaRead moreIt is the latest push in a maximum-pressure campaign to bring down the island’s government that began with the abduction of Nicolás Maduro, the president of Cuba’s ally Venezuela, on 3 January. Since then, Cuba has been brought to a standstill by the oil blockade, sanctions on foreign businesses and threats of a military intervention.It also seems to be an attempt to “other” those on the US left before November’s midterm elections.“Cuba’s assault on the United States was never, at root, a quarrel over economic policy or sovereignty or even ownership of a particular territory,” the report reads. “It was a revolution against western civilisation itself – waged, in part, via the novel and insidious method of persuading the children of the west to turn against their own inheritance.”“Fidel would have been quite proud of this,” said one amused Cuban as he read it.The US state department, run by the Cuban-AmericanMarco Rubio, refused to name the author, but it is clearly someone who knows their history. “Almost immediately after taking Havana in January 1959, Castro began plotting armed assaults against neighbouring governments – launching expeditions against Haiti, the Dominican Republic, Nicaragua and Panama in just that year alone,” it reads.What it fails to mention is that Haiti was then run by François “Papa Doc” Duvalier, who killed an estimated 60,000 of his own people; the Dominican Republic by Rafael Trujillo, who launched the “parsley massacre”, killing between 20,000 to 35,000 Haitians; and Nicaragua by the Somoza family, who are credited with the murder of up to 50,000 Nicaraguans.Although the report provides no new evidence, there is a suspicious emphasis on race. The supposedly remarkable success of Cuban intelligence at infiltrating US institutions was due to a soft spot for “glamorous Latins”, it quotes an intelligence officer saying to the Miami Herald in 2002: “‘We’re much more open to Latins than we are to people with steel teeth and a Slavic accent.’”It also bemoans the ideological commitment of the spies Cuba recruited, while failing to mention either that several were inspired by the CIA’s attempts to assassinate Castro, or the shelter the US offered to the exiles Luis Posada Carriles and Orlando Bosch, who organised the bombing of Cubana flight 455 in 1976, killing 73 people.Most jarringly, the report groups together the US’s long internal struggle around civil rights and social reform and reframes it as a battle between the forces of foreign actors and “western civilisation”, lingering over groups such as the Black Panthers, which it credits to Cuba.While several black revolutionaries found sanctuary on the island, most famously Assata Shakur, who had a $2m price on her head for the killing of a New Jersey state trooper, the experience was not perhaps as conspiratorial as the author suggests. In interviews, Nehanda Abiodun, one of the mentioned fugitives, said her politics and revolutionary rage came from her experience growing up in 1970s New York, not out of any love for Cuba, whose government she came to resent for muzzling her in return for refuge. She used to say that the first thing that happened on landing was being racially profiled by the Cuban police. If she was part of a well-oiled international conspiracy, it didn’t show.For five years the Cuban government has not even been able to afford to feed its 9.5 million people, who mostly blame it for the crisis. But the report is ready for this: “To mistake Cuba’s poverty for harmlessness is to misunderstand the threat entirely. Its material weakness was never the measure of its danger.”It concludes: “Today, Cuban agents and operatives maintain a foothold in hundreds of front groups, activist organisations, solidarity centres, and ideological NGO and nonprofit networks. These groups collectively represent one of the single largest, most sophisticated and dangerous vectors for hostile foreign influence in modern American history.”In the run-up to the report’s release, Miguel Díaz-Canel, Cuba’s president, said the US had fallen prey to a “new and more dangerous version of McCarthyism”.But perhaps the more damning criticism came from a Cuban academic who preferred to remain nameless: “It reads just like a report Cuba’s state media might write about the United States.”Explore more on these topicsCubaAmericasCaribbeanUS foreign policyMarco RubioanalysisShareReuse this content

title: We received no alert, say leaders of First Nations communities razed by Canadian wildfires | Canada | The Guardian
description: Chiefs in Ontario say they were left to self-evacuate before homes burned to ground, after being told there was no imminent threat
Author: Olivia Bowden
Headline: Chiefs in Ontario say they were left to self-evacuate before homes burned to ground, after being told there was no imminent threat
Content: Smoke from wildfires settling in the sunset, seen from the shores of Neskantaga First Nation, Ontario.Photograph: Canadian Press/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenSmoke from wildfires settling in the sunset, seen from the shores of Neskantaga First Nation, Ontario.Photograph: Canadian Press/ShutterstockCanadaWe received no alert, say leaders of First Nations communities razed by Canadian wildfiresChiefs in Ontario say they were left to self-evacuate before homes burned to ground, after being told there was no imminent threatOlivia Bowdenin TorontoTue 21 Jul 2026 07.45 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFast-moving wildfires have burned two First Nations communities inCanadato the ground, as members said they were left to self-evacuate without government help or emergency alerts.Thirteen communities in Ontario province have been ordered to evacuate because of wildfires, most of them First Nations. The Ontario premier, Doug Ford, told reporters on Monday that close to 2,000 people had been evacuated so far to cities including Toronto and Niagara Falls.There are nearly 1,000 wildfires burning across Canada, with about 200 in Ontario. The prime minister, Mark Carney, said heatwaves and dry weather had combined to create “one of the most intense wildfire seasons in Canadian history”.View image in fullscreenSmoke rising from the Fiftynine Creek wildfire in British Columbia.Photograph: BC Wildfire Service/ReutersNamaygoosisagagun First Nation, located north of Thunder Bay in north-western Ontario, was engulfed by fire on 15 June. About two dozen people quickly boarded boats packed with their possessions and raced across the nearby lake.“If this community waited for a mandatory evacuation process, we’d be doing a recovery mission for the bodies of children, of elders, of men and women,” said Linda Debassige, grand council chief of the Anishinabek Nation, which represents 39 Ontario First Nations.All members safely escaped but the community’s buildings and other infrastructure were completely destroyed. The Namaygoosisagagun people evacuated to Thunder Bay, where the Anishinabek Nation has been providing assistance.Debassige said that Namaygoosisagagun’s chief, Helen Paavola, had made inquiries to the federal government in the days leading up to the fire, but the community had been assured there was no imminent threat.Ford travelled to Thunder Bay over the weekend and said the province was willing to spend whatever was necessary to fight the fires.View image in fullscreenThe Whitewater Lake First Nation in north-west Ontario has been burned to the ground.Photograph: Nishnawbe Aski Nation/ReutersHowever, First Nations leaders, including from the Anishinabek Nation, accused the premier of being unclear about what support the province was providing and, and of disorganisation in coordinating with First Nations.The CBChas reportedthat although Ontario spent $271m on emergency firefighting in 2025-26, it plans to spend only $150m in 2026-27.The Ontario government did not respond to requests for comment.First Nations communities are acutely vulnerable to wildfire danger in Canada, with 80% of Indigenous communities located in areas more susceptible to fires, according to a 2019 study from the Journal of Disaster Risk Reduction. The Namaygoosisagagun First Nation faces an additional challenge in that, although it is recognised as a nation by many other First Nations, it is still in the process of being officially recognised by the federal government, making it more difficult to gain emergency funding.Debassige said Canada’s federal government had agreed to provide the funding for now but that the Anishinabek Nation had had to step in and offer financial support because of delays on the government side.The Whitewater Lake First Nation, about 250km north of Thunder Bay, was also destroyed by the recent wildfires. Though the community is made up of mostly seasonal residences with no full-time residents, First Nations leaders say the community’s infrastructure is now gone.Nicole Redvers, an associate professor at Western University in London, Ontario, said there was a disproportionate wildfire impact on Indigenous communities due to proximity to traditional territories that livelihoods are based around, which include forests.That vulnerability, alongside Canada’s lack of planning around community resilience on climate change, made for scenarios like what the Namaygoosisagagun had to contend with, said Redvers.“A community had to evacuate themselves without any warning,” she said. “If we don’t have the preparedness capacity within Canada to ensure that all communities have climate adaption plans in place … we’re definitely not well placed to have resilience within our communities going forward.”Explore more on these topicsCanadaWildfiresCanada wildfiresAmericasIndigenous peoplesEmergency servicesClimate crisisnewsShareReuse this content

title: Perth teenager charged with murdering his mother and attempting to kill sister | Perth | The Guardian
description: Police found woman dead in Bentley home and seriously injured girl on nearby highway
Author: Caitlin Cassidy
Headline: Police found woman dead in Bentley home and seriously injured girl on nearby highway
Content: Police have charged a 16-year-old boy in Perth with the murder of his mother and attempting to unlawfully kill his sister.Photograph: Tony Mcdonough/AAPView image in fullscreenPolice have charged a 16-year-old boy in Perth with the murder of his mother and attempting to unlawfully kill his sister.Photograph: Tony Mcdonough/AAPPerthPerth teenager charged with murdering his mother and attempting to kill sisterPolice found woman dead in Bentley home and seriously injured girl on nearby highwayFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastCaitlin CassidyTue 21 Jul 2026 19.46 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 20.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA teenage boy has been charged with murder after he allegedly killed his mother with a knife and stabbed his little sister, who managed to flee to get help.The 16-year-old boy was charged on Wednesday with one count of murder and one count of attempting to unlawfully kill after police attended a home at Bentley, in Perth’s south-east, on Tuesday morning.The 35-year-old mother was found dead by officers when they arrived at the family residence.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailThe 12-year-old girl was also stabbed but managed to flee the house, making it to a nearby highway for help.Bystanders provided the girl with life-saving first aid and she was taken by ambulance to Perth children’s hospital, where she remains in a critical but stable condition.Western Australia police acting inspector Jessica Securo said the incident had shocked the community.“This incident will have a significant impact on the family, friends and loved ones of all those involved,” she said.“Our thoughts are with them all, particularly the 12-year-old girl.”The 16-year-old was due to appear before the Perth children’s court on Wednesday.Explore more on these topicsPerthWestern AustralianewsShareReuse this content

title: RBA now twice as likely to hike interest rates as US-Iran war drives fuel prices higher | Australian economy | The Guardian
description: Experts warn global energy market at a ‘critical juncture’ with escalating Middle East crisis expected to further drag on a slowing Australian economy
Author: Patrick Commins
Headline: Experts warn global energy market at a ‘critical juncture’ with escalating Middle East crisis expected to further drag on a slowing Australian economy
Content: Economists warn another prolonged closure of the strait of Hormuz as a result of renewed US-Iranian conflict could force the Reserve Bank of Australia to raise interest rates.Photograph: EyeOfPaul/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenEconomists warn another prolonged closure of the strait of Hormuz as a result of renewed US-Iranian conflict could force the Reserve Bank of Australia to raise interest rates.Photograph: EyeOfPaul/Getty ImagesAustralian economyRBA now twice as likely to hike interest rates as US-Iran war drives fuel prices higherExperts warn global energy market at a ‘critical juncture’ with escalating Middle East crisis expected to further drag on a slowing Australian economyFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastPatrick ComminsEconomics editorTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 19.08 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe likelihood of a Reserve Bank interest rate hike has doubled, according to market forecasts, as the escalating Middle East crisis once again drives fuel prices higher amid dwindling global oil reserves.Thetotal breakdown in the fragile ceasefirebetween the US and Iran has sent the international Brent crude benchmark surging by 23% over the past two weeks and back within reach of $US90 a barrel.As experts warn the global energy market is now at a “critical juncture”, Australian motorists are once again faced with climbing fuel costs, albeit without the panicked buying and accompanying shortages that accompanied the start ofthe US-Israel war on Iranin early March.Diesel, which has consistently been most exposed to the global disruptions, has jumped by 40 cents in July to about $2.10 a litre in the major cities on the east coast, according to Motormouth.US launches 10th round of strikes as Iran says it is in ‘full-scale war’Read moreThe removal of some of the federal government’s fuel excise relief from the start of this month has also contributed to higher costs, with unleaded petrol up by 25 cents to about $1.75.Amid already depleted global oil stockpiles, analysts warn of a coming tipping point after Iran’s leaderdeclared “full-scale war”with the US and Houthi rebels threatened to blockade millions of Saudi Arabian oil passing through the Red Sea.Luke Yeaman, the CBA’s chief economist, said a total lack of trust between the warring parties made it difficult to judge the trajectory of the conflict, which he said would send a fresh stagflationary pulse through theAustralian economy.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia email“In the current dynamic, we believe this will drag on for at least several weeks and possibly longer,” Yeaman said.With inflation already tracking too high for comfort, traders in financial markets are upping bets that the Reserve Bank could be forced to hike rates for a fourth time.Markets now place a nearly 30% chance of an interest rate rise on 12 August, up from 16% two weeks ago. The probability of a hike by November has doubled to 80% over the same period, according to ANZ.Yeaman said he was sticking to his forecast for no more rate rises this year – at least for now.“If we see a prolonged closure of the strait and a big jump in oil prices, that will feed through to higher inflation, but it will also slow growth. In the short term that could mean the case for one further rate hike is higher.“But calls for multiple … rate hikes are a little overblown.”Yeaman warned that global oil prices could push as high as US$150 a barrel if no negotiated solution was reached by late August or early September, conjuring the spectre of worst-case scenarios contemplated during the first phase of the conflict.“Our expectation is that before some of those extreme points are reached that there is some resolution. I expect that if oil prices really spiked again then the government would step in and shield households by reinstating the full fuel excise discount.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionRegardless, higher energy prices and renewed global conflict is set to further drag on an Australian economy that is already slowing sharply under the additional weight of three interest rate hikes and a falling housing market.Yeaman said he had forecast economic growth to slow to 1.5% by the end of this year, from 2.5% in 2025.“Were you to see a serious escalation in the conflict and a prolonged closure of the strait [of Hormuz], then growth could slow much more sharply.”Mission in Iran war has pivoted toward ensuring oil flow, Trump official saysRead moreDaniel Hynes, a senior commodity strategist at ANZ, said the drop in oil prices during the ceasefire into the low 70s had not reflected the structural hit to global supply and the system’s “fragility”, and that $US80 to $90 a barrel was a more realistic level.“The $US100-a-barrel mark would potentially be within sight if we are here in a few weeks’ time and things have gotten worse.”Hynes said there were “technical” limits to how low oil inventories could go without triggering a breakdown of infrastructure, and that these limits were already being breached in the US.That would push buyers there to look further afield for oil, increasing competition for scarce seaborne cargoes and driving up prices.“At the moment there’s a feeling that if it [renewed conflict] doesn’t persist too much longer, they will be able to suffer through this period without too much damage,” Hynes said.“Obviously the risks rise day by day; the market is at a critical juncture.”Explore more on these topicsAustralian economyReserve Bank of AustraliaEconomicsIranStrait of HormuzEnergyOilnewsShareReuse this content

title: Kate Ellis is back – and the heat of political debate is higher than ever, incoming Labor president says | Labor party | The Guardian
description: The former Rudd/Gillard government minister has a plan for fighting back against populists including Pauline Hanson
Author: Tom McIlroy
Headline: The former Rudd/Gillard government minister has a plan for fighting back against populists including Pauline Hanson
Content: Beginning on Thursday, Kate Ellis will chair three days of deliberations by thousands of party officials and rank-and-file members in Adelaide.Photograph: Tracey Nearmy/The GuardianView image in fullscreenBeginning on Thursday, Kate Ellis will chair three days of deliberations by thousands of party officials and rank-and-file members in Adelaide.Photograph: Tracey Nearmy/The GuardianLabor partyKate Ellis is back – and the heat of political debate is higher than ever, incoming Labor president saysThe former Rudd/Gillard government minister has a plan for fighting back against populists including Pauline HansonFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastTom McIlroyPolitical editorTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 22.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe incoming Labor president,Kate Ellis, says toxicity in politics is spilling into the broader community and public debate, warning that threats against MPs’ safety are limiting how elected politicians do their jobs.Due to succeed Wayne Swan as the leader of Labor’s organisational base at this week’s national conference, the former Rudd/Gillard government minister said fighting back against populists including Pauline Hanson was best achieved by demonstrating policies that improve voters’ lives.Israel criticises Labor’s draft Palestine policy in rare foreign intervention ahead of party conferenceRead more“My sense is that people are angry and that people are hurting, and I think it’s really important to acknowledge that,” she told Guardian Australia.“I don’t think there’s any value in denying that there are people out there who are doing it tough and who wish that life could just be a bit easier.“I think the focus for Labor needs to be on showing that we’re the ones that actually deliver the reforms that make a real difference to people’s everyday reality.”Beginning on Thursday, Ellis will chair three days of deliberations by thousands of party officials and rank-and-file members in Adelaide, with debates expected on policy positions on tax, AI, gambling advertising andPalestine.A cross-party group of MPs, including the climate change assistant minister, Josh Wilson, and the former attorney general Mark Dreyfus, useda new video, produced by the International Campaign to Abolish Nuclear Weapons, to call on Labor to sign and ratify the UN treaty on the prohibition of nuclear weapons.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailEllis represented the federal seat of Adelaide for 15 years, serving as a minister from 2007 until 2013.Elevated to the ministry at just 30, she faced entrenched sexism, sexual advances from men and gossip in Canberra. She revealed after leaving politics that a Liberal staffer had aggressively challenged her to say how many men she had “fucked in order to get into parliament”.View image in fullscreenEllis says gender diversity in politics has greatly improved since she retired.Photograph: Tracey Nearmy/The GuardianAfter holding portfolios including sport, employment participation and early childhood education, Ellis quit politics in 2019 to be closer to her family. A former Adelaide Crows Football Club board member, she is the chief executive of the non-profit Raising Literacy Australia.“There’s no doubt that the heat of political debate has been turned up since I left, and that’s horrifying to me because I think it was bad enough at times as it was,” she said.“In terms of the threat to people’s safety and the way that people can go about doing their jobs, I think that that is really deeply concerning. It means that there is a responsibility on everyone in political discussions to try and remain focused on having legitimate policy debates, but also doing so in a respectful way.”A former minister for the status of women, Ellis said gender diversity had greatly improved in politics since she retired, along with the treatment of female MPs, but there were areas of backsliding in society more broadly.“With politics overall becoming more toxic, that then flows into a whole range of other areas in the public debate,” she said.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThere will be disagreement at next week’s Labor conference – but don’t expect the feuds of the pastRead moreExpected to be elected to the presidency unopposed, Ellis rejected suggestions that the party’s 50th annual conference – which includes debates on changes to Labor’s platform set to guide policy up to the next election –was overly stage-managed to limit conflict.“If there’s huge policy debates on a range of issues, commentators will say that the party is divided, and if not, they’ll say it’s all stage management,” she said.“There’ll be policy debates, some of which probably will not even be anticipated.”Ellis backed Albanese’s aspirations to take a policy of universal childcare access to the next election.“We know that if we can get the settings right to support families in those years when they are juggling work and family commitments before school, then that’s incredibly important to the population,” she said.“I’d argue that, even more importantly, we know that if we can deliver access to quality early childhood education, we can start making sure that there aren’t so many Australian children falling behind before they even reach school age.“I think that is the ultimate goal of trying to increase equality and opportunity.”Labor has commissioned consulting giant Deloitte to help design a universal childcare system in Australia, launching a two-year study of demand and costs, as Albanese chases a political legacy to rival Medicare. The report is due to be handed to the governmentby early 2027.Labor’s chief campaign strategist, national secretary Paul Erickson, will be reappointed unopposed, confirming his partnership with Anthony Albanese as Labor seeks a third term at the 2028 poll.Explore more on these topicsLabor partyAustralian politicsWomenChildcare AustralianewsShareReuse this content

title: The Queensland government promised to put victims first. But it has buried a report from domestic violence survivors | Domestic violence | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Leaked document from LNP-convened panel heavily criticises police responses to family violence and calls for civilian oversight
Author: Ben Smee
Headline: Exclusive: Leaked document from LNP-convened panel heavily criticises police responses to family violence and calls for civilian oversight
Content: The government convened a panel of people with lived experience of domestic, family and sexual violence to identify gaps in the system and guide reforms.Photograph: Dan Peled/AAPView image in fullscreenThe government convened a panel of people with lived experience of domestic, family and sexual violence to identify gaps in the system and guide reforms.Photograph: Dan Peled/AAPDomestic violenceThe Queensland government promised to put victims first. But it has buried a report from domestic violence survivorsExclusive:Leaked document from LNP-convened panel heavily criticises police responses to family violence and calls for civilian oversightFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastBen SmeeQueensland correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 18.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheQueenslandgovernment has buried the findings of its own working group of domestic violence victim-survivors after their report was heavily critical of police responses and called for a “civilian review mechanism” to escalate complaints about police.The Liberal National government has repeatedly said it would “put victims at the heart” of the state’s justice policies.In early 2025, the government convened a panel of people with lived experience of domestic, family and sexual violence to identify gaps in the system and guide reforms.Astatement announcing the working group, released by the domestic and family violence prevention minister, Amanda Camm, criticised the former Labor government and said it had “failed to implement recommendations” related to the issue.The working group concluded its work in December but a summary report including its recommendations has never been released. Camm’s office refused a Guardian request to release the report this week.Guardian Australia has seen a copy of the summary report, which noted it was “developed for broader distribution” and to address any concerns related to victim-survivor confidentiality.Panel members say they cannot speak publicly about the process.
