One source said on condition of anonymity: “You can say all that you want about being tough on crime and putting victims first, but if it doesn’t come with CCTV footage for the news, then it’s clearly not a government priority.”Sources familiar with the process believe the government’s reluctance to release the report and its findings relates to the document’s prominent and strong criticisms of police, including calls for independent oversight.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailSince the LNP came to office, the Queensland police service hasscrapped its specialist domestic and family violence commandand released a reform plan that said domestic and family violence case management wasnot “core business”. Police have been given additional powers to issue “on the spot” directions that have seriously concerned women’s advocates.At the same time, the recommendations from several reports that have raised concern about police responses to domestic violence have stalled. These include a long-overdue recommendation to establish a civilian-led police integrity unit to handle complaints.The leaked report by the victim-survivor working group devotes its first and most detailed section to policing. It said police responses were “inconsistent” and affected by “limited understanding and attitudinal concerns”.It said policing responses were often dictated by individual officer attitudes rather than “consistent operational approaches”.“Victim-survivors reported delays in police responses, reporting processes and follow up actions,” it said. “Some members felt breaches of DVOs were minimised or not treated with the level of seriousness expected.“An entrenched culture within policing was highlighted as a concern, characterised by inconsistent identification of breaches, and the application of personal judgement, undermining consistent and appropriate responses for victim-survivors.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionPolice failed to act on repeated warnings about a violent man before he killed a toddler. But that’s not the most shocking thing about this caseRead moreThe panel made several recommendations in relation to police responses, including calls for the state to adopt nationally standard risk assessment tools and better victim-centred practices.It also called for “a civilian review mechanism for escalating concerns about inadequate police responses”.The report also made wide-ranging recommendations about the need for reforms to child protection, mental health, housing, the legal system and specialist domestic and family violence services. It called for “efforts to address ongoing experiences of systemic racism”.Guardian Australia approached Camm on Monday asking why no report or recommendations had been made public and if the minister would release them.Her office refused the request and did not provide a written response.Asked again about the report on Tuesday, Camm did not address questions about why the report had not been released.She sent a statement that said the government had made a “record high” investment in domestic and family violence prevention and that the government planned to deliver more services, police and affordable housing.“It is correct to report, following Labor’s decade of decline, the Crisafulli Government has given a voice to victims of domestic and family violence and has prioritised turning around the deteriorating services we inherited from the former government,” Camm said.“For example, our government has increased DV Connect’s answer rate from 41.9% under Labor to 84%.”One source familiar with the report said they believed it was “very clear” that the government’s reluctance to release the summary report was because it was “unwilling to act on many of the recommendations”, especially those related to police.“You can’t be for victims but only when it suits your agenda,” the source said.Since the 2024 election, the Crisafulli government has taken a hardline and “victim first” approach to youth crime, including several tranches of legal reforms.At the same time, critics said the government had done comparatively little to address gender-based violence. Funding for domestic and family violence prevention wascut in the state budget, and community sector workers said their services were overburdened and under-resourced.In Australia,domestic and family violence counsellingis available from Full Stop Australia on 1800 385 578. Other international helplines may be found viawww.befrienders.orgExplore more on these topicsDomestic violenceQueensland politicsQueenslandLiberal National partyAustralian police and policingCrime - AustralianewsShareReuse this content

title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’ | Ukraine | The Guardian
description: After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to government
Author: Luke Harding
Headline: After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to government
Content: Oleksandr Syrskyi was credited with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024.Photograph: Vitalii Nosach/EPAView image in fullscreenOleksandr Syrskyi was credited with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024.Photograph: Vitalii Nosach/EPAUkraineZelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to governmentLuke Hardingin DniproTue 21 Jul 2026 17.34 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVolodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparkednationwide protests.In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said.Zelenskyy said he was bringing Mykhailo Fedorov back into government after hisremoval as defence ministerprompted thousands of people to demonstrate in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, in one of the biggest political crises since Russia’s 2022 invasion.View image in fullscreenVolodymyr Zelenskyy with Oleksandr Syrskyi at the handover of drone missiles to the Ukrainian army in Kyiv in December 2024.Photograph: Sergey Dolzhenko/EPAUkraine’s president paid tribute to Syrskyi and credited him with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024, in which foreign tanks rolled on to Russian territory for the first time since the second world war.“We have come a long way. The defence of Ukraine continues. And every soldier deserves to be treated with respect,” Zelenskyy said, adding that he was grateful to Syrskyi and to “every one of our soldiers” fighting on the frontline.Zelenskyy will be hoping that his radical reshuffle of the army’s leadership will assuage protesters who have taken to the streets demanding Syrskyi’s removal. They had given a deadline of Friday for Fedorov to be reinstated and promised “indefinite protests” if their request was ignored.1:32Why Ukrainians are protesting against Zelenskyy’s dismissal of defence minister – videoFedorov welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment. “This brings a breath of fresh air and new hope to the struggle of free people for freedom and justice. It is a voice for change that could not be ignored,” he said.He added: “The high expectations of the Ukrainian people must be met with a clear vision for ending the war for independence, a strong team and decisive actions aimed at saving the lives of our soldiers, automating the frontline, delivering asymmetric strikes and crippling the Russian economy.”The Guardian view on Ukraine’s challenges, inside and out: tensions at home arise from the struggle against Russia | EditorialRead moreDrapatyi, the new commander-in-chief, is well-regarded within Ukraine’s army and is liked by the public. He is viewed as an ally of Fedorov, whose attempts to reform the military and force it to adopt modern technology and data led to a bitter clash with the old-school Syrskyi.A major general, Drapatyi is commander of Ukraine’s joint forces group. He has been fighting Russia since its covert attempt in 2014 to take over the eastern Donbas region. He became famous nationally when hedrove an infantry fighting vehicle over a barricadeset up by pro-Russian militants in the city of Mariupol.Ukraine war briefing: Russia behind deadly strike on cargo ship, says Ukraine, as Putin thanks North KoreaRead moreThe Ukrainian media outlet Babel described Drapatyi as one of Ukraine’s most capable battlefield commanders. It said his new job would be fundamentally about reforming a large institution rather than commanding troops in combat.Zelenskyy said he and Drapatyi agreed that Ukraine’s long-range drone attacks on targets in Russia such as oil refineries would continue. “Our programme of precision strikes will be implemented with absolute precision,” he said, promising a smooth and coordinated handover following Syrskyi’s departure.He also said the new commander-in-chief would be tasked with updating the country’s defence strategy and reforming its structure. Another pressing issue is mobilisation, at a time when after nearly five years of full-scale war few Ukrainians are ready to volunteer.It is unclear what Fedorov’s new role will be, amid reports that he was insisting on being given his old job back as defence minister. This has now been filled by Yevhen Khmara, a former special operations commander.View image in fullscreenYevhen Khmara replaced Mykhailo Fedeorov as Ukraine’s defence minister.Photograph: Ukrainian presidential press service/AFP/Getty ImagesZelenskyy said he met Fedorov on Tuesday. “I offered Mykhailo a prominent position within the government that would enable him to unify the technological sector of our state and ensure its development,” he said, without giving further details.Zelenskyy added: “Our shared wish is one – victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace.” He said “a significant path has been travelled, the defence of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment”.Explore more on these topicsUkraineVolodymyr ZelenskyyRussiaEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: France becomes first EU country to ban social media access for under-15s | France | The Guardian
description: Parliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel Macron
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Parliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel Macron
Content: Emmanuel Macron visits a children's health centre in Paris on Tuesday, the same day the French parliament voted to ban children from social media.Photograph: Michel Euler/APView image in fullscreenEmmanuel Macron visits a children's health centre in Paris on Tuesday, the same day the French parliament voted to ban children from social media.Photograph: Michel Euler/APFranceFrance becomes first EU country to ban social media access for under-15sParliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel MacronAFPTue 21 Jul 2026 15.14 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFrance’s parliament has approved a bill banning social media access for children under 15, making it the first EU country to bar children from apps such as TikTok.The president, Emmanuel Macron, has championed the ban as a key reform of his final term in office and pledged to enforce it by September.“France is leading the way inEuropewhen it comes to protecting our children and teenagers,” Macron said in a video posted on social media, hailing “a major step forward”.He thanked members of parliament for backing the legislation on Tuesday.“The Constitutional Council must now rule on it, and then it will be time to take action to make this measure a reality and protect our children online,” he added on X.The social media ban sceptic: are we getting it wrong on kids, tech and mental health?Read moreAfter approval by the Senate earlier on Tuesday, members of the National Assembly passed the bill by 279 votes to 81.A growing number of countries are taking steps to restrict social media access amid multiplying warnings over its harmful effects on children.The ban was to be introduced in two stages, with under-15s blocked from creating new accounts from 1 September. The ban would apply to existing accounts from January 2027, according to the legislation.The digital minister, Anne Le Hénanff, said before the vote that the timeline was realistic, “because age-verification tools already exist” and others are still in the works, and the onus was on the platforms to impose the rule.“For four months, all of us in France will have to prove our age,” she told journalists. “If someone is under 15, the account will be closed.”The minister also gave assurances that users’ personal data would be protected.Enforcing the measure by September would ensure that one provision of the bill – a ban on mobile phones in high schools – takes effect at the beginning of the French school year.France’s public health watchdog last year said platforms such as TikTok, Snapchat and Instagram were harmful to adolescents, particularly girls, though this was not the sole reason for their declining mental health.Lawmakers agreed on the need for regulation but the two chambers had disagreed on the approach.French senators had opted for a two-tier system distinguishing between blacklisted platforms flagged as harmful to a child’s development, and those that could still be accessed with parental consent.But the lower house’s blanket ban prevailed, despite criticism from some on the left over the age verification process, the speed of its implementation and risks of circumvention as well as privacy concerns.Exceptions to the ban are provided for sites such as online encyclopedias and educational platforms, and the text does not provide for any penalties for children or parents.“The major platforms are, in my view, largely ready,” assembly member Laure Miller told AFP.The blacklisting system would have taken more time, including fresh consultations with the European Commission on the criteria, and carried a “small risk” of non-compliance with European law, said centrist senator Catherine Morin-Desailly.The European Commission has been considering a social media ban after a push by member states, including France, Greece and Spain, for limiting access.Last week, Ursula von der Leyen, the president of the commission, said children should have “phased and gradual access” to social media.The European Commission has also unveiled an age verification app that EU member states, including France, have begun testing in recent months.After Macron’s government suspended pensions reforms last year, a social media ban could be his last major domestic change before he steps down next year.France will be closely watched by other European states – it is one of 20 countries worldwide to haveproposed or introduced such measures.Last December, Australia became the first country in the world to require TikTok, YouTube, Snapchat and other top sites toremove accounts held by under-16s or face heavy fines.Explore more on these topicsFranceSocial media bansChildrenEuropeSocial mediaDigital medianewsShareReuse this content

title: Airbus to test folding wings for next generation of bestselling planes | Airbus | The Guardian
description: Such technology solves problem of longer, thinner, energy-saving wings not fitting in airports around the world
Author: Jasper Jolly
Headline: Such technology solves problem of longer, thinner, energy-saving wings not fitting in airports around the world
Content: What an Airbus plane with folding wings might look like. The company says ‘the value of being able to fold the wings is huge’.Photograph: AirbusView image in fullscreenWhat an Airbus plane with folding wings might look like. The company says ‘the value of being able to fold the wings is huge’.Photograph: AirbusAirbusAirbus to test folding wings for next generation of bestselling planesSuch technology solves problem of longer, thinner, energy-saving wings not fitting in airports around the worldJasper JollyTue 21 Jul 2026 13.50 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAirbus has said it will test folding wings for the next generation of its bestselling planes, as the world’s largest planemaker races to work out the shape of future commercial aircraft by the end of the decade.Longer, thinner wings made of light but strong composite materials offer the promise of energy savings but present a problem for manufacturers because they will not fit in airports around the world, many of which are already at or near full capacity.“The value of being able to fold the wings is huge,” said Sue Partridge, the head of Airbus’s wing of tomorrow programme, speaking at the Farnborough airshow, south-west of London, where the global industry gathers for a major conference every two years.Folding wingtips offer a solution. Boeing has already installed themon its larger 777X, which has two aisles, but the technology has not been used on any single-aisle planes. Boeing is also looking at new technologies, including using a “truss” to brace longer wings.Airbus said it will fit extensions of about four or five metres to the wings of one of its A321neo planes to test how different shapes cope in real-world conditions in the second half of next year. The model is part of the A320 family of aircraft, the bestselling model of all time.The European manufacturer and its bitter US rival Boeing are gearing up to make the next generation of planes after decades of selling their respective A320 and 737 models. Both companiesare looking at technologiesthat they hope will significantly cut down energy usage, including new engines, hybrid electric systems and longer wings.Airbus is about to start the assembly of the wing extension in its factory in Broughton, northWales. However, its first phase of tests will only include the bolts that will fix it in place and not yet the hinges that will allow it to fold up.The company has said it will decide on the shape of its new aircraft by 2030. Partridge said Airbus already knows that the next generation will use relatively heavy engines with a large diameter and that the wings will be bent like a gull’s for extra aerodynamic benefit.Airbus and Boeing will also have to choose new engines. Airbus is considering whether to go with covered engines that encase the fan blades, oran “open fan” option with the blades exposed.The British manufacturer Rolls-Royce is hoping to break into the marketfor higher-selling single-aisle planes, also known as narrowbodies, when Airbus and Boeing make their next generation. The Rolls-Royce chief executive, Tufan Erginbilgiç, on Tuesday told reporters at Farnborough that the company had only two years to persuade the plane manufacturers to choose its engines.He urged the UK government under its new prime minister, Andy Burnham, to back the company’s investments in its UltraFan engine programme. The new chancellor, John Healey, was previously the defence secretary and so “understands Rolls-Royce and our agenda”, Erginbilgiç added. However, the plea for subsidy from the highly profitable manufacturer will have to be weighed against Burnham’s other spending prioritiessuch as social careandending rough sleeping.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionRolls-Royce isdeveloping two variants of its UltraFan engine– one for larger, widebody jets and another narrowbody version. Erginbilgiç said it wasworth supportingbecause it was the “single biggest opportunity for the next 50 years” for economic growth for the UK, and that it would “transform advanced manufacturing” in the country.“We would like to do it in the UK because it is our origin,” Erginbilgiç said. “But we have other options.”Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS Group, a lobby group for the British aerospace and defence industry, said the UK government should “focus on the champions for UK aerospace” in trying to win “probably the most consequential decision in our industry”.Explore more on these topicsAirbusAerospace industryManufacturing sectorAirline industryAir transportEuropeBoeingnewsShareReuse this content

title: Russian warship carries out rare live-fire exercise 45 miles off UK coast  | UK news | The Guardian
description: New defence secretary Wes Streeting denounces drill by Neustrashimy as ‘performative and irresponsible’
Author: Dan Sabbagh
Headline: New defence secretary Wes Streeting denounces drill by Neustrashimy as ‘performative and irresponsible’
Content: The Russian frigate Neustrashimy off Simonstown, South Africa. If arrived off the British coast earlier this month.Photograph: Gallo Images/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe Russian frigate Neustrashimy off Simonstown, South Africa. If arrived off the British coast earlier this month.Photograph: Gallo Images/Getty ImagesUK newsRussian warship carries out rare live-fire exercise 45 miles off UK coastNew defence secretary Wes Streeting denounces drill by Neustrashimy as ‘performative and irresponsible’Dan SabbaghDefence and security editorTue 21 Jul 2026 11.23 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 04.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA Russian warship fired its main cannon in a short notice test 45 miles south of Plymouth on Monday, the day Andy Burnham was appointed as prime minister.The Neustrashimy suddenly announced it intended to fire its guns in the morning and requested that a British warship, which had been tracking it, retreat a couple of miles to a safe distance.The patrol vessel HMS Tyne repositioned five miles away, allowing the Russian frigate to conduct a 30-minute firing drill at about 9.30am in international waters to the south of the UK.Wes Streeting, on his first full day as UK defence secretary, described the incident as “performative and irresponsible”. “It’s the tip of the iceberg of the daily threats that this country faces and our allies face.”Russian warships have been sailing around British waters for much of 2026. Last month another frigate, theAdmiral Grigorovich, fired warning shotswithin a few hundred metres of a couple on a British pleasure yacht, south of the Isle of Wight.Conducting a live-fire exercise at short notice in international waters close to another country is a rare event and usually conducted with due notice. However, if proper safety warnings are given it is legal.The 130-metre-long Neustrashimy is armed with a 100mm cannon capable of firing 50 rounds a minute at a range of 20km. It is also armed with anti-ship cruise missiles and has Gatling rotary machine guns as part of its air defence systems.British defence sources said they could not remember the last time a Russian warship had test-fired so close to the UK. Firing exercises are usually conducted with particular care in the Channel, one of the world’s busiest commercial waterways.The Neustrashimy arrived off the British coast earlier this month, replacing theAdmiral Grigorovichwhich had been on an extended three-month deployment, typically in the Channel or the North Sea.On Tuesday morning the Neustrashimy was sailing east of the Isle of Wight, and thought likely to be heading into the North Sea, where it may refuel. It is now being tracked by HMS Mersey, which has taken over shadowing responsibility from HMS Tyne.Maritime tensions between the UK and Russia are heightened. A Russia-linked oil tanker wasseized in the Channel by the Royal Marines last month, prompting warnings that there could bea Russian retaliation.Russian warships have been used to escort some oil tankers, though at other times their presence is designed to be psychological and tie up UK naval resources in monitoring them.Dmitry Peskov, the Kremlin’s spokesperson, said Russian warships always acted “in strict accordance” with international and maritime law. Asked whether the Russian government felt relations with the UK would improve under the new prime minister, Peskov said it had “no such hopes”.A Ministry of Defence spokesperson said: “A Russian naval vessel shadowed by the Royal Navy conducted a live-fire weapons exercise in international waters approximately 40 nautical miles south of Plymouth yesterday.“The Royal Navy monitored that exercise throughout, continues to track the vessel’s activity closely, and stands ready to protect UK national security.”Explore more on these topicsUK newsRussiaMilitaryEuropenewsShareReuse this content

title: Stand still, make eye contact: Denmark tells tourists how to behave in a wolf encounter | Denmark | The Guardian
description: Government urges visitors not to approach or feed wolves as it manages safety amid growing population
Author: Miranda Bryant
Headline: Government urges visitors not to approach or feed wolves as it manages safety amid growing population
Content: Wolves started returning to Denmark in 2012 after they were eradicated 200 years earlier.Photograph: Rolf Larsen/Getty Images/500px PlusView image in fullscreenWolves started returning to Denmark in 2012 after they were eradicated 200 years earlier.Photograph: Rolf Larsen/Getty Images/500px PlusDenmarkStand still, make eye contact: Denmark tells tourists how to behave in a wolf encounterGovernment urges visitors not to approach or feed wolves as it manages safety amid growing populationMiranda BryantNordic correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 09.45 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDanish authorities have advised tourists to stand still, make eye contact and put up “maximum resistance” in the event of attack if they encounter a wolf while on holiday.After a 200-year absence, wolvesstarted returning to Denmarkin 2012. There are now estimated to be 49 wolves on the Danish peninsula of Jutland.Generally, it is rare for wolves to even approach humans, but in the area around the popular tourism destination of Oksbøl, young ones were spotted doing so earlier this year.The municipality of Varde and Visit Vesterhavet have distributed flyers containing the government advice around holiday homes, campsites and tourist offices in Danish, English and German.Tourists are told to keep calm and still in the presence of a wolf, hold eye contact, take a picture of the animal and, if it will not go away of its own accord, shout, clap hands and wave arms. They are advised not to follow or to turn their backs on an approaching wolf. The tip is to “stare directly at it” to “show dominance”.While there have been no wolf attacks on humans inDenmark, in the event of it happening, tourists are urged: “You must offer maximum resistance: hit and kick and use any blunt weapon.”The government advice also states that children under the age of 10 should be accompanied by an adult in the Oksbøl area.Wolves have long had a presence inDenmark– the oldest wolf bones in the country are 13,000 years old – but after the last wolf was shot in 1813 they only started to make a comeback in the last two decades.Their presence has received a mixed response, often dividing people along political lines. In April, two ponies were killed by wolves in a fortnight. Soon after, four MPs from the far-right Danish People’s party werereported to policefor sharing a Facebook post calling on people to “Shoot the wolf”.Wolves are protected by EU law.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenThe return of wolves has divided opinions in Denmark.Photograph: Ricochet64/Getty Images/iStockphotoThe wolf expert Peter Sunde, a wildlife ecology professor at Aarhus University, said: “During the winter and spring of 2026, at least two young wolves were observed approaching humans and exhibiting markedly reduced flight distances in the area around Oksbøl that is a tourist hotspot. One of these wolves is still alive.”This type of behaviour, he said, usually happens when young wolves are repeatedly exposed to humans. “It has been discussed whether tourists, who may be unaware both of the presence of wolves in the area and of the basic recommendations regarding wolf encounters, may have contributed to the development of this behaviour,” he added.“To prevent similar behaviour from developing in future generations of young wolves, it is important that people who live in or visit wolf habitats – including tourists – understand that they should neither approach wolves nor leave food that may attract them.”Varde’s second deputy mayor, Sandie Eis Ravn, said the campaign was aimed at informing tourists at the request of local businesses. “If you are visiting somewhere and you know there is a wolf in the nearby forest, to make sure you have the right code of conduct when you meet this creature,” she said.Explore more on these topicsDenmarkEuropeWildlifenewsShareReuse this content

title: Hegseth tells Senate war on Iran has so far cost $37.5bn at hearing to request more funds | Pete Hegseth | The Guardian
description: Defense secretary has asked Senate appropriations committee for nearly $88bn more to continue effort
Author: Joseph Gedeon
Headline: Defense secretary has asked Senate appropriations committee for nearly $88bn more to continue effort
Content: Pete Hegseth speaks during a briefing amid the US-Israeli war on Iran, at the Pentagon in Washington DC.Photograph: Jonathan Ernst/ReutersView image in fullscreenPete Hegseth speaks during a briefing amid the US-Israeli war on Iran, at the Pentagon in Washington DC.Photograph: Jonathan Ernst/ReutersPete HegsethHegseth tells Senate war on Iran has so far cost $37.5bn at hearing to request more fundsDefense secretary has asked Senate appropriations committee for nearly $88bn more to continue effortJoseph Gedeonin Washington andLauren GambinoTue 21 Jul 2026 19.15 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 15.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe US defense secretary,Pete Hegsethtold theSenateon Tuesday that theUS-Israel war on Iranhad cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members.During an appearance before the Senate appropriations committee, Hegseth, whose opening remarks were repeatedly interrupted by protesters, provided an updated account of the war’s cost – up from previous estimates – and defended the Trump administration’s roughly $88bn supplemental funding request, calling it “urgent, necessary”.Alongside the chair of the joint chiefs, Gen Dan Caine, Hegseth argued that the Pentagon was running low on cash to keep the fight going. Of the total request, as much as $67.1bn is earmarked specifically to cover the costs of theIranwar effort.“Without these funds, we face critical shortfalls,” Hegseth told the Senate panel, warning that it would threaten the defense department’s ability to “pay our service members, rapidly replenish equipment and munitions, and sustain vital operations without disruption”.It was the defense secretary’s first sustained public questioning since a brief ceasefire collapsed and the war roared back to life. At least two US soldiers were killed in an Iranian attack on Friday, and another died in Iraq destroying an Iranian drone, pushing the American death toll since February to 17. Nearly 100 troops have been wounded in the conflict, officials confirm, though most injuries were minor.Patty Murray, the committee’s Democratic vice-chair, demanded Hegseth explain what the war had actually achieved, accusing the administration of shifting its rationale by the week and warning the conflict risked becoming “another forever war”.“We hear a different rationale for this war every few days,” Murray said in her opening remarks, noting the justification had moved from pre-empting threats that “did not exist” to regime change, then to dismantling Iran’s nuclear programme and navy, and now, she said, to simply reopening the strait of Hormuz, a waterway that was “fully open, toll-free, before this war began”.“Here we are, five months later”, she continued. “The strait is closed. The ceasefire hasn’t just disintegrated – this war is now spiraling out of control again.”Under questioning from lawmakers, Hegseth cast the funding request as the price of deterrence rather than admitting failure, and framed the Pentagon’s overhaul under the US president as a break from what he described as years of slow bureaucracy.Doctors question evidence behind Pentagon plan for testosterone screeningRead more“The best way to create peace is to prepare for war, to deter it,” Hegseth told the panel, arguing the department was “building a force so capable that it would be absurd for anyone to challenge it”.Hegseth said the $21bn readiness portion of the request would cover military pay, replace equipment used in recent operations and stabilize fuel shortfalls, while the $46bn capability portion would expand production of “solid rocket motors, Jdams [joint direct attack munitions], hypersonics and counter-drone capabilities”.Without the funds, he warned, the department faced “critical shortfalls” that threaten its ability to pay troops and replenish munitions, and he cautioned that failing to fund the Pentagon at $1.5tn was “the greatest threat that our nation faces”.Some Democrats, including Chris Coons of Delaware, plan to direct criticism at Russell Vought, director of office of management and budget, who is not testifying but who earlier toldUS Congressthat the war’s running cost was about $30bn, a figure that is largely believed by experts to be a severe undercount.Hegseth and Caine faced potential threats from within their own party. Susan Collins of Maine, chair of the committee, and Alaska’s Lisa Murkowski, two prominent Republicans, have both crossed the aisle to back war-powers resolutions aimed at reining in the president’s authority over the conflict. Collins, who is facing a competitive re-election contest, has previously voiced frustration that the administration’s strategy for the war seems to shift almost daily.During the hearing, she asked Hegseth about the effect on military exercises if the supplemental funding is not provided.The defense secretary said that current and future training “would have to be curtailed” if the Pentagon does not get its budget needs met urgently.Senator John Kennedy, a Republican of Louisiana, pressed the panelists on the situation in the strait of Hormuz, asking if Iran will impose tolls on commercial vessels passing through the vital waterway which carried about a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war.“That’s a hypothetical question,” Caine replied, “and I don’t know the answer to that.”“Guys, we need straight answers,” Kennedy shot back.Both congressional chambers have passed a war-powers resolution urging the president to seek congressional authorization to continue military operations in Iran. Asked why the administration had not yet sought congressional approval for the war, Hegseth said it was the view of his department that “standalone Article II authorities exist for us to conduct this operation on behalf of the defense of the American people”. The defense secretary evaded the lawmaker’s question about whether he would use the approval of any supplemental funding as a “substitute for explicit congressional authorization”.House representatives have separately accused the Pentagon of stonewalling on basic figures: Betty McCollum, the top Democrat on the House defense appropriations panel, said the department has missed a deadline to detail war costs and has not disclosed how many precision munitions remain after the military burned through thousands of them in Iran.Explore more on these topicsPete HegsethUS-Israel war on IranUS SenateIranUS militaryTrump administrationUS politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Houthis threaten to attack shipping tankers if they use Saudi Arabian ports on Red Sea | Houthis | The Guardian
description: Warning to shipping companies, on top of Iran’s chokehold on strait of Hormuz, may severely affect Saudi oil exports
Author: Peter Beaumont
Headline: Warning to shipping companies, on top of Iran’s chokehold on strait of Hormuz, may severely affect Saudi oil exports
Content: The Bab el-Mandeb strait, where Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis are attempting a blockade.Photograph: ReutersView image in fullscreenThe Bab el-Mandeb strait, where Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis are attempting a blockade.Photograph: ReutersHouthisHouthis threaten to attack shipping tankers if they use Saudi Arabian ports on Red SeaWarning to shipping companies, on top of Iran’s chokehold on strait of Hormuz, may severely affect Saudi oil exportsMiddle East crisis live – latest updatesPeter BeaumontSenior international correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 13.27 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleYemen’s Iranian-aligned Houthis have warned shipping companies that their tankers risk being attacked if they use Saudi Arabian ports, as the militia moved quickly to enforce a blockade announced the previous day.The Houthi warning for the Red Sea, coming on top of Iran’s threat to shipping on the other side of the Arabian peninsular in the Gulf, could affect the substantial Saudi oil exports that have so far been unscathed by the months-long conflict and deepen the rapidly escalating crisis in the region.Underlining how seriously the Houthi threat is being taken, two oil tankers that had loaded Saudi crude ⁠this week for China and India made U-turns on Tuesday away from Houthi-controlled coast at the mouth of the Red Sea and headed towards the Suez canal. TheHouthisclaimed six ships had changed course.Donald Trump on Tuesday said the US would “​just have to ​take ‌care ​of ​business” if the Houthis followed through on their threats. “You know, we’ve done that with the Houthis before, and we haven’t heard from them in a while since we did what we did originally,” he said at White House meeting with the Lebanese president, Joseph Aoun.The Houthi threat came as diplomats sought to salvage the collapsed interim deal between Iran and the US even as both countries continued to launch attacks in a 10th day of renewed fighting, including a visit to Pakistan by the Iranian interior minister, Eskandar Momeni, an important mediator in the conflict.View image in fullscreenYemenis take to the streets in Sana’a to protest against Saudi Arabia’s military action in north Yemen.Photograph: Mohammed Hamoud/Getty ImagesSources suggested that behind the scenes there were indications that both sides might be seeking a way out of the latest round of intensive strikes. The news site Axios suggested that the Trump administration was weighing whether to escalate the conflict further or to seek a 10-day ceasefire to create the space for a new round of talks.The US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, said Washington remained open to negotiating with Iran but that “it has to be real”.The Houthis control ⁠northernYemen, including territory along the Bab el-Mandeb, the strait at the southern end of the Red Sea. While the Houthis have not formally announced the closure of the waterway, as some had feared might happen, the militia group’s threat to vessels using Saudi ports has raised serious questions over the safety of navigating a channel used by 7% of global maritime traffic.9:57US troops killed: has Trump lost control of Iran war? - The LatestShipping companies were told in an email from the Houthis on Monday that they should not load or ⁠discharge cargo at Saudi Arabian ports and such ⁠activity may result in ⁠being ​targeted “in any location” within its reach.“We strongly recommend that your company exercise due diligence and the utmost care in all its dealings,” ‌the email said, which was sent to multiple ​companies shortly after the Houthis said Monday they were imposing a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia.The significance of the move was underlined by Allison Minor, a Middle East expert atthe Atlantic Council thinktank. “The Houthis have not yet clarified which ships they will target, but their intent to blockade Saudi ports implies that any ship visiting Saudi ports and carrying Saudi oil would be in the crosshairs,” she said.“The oil currently transiting the strait from the north is going primarily to Asian markets. During their earlier Red Sea campaign, the Houthis cut deals with Russia and China to permit their ships to pass – but the Houthis may be less willing to cut deals with Chinese ships carrying Saudi oil.”Between 2023 and 2025, the Houthis had used small boats, drones and missiles to attack more than 100 commercial ships and render “the waters of the 20-mile-wide strait too risky for most shippers,” she added.RBA now twice as likely to hike interest rate as US-Iran war drives fuel prices higherRead moreWhile the Houthis have their own agenda relating to their long-term hostilities with Riyadh, which has at times seen them target Saudi energy facilities with long range attacks, Iran has also been trying to drag Yemen into a wider conflict aimed at throttling global energy supplies. It has previously pressed the Houthis to close the Bab el-Mandeb if the US continued to attack Iranian power infrastructure and Tehran has made clear it sees threats to maritime traffic around the Bab el-Mandeb as a counterweight to US efforts to block Iran in the strait of Hormuz, demonstrating Iran’s continued room for manoeuvre a week and a half into renewed full-scale hostilities.View image in fullscreenThe Lebanese president, Joseph Aoun, is greeted by the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, in Washington DC.Photograph: Lebanese Presidency/AFP/Getty ImagesWith 25% of oil and gas shipments passing through the strait of Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb combined, any significant restrictions on traffic would have a major global economic impact.Heavy strikes have meanwhile continued on both sides of the conflict.US military’s central command said on Tuesday it had targeted “Iranian military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and air defence systems”. Iranian state media reported that explosions were heard in the provinces of Fars, Hormozgan, Ilam, Kerman, and Sistan and Baluchestan.Tehran also attacked US allies throughout the Middle East. Jordan’s military said Iran had targeted it with five drones and three missiles, all of which were shot down.The Kuwaiti government said Iran had struck desalination and power plants for a fourth straight day on Monday night. Authorities said the attacks sparked fires and caused damage.The US state department has also issued a warning to Americans that “Iran and groups supportive of Iran may target other US interests overseas or at locations associated with the United States and Americans throughout the world.”Explore more on these topicsHouthisYemenSaudi ArabiaMiddle East and north AfricaIrannewsShareReuse this content

title: Zendaya faces backlash for wearing ancient Iranian jewellery at Odyssey event | Archaeology | The Guardian
description: Film star promotes Christopher Nolan film epic wearing gold artefacts dating back almost 3,000 years
Author: Ellie Violet Bramley
Headline: Film star promotes Christopher Nolan film epic wearing gold artefacts dating back almost 3,000 years
Content: Zendaya at a photocall in London for The Odyssey. The earrings are fashioned from the Ziwiye hoard, which was uncovered in Iran in 1947.Photograph: Scott A Garfitt/APView image in fullscreenZendaya at a photocall in London for The Odyssey. The earrings are fashioned from the Ziwiye hoard, which was uncovered in Iran in 1947.Photograph: Scott A Garfitt/APArchaeologyZendaya faces backlash for wearing ancient Iranian jewellery at Odyssey eventFilm star promotes Christopher Nolan film epic wearing gold artefacts dating back almost 3,000 yearsEllie Violet BramleyTue 21 Jul 2026 11.01 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Odyssey is a story about a Greek king’s long journey home. But a pair of earrings worn on the new film adaptation’s press tour – and their long separation from their homeland – is sparking controversy.At a photocall in London, Zendaya, who plays the goddess Athena in theChristopher Nolan film, wore a pair of earrings fashioned from Iranian artefacts dating back to 700BC.The backlash was swift, with archaeologists criticising the use of precious and culturally significant artefacts as accessories in the promotion of a Hollywood film, claiming it was a “hijacking” of Iran’s contributions to humanity.The earrings belong to Barron London, a Mayfair-based gallery and dealerwhose website states: “With a reputation for discretion, the gallery works privately with collectors, estates and institutions to uncover and place pieces of significance.”According to a formal statement from Barron the artefacts, which were bought from another London jeweller, Glenn Spiro, in 2025, “are attributed to the Ziwiye hoard, discovered in north-western Iran in the late 1940s”.The hoard was a trove of gold, silver, bronze and terracotta found in 1947 and looted by shepherds.According to a 2015 research paper, the surviving pieces were “sold in antique markets out of Iran in the 1960s” and “are now scattered in different museums and private collections”.Prof Sussan Babaie, an Iranian-born art historian and Courtauld Institute professor, said it was “shocking to see that no one thought maybe this is not a good idea”.Expressing “outrage” thatZendayawas wearing the ancient objects, she said: “There is not enough cultural knowledge and cultural sensibility among those who dress [celebrities], those who accessorise them, those who put them up as the symbols of beauty and power and an expression of the movie’s high-mindedness.” Representatives for Zendaya and her stylist have been contacted for comment.The row comes amid scrutiny of western institutions that hold ancient artefacts. The British Museum has faced criticism over itslooted Benin bronzes,sacred Ethiopian altar tabletsand the Parthenon marbles.Kim Kardashian wore a dress that belonged to Marilyn Monroe at the Met Gala in 2022. As Scott Fortner, who has a collection of Monroe artefacts,told the BBC at the time: “When you buy something of such significance, you really need to stick to your word to protect and preserve it … and clearly that didn’t happen.” The gown’s owners denied that any damage had been done to the dress.View image in fullscreenA gold plate from the Ziwiye hoard showing a character fighting a lion. The object, which dates back to 700BC, is in the Louvre in Paris.Photograph: Lanmas/AlamyDr Annelise Baer, an archaeologist and producer whose video about Zendaya’s earrings garnered criticism for what some perceived as condoning the use of the artefacts, said the main ethical issue was “that the earrings themselves are made out of looted antiquities”.She added: “Zendaya wearing them is kind of a secondary fact … but because she has worn them on a red carpet for a major international movie premiere, this has suddenly put all of that into the public eye in a way that we haven’t really seen before.”According to Barron’s statement, the earrings “are responsibly stewarded in accordance with their documented provenance and all applicable legal requirements”.Babaie said the jewellery probably had “extremely important spiritual connotations”, adding: “These are objects that are not meant to be displayed as accessories… It’s human heritage. It’s world heritage, not just accessories.”Christopher Nolan’s Odyssey used occupied land as a film set. That feels like a betrayal | Mohamed Sleiman LabatRead moreThe effect, she said, was to strip the earrings “of all meaning”. It is, she claims, “hijacking, flattening and transforming [ancient Iran’s] cultural contributions to humanity”.The timing is also causing further anger. Babaie highlighted a “maddening” situation in which the press tour for The Odyssey is “glamourising the antiquities of Iran … just as billions of American dollars are being used to bomb Iran”, destroying sites of cultural and historical interest.In its statement, Barron added: “The intention behind the loan was never commercial. It was to celebrate the remarkable artistry of ancient Persian goldsmiths and to encourage wider appreciation of one of the world’s great artistic traditions. At a time when Iran is so often represented through the lens of contemporary politics, we hope these earrings can also remind audiences of the country’s profound cultural, artistic and historical legacy.”The Odyssey has also been criticised over one of its filming locations.Sahrawi film-makers are calling for a boycottof the movie over the use of the city of Dakhla in occupied Western Sahara – a stretch of land under Moroccan control sometimes referred to as “Africa’s last colony”.Explore more on these topicsArchaeologyIranZendayaThe OdysseyHistoryMiddle East and north AfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: IDF accused of working with settlers after latest deadly West Bank raid | West Bank | The Guardian
description: Residents of Deir Jarir say raids that killed two Palestinians late on Sunday are part of pattern of violence
Author: Andrew Roth
Headline: Residents of Deir Jarir say raids that killed two Palestinians late on Sunday are part of pattern of violence
Content: The bodies of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu, killed in Israeli raids at the village of Deir Jarir, are taken to a burial ground on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyView image in fullscreenThe bodies of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu, killed in Israeli raids at the village of Deir Jarir, are taken to a burial ground on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyWest BankIDF accused of working with settlers after latest deadly West Bank raidResidents of Deir Jarir say raids that killed two Palestinians late on Sunday are part of pattern of violenceAndrew RothandSufian Tahain Deir JarirTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.53 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEven by the brutal standards of militant settler violence in theWest Bank, the raid on Deir Jarir late on Sunday night that killed two Palestinians and left another in hospital with a gunshot wound was extreme.Palestinians in the town said settlers from a nearby outpost arrived after 9pm on Sunday claiming that they were searching for stolen livestock. Israeli soldiers and police soon joined them, protecting them as they sought to take several villagers’ sheep by force.But when villagers raised the alarm and a crowd gathered, soldiers shot two men, beginning a night of three successive raids by settlers from nearby outposts and Israeli soldiers that lasted until after 2am, according to Palestinian residents of Deir Jarir.Saber Ajaj, a relative of one of the victims, 53-year-old Odeh Farajna, said he found him moments after he had been shot through the chest. Farajna, a member of the village council, owned a flower shop and helped with the stonework at the local graveyard.“The moment I put him in the car I knew that he was dead,” Ajaj said in an interview during a reception held after the funeral. “This shooting was meant to kill.”In interviews as they buried the two victims on Monday, family and friends of the dead Palestinian men described several attacks each month in which they said settlers, backed by security forces, arrived in houses on the outskirts of the village and then stole livestock.“It’s a partnership between the army and the settlers,” said Adel Rashid, the father of the younger victim, Abdul Adel Rashid Abu Mukh, 26.Rashid said local settlers on Sunday had stolen 21 of his sheep, while 50 more had been taken from another member of the village. Video of Sunday’s incident showed a settler protected by four IDF soldiers in green camouflage leading a sheep out of a local house.The raids are part of a picture of worsening settler violence in the region as Benjamin Netanyahu’s far-right allies have accelerated a land grab in the occupied West Bank.View image in fullscreenThe funeral of Awda Abulrahim Farajna and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyArbitrary checkpoints and other restrictions to cut off towns like Deir Jarir after the7 October 2023 attacksby Hamas have been bolstered by plans for the government to endorsenew settlementsin the West Bank before nationalelectionsthat could challenge the Netanyahu government’s mandate.On the hill above the village is a settlement built last year that appears to share water infrastructure with an Israeli military base, according to Dror Etkes, an Israeli who founded Kerem Navot, an organisation that monitors Israeli land policy in occupiedPalestine. Residents said the settlers and military appeared to have coordinated the raid.Seven people have been killed in the village since 7 October 2023. Their portraits have been spray-painted on the walls of the town, along with the date they died.“The killing in Deir Jarir is one example of how Israel promotes ethnic cleansing in the West Bank: settlers invade and steal, while the military and police accompany them and shoot Palestinians who try to protect their homes and property,” said Yuli Novak, the executive director of B’Tselem, a human rights organisation based in Israel that catalogues violations by the security forces.A Guardian analysis has shown that Israel has not prosecuted its citizens for killing Palestinian civilians in the occupied West Bank since the start of this decade, creating impunity for a campaign of violence.The West Bank attacks are intensifying. Fadi Hamdallah al-Naasan, a 17-year-old from the town of al-Mughayyir, north-east of Ramallah, died on Saturday after succumbing to a gunshot wound to his leg during a raid on his house by the Israeli military and settlers on 11 July.Nearly a thousand mourners, mostly men, gathered in Deir Jarir on Monday shortly after noon for the burial. They marched from the mosque in the centre of the town, carrying the two bodies down to the old graveyard.On the road entering the area stands a new set of gates at a checkpoint established after 7 October that security forces are said to open or close arbitrarily, effectively cutting off the villages from the outside world.“Every time they come to attack us, they are trying to make life here unlivable,” said Iyad Odah, 56, a member of the village council who knew Farajna. Since 7 October, he said, the town’s farmland, which once occupied thousands of hectares, was inaccessible as local settlers beat back farmers, attempting to access their orchards and grazing land.The Israeli military on Monday saidthey had gone to Deir Jarir to escort Israeli settlers, who claimed their livestock had been stolen and wanted to take it back. The military said they opened fire after Palestinians threw rocks and concrete blocks at the security forces. They did not respond to questions clarifying why they did not use non-lethal crowd control instead of live ammunition.Responding to the IDF’s claim that they had been attacked by “terrorists” in the village, Odah, of the town’s village council, said: “They are the real terrorists.”Explore more on these topicsWest BankPalestineMiddle East and north AfricaIsraelnewsShareReuse this content

title: India’s Cockroach movement protesters show no sign of backing down | India | The Guardian
description: Thousands of people again on the streets of Delhi demanding education reforms despite police violence on Monday
Author: Hannah Ellis-Petersen
Headline: Thousands of people again on the streets of Delhi demanding education reforms despite police violence on Monday
Content: CJP supporters before a march to parliament during a protest demanding the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan.Photograph: Jai Thakur/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenCJP supporters before a march to parliament during a protest demanding the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan.Photograph: Jai Thakur/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockIndiaIndia’s Cockroach movement protesters show no sign of backing downThousands of people again on the streets of Delhi calling for education reforms despite police violence on MondayHannah Ellis-Petersenin DelhiTue 21 Jul 2026 12.13 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTaru had come to the protest prepared this time. In one hand was an anti-government poster and in the other was a bicycle helmet. “We need to have protection in case Delhi police beat us up again,” said the 25-year-old, who stood among her friends. “We are not scared of them and we won’t run away, no matter what they do. We’ll stay here till they listen.”On Tuesday, central Delhi still thronged with thousands of protesters, as an unprecedented expression of dissent against the powerful rulingNarendra Modigovernment showed no sign of abating.Just 24 hours earlier,more than 50,000 people took to the streets in protest, swathes of them in their late teens and early 20s, mobilised by a new youth-led political movement that calls itself the Cockroach Janta party (CJP). Their rallying call was the resignation of the education minister and for reform of a corrupt and broken education system.Who are India’s Cockroach Janta party and why are they staging protests?Read moreBut what began peacefully on Monday – people fanning each other in the gruelling humidity, sharing snacks and chanting slogans – turned into scenes of brutality by the afternoon. Delhi policedenied the protesters the right to march on parliamentand kettled the expanding crowd into a restricted area. The internet was shut down and barricades were erected across the capital. The came the police batons and the teargas.Police said they were responding to agitation but organisers and witnesses claimed the police carried out unprovoked attacks, including against women and children in the crowd. About 60 suffered injuries and at least one protester is on a ventilator in hospital intensive care.View image in fullscreenAbhijeet Dipke, founder of the Cockroach Janta party, next to a portrait of hunger-striking activist Sonam Wangchuk during a protest in Delhi on Tuesday.Photograph: Shekhar Yadav/APView image in fullscreenAbhijeet Dipke at a march organised by the CJP on Monday demanding the resignation of Dharmendra Pradhan.Photograph: Kabir Jhangiani/NurPhoto/Shutterstock“The day was so wholesome to start with, there was so much love,” said Taru. “But after 3pm the police started attacking us. They were bashing us like anything. I saw them come up and hit people on the head as they were sitting down on the pavement doing nothing. And then as we were running, they opened up teargas on us.”Taru described the response by Delhi police – which are under central government control – as a “big mistake. Now we are more agitated than ever.”Cockroach movement supporters stage protests in cities across India – in picturesRead moreOn Tuesday, the heavy hand of the Delhi police did little to keep supporters away from the historical site of Jantar Mantar in Delhi, which has become the focal point of the CJP protests over the past month. The crowds were thick and lively as they shouted slogans against the Modi government with abandon, calling for both the education minister and the prime minister to resign.Some had travelled hundreds of miles to be there and said they intended to camp out until the government responded to the demands of the country’s millions of young people. “The government needs to understand: we are not going anywhere,” said Ranjit Verma, 23.India’s main opposition Congress party had also seized upon the mood, calling for Modi to resign “for destroying the future of India’s youth” as it held its own protest in Delhi on Tuesday.View image in fullscreenMembers of the Rapid Action Force deployed at the protest site near Jantar Mantar in New Delhi on Monday.Photograph: Jai Thakur/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenThe protest site near Jantar Mantar in New Delhi on Sunday.Photograph: Jai Thakur/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockWhile the CJP movement has primarily focused on accountability for devastating leaks of exam papers and calling for the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, the overriding feeling in the aftermath of Monday’s protest was that this was now so much bigger than one issue. For some, this was the moment India’s youth – totalling more than 600 million – had found its political voice.“The solidarity on the streets yesterday filled my heart,” said Kuhoo Kaushik, 26, a content creator from Delhi. “How the government responded was really sad, police beating up 14-year-olds is horrible. But personally, I feel like it’s really awoken something in me.“That’s why I’ve come back today: we won’t be moved until the government listens to us. It’s become about so much more than education – but one step at a time”’Speaking at a press conference on Tuesday, CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke vowed that the protests would continue, though did not state what the next steps for the movement would be to capitalise on their momentum.Standing by the stage at the protest site, Ratna Singh, a CJP spokesperson, said the movement was very aware of ways in which the Modi government had successfully cracked down on previous movements and figures of dissent during its 12 years in power.“Anyone who has tried to speak up against this government has faced terrorism charges or tax raids or jail and there is so much fear,” she said. “But I am very proud at how many people have showed up and come out on the streets. The police showed us no mercy – but no matter what barbaric treatment they give us, we will not be silenced.”While a senior government minister met two CJP spokespeople on Monday to discuss their demands, Singh said the government had not responded to them on Tuesday and the “prime minister has remained silent about what happened”.It was not only young people who returned to the protest site on Tuesday. Shobha Varma, 80, was attending with her granddaughter. “Our education system has been failing young people for years,” she said. “But when I look out at everyone here, I really feel hope.”Explore more on these topicsIndiaNarendra ModiProtestSouth and central AsianewsShareReuse this content

title: Rescuers in Afghanistan search for missing and dead after flash floods destroy homes | Afghanistan | The Guardian
description: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in eastern province of Nuristan
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in eastern province of Nuristan
Content: People inspect buildings damaged by heavy flooding in the eastern province of Nuristan, Afghanistan.Photograph: Wahidullah Kakar/APView image in fullscreenPeople inspect buildings damaged by heavy flooding in the eastern province of Nuristan, Afghanistan.Photograph: Wahidullah Kakar/APAfghanistanRescuers in Afghanistan search for missing and dead after flash floods destroy homesNational disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in eastern  province of NuristanAssociated Press  in KabulTue 21 Jul 2026 06.09 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRescue crews inAfghanistanare searching for survivors and bodies among the rubble of houses destroyed by devastating flash floods that authorities said had killed nearly two dozen people and left more than 100 people missing.The Afghanistan national disaster management authority said on Tuesday that 23 people had died, 80 had been injured and more than 100 were missing after floods struck the eastern province of Nuristan on Monday, causing an undetermined number of buildings to collapse. The provincial capital city of Parun was among the areas affected, authorities said.More extreme weather has been forecast, with heavy rain, thunderstorms, strong winds accompanied by dust and sand, and flash floods predicted in 10 of the country’s 34 provinces, the ministry of transport and civil aviation’s meteorology department said on Monday evening. The ministry warned people to avoid riverbanks and other areas at risk of flooding.View image in fullscreenResidents and rescue workers clear debris in Parun, Nuristan province.Photograph: EPAAfghanistan is highly vulnerable to extreme weather events, with snow and heavy rain that trigger flash floods, often killing dozens or even hundreds of people at a time. In 2024,more than 300 people died in springtime flash floods. Earlier this year, at least 110 people were killed by widespread flooding and landslides in several parts of the country.Decades of conflict, along with poor infrastructure, a struggling economy, deforestation and the intensifying effects of climate breakdown have amplified the impact of such disasters, particularly in remote areas where many homes are built of mud and offer limited protection against sudden deluges or heavy snowfall.Explore more on these topicsAfghanistanClimate crisisSouth and central AsiaFloodingExtreme weathernewsShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham urges cabinet to examine ‘all possible ways’ to tackle cost of living crisis | Politics | The Guardian
description: As new prime minister briefs cabinet on ‘cost of living government’, critics ask where money will come from
Author: Kiran Stacey
Headline: As new prime minister briefs cabinet on ‘cost of living government’, critics ask where money will come from
Content: Andy Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for rolling out a national care service.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/ReutersView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for rolling out a national care service.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/ReutersPoliticsAndy Burnham urges cabinet to examine ‘all possible ways’ to tackle cost of living crisisAs new prime minister briefs cabinet on ‘cost of living government’, critics ask where money will come fromKiran StaceyandPeter WalkerTue 21 Jul 2026 15.53 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham is under mounting pressure to explain how he will pay for his policy pledges as he implored his new cabinet to examine “all possible ways” to tackle the cost of living crisis.On his first full day in power, the prime minister faced questions about how he would fund the removal of VAT from electricity bills and an expected uplift in defence spending – an issue that led his new chancellor, John Healey, to resign as Keir Starmer’s defence secretary last month.Sources say measures to ease the cost of living will include reducing the national bus fare cap by a third, from £3 to £2, with the prime minister set to announce the move on Wednesday.The Guardian has also learned Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for implementing his promise to roll out a national care service, a move that could cost further money before the election.Gathering his cabinet in Downing Street for the first time, the prime minister told ministers his would be a “cost of living government”, adding that his priority would be to make life cheaper for voters – albeit within existing borrowing rules.“What can we do just to take that little bit of pressure off people’s shoulders, just to give them that little extra sense that help is coming so that they can have that bit of hope that things are getting better?” he asked colleagues. “And that’s the mission that we’ve got to set ourselves together.”He asked his colleagues to look into what their department could do to ease cost of living pressures, both “big and small”. “We need to be a cost of living government, getting that cost of living down, looking at all possible ways of doing that.”Burnham began the process on Tuesday morning with an announcement that VAT would be removed from electricity bills from 1 October, reducing the electricity price cap by £45 at a total cost of £850m next year.No 10 has ruled out using defence bonds to pay for higher military spending.The move came alongside major changes to Whitehall, which will see the creation of a powerful new Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet and hand responsibility for the UK’s growth strategy to the new No 10 North, which will be overseen by Louise Haigh, Burnham’s de facto deputy prime minister.View image in fullscreenDarren Jones, who was in charge of the now scrapped ID policy, said money for that scheme had not yet been identified.Photograph: Thomas Krych/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockBurnham’s promise to pay for the VAT cut by scrapping the government’s digital ID scheme was immediately called into question, however, by Darren Jones, the minister in charge of the policy until recently, who said the money for that scheme had not yet been identified.“The DigitalID program was unfunded,” he posted on X on Tuesday. “The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget.”Downing Street said on Tuesday that the tax cut was funded for the next financial year, but money would have to be found in future years from existing departmental budgets, suggesting spending cuts to come. No 10 has also ruled out using defence bonds to pay for higher military spending.The intervention from Jones came amid wider disquiet among allies of Starmer over Burnham’s pledge to end factional infighting, after more than half of the outgoing prime minister’s cabinet were removed or demoted in Monday’s reshuffle.One outgoing official said: “It’s less a purge of Starmerites than getting rid of people who were loyal to aLabourleader, while promoting people who were serially disloyal. That’s a dangerous message to send your MPs.”Burnham is set to continue making ministerial appointments this week, including giving the Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar a seat in the Lords and a job in the business department. Sarwar was the first major Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer to resign as prime minister earlier this year.Healey is looking at longer-term plans to cut the cost of living as part of a budget to be delivered later this year. A spokesperson for the prime minister added he had also ruled out using higher borrowing to pay for defence spending – something previously advocated within government by Healey. “It is not something we’re looking at,” they said.Sources have told the Guardian Burnham is now working on a major social care announcement in the coming weeks as he looks to begin laying the groundwork for a social care service before the election.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThis would involve bringing forward the review of social care by Louise Casey, who is due to report by 2028, but is now likely to do so as soon as next year. Those consulted on the policy say Burnham will give Casey scope to work up a range of options, including a new national service funded by taxes or a social insurance model that could see social care costs capped for a limited period of time.They admit, however, that any funding model is likely to trigger a political backlash with previous proposals having been abandoned amid a storm of controversy about the prospects of a new “death tax” being used to pay for them.View image in fullscreenJohn Healey is looking at longer-term plans to cut the cost of living as part of a budget to be delivered later this year.Photograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockBurnham told reporters this week: “I am ready to use some of my political capital on this issue … I would not want to leave office having done what Westminster has done, not just for 15 years – actually, I think you’re getting close to 30 years.“It’s not defensible. How many people have lost their homes and their savings in that time? We need to face it and fix it.”A spokesperson said the announcement would come “in due course”.Burnham admitted on Tuesday that he would face financial constraints as he looks to both ease the cost of living for voters and implement lasting social reforms. “We have got to show fiscal discipline, we have got to show our commitment to the fiscal rules is real,” he told his cabinet.As a result, allies say he has abandoned a nascent plan to raise the threshold at which workers start paying income taxe, which has been frozen for several years.The prime minister told the Times this week the issue had come up more than any other during the Makerfield byelection, but he is now understood to have dropped expensive plans to reverse the policy.This could have involved increasing the top rate of income tax to pay for it, but Burnham’s advisers believe this could be seen as a violation of Labour’s manifesto pledge not to raise income tax, national insurance or VAT.Burnham will spend the end of the week in Manchester, with the government encouraging civil servants working on growth to move to the city. A spokesperson said there would be relocation packages on offer for anyone choosing to do so.Explore more on these topicsPoliticsAndy BurnhamLabournewsShareReuse this content

title: Anas Sarwar reportedly set to quit as Scottish Labour leader to be minister in Andy Burnham’s government – as it happened  | Politics | The Guardian
description: Sarwar expected to be elevated to Lords to take up a ministerial post at revamped Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade
Author: Yohannes Lowe
Headline: Sarwar expected to be elevated to Lords to take up a ministerial post at revamped Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade
Content: 15.12 EDTAnas Sarwar is set to quit as Scottish Labour leader to be a minister in Andy Burnham’s government, according to reports.Sarwar is expected to be elevated to the Lords to take up a ministerial post at the revamped Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade, headed by Jonathan Reynolds, according to media including the Scottish Sun.Share

title: Former Met police chief admits surveillance of Stephen Lawrence’s family was institutionally racist | Undercover police and policing | The Guardian
description: Paul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abuses
Author: Rob Evans
Headline: Paul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abuses
Content: Paul Condon, pictured in 2008, said officers in the unit ‘were told daily as their job to lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life and what they’re asked to do’.Photograph: Luke MacGregor/ReutersView image in fullscreenPaul Condon, pictured in 2008, said officers in the unit ‘were told daily as their job to lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life and what they’re asked to do’.Photograph: Luke MacGregor/ReutersSpycops scandalUndercover police and policingFormer Met police chief admits surveillance of Stephen Lawrence’s family was institutionally racistPaul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abusesRob EvansTue 21 Jul 2026 15.02 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA formerMetropolitan policecommissioner has admitted that the surveillance of the family of Stephen Lawrence by undercover officers was institutionally racist, the spycops inquiry has heard.While Paul Condon was in command of the Metropolitan police in the 1990s, a controversial undercover unit spied on the family as they sought to persuade Scotland Yard to properly investigate Lawrence’s racist murder.The officers reported information about the separation of Lawrence’s parents, Doreen and Neville, to their superiors.Giving evidence on Tuesday, Condon said the covert monitoring of the Lawrences and their campaign “meets very well the parameters of institutional racism”.The failure to rigorously investigate the 1993 murder was one of the biggest and most contentious issues he had to handle, and caused enormous damage to the reputation of the UK’s biggest police force.Speaking more widely about the undercover unit, Condon also said the Metropolitan police had failed over decades to control the special demonstration squad (SDS), which committed “horrendous” abuses.He said he was appalled by the wrongdoing of the unit, which spent 40 years spying on tens of thousands of political activists. He told the inquiry there were “rogue officers who’ve done some bad things” but that the greater fault lay with the Met’s systemic failure to supervise the unit rigorously.He said the undercover officers were told daily to “lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life”, but added that the Met should have realised the covert work “can be corrupting” if strong rules were not in place.Condon, who became a member of the House of Lords after his police career, is the highest-ranked officer to have given evidence to the long-running public inquiry, which is headed by the retired judge Sir John Mitting.View image in fullscreenDoreen and Neville Lawrence, seen here in 1995, were covertly monitored as they campaigned for Scotland Yard to properly investigate their son’s murder.Photograph: PA Images/AlamyThe inquiry was set up after the Guardian revealed how the SDS spied on the Lawrences and their supporters in the 1990s.Mitting is examining wide-ranging misconduct by the undercover officers. This includes how they monitored grieving parents while theysought justice for relativesor campaigned to expose police malpractice,regularly deceived women into long-term relationshipsand even fathered children, stolethe identities of dead childrenandlied in court.The SDS undercover officers adopted false identities to infiltrate mainly leftwing groups between 1968 and 2008 in deployments that typically lasted four years.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWho were the undercover cops who deceived women into sexual relations?Read moreAt the outset of his testimony, Condon said he wanted to “personally apologise for every single person who has been damaged by the actions of officers in the special demonstration squad over the decades, and I am appalled by so much of what I have learned throughout this inquiry and I sincerely apologise to every single person who has been damaged in this process”.He said there were “horrendous things that went wrong” in the SDS and “appalling behaviour by individual officers”.
