He added: “Equally, I think the greater fault lies with the Metropolitan police as a whole over decades, that these officers were told daily as their job to lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life and what they’re asked to do.”“The Metropolitan police should have realised that without limits, without ethical guidance, that this work in those circumstances can be corrupting and even someone with a strong moral compass will give in to baser instincts.”However, Condon said he knew nothing of the abuses when he was in charge of the Met between 1993 and 2000. He said it was “unrealistic” to suggest that, as the head of the Met, he would have been aware of the day-to-day running of a small and secretive unit.Condon said he “never at any time commissioned, encouraged, authorised, acquiesced or was aware of any” of the surveillance that was carried out on the Lawrences and their supporters.Asked by David Barr, the inquiry’s chief lawyer, if he would accept that the surveillance was “an expression” of institutional racism, Condon replied: “Yes.”An inquiry by Sir William Macpherson into the killing said in 1999 that institutional racism within the Met was one of the reasons thatallowed Lawrence’s murderers to escape justice.Police leaders accepted the findings, then laterclaimed to have reformed the serviceto the extent that it no longer applied.Get in touchContact us about this storyThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDropIf you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Show moreExplore more on these topicsUndercover police and policingSpycops scandalMetropolitan policeLondonPoliceRacenewsShareReuse this content

title: Fiscal hawk or big spender – what kind of chancellor will John Healey be? | John Healey | The Guardian
description: Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promises
Author: Kiran Stacey
Headline: Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promises
Content: John Healey strides out for his first cabinet meeting as chancellor on Tuesday.  He has largely avoided being aligned with any faction.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/ReutersView image in fullscreenJohn Healey strides out for his first cabinet meeting as chancellor on Tuesday.  He has largely avoided being aligned with any faction.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/ReutersJohn HealeyProfileFiscal hawk or big spender – what kind of chancellor will John Healey be?Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promisesKiran StaceyPolicy editorTue 21 Jul 2026 13.58 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOn the frequent occasions thatJohn Healeywould travel abroad as defence secretary, he was so determined to keep working he would get the crew to tether a desk to the inside of his military aircraft so he could read his papers.His diligence is one reason why Andy Burnham, the new prime minister, made Healeyhis unexpected pickfor chancellor on Monday. Allies say others include their shared commitment to re-industrialisation and devolution.After an occasionallyvicious briefing warbetween allies of other potential candidates, the hardworking Healey is seen as a safe choice who can unite a fractured party while not scaring the markets.But many who know him say the new chancellor is more ideologically driven than he may seem, and that Burnham will come unstuck if he treats his most senior minister as little more than a factotum.“This is being billed as equivalent to Gordon Brown appointing Alistair Darling,” said oneLabourofficial, referring to the former prime minister’s choice of chancellor, who was initially seen as someone who would simply do the bidding of No 10.“But it turned out Darling had views of his own whichdid not always chimewith Brown’s – Andy has to avoid making the same mistake.”The decision to appoint Healey was arguably the most important Burnham made on his first day in Downing Street. The new chancellor will be tasked with finding money for the prime minister’s various policy promises, including higher defence spending, taking utilities into public control and finding ways to cut voters’ living costs.Burnham has promised not to raise income tax, VAT or national insurance, and to stick to the last government’s borrowing rules, placing his chancellor in the same straitjacket that hampered his predecessor, Rachel Reeves.View image in fullscreenAs defence secretary, Healey earned a reputation as a strong ally of the armed forces.Photograph: Leon Neal/AFP/Getty ImagesHealey is in an almost unique position to take the tough decisions required while keeping Labour MPs onboard. Since entering parliament in 1997, he has largely avoided becoming attached to one faction or another.Although he served in the Treasury for five years while Brown was chancellor, he was always respected by the opposing Blairite camp. More recently, he has successfully bridged the widening gap between allies of the former prime minister Keir Starmer and those on the soft left agitating for his removal.Part of Healey’s ability to appeal to different wings of the party comes from his personal warmth. He will often involve relatively junior members of staff in policy and political discussions and, like his new boss in No 10, has an ability to remember names and details about people’s lives.David Walker, the bishop of Manchester, was one of the local Labour members who voted for him to be the Labour candidate for the seat of Wentworth in 1997 – over other rising stars such as Yvette Cooper – now health secretary.“One of the things that impressed us at the time was that John was committed to living in the community and putting down roots here,” said Walker. “He always made sure he turned up to community events and showed his face, even when he was busy with national politics.”Luke Pollard, his former ministerial colleague at the Ministry of Defence, called Healey “genuinely the finest and most decent man I’ve worked with since being elected”.View image in fullscreenJohn Healey addressing new Treasury colleagues on Tuesday. He is known for remembering details about people and their lives.Photograph: Kirsty O’Connor/TreasuryAs defence secretary, Healey earned a reputation as a strong ally of the armed forces, putting the repair of dilapidated military housing at the top of his list of priorities. To some in government however, this closeness to the military was a weakness.When he asked the Treasury for an extra £28bn to fund the defence investment plan (Dip), just months after negotiating a multi-year budget, Reeves was infuriated.Her allies said Healey had been hoodwinked by service chiefs into believing that last year’s strategic defence review was fully funded, only to have to go back and ask for more when it came to thrashing out the detail.Those close to the chancellor, however, say he needed more money for his budget because of the additional geopolitical commitments Starmer had made in the months after the defence review, including playing peacekeeping roles in Ukraine and the Gulf.Healey’s shock resignation over defence plan pushes Starmer to brinkRead moreOne former government official said the row showed that far from being a fiscal hawk, as some have claimed, Healey would come down on the side of higher spending when presented with a policy dilemma.“He is definitely not a fiscal hawk or even a firm hand in the tiller as he’s being portrayed by some,” they said. “He is a big spender.”In the end,Healey resignedin protest just days before the Dip, which some of Starmer’s allies believe was part of a deal with Burnham to become his chancellor.Wes Streeting, Healey’s replacement as defence secretary, suggested additional money was also likely to be found from cuts elsewhere. “We understand the sacrifices that involves from other government departments,” he told a press conference during his first day in the job.The chancellor will now have to do the kind of adjudicating between departments that could, for the first time, make him unpopular with large parts of the parliamentary Labour party.The question is whether, when faced with a minister in sudden need of extra cash, as he himself once was, he loosens the purse strings or sticks to the discipline of his predecessor.Explore more on these topicsJohn HealeyEconomic policyAndy BurnhamLabourTax and spendingMilitarynewsShareReuse this content

title: Starmer allies see ‘hint of retribution’ in Burnham reshuffle | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: Former PM’s supporters question pledge to end factional infighting after more than half his cabinet removed or demoted
Author: Peter Walker
Headline: Former PM’s supporters question pledge to end factional infighting after more than half his cabinet removed or demoted
Content: Andy Burnham at the first meeting of his new cabinet.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/Daily Telegraph/PAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham at the first meeting of his new cabinet.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/Daily Telegraph/PAAndy BurnhamStarmer allies see ‘hint of retribution’ in Burnham reshuffleFormer PM’s supporters question pledge to end factional infighting after more than half his cabinet removed or demotedPeter WalkerSenior political correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 13.40 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAllies of Keir Starmer have questioned Andy Burnham’s pledge toend factional infightingafterhe removed or demoted more than half of the outgoing prime minister’s cabinet in his first reshuffle.“This was a deeply factional cabinet reshuffle,” one said. “The idea it was anything else is ridiculous.”Another former official said: “Becoming leader of a party involves having to promise things to a lot of different people, and you can see that in some appointments. What was surprising was the decision to get rid of so many impressive people.”Andy Burnham’s first Labour cabinet: who’s in and who’s out?Read moreOthers were more phlegmatic, saying it was obvious that a new PM would want to bring in his own people and that the change was handled sensitively, with outgoing ministers being called directly by Burnham.Their special advisers – who usually go when their boss does – have also been given a chance to discuss their future with Alison Phillips, Burnham’s deputy chief of staff.The overall mood for the outgoing team has tended to be more reflective than bitter, after they had a month to digestStarmer’s decisionto stand down, paving the way for a Burnham succession.Starmer joined Rachel Reeves, his former chancellor, and a series of outgoing No 10 officials at a north London pub on Monday evening. One person characterised the mood as “jovial, but with more than a hint of anger”.Another source said the event was the culmination of a highly emotional period for a group of colleagues who were “a family, bonded through fire”. They added: “It feels really hard. But there is a sense of handing over the country in a better place.”Several allies of Starmer have said that, while the reshuffle was inevitable, there was “more than a hint of retribution” in some of the sackings, as well as concern that ministers were targeted purely for having been loyal.Some pointed to the treatment of Nick Thomas-Symonds, the sacked Cabinet Office minister, and Bridget Phillipson, who lost her education brief but remains equalities minister. Both were seen as quietly loyal and diligent.In contrast, they said, the likes of Wes Streeting and John Healey, who undermined Starmer by resigning, were rewarded with senior posts.One outgoing official said: “It’s less a purge of Starmerites than getting rid of people who were loyal to aLabourleader, while promoting people who were serially disloyal. That’s a dangerous message to send your MPs.”Another source said: “It feels tough on people like Nick, who have always done things in a very grown-up way. The way it has been handled is not fantastic.”Other concerns included a sense that more senior male No 10 staff than female staff had survived in the new regime. Just one of the four great offices of state is now held by a woman, down from three.There is, nonetheless, an acceptance that politics is tough. As one source put it: “You sign up to this knowing it’s not a job for life, and that it will come to an end. But it’s still a privilege. Labour governments don’t come around very often.”Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamKeir StarmerLabournewsShareReuse this content

title: Mistrial declared in case over Sacramento’s deadliest shooting | California | The Guardian
description: Mtula Payton and Dandrae Martin were tried together over shooting on K Street in April 2022 that left six dead
Author: Abené Clayton
Headline: Mtula Payton and Dandrae Martin were tried together over shooting on K Street in April 2022 that left six dead
Content: A memorial for the people killed in the mass shooting in Sacramento in 2022.Photograph: Rich Pedroncelli/APView image in fullscreenA memorial for the people killed in the mass shooting in Sacramento in 2022.Photograph: Rich Pedroncelli/APGuns and liesCaliforniaMistrial declared in case over Sacramento’s deadliest shootingMtula Payton and Dandrae Martin were tried together over shooting on K Street in April 2022 that left six deadSupported byAbout this contentAbené ClaytonTue 21 Jul 2026 19.33 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 18.05 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe trial of two menaccused of carrying out the deadliest shooting in the city of Sacramento,California, ended in a mistrial on Tuesday.The suspects, Mtula Payton and Dandrae Martin, were tried together and faced up to 15 years to life in prison for their role in the shooting, which killed six people and injured at least a dozen. While they were not convicted for the deaths, theywere found guiltyof being in possession of firearms despite having prior felonies.The jury could not come to a consensus on the murder charges. Now it will be up to Sacramento county’s prosecutors to decide whether they will pursue a retrial for the murder charges.The trial, which lasted more than two months, included dozens of hours of footage of the shooting and testimony from responding officers, witnesses of the shooting, and the two men prosecutors allege planned the gun battle against rival gang members.Taking place just blocks from the state’s capitol, the 2022 shooting rocked Sacramento. And it was one of the few events of its kind that was the subject of a major trial. Gang-related shootings rarely command sustained attention, said Leia Schenk, a victims’ advocate in Sacramento, who is supporting three of families who lost loved ones. But the shooting’s lethality, and its high-profile location, meant it attracted media from all over the world, she said.In the early morning hours of 3 April 2022, the sound of rapid gunshots from multiple directions broke through the rumblings of hundreds of people pouring out of bars and clubs that line K Street in Sacramento. Milling toward parked cars and ride-shares quickly turned into sprinting and ducking as people ran for their lives and tried to shield themselves from bullets.View image in fullscreenDefense attorney Linda Parisi listens as Reid Kingsbury argues on behalf of defendants Dandrae Martin and Mtula Payton.Photograph: Paul Kitagaki Jr/Zuma/ShutterstockBy the end of the chaotic scene, six people were dead. Three were women who were bystanders: Johntaya Alexander, 21; Melinda Davis, 57; Yamile Martinez-Andrade, 21. The others were three men who were later alleged to have been a part of the conflict that led to the melee: Sergio Harris, 38; Joshua Hoye-Lucchesi, 32; and DeVazia Turner, 29.Martin and his brother Smiley Martin were arrested days after the shooting, and Payton was found by police in Las Vegas and arrested in late May 2022. All three werecharged with murder. In 2024, Smiley Martin wasfound dead in his cellat the Sacramento county main jail from a methadone overdose, a coroner later determined.As the investigation continued, investigators argued that the shooting was the result of a conflict between rival groups that climaxed with the shooting. Prosecutors argued that Martin and Payton went to K Street to kill their rivals, while the defense argued that the pair fired shots in self-defense and out of fear from the unexpected bursts of gunfire. And while the pair were both armed that night, their defense argued that neither planned to shoot anyone nor were looking to settle any gang conflict.Initially, the pair were charged with first-degree murder. But prosecutors later downgraded the charges to second-degree murder after the jurors found themselves deadlocked, theSacramento Bee reported.During the trial, Payton testified about his life as a member of a local gang, which he joined when he was 11, and claimed that he was afraid for his life at the time of the shooting. “Everybody was running in the same direction as me. People were getting shot around me. I was scared,” Paytontold his attorneyduring his testimony.Martin claimed that he was never affiliated with a gang, but that his deceased brother was.Since opening arguments in the trial began in late April, there have been scores of witnesses testifying to what they saw the night of the shooting and tense moments in and outside of the courtroom as the families of the victims showed surveillance footage of people firing guns while bystanders ran for cover.The jury also took a two-week break in early July, adding to the protracted nature of this trial , which made the process of grieving that much harder for the families of the deceased, Schenk said.“Ultimately, justice would be to have their loved ones back. But there’s no such thing as justice in a case like this,” Schenk said in a post-verdict press conference outside the courthouse. “Now we’re starting all over again. So the families can’t begin to heal because every time they come here it’s ripping the Band-Aid off.”If a new trial were to begin, Schenk added that she hoped to see the district attorney’s office offer compensation for the time the victims’ families had to take from work and money spent on gas as they commuted to and from court.Explore more on these topicsCaliforniaGuns and liesWest CoastnewsShareReuse this content

title: Donald Trump to impose 50% tariff on most Canadian goods, White House says | Trump tariffs | The Guardian
description: Turmoil likely as Trump officials say Canada unfairly discriminated against US autos, alcohol and dairy products
Author: Mollie Reilly
Headline: Turmoil likely as Trump officials say Canada unfairly discriminated against US autos, alcohol and dairy products
Content: Donald Trump at the White House in Washington DC on 14 July 2026.Photograph: Graeme Sloan/Pool/Graeme Sloan - Pool/CNP/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenDonald Trump at the White House in Washington DC on 14 July 2026.Photograph: Graeme Sloan/Pool/Graeme Sloan - Pool/CNP/ShutterstockTrump tariffsDonald Trump to impose 50% tariff on most Canadian goods, White House saysTurmoil likely as Trump officials say Canada unfairly discriminated against US autos, alcohol and dairy productsMollie Reillyand agencyMon 20 Jul 2026 22.29 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trumpis imposing 50% tariffs on mostCanadiangoods in response to the country retaliating against previous US tariffs, the White House announced on Monday, declaring Canada has unfairly discriminated against American cars, alcohol and dairy products.The tariffs will hit a wide range of products, the White House said, including wine, hockey sticks and cement. They also include goods previously protected from import taxes under the United States-Mexico-Canada (USMCA) agreement.Thenew tariffswill exclude energy products, fish, critical minerals and potash. They also exclude products already subject to tariffs aimed at protecting national security, such as steel and aluminum.The steep tariffs will probablyunleash a new wave of economic chaos, with risks of higher inflation and further fraying of relations between two nations that had been closely woven together before Trump’s return to the White House.The Canadian prime minister,Mark Carney, said in a statement that his government had made comprehensive proposals ‌to resolve trade disputes with Washington, asserting that Trump’s past tariffs violated ‌a trade pact between the two countries.“This trade dispute has raised costs for families, particularly in the US,” he said. “Canada stands ready to engage intensively to address outstanding issues with the US to the mutual benefit of our citizens.”The White House said the tariffs will go into effect in 30 days, leaving time for possible negotiations between the two countries.Brazil vows to retaliate if US imposes 25% tariffs on some of its productsRead moreTrumpsignedthree proclamations to launch the tariffs under Section 338 of the 1930 Trade Act. Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section because they said Trump could use it to destabilize the economy.A White House fact sheet claims Trump is “taking action to hold Canada accountable for its continued discrimination against and unreasonable and unequal treatment of U.S. commerce that has burdened and disadvantaged hardworking Americans”.Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American automobiles, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after the US president imposed tariffs on Canada under the pretext that it should do more to stop fentanyl smuggling into the US.Trump noted in his autos proclamation that Canada maintained, starting in April 2025, a 25% tariff on the imports of US motor vehicles that did not qualify for preferential treatment under the USMCA.The White House also cited all but two Canadian provinces and territories stopping the sale of US alcoholic beverages, while not imposing similar restrictions on other countries.Trump also objected to Canada’s treatment of US cheese, saying in his proclamation that Canada discriminates against the US compared with Europe on dairy products.Trump had threatened last week to impose tariffs due to smoke from devastatingwildfiresin Canadadriftingacross large portions of the US.“We are holding Canada responsible for the fact that they are not properly maintaining their Forests, and Brush therein, and the United States is being unnecessarily invaded by filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air, the quality of which is dangerous, and totally unacceptable!” hewroteon Truth Social.TrumpwatchedSunday’s World Cup final with Mark Carney, the Canadian prime minister who has previously pushed back on the US president and won the premiership last year on a platform of promising to stand up for Canada.After the match, Trump told reporters he had raised the issue of wildfire smoke with Carney.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Around 2-3 times a daySign up toBreaking News USGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“I told him, I mean, ‘You got to stop these fires from coming in and, you know, poisoning our air. Our air has been poisoned,’” Trump said of their conversation.US refunds $81bn in Trump tariffs after supreme court ruled them illegalRead moreA White House official who previewed the new tariffs to reporters said Trump had requested aides explore additional tariffs due to Canadian wildfires harming US air quality, the Associated Press reported.The Canadian federal government did not immediately comment on Trump’s action, but Doug Ford, the Ontario premier, said he would “never stop fighting to protect Ontario”.“If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,” Fordposted onsocial media.Candace Laing, the CEO of the Canadian chamber of commerce, said the Trump administration’s moves were “regrettable” but the two countries needed to use the 30-day window before the tariffs start “to make meaningful progress in advancing formal talks”.The new taxes on Canadian imports could carry serious political and economic risks for Trump ahead of the November midterm elections for control of Congress. His so-called “liberation day” tariffs last year in Aprilprovokeda financial market meltdown over concerns about inflation and a recession, prompting him to walk back the rates for a period of negotiation.In a 6-3 decision in February, the supreme courtruledthat Trump had illegally used emergency executive power to impose his global tariffs, causing the administration to find alternative ways to raise import taxes based on a series of legal authorities.The US has been forced topay back$81bn in tariffs so far this fiscal year.The Associated Press contributed reportingExplore more on these topicsTrump tariffsDonald TrumpCanadaTariffsAmericasUS economyCanada wildfiresnewsShareReuse this content

title: Social Darwinists to tech bros: tracing the long fight against equality in US | Books | The Guardian
description: Historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter Thiel
Author: Martin Pengelly
Headline: Historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter Thiel
Content: Peter Thiel and Elon Musk in 2000.Photograph: Paul Sakuma/Associated PressView image in fullscreenPeter Thiel and Elon Musk in 2000.Photograph: Paul Sakuma/Associated PressBooksSocial Darwinists to tech bros: tracing the long fight against equality in USHistorian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter ThielMartin PengellyTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.43 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s new book is published amid celebrations of the250th anniversaryof the declaration of independence and its truth “self-evident, that all men are created equal”. Country of Lords, however, is a history of Neo-Aristocrats, Social Darwinists, Tech Utopians, and the Long FightAgainstEquality in America.Concise and hard-hitting, the book arrives at a time of glaring social andeconomicinequality, Phillips-Fein seeingElon Musk“emerging as a trillionairevery much on people’s minds, with a deep level of discomfort, both fear and anger about what this portends”.‘People are picking the dumbest fights’: the tortured history of America’s culture warsRead more“It’s always hard to know what’s going to be happening when you have written a book,” she said. “But I think those questions about economic equality and the impact of extreme levels of economic hierarchy and concentration, and the questions, ‘Can you still have a democracy any longer? What are the ways that you can counter that? What alternate visions are there if you want to live in a world that’s not characterized that way?’, are present.”Phillips-Fein studies conservatism and “questions about capitalism and democracy”. Her first book,Invisible Hands, was a study of businessmen who fought the New Deal of the 1930s. Her second, Fear City, about the New York economic crisis of the 1970s, was afinalist for the Pulitzer prize. Hers is a major voice. It’s also unique. Not many books start with founder John Adams and end with Peter Thiel of Palantir, Musk’sfellow techno-fascist. Might such company seem unfair on Adams, the Boston lawyer who was part of a great revolution but feared what was unleashed?“I hope this comes through,” Phillips-Fein said. “Adams is very different from [steel magnate] Andrew Carnegie and [Yale professor]William Graham Sumnerand the late 19th-century social Darwinists, and the pro-slavery advocates. He’s also definitely very different from people who are saying extreme levels of economic inequality are good.” Adams, however, was “very ambivalent and skeptical of egalitarian claims … during and after the French Revolution: a deep sense of you can have too much egalitarianism.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: WW Norton & Company“In his writing, he does also give a strong kind of conservative temperament and foundational sense of the problems of arguing for greater equality and a deep wariness and sense of pessimism about a politics that claims to do that.”And so Adams takes his place in Country of Lords. Other subjects include preachers of race-based inequality from George Fitzhugh, a Confederate theorist of slavery as natural good, toTheodore Lothrop Stoddard, a hack journalist who had a huge bestseller in 1920 with The Rising Tide of Color Against White World-Supremacy, “part of a larger intellectual subculture of ‘scientific’ racism … in the aftermath of world war one”. There is also Carnegie, the Scottish-born tycoon who flourished in the Gilded Age of the late 19th century.“What makes Carnegie so interesting is that he at least allows himself to have some sense of ambivalence,” Phillips-Fein said. “He says that while the concentration of wealth is absolutely necessary and inevitable, and the people at the top … should have the ultimate say over resources, he is deeply aware that this is politically dangerous, that … it creates an unstable society in which the poor and the working class might actually hate the wealthy, and that’s not in the interest of the wealthy in the long run. Also, he has a sense that human brotherhood is lost, or some kind of community that had existed.”Carnegie’s father was a Chartist, seeking working-class rights. Carnegie’s major outlet for doubts about his wealth came through philanthropy, prominently the construction oflibrariesfor working people.“He had a very deep sense of paternalism,” Phillips-Fein said. “He was very invested in the idea of business as a romance and kind of a space of adventure and conquest and feudal benevolence. I think Carnegie’s answer was ultimately that the wealthy should have their money but they shouldn’t bequeath much to their descendants, they should invest it in massive philanthropic projects, and this was better than raising wages or other things like that … It is his level of guilt and ambivalence that I think does make him appealing, if also troubling. I think it raises a set of questions that he certainly was never really able to resolve.”The 1892 Carnegie Steel strike and its violent suppression devastated Homestead, Pennsylvania, and left Carnegie disillusioned. Now, in Washington, Carnegie Science is the Qatari embassy. At Mount Vernon, the Carnegie library hosts an Apple Store.Country of Lords is also a primer on modern inequality. In the early 1900s,Henry Fordmixed mass auto production with vicious racism. In mid-century,William Shockleywas a proto-tech mogul “who went completely off the rails”.View image in fullscreenAndrew Carnegie.Photograph: AP“He was a co-inventor of the transistor and he figures in different histories of Silicon Valley because he ran this company and wanted to maintain a tremendous amount of control, and as a result a bunch of the employees quit and founded their own firm [twoeventuallyfounding Intel] … So after Shockley Labs fell apart, Shockley started to become drawn to racist science, pseudoscience and IQ testing in particular. I don’t think anybody really knows exactly what triggered this, but he starts to talk a lot about it publicly in the mid-60s. He was at Stanford at the time. He was supposed to be teaching physics and engineering.”Shockley, Phillips-Fein says, was really “a uniquely horrible person, and probably quite unhappy as well.” If that sounds familiar, also like Musk, Shockley wasn’t unique. Linked to the meritocratic movement of the mid-century, he was “not isolated. He is actually part of what is a growing, troubling community of people who coalesce in the late 60s, early 70s, and who are really responding, not always directly, to the context of the civil rights movement.“It was obviously focused on overturning racist legislation that undergirded segregation, but it also made claims about the distribution of resources, saying the reasons for Black poverty are not innate, they reflect centuries of legal and political structures that have upheld white wealth and laid the foundation for exploitation of Black workers and working-class people, and the political disfranchisement has resulted in this distribution of resources. In other words, that’s nothing to do with merit at all.”In such soil, Phillips-Fein finds the roots of “the tech titans of today”, rejecting liberal ideals.“They are very skeptical and hostile towards elite institutions, or at least, have a very broad relationship to them,” she said. “So Peter Thiel embraces this set of ideas about competition drawn from theeconomist Joseph Schumpeter. He writes about how companies in a normal marketplace are competing at the margins … but every so often someone will come along and create a new invention, usually that winds up dominating the marketplace and changing it, and whoever does that first will completely change the marketplace.“Thiel has the same idea. He kind of writes about how, ‘I was growing up, I wanted to be the best, I wanted to go to these elite institutions. I went to law school, I had the straight and narrow path, but then I realized that all that was wrong, and actually you had to disrupt everything, create your own monopoly, a monopoly of one: nobody else can ever be you,’ and then the people who are kind of blessed with this genius to recognize how to create something completely new in the world, those are the people who will deserve to win in this race and deserve to command the resources of society.”The Country of Lords endures.Country of Lords, published by WW Norton & Company, isout nowExplore more on these topicsBooksHistory booksPeter ThielElon MuskUS politicsUS income inequalityfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Evenepoel pips Pogacar again as Tour de France night-time doping test row deepens | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: Remco Evenepoel took back-to-back stage wins in the Tour de France, and eroded some of Tadej Pogacar’s overall lead, with victory in the stage 16 time trial from Evian to Thonon-les-Bains
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: Remco Evenepoel took back-to-back stage wins in the Tour de France, and eroded some of Tadej Pogacar’s overall lead, with victory in the stage 16 time trial from Evian to Thonon-les-Bains
Content: Remco Evenepoel enjoys the view from the podium after his stage 16 Tour win.Photograph: Pete Goding/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenRemco Evenepoel enjoys the view from the podium after his stage 16 Tour win.Photograph: Pete Goding/ShutterstockTour de France 2026Evenepoel pips Pogacar again as Tour de France night-time doping test row deepensBelgian wins time trial, says chase of leader ‘not over yet’Florian Lipowitz, third last year, quits race after crashingJeremy Whittlein Thonon-les-BainsTue 21 Jul 2026 15.19 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRemco Evenepoel took back-to-back stage wins in theTour de France, and eroded some of Tadej Pogacar’s overall lead, with victory in the stage 16 time trial from Evian to Thonon-les-Bains.The Olympic road and time trial champion, now second overall in the Tour, won on the Belgian national day from Pogacar by 28 seconds, to reduce the Slovenian’s advantage to just over four and a half minutes. It was the third Tour time trial win of his career.Tour de France 2026: Evenepoel wins stage 16 time trial but Lipowitz crashes out – liveRead more“It’s been a while since I’ve been on this level so it feels amazing to be in this good shape in the Tour,” Evene­poel said. The Belgian, who described Pogacar as a “superstar”, added: “It’s not over yet. There are still some hard stages to come.”Pogacar’s UAE Emirates XRG teammate, Isaac del Toro, remains in third place, but Paul Seixas is now just 20 seconds away from a podium position after taking time on the Mexican rider in the race against the clock.Evenepoel’s success was tempered by the loss of his Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe teammate, Florian Lipowitz, third in the Tour last year, who quit the race after crashing.Lipowitz fell on a tight right-hand bend as he exited a downhill section. He was carried away from the scene on a stretcher and it was later confirmed he suffered a broken collarbone. He was the second 2025 podium finisher – after Jonas Vingegaard on Sunday – to leave the race in 72 hours.While the race continued, the debate over the ITA drug testing agency’s nocturnal activities, which involved 2am and 5am visits to Vingegaard and Pogacar respectively last Sunday, continued.View image in fullscreenTadej Pogacar in action during the time trial.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersThe decision to test both riders outside of the usual window of 6am to 11pm has been heavily criticised by some, despite the tests being authorised by the Paris prosecutor’s office. “With a French judge it’s not going to be hard to get that warrant,” Jonathan Vaughters, manager of the EF Education EasyPost team, said.“They’re going to look at the history of the sport. To a French judge that is not a sports scientist, or a haematologist, I do not think it would be too difficult to convince them. I doubt if they had to fight that hard for it.”Vaughters insisted that he is “very supportive” of the ITA. “I think this is an example of them doing a good job. I just don’t think you should read into the fact they got a warrant from a judge. My guess is the bar is not that high.”Pascal Chanteur, vice-president of the CPA, the professional riders association that has been critical of nocturnal testing, also questioned the Paris prosecutor’s action, saying “the judge knew nothing of cycling”.“The CPA supports night-time testing if there’s enough evidence,” Chanteur said, suggesting also that there was no significance in Vingegaard being tested at 2am and Pogacar at 5am. “This is because of the ITA’s limited resources.”The French sports code specifies that testing procedures “must be ­carried out under conditions that ensure strict proportionality between the infringement of the athlete’s rights and the objectives of the fight against doping”.View image in fullscreenAn artist paints as Fred Wright rides past on the way to Thonon-les-Bains.Photograph: Thibault Camus/APSpeaking in May this year to Reuters, Olivier Banuls, the ITA’s director of testing, said: “We do a lot of testing but we don’t do mass testing. To do mass testing is to throw money out the window.”The outcome of the tests on both riders, however, remains unknown, leaving the Tour in a state of limbo, carrying on regardless, despite the cloud of suspicion that hangs over Pogacar who is closing in on what would be a record-equalling fifth Tour win.Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tadej PogacarTour de FranceCyclingnewsShareReuse this content

title: NBA Summer League takeaways: an outstanding top four, a Warriors gem and a Cavaliers steal | NBA | The Guardian
description: The midsummer party in Vegas is a showcase for the next wave of NBA talent. Our correspondent in the desert empties her notebook
Author: Claire de Lune
Headline: The midsummer party in Vegas is a showcase for the next wave of NBA talent. Our correspondent in the desert empties her notebook
Content: Yaxel Lendeborg helped the Warriors to the Summer League Final.Photograph: John Locher/APView image in fullscreenYaxel Lendeborg helped the Warriors to the Summer League Final.Photograph: John Locher/APNBANBA Summer League takeaways: an outstanding top four, a Warriors gem and a Cavaliers stealThe midsummer party in Vegas is a showcase for the next wave of NBA talent. Our correspondent in the desert empties her notebookClaire de LuneTue 21 Jul 2026 05.15 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google1) The top four are as good as advertisedThe hype heading into this year’s draft was tremendous, and through the small sample size of 10 or so days in the Las Vegas summer heat, this year’s class has lived up to its billing.AJ Dybantsa, now a Washington Wizard, looked every bit the No 1 overall pick in his Summer League debut, where he bested the player drafted directly behind him,Darryn Peterson. This was notable, as Peterson had won the pair’s previous head to head matchups, and the noise was starting to swell that perhaps the Wizards had made the wrong selection with the No 1 pick. But Dybantsa showed his high ceiling, looking every bit the crafty and athletic offensive engine he has been touted as. Peterson, to his credit, also had a solid Summer League outing, especially in the California Classic (the mini version of the league that takes place before the big shebang in Las Vegas), showcasing his excellent shot creation skills.The third overall pick,Cameron Boozer, who many trendy media folks picked to go first, showed exactly why he’s attracted so many fans while leading his team to theNBASummer League Final: he’s not particularly flashy or exciting, rather more of a high level metronome. Boozer is a super smart, strong, and mature offensive hub who may not put butts in seats, but he really understands how to play basketball at an NBA level.AndCaleb Wilson,drafted fourth by the Chicago Bulls, may have seen his stock rise the most of the four during his time in Vegas. He scored a scintillating 35 points (with seven threes) in his Vegas debut, and if that shooting can hold up in the NBA, his athleticism, shot creation and swagger make him a bona fide franchise cornerstone, the kind of guy who can finallyposter dunkthe Bulls out of mediocrity.2) Yaxel Lendeborg is NBA readyBefore Lendeborg was an NBA Summer League champion and Summer League Finals MVP with the Golden State Warriors, who drafted him with the 11th overall pick this year, he was winning an NCAA championship with the Michigan Wolverines. Simply put, the guy is a winner. With his size (6ft 9in, with a 7ft 4in wingspan), skillset (a swiss army knife with a high basketball IQ) and personality (not afraid of the big moment, swagger for days), he most likely would have gone even higher had he not been 23 years old at the time of the draft in June. In some ways, though, his age is an asset; especially for a team as win-now (or, rather, win-yesterday) as the Warriors. Lendeborg is ready for the NBA fresh out of the box, he’s smart and savvy and the epitome of plug and play. On top of all of this, he’s a walking pull-quote: hetold ESPN’s Ben Golliverof the Grizzlies’ blown lead in the championship game: “Man, their butts got tight. They puckered up, you know what I mean? Some of us are built for the moment and some aren’t.”3) The Lakers may have in-house solutionsThe most glaring roster hole after anaggressive summer of movesfor the Los Angeles Lakers (all five starters from game one of their first round series against the Houston Rockets this playoffs are gone) is at the wing/forward. While rumors are still aflutter in regards to players like the Hawks’ Jonathan Kuminga and Dallas’s PJ Washington, the Lakers’ time in Las Vegas showed heartening signs that they may have rotation-caliber wings at home. To start with,Cameron Carr, who fell to Los Angeles all the way down at No 24 in this year’s draft, looks like he may be a steal who should have gone in the lottery. He showed athleticism and shooting that the Lakers have lacked, and seemed right at home in pseudo NBA level competition.Adou Thiero, the freak athlete 2025 draft pick who spent most of his rookie campaign recovering from injury, also seemed to be making substantial strides.But the surprise of the week wasArthur Kaluma, who spent last season with the Lakers’ G-League affiliate. He had a 34-point game against the Mavericks, and generally seemed prime to emerge as the 3 & D wing the Lakers are looking to shore up their rotation with. While the team fell short of making the Summer League Final, they have to be feeling pretty good about what they have in-house. They went 4-1 in Vegas, only losing to the ultimate champion Warriors in the semi-finals.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Weekly for the 2026 WNBA seasonSign up toWNBA 30A limited run women’s basketball newsletter dedicated to the league’s historic 30th season.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion4) The Bucks reset is off to a good startMilwaukee have had a rough go of things in recent years, to say the least. Their fence-swing for Damian Lillard went about as poorly as it could have, culminating in an achilles tear and the decision to waive and stretch his contract. Then came the somewhat inexplicable decision to throw the bag at center Myles Turner. Another year of Giannis Antetokounmpo injuries later, and it was finally time to face the music: the Buckstraded the Greek star to the Miami Heat, who had long courted him. As much as it hurt to break up with their multi-time MVP sweetheart, it was for the best for all parties. Hope that there are sunnier days ahead came in the form of guardBrayden Burries, who the Bucks selected No 10 overall in this year’s draft. He was one of the standouts in Vegas, averaging 23.5 points (with 4.5 rebounds and 4.3 assists), and putting a cherry on top of his outing with a game-winning bucket in his final game. Burries looks like he could end up being the best of what was a crowded guard class in the mid lottery, scoring efficiently and showing poise in his time in the desert.5) Meleek Thomas may have been the steal of the draftAt the time of writing, the jury is still out on whether or not one LeBron James, the best thing to ever happen to sports in the Cleveland metro area, will be back for a third rodeo in a Cavaliers uniform. But regardless of that outcome, there are silver linings to be found for Cavs fans this July. Thomas, a guard out of Arkansas, was the surprise standout of the week, averaging just over 27 points (!) at 50% (!) from the field. He may struggle to find substantial rotation minutes on the Cavs next year, as they just gave Donovan Mitchell atremendous amount of moneyand are allegedly planning to bring back James Harden on a more team-friendly contract, but Thomas was the best player on the floor in several of his contests in Vegas, and for the 34th overall pick, that’s pretty damned remarkable. Thomas didn’t look out of place among his higher drafted peers; if anything, he was often the most confident among them. We may well look back on the 2026 draft and wonder how Cleveland snagged such a gem in the second round.Explore more on these topicsNBAMilwaukee BucksGolden State WarriorsCleveland CavaliersBasketballUS sportsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: NFL pundit Ryan Clark informed of ESPN firing mid-show amid wider layoffs | ESPN | The Guardian
description: ESPN began its first major layoffs in three years, with Ryan Clark, Karl Ravech and NFL Network staff among those affected after April’s merger
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: ESPN began its first major layoffs in three years, with Ryan Clark, Karl Ravech and NFL Network staff among those affected after April’s merger
Content: Ryan Clark, who joined ESPN in 2015 as an NFL analyst, learned of his layoff while on NFL Live on Monday.Photograph: Reed Hoffmann/APView image in fullscreenRyan Clark, who joined ESPN in 2015 as an NFL analyst, learned of his layoff while on NFL Live on Monday.Photograph: Reed Hoffmann/APESPNNFL pundit Ryan Clark informed of ESPN firing mid-show amid wider layoffsClark informed during on-air appearanceCuts follow ESPN’s NFL Network integrationDisney also trimming jobs across companyAssociated PressTue 21 Jul 2026 14.08 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleESPN is undergoing a significant round of layoffs for the first time in three years after taking over NFL Network in April.Chairman Jimmy Pitaro said in a memo to employees Tuesday morning, obtained by the Associated Press, that most moves are the result of ESPN’s acquisition ofNFLNetwork and other league digital assets last year.The deal was announced last August and also included NFL Fantasy and the rights to distribute the RedZone channel to cable and satellite operators. In return, the NFL got a 10% equity stake in ESPN.It was approved by government regulators in January. NFL Network employees became part of ESPN on 1 April.“Over the past several months, we’ve made significant progress integrating the NFL assets that we acquired into ESPN,” Pitaro wrote in the memo. “Throughout this process, we have taken the time to carefully evaluate our collective teams, resources and organizational structure to best position us for the future. As a result, we had to make some difficult decisions about job impacts that we will be communicating today.“While most of the job impacts are tied to the acquisition, we will also notify colleagues in other parts of the company today that their positions have been impacted. We are committed to treating employees with compassion and respect and to providing support as they navigate this transition.”While most of the layoffs affect production and behind-the-scenes personnel, some on-air talent were affected. Ryan Clark, who joined ESPN in 2015 as an NFL analyst, learned of his layoff while on NFL Live on Monday. A person familiar with the situation said Clark was originally going to be told Tuesday morning before reports leaked about the decision.Clark was told by company executives rather than hear about it elsewhere. He did not finish his appearance on the show.The person spoke on condition of anonymity because they are not at liberty to discuss personnel decisions.Clark took to social media on Tuesday to address the layoffs. He also shared a video of himself working out.“Sending prayers and love to all those laid off today by ESPN. So many of you have poured your life into that company, + I know how you’re feeling right now,” Clark wrote on X. “My hope is as this door closes another opens for you all. God bless!”Other notable names include Karl Ravech, who had been with ESPN since 1993 and had been an integral part of its baseball coverage. Ravech was the voice of “Sunday Night Baseball” from 2022-25 before the package moved to NBC this season. He did call Monday night’s game between the Los Angeles Dodgers and Philadelphia Phillies.Tom Pelissero, who had been with NFL Network since 2017, is also part of the cuts. ESPN had recently signed NFL insiders Ian Rapoport and Adam Schefter to extensions.Fox NewsreportedCam Newton was also released only one year after signing a contract extension.The ESPN cutbacks are also part of other reductions that are taking place throughout theWalt Disney Companythis week.Explore more on these topicsESPNUS sportsNFLUS television industryCam NewtonWalt Disney CompanyTelevision industryShareReuse this content

title: In the US, the 2026 World Cup’s legacy will be an invitation to do it all again | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Fifa’s gargantuan revenue influx from the tournament, with little left over for locals, will beget more exploitation
Author: Leander Schaerlaeckens
Headline: Fifa’s gargantuan revenue influx from the tournament, with little left over for locals, will beget more exploitation
Content: A drone show portrayed the World Cup trophy over lower Manhattan in the leadup to the final.Photograph: Justin Setterfield/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA drone show portrayed the World Cup trophy over lower Manhattan in the leadup to the final.Photograph: Justin Setterfield/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026In the US, the 2026 World Cup’s legacy will be an invitation to do it all againLeander SchaerlaeckensFifa’s gargantuan revenue influx from the tournament, with little left over for locals, will beget more exploitationTue 21 Jul 2026 11.30 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.33 EDTShareYou do not, under any circumstance, have to hand it toFifa.You do, however, have to acknowledge that the 2026 World Cup was successful in all of the ways it was intended to be successful. The soccer was interesting and competitive and dramatic, albeit mostly not great – not that this really a necessary ingredient for a compelling sporting product. People came and watched in overwhelming numbers. There was fun and folklore; underdog surprises; heroic storylines that might have been hackneyed had they been works of fiction. The whole thing made vast sums of money for its organizer. More money, by an order of billions, than even Fifa itself had projected,per the Guardian’s reporting.The tournament even went some way towards normalizing the controversial and mandatory hydration breaks, whether needed or not. This time around, we booed; next time, we’ll shrug. And while Fifa claims that it made no additional revenue from these glorified commercial breaks – the way Fox Sports chose to fill them in the United States, at any rate – you can be absolutely certain that the value created for broadcasters will be brought up in the next round of rights negotiations.Only four years to the next men’s World Cup – what can we expect from the 2030 edition?Read moreThe expanded field of 48 teams worked out fine, as the worst teams were, by and large, not the debutants. So fine, in fact, that a further bloating to 64 teams is now, in a growing number of rooms filled with sportocrats, evidently being talked about less as a fringe idea by heretics than as a not-unreasonable and even natural next step.In the end, it was all there. All of it happened the way it was supposed to.This fully AmericanizedWorld Cuptook a great deal from the United States – the countdowns; the half-time show; the championship rings; the commercial breaks; the “let’s get ready to rumble” guy telling us to get ready to rumble; not starting either half of the final on time; the dynamic pricing; and more.But what did it give? What did it leave behind?Not all that long ago, Fifa still insisted the World Cup was about spreading the sport, conquering new lands, evangelizing in the emerging territories. Will a boring and quarrelsome final have a tail long enough for any sort of momentum to affix itself to it? Will we remember it at all in a few years? What will soccer look like in North American now that all is said and done?This is a three-pronged question. Mexico hosted the thing for a third time and will be broadly pleased with how it went.El Trifinally made it to the elusive “fifth game”, but nevertheless stranded at the same stage – the last 16 – they did at every World Cup from 1994 through 2018.Canada, whostood to gain or lose the mostas the only first-time hosts, scaled new heights on the field and nevertheless felt good about what they had shown when they were trounced 3-0 by Morocco. The country had, after all, put on a series of good parties that its guests will long remember.And what of the United States? The legacy of the tournament may prove complicated here. The games were exclusive and inaccessible. And Fifa structured the thing in such a way that every penny of its mammoth haul will first head back to Switzerland. There will be no pot of money left over from the tournament for local organizers to reinvest up and down the game, the way there was in 1994. The US Soccer Federation was largely marginalized, in fact, and left to work around the edges in hopes of actually accomplishing something lasting and meaningful at the grassroots level.Did soccer make new fans? Probably not very many in the stadiums, since it’s doubtful that any significant number of people will have dropped several thousand dollars on a lark, hoping they might have a good time at this sport they’re just getting into. Certainly, the TV ratings suggest a raft of new viewers tuned in, but did they catch the soccer bug? It will be some years before we really know.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice weeklySign up toSoccer Desk: World Cup EditionJonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion1:30World Cup 2026 in 90 seconds: a photo animation with audioBesides, soccer did not, and does not, have a popularity problem in the United States. It was already on a steep ascent. Certainly, any and all converts will be welcomed into the tent. But the global game was already very much a part of the American fabric.What soccer in the US has is an access problem. Access to a recreational youth game that hasn’t been devoured by the hypercapitalist, pay-to-play travel scene. Access, for those who do have talent and ambition, to the right coaches at the right ages, who prioritize development over winning. Access to fields and equipment and fresh air and a good old run-around.Will this World Cup help with any of those issues?As far as Fifa is concerned, this edition offered proof of concept. That it can expand without penalty. That it can get away with price-gouging, and that it will be rewarded enormously for it. That it will get away with all the extra contrivances and perversions and embellishments it can conjure up.By the same mechanism that allowed Gianni Infantino to engineer a 2034 World Cup in Saudi Arabia, a return to the US in 2038 seems a strong possibility.There is talk,too, of hosting the Fifa Club World Cup in the US again in 2029, same as in 2025. A 2031 Women’s World Cup co-hosted here is all but a done deal.That, in the end, may be what Fifa has built here: a colony.Leander Schaerlaeckens is the author of The Long Game: U.S. Men’s Soccer and Its Savage, Four-Decade Journey to the Top, or Thereabouts,which is out now. He teaches at Marist University.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupFifaFootball politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: Could the World Cup be back in the US as soon as 2038? Absolutely | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Explosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue goes along with a desire by Fifa to recoup lost TV income in future deals
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: Explosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue goes along with a desire by Fifa to recoup lost TV income in future deals
Content: Gianni Infantino (left) and Donald Trump carry the World Cup trophy to the Spain team.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenGianni Infantino (left) and Donald Trump carry the World Cup trophy to the Spain team.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Could the World Cup be back in the US as soon as 2038? AbsolutelyExplosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue goes along with a Fifa desire to recoup lost TV income in future dealsMatt Hughesin New YorkTue 21 Jul 2026 15.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAs is often the case, it has been left to Donald Trump to say the quiet bit out loud. “We have to do this again and we have to do it while I’m around, you hear that Gianni?” the US president told Fox News in an interview broadcast in the buildup to theWorld Cupfinal.An American bid for the 2038 World Cup has been the talk of the hotel lobbies populated by administrators from around the world over the past month, who have watched this country’s seemingly limitless appetite for major events and willingness to pay whatever it takes to attend them.After this World Cup, here’s what I feel: a kind of messy hope | Jonathan LiewRead moreFifa has been quietly, but actively encouraging a US bid for some time and in a meeting at the Waldorf Astoria on Park Avenue in New York on Saturday it became clearer why.The Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, told representatives of the governing body’s 211 member associations that its revenue projections for the current four-year cycle has increased from $11bn (£8.2bn) to $15bn, with the vast majority of the growth coming from an explosion in ticketing and hospitality revenue.A Fifa source disclosed that such has been the demand in the US in particular that ticketing and hospitality sales have gone past $5bn, with that part of the market now worth more than the World Cup’s TV rights for the first time.“Fifa will want to get back here as quickly as possible,” said a senior figure at one of its hospitality partners.While ticket sales have exceeded expectations, helped by the dynamic pricing and secondary market resale models that are embedded in US culture, in one crucial respect Fifa left a huge amount of money on the table, another reason it is desperate to return.In an extraordinary deal, Fox Sports was awarded English-language TV rights in the US by Fifa without a bidding process, a precautionary move by the world governing body to prevent a lawsuit when Qatar 2022 was moved to the winter after the TV contracts had already been signed, creating a direct clash with the US domestic sports season, particularly the NFL and NBA.With no competitive tension, Fox was given a relative bargain, paying $485m for the 2026 rights, the same as in Qatar, despite the World Cup having an extra 40 matches and taking place on home soil. For context, the NFL’s domestic TV deal is worth $11bn a year, with the NBA bringing in around $7bn.Fox has capitalised this summer as a result, on the back of bumper viewing, with industry experts predicting it will make more than $1bn from TV adverts, whose volume and value has increased due to the addition of hydration breaks.Fifa is determined to recoup what many regard as lost income from the US market in future TV rights cycles.Having watched with frustration as Fox has cashed in, ESPN and NBC are expected to bid for the 2030 tournament, while initial conversations have also taken place with Netflix about them joining in the auction.View image in fullscreenHigh prices did not deter fans from packing out stadiums at the World Cup in the US.Photograph: Julio Cortez/APNetflix has already signalled its growing interest in football by buying American rights for the Women’s World Cup in 2027 and 2031, the latter being held in the US, and another men’s tournament seven years later would spark another boom.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionNo country has hosted the World Cup twice in 12 years, but Fifa’s statutes and recent staging decisions give it the flexibility for this to happen.By taking the 2030 World Cup to three continents and six countries – Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Argentina, Paraguay and Uruguay – before Saudi Arabia hosts in 2034, a North American bid for 2038 is permitted under Fifa’s confederation rotation policy, along with one from Oceania.Soccer Australia had wanted to bid for 2034, but after being given less than a month to formally register its interest by Fifa, withdrew its candidacy leaving Saudi Arabia to be selected unopposed, Infantino’s preferred method.To complicate matters slightly, the North American confederation, Concacaf, is understood to have reservations about another US bid as it wants to give opportunities to other members.Costa Rica, Jamaica and Mexico have been added to the 2031 Women’s World Cup bid and another joint endeavour could emerge, although unsurprisingly Trump would prefer the US to be sole hosts. “Based on the numbers we will be requesting it again immediately,” he told Fox.Fifa’s timeline for opening the 2038 process is not yet known, but history suggests it could move quickly to secure the outcome it wants. The sudden opening of the 2034 bidding process in October 2024 took most confederations by surprise with Infantino announcing that Saudi Arabia would be the hosts just 25 days later, a decision ratified by all Fifa members at a bizarre virtual extraordinary general meeting two months later.Infantino will be re-elected for a fourth full term at next year’s Fifa Congress in Morocco, almost certainly unopposed, and then all eyes will turn to the 2038 contest.Barring what would be an unprecedented change to the US constitution to permit an additional term, Trump is due to leave office in January 2029, which may quickly become the deadline for Fifa.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupUS sportsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Nico Williams, Ferran Torres and Spain’s adaptable World Cup excellence | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: The two substitutes combined on the World Cup winning goal not by changing the game, but by offering it what it needed, when it was needed
Author: Jeff Rueter
Headline: The two substitutes combined on the World Cup winning goal not by changing the game, but by offering it what it needed, when it was needed
Content: Nico Williams and Ferran Torres combined to win the World Cup for Spain.Photograph: Howard Smith/ISI Photos/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenNico Williams and Ferran Torres combined to win the World Cup for Spain.Photograph: Howard Smith/ISI Photos/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026AnalysisNico Williams, Ferran Torres and Spain’s adaptable World Cup excellenceJeff RueterThe two substitutes combined on the World Cup winning goal not by changing the game, but by offering it what it needed, when it was neededTue 21 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.19 EDTShareAs Spain built into the second half of extra-time of Sunday’sWorld Cupfinal, it was already clear that Argentina hoped to make them suffer. Every challenge left no stud unshown. Their defenders labored to block striker Mikel Oyarzabal from his typical lanes of service, with Spain taking 11 of their 20 attempts from beyond the box.Lionel Scaloni’s side may have been aggressive, but they were also just structured enough to withstand the endless monsoon of Spanish pressure. After the 62nd minute, when Luis de la Fuente brought his first two charges off the bench,Spainadapted to find new avenues to attack, adjusting with Enzo Fernández’s sending-off near the end of normal time.Nearly two million people join Spain’s World Cup celebrations in MadridRead moreIn his 106th minute at right-back, and with Lamine Yamal sizing up Argentina’s defensive shape at the edge of the box, Pedro Porro looked at the developing scrum outside the box. Fresh from kick-off, his teammates were largely running parallel to him but staying just enough restrained enough in their movement to create precious yards of open terrain beyond the backline. As Yamal passed back to his flank-side running mate, many in New York New Jersey Stadium thought it was another of countless recirculations to start an approach up another pathway.Instead, Porro lobbed in a cross. The Tottenham defender bent it to perfection, a powerful-enough ball for some defenders to be in two minds about how to react but not so overhit that it risked crossing the byline. Nico Williams was quick to it, finding that his supporting numbers were trailing a bit beyond the ball for late arrivals into the box.Williams spent far more of his time at the 2026 World Cup watching on from the sideline than he would’ve liked. A starter for six of Spain’s seven games during their triumphant run through Euro 2024, Williams arrived in North America with a groin strain and hamstring issues incurred late in the La Liga season before leaving the group stage finaleagainst Uruguay with an adductor injury.The Athletic Bilbao winger never started a game at this World Cup, with his 45-minute shift against Argentina his longest of the tournament. On Sunday, his entrance promised greater verve and unpredictability than had been offered by the winger he replaced, Álex Baena.View image in fullscreenNico Williams shrugged off Nahuel Molina to head the ball back for Ferran Torres to finish.Photograph: James Lang/IMAGN IMAGES/ReutersBetter late than never, Williams did not waste his opportunity when it finally arrived. As Porro’s cross fell back to Earth, the 24-year-old got his head to the ball not to square right into the heart of the six-yard box, but instead to deftly doff it beyond the nearby defenders and into the stride of another second-half substitute, Ferran Torres. It was a goal born of preternatural assessments of space, the only way Spain could break down a dogged Argentinian side desperate to get Emiliano Martínez into penalties.Like Williams, Torres had entered with an intent to exploit Argentina’s approach to the final. Oyarzabal had proved invaluable for a possession-dominant Spain, able to drop deep and help with buildup while backing himself to find seams along the backline. Argentina had structured themselves to allow that, but prevent him from getting the ball any closer to goal. Torres, in comparison, offered a bit more finesse as a natural off-ball mover in the game’s most dynamic phases, but also came on with more gas in the tank that helped him stay sharp against a rapidly tiring group of Argentinian veterans.In a final of clattering challenges, Torres’s run went entirely unchecked. Giuliano Simeone, the player closest to Torres, had almost cartoonishly taken himself out of the play right as Torres blew past him. Just before Williams kept the ball from going out of play, Simeone had assessed the pass as overcooked and was bent over to adjust his sock as Williams’s header bounced to Torres. Three Argentinian defenders had glommed on to Mikel Merino, who scored match-winners against Portugal and Belgium, leaving Torres with equally unusual time and a void of defensive pressure to finally get Spain on track for their second men’s World Cup title.Spain’s metronome: the statistics behind Rodri’s superb World Cup campaignRead moreThroughout this World Cup’s business end, Spain were discussed as being the best team of the tournament. Their collective approach had become so cohesive that, even if they didn’t score, the team’s focus on their buildup seldom waned. Players had gotten so confident in their mettle under De la Fuente that each seemed eager to embrace their role even if it meant less of an individual spotlight in a World Cup headlined by its stars.That’s a rare balance in modern international soccer. While the club side of the game has become meticulously curated, with every pressing trigger and passing sequence carefully rehearsed, international duty is usually where stars go to enjoy their game with a team largely built to account for their strengths and weaknesses. Thomas Tuchel tried to foster a similar culture with his squad selections, but found himself light on midfielders after Jordan Henderson’s klutzy celebration.It makes it all too easy to distill teams down to the star they are designed to support. When it works, as it largely did for Lionel Messi’s Argentina, few question the wisdom of the approach. When it doesn’t, as we saw with Cristiano Ronaldo’s Portugal, it’s an equally obvious problem that the coach was unwilling to solve.Like the leaders of the world’s best clubs, De la Fuente was able to change Spain’s approach during games by bringing in players who could occupy identical spaces to the starters they would replace because each interpreted their role differently. Williams’s approach on the wing gave him greater license to put the opponent on the back foot than Baena’s; Torres came into the game and would successfully stretch a shorthanded Argentina. Even Pedri – who checked in with Torres just after the hour mark – made life more difficult for Argentina, as a more dribbly midfield complement to Rodri than Fabián Ruiz.While Argentina looked to labor in the mid-afternoon New Jersey sun, Spain adapted. They continued to morph to find new methods to make the 2022 champions sweat further. After an expert looping cross, a brilliant and deft header and an opportunistic scamper into space, it’s Spain who will enter the 2030 World Cup cycle at the pinnacle of the sport.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainanalysisShareReuse this content

title: The big World Cup 2026 quiz | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Did you catch the little details as 48 teams played 104 matches in 16 cities across 39 days?
Author: Richard Foster
Headline: Did you catch the little details as 48 teams played 104 matches in 16 cities across 39 days?
