title: Neuer to Neymar: 11 World Cup players who retired from international football | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Germany’s 2014 World Cup winning goalkeeper and a Brazilian icon are among those who have have called time after the 2026 tournament
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Germany’s 2014 World Cup winning goalkeeper and a Brazilian icon are among those who have have called time after the 2026 tournament
Content: Neymar, Riyad Mahrez and Patrik Schick are some of the players that have ended their international career following the World Cup.Composite: Getty Images, Guardian designView image in fullscreenNeymar, Riyad Mahrez and Patrik Schick are some of the players that have ended their international career following the World Cup.Composite: Getty Images, Guardian designWorld Cup 2026Neuer to Neymar: 11 World Cup players who retired from international footballGermany’s 2014 World Cup winning goalkeeper and a Brazilian icon are among those who have have called time after the 2026 tournamentCalvin BurtonTue 21 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleManuel Neuer (Germany, 40)First participated in the World Cup in 2010 before winning the trophy four years later. Germany have had little success since and theirpenalty shootout defeat by Paraguaymarked the end of Neuer’s international career. He initially retired after Euro 2024 but reversed that decision a few weeks before this tournament. “Despite the bitter end, I don’t regret this decision,” he said after his 23rd and final World Cup appearance. The former Germany defender Robert Huth criticised the decision to bring Neuer out of retirement: “I think the big one that really unsettles the squad was the Neuer inclusion. I think [it was] really unexpected, he came late to the table.”Patrik Schick (Czechia, 30)Made his World Cup debut this summer having left an impression at Euro 2020. Schick failed to score as Czechia went winless and were eliminated in the group stage. The striker announced his international retirement hours after the final group game. “Today, my national team chapter is coming to an end,” he said. The official Czechia statement paid tribute: “Thank you for every match, every goal, and the pride with which you represented the Czech Republic.”Guillermo Ochoa (Mexico, 41)Cemented himself as a fans’ favourite in 2014 when he kept a famous clean sheet against the World Cup hosts, Brazil. His playing time was limited this summer, but hegot 12 minutes against Czechia, where he was celebrated by teammates. After full time the manager, Javier Aguirre, said: “The moment he stepped on to the pitch, it overshadowed everything else. He is a world-class legend, and I am incredibly proud of him.” Ochoa later posted: “It was the greatest privilege of my life. Thank you for believing, for always accompanying us. I love you Mexico.”View image in fullscreenGuillermo Ochoa shows his emotions after Mexico’s exit against England.Photograph: Álex Cruz/EPARiyad Mahrez (Algeria, 35)Having made one appearance at the 2014 finals, Mahrez had to wait 12 years to add to his tally. He scored two goals against Austria before Algeria were eliminatedin the last 32 by Switzerlandin his final game. Mahrez said: “Representing Algeria has been a dream of mine ever since I was young, to play for my country. It’s been an immense honour and a great source of pride.” A Confederation of African Football’s statement said: “You gave us memories that time can never take away. Thank you, Riyad Mahrez.”mahrezEnner Valencia (Ecuador, 36)The forward got six goals in as many matches across the 2014 and 2022 finals but was unable to add to that tally for an Ecuador teamwho lost to Mexicoin the last 32. “I’ve played my last match with the national team,” he said in the tunnel after. Ecuador’s official X account showed appreciation to their record scorer: “The GOAT. Super Enner. Thank you for giving yourself to the country and representing it in the best way.”Nicolás Otamendi (Argentina, 38)The uncompromising defender played every minute as Argentina triumphed in Qatar, having made his World Cup debut in 2010. Playing time was harder to come by in North America, though he featured in every game bar one, having revealed his intention to retire in March. “It’s my last World Cup, from there I’ll be a fan,” he said. Otamendi’s final home match became a tribute, with Lionel Messi letting him score a penalty at La Bombonera.Marko Arnautovic (Austria, 37)This was the striker’s first and last World Cup. He scored twice as Austria reached the last 32,where they were defeated3–0 by Spain. Arnautovic revealed his departure in an emotional post-match interview, saying: “Playing for Austria was the biggest highlight. It hits me that from now on I won’t see my second family any more.” In a social media post, the Austrian Football Association said: “What a career. What a character. Thank you for everything.”Sadio Mané (Senegal, 34)Participated in 2018 but injury ruled him out four years later. Following Senegal’scapitulation against Belgium, Mané released a statement regarding retirement via Senegalese outlet Le Quotidien – although there have been questions about its authenticity – saying: “Know that I sacrificed everything for this flag. I gave the very best of myself and I always fought fiercely for our homeland.” Pape Thiaw was asked about Mané’s retirement in January and replied: “We want to keep him as long as possible … when we say it’s his decision, it doesn’t just belong to him, he belongs to the people of Senegal, and they want to see him carry on.”View image in fullscreenSadio Mané has called time on his international career with Senegal.Photograph: Jared C Tilton/FIFA/Getty ImagesCraig Gordon (Scotland, 43)The goalkeeper amassed 84 caps, making his debut in 2004, but did not get off the bench at his first World Cup. “I’ve never wanted it to end, but it must,” Gordon said, calling time on his club and international career. Gordon’s spell was described as “a career unlike any other” by the Scotland national team’s social media account.Jean Michaël Seri (Côte d’Ivoire, 35)Made his World Cup debut in his side’s 2–0 victory over Curaçao, his 64th cap. In a social media post, the former Fulham midfielder later said: “After 11 years in the national team, and one World Cup played, I announce the end of my international career today.” Seri received a wealth of tributes below his post. “Congratulations on all that you’ve accomplished with the national team,” replied the captain, Franck Kessié. “Thank you for all these moments shared under our colors.”Neymar (Brazil, 34)Announced himself on the World Cup stage with two goals on debut in 2014. Restricted by injury at the current tournament, he managed 55 minutes beforeBrazil’s exit against Norwayand announced his international retirement shortly after full time. “It started here at MetLife Stadium,” he said, referring to his debut against the US in August 2010, “and I finished here. It is now over.” Zlatan Ibrahimovic, working as a pundit for US television, said: “An amazing player, and the skill set he had was just crazy. So, sad moment for Brazilian football.”Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026Manuel NeuerNeymarfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: French Open becomes first grand slam to offer players share of tournament revenue | Tennis | The Guardian
description: The French Open is to offer players a share of the tournament’s revenue as prize money, the first grand slam to do so
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: The French Open is to offer players a share of the tournament’s revenue as prize money, the first grand slam to do so
Content: The French Open’s willingness to commit to a profit-share model is a major development.Photograph: James Fearn/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe French Open’s willingness to commit to a profit-share model is a major development.Photograph: James Fearn/Getty ImagesTennisFrench Open becomes first grand slam to offer players share of tournament revenueMove is significant step in row over prize moneyUS Open will come under pressure to follow suitMatt HughesTue 21 Jul 2026 11.34 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheFrench Openhas become the first grand slam tournament to offer players a share of the event’s revenue in a significant step forward in the row over prize money. Officials from Roland Garros made the offer to the players’ representative, Larry Scott, in talks at Wimbledon a fortnight ago in a move that will increase pressure on the US Open, which is due to announce its prize fund for this year’s tournament at the start of next month.While an agreement has yet to be reached, the French Open’s willingness to commit to a revenue-share model to determine prize money is a major development, and sets Roland Garros apart from the three other grand slam tournaments.The summer of the underdog: why outsiders are the most compelling sights in sport | Natalie TanRead moreDebbie Jevans, chair of the All England Club, angered the players last month by saying that it made “no sense” to use tournament revenues to determine prize money, which led them tothreaten a media boycottfor the first week of Wimbledon, althoughthat protest was cancelled.The French Open appears to have taken a different approach, with the commitment to revenue sharing being accompanied by a willingness to contribute to player pensions and healthcare and give the players a greater say in the running of the tournament.The players are seeking guarantees that all the grand slam tournaments will pay 16% of their revenue in prize money with immediate effect, rising to 22% by 2030. The grand slam tournaments have offered significant increases in prize money in recent years, but the players want an agreed formula based on revenue sharing rather than having to wait each year for notification of the annual award.The US Open is under particular pressure as it has had longer to reach an agreement with the players and the tournament next month coincides with the arrival of a new chief executive of the USTennisAssociation, Craig Tiley.A number of players – including the men’s world No 1, Jannik Sinner – have threatened not to play in the US Open’s mixed doubles that precedes the singles at Flushing Meadows, if significant progress is not made.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |FortnightlySign up toThe HotspotA new fortnightly newsletter covering the intersection of sport and the climate crisisPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast year the US Open increased its prize money by 20% to $90m, compared with $75m the year before. A similar rise this year would mean the players receive a total prize pot of more than $100m for the first time.Explore more on these topicsTennisFrench OpenUS Open tennisWimbledonUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Morgan Rogers ‘excited to join biggest club in London’ after making £117m Chelsea move | Chelsea | The Guardian
description: Morgan Rogers has joined Chelsea from Aston Villa for a fee of £117m, signing a seven-year deal to become the Blues’ record signing
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Morgan Rogers has joined Chelsea from Aston Villa for a fee of £117m, signing a seven-year deal to become the Blues’ record signing
Content: Morgan Rogers was enthused by the chance to work with Xabi Alonso and said Chelsea was a club he always admired from a young age.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenMorgan Rogers was enthused by the chance to work with Xabi Alonso and said Chelsea was a club he always admired from a young age.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesChelseaMorgan Rogers ‘excited to join biggest club in London’ after making £117m Chelsea moveEngland international signs seven-year contractXabi Alonso was key factor in Chelsea’s record signingPA MediaTue 21 Jul 2026 17.16 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMorgan Rogers has joined Chelsea fromAston Villafor a fee of £117m, signing a seven-year deal to become the Blues’ record signing.A deal was agreed on Saturday for the 23-year-old to move to Stamford Bridge as the club stole in ahead of Arsenal, who had been in talks to sign the England international and had appeared to be favourites to secure his signature.Men’s transfer window summer 2026: all deals from Europe’s top five leaguesRead moreIt is understood that the chance to work with the new Chelsea managerXabi Alonsowas a key factor in persuading Rogers, who has just returned from helping England to a third-place finish at the World Cup, to choose west London.He started in the2-1 semi-final defeat to Argentinain Atlanta last Wednesday as well as the6-4 win over France on Saturdaythat earned the bronze medal. He told his new club’s website: “I’m so excited. For me, Chelsea are the biggest club in London and a club I’ve always admired since I was a kid. I’m really excited about the project with the new manager, the players we’ve got and where the club is heading. That’s why I’m here and I can’t wait to get started.”A versatile forward who satisfies Chelsea’s need for a player that can play multiple attacking roles, he has enjoyed a rapid rise at Villa Park following his move from Middlesbrough in January 2024 and helped fire Villa to a top-four finish and the Europa League last season.He leaves Villa having scored 31 times in 125 appearances under Unai Emery.The deal represents one of the most significant buys of Chelsea owners BlueCo’s four-year reign. Co-owner Behdad Eghbali indicated towards the end of last season that the club had learned the lessons of an underwhelming campaign in which the team finished 10th and were thrashed in the Champions League last 16 by Paris Saint-Germain, and that they would be targeting players in the market that would be in a position to help them challenge for the title.View image in fullscreenRogers walks on the pitch at Stamford Bridge.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesThe former Liverpool, Real Madrid and Bayern Munich midfielder Alonso, a two-time Champions League winner as a player, was hired partly in order to convince the right calibre of player to move to Chelsea.The deal for Rogers is arguably the biggest the club have pulled off since Cole Palmer moved from Manchester City in 2023. Rogers becomes the fourth signing of the summer as the Blues overhaul their squad, following the arrivals of defender Marco Palestra, striker Emmanuel Emegha and winger Geovany Quenda.In June, the club failed in an attempt to reunite Alonso with Sunderland’s Granit Xhaka, with Chelsea unwilling to meet the Black Cats’ valuation of a player who will turn 34 in September.Explore more on these topicsChelseaXabi AlonsoTransfer windowAston VillanewsShareReuse this content

title: Iraola determined to keep Mac Allister and Alisson at Liverpool this season | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Andoni Iraola said Liverpool must strengthen a squad that will be without Hugo Ekitiké and Conor Bradley for several more months
Author: Andy Hunter
Headline: Andoni Iraola said Liverpool must strengthen a squad that will be without Hugo Ekitiké and Conor Bradley for several more months
Content: Alexis Mac Allister (left) and Alisson, shown enjoying Liverpool’s title celebrations 2025, are both part of next season’s plans.Photograph: Adam Vaughan/EPAView image in fullscreenAlexis Mac Allister (left) and Alisson, shown enjoying Liverpool’s title celebrations 2025, are both part of next season’s plans.Photograph: Adam Vaughan/EPALiverpoolIraola determined to keep Mac Allister and Alisson at Liverpool this seasonCoach aims to strengthen with Ekitiké and Bradley outMac Allister has been linked with move to Real MadridAndy Hunterin ChicagoTue 21 Jul 2026 17.42 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndoni Iraola has said he is determined to keep Alexis Mac Allister and Alisson for his first season as Liverpool head coach and must strengthen a squad that will be without Hugo Ekitiké and Conor Bradley for several more months.The dust had barely settled on Argentina’sWorld Cup final defeat by Spainwhen Mac Allister was linked with a move to Real Madrid. Iraola, in Chicago for the start of Liverpool’s pre-season tour of the US, accepted the midfielder is unlikely to be 100% ready for the Premier League opener at Newcastle due to a post-World Cup holiday. But he allayed doubts over Mac Allister’s long-term future at Liverpool, where he is under contract until 2028, by insisting the 27-year-old is part of next season’s plans.Liverpool owner FSG in talks to sell 30% stake to Amit Bhatia consortium for £1.35bnRead moreIraola said: “I think Alexis has been one of the best players at the club for the last few years, and he has had a very good World Cup, so it is normal that other clubs want our best players. It always happens in other markets. But I want to keep my good players. I’m looking forward more to signing new players rather than losing the ones we have here.”Alisson is another established figure whoseLiverpoolfuture has been questioned, with the Brazil international strongly linked with Juventus at the end of last season. Iraola has not met Alisson or Mac Allister in person due to their World Cup commitments but has spoken with both, and guaranteed the former will remain a Liverpool player next season.“I am very happy with the situation we have right now in the goalkeeping area,” said Iraola. “We have Alisson, we have Giorgi [Mamardashvili]. Now we are going to send Armin [Pecsi] on loan but we have the guarantee of the three keepers here. Freddie [Woodman] played some games last season, I think it is a guarantee for us.“Alisson is also someone in a changing room that has lots of references, in terms of experience. He is someone that definitely will help us in this process of adaptation of the new signings and the young players. We need the references like Virgil [van Dijk], Alisson, Joe Gomez; players that have been here for a long time, and I am sure we will have this help.”Liverpool have brought a young squad on their US tour, which currently involves a training camp at Chicago Fire before the first game against Sunderland in Nashville on Saturday. Florian Wirtz, Alexander Isak and Ryan Gravenberch will join the tour next week when Van Dijk, Cody Gakpo and Alisson will begin their pre-season training regimes back on Merseyside.Iraola admitted Liverpool are “very thin” in terms of defensive numbers and while a fit Bradley would be considered first choice at right-back, his recovery from a serious knee injury sustained in January means the club could be forced into the market.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Stefan [Bajcetic] and Wataru [Endo] are close to training with the group,” he said. “With the others [injured players], we’ll have to wait more. The closest one to returning after that is [Giovanni] Leoni. Conor and Hugo are way off. We are talking about months.”Explore more on these topicsLiverpoolAndoni IraolanewsShareReuse this content

title: Liverpool owner FSG in talks to sell 30% stake to Amit Bhatia consortium for £1.35bn | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Liverpool’s owner, Fenway Sports Group, is negotiating a deal to sell a 30% stake in the club to a consortium led by Amit Bhatia for £1.35bn
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: Liverpool’s owner, Fenway Sports Group, is negotiating a deal to sell a 30% stake in the club to a consortium led by Amit Bhatia for £1.35bn
Content: Fenway Sports Group bought Liverpool in 2010.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PAView image in fullscreenFenway Sports Group bought Liverpool in 2010.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PALiverpoolLiverpool owner FSG in talks to sell 30% stake to Amit Bhatia consortium for £1.35bnBhatia resigns as a director of QPRMinority stake deal values the club at £4.5bnMatt HughesandWill UnwinTue 21 Jul 2026 16.13 EDTFirst published on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.20 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLiverpool’s owner is in talks over ­selling a minority stake to a consortium led by the former Queens Park Rangers co‑owner Amit ­Bhatia and backed by his father-in-law ­Lakshmi Mittal, the billionaire Indian steel magnate. The Guardian understands a sale of 30% of the club is being discussed, with an offer of around £1.35bn on the table.Fenway Sports Group confirmed in a statementto the Financial Timesthat it had begun negotiations with a group led by Bhatia over a ­potential minority share sale that would value ­Liverpool at about £4.5bn.Manchester United supporters groups in uproar over suspension of season ticketsRead more“An investment consortium led and ­managed by Amit Bhatia has expressed ­interest in ­making a strategic ­minority ­investment in Liverpool Football Club,” the statement said.It is believed that talks over the deal have been going on for three months.FSG paid £300m to take ­control of Liverpool in 2010 and is poised to secure a spectacular return on its investment. The ­Boston‑based group, led by John W Henry, which also owns the ­Boston Red Sox and Pittsburgh ­Penguins, has pre­viously held discussions over a minority sale without reaching agreement but, with the Mittal family ­possessing an estimated net worth of £23bn, there is no doubt the buyers have the resources to execute the transaction.Industry sources expressed surprise at the purported £4.5bn valuation but it is in line with recent deals.Chelsea were bought four years ago by the Todd Boehly/Clearlake Capital group for an overall £4.25bn, although £2.5bn was to purchase the shares from Roman Abramovich. These proceeds were deposited into a frozen UK bank account while £1.75bn was a legally binding commitment for future investment directly into the club. Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s purchase of 25% of Manchester United in 2024 valued the club at £4.3bn.In an indication of the ­seriousness of the consortium’s bid for ­Liverpool, Bhatia resigned as a director ofQPRon Tuesday after 19 years. The ­London-born entrepreneur has held numerous leadership roles at QPR since investing in the club in 2007. He said: “QPR has been a deeply important part of my life, and of my family’s life … I step back from my formal responsibilities with pride, gratitude and affection.”Liverpool are in the US under their new head coach, Andoni Iraola, for a pre-season tour which begins with a game against Sunderland in ­Nashville on Saturday.Explore more on these topicsLiverpoolQPRFenway Sports GroupBusinessnewsShareReuse this content

title: Manchester United supporters groups in uproar over suspension of season tickets | Manchester United | The Guardian
description: Manchester United fan groups have complained to the club over supporters being “‘treated as suspects’ after some had season tickets suspended
Author: Jamie Jackson
Headline: Manchester United fan groups have complained to the club over supporters being “‘treated as suspects’ after some had season tickets suspended
Content: Manchester United have said season tickets will be reissued before the first home game of the season.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAView image in fullscreenManchester United have said season tickets will be reissued before the first home game of the season.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAManchester UnitedManchester United supporters groups in uproar over suspension of season ticketsFans claim they are being ‘treated as suspects’Club planning to reinstate tickets before season beginsExclusive byJamie JacksonTue 21 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 17.17 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleManchester United’s official Fans’ Forum,Manchester UnitedSuppor­ters Trust (Must), The 1958 and five other such groups have complained to the club over supporters being “treated as suspects” after some had season tickets suspended as part of a move to stop the illegal reselling of seats.United last week contacted fans who used six or more devices to access accounts linked to the club or to forward tickets, as in their view this suggested the potential buying of seats to resell. Members are allowed only to forward tickets to other members, or sell them back to United.Why did Manchester United pay Chelsea £48m for Andrey Santos?Read moreThose contacted were asked why multiple accounts were accessed, to identify the differing devices they used, and for photo ID to prove their validity. “Following a detailed review of acti­vity across our ­ticketing and digital platforms, we have ­identified sustained activity associated with your account, or with accounts that appear to be connected to or ­controlled by you, which may indicate a breach of the club’s ticketing terms and conditions,” the email from United said.In a joint ­statement, ­supporter groups said: “Professional touting, automated purchasing and those who exploit ­supporters for ­financial gain have no place at ­Manchester United and deserve robust action. However, that action must be fair, ­proportionate and ­transparent. Longstanding supporters who have followed Manchester United for decades, often across multiple generations of the same family, should never feel they are being treated as suspects ­without clear evidence, due process or a meaningful opportunity to respond to the allegations made against them. The current approach has created a growing perception among supporters of ‘ban first and ask questions later’.“The Fans’ Forum has rightly called on the club to pause the ­issuing of further suspensions and to halt any cancellation of season tickets while meaningful discussions take place. We support this.”Reasons given by fans for the use of multiple devices to access club accounts includes being able to act for a group of friends or for elderly family members who struggle to use technology. United’s position is understood to be that no bans or formal sanctions have been applied and that any season tickets that have been revoked will be reissued before the team’s first home game of the season, which is against Ipswich Town on 30 August.United have applied restrictions to accounts and offered only three days to appeal once a verdict is reached, as the club believe some of these are being used for illegal resale and the usual 14 days could be used to sell tickets on.Explore more on these topicsManchester UnitednewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘The scrutiny is constant’: referee Anthony Taylor retires from football | Referees | The Guardian
description: Anthony Taylor has announced his retirement from football with immediate effect after officiating in his second World Cup
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: Anthony Taylor has announced his retirement from football with immediate effect after officiating in his second World Cup
Content: Anthony Taylor during the 2026 World Cup match between Portugal and Spain.Photograph: EYE4images/NurPhoto/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAnthony Taylor during the 2026 World Cup match between Portugal and Spain.Photograph: EYE4images/NurPhoto/ShutterstockReferees‘The scrutiny is constant’: referee Anthony Taylor retires from footballThe 47-year-old took charge of five World Cup matchesTaylor has refereed in the Premier League for 16 yearsMatt HughesTue 21 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAnthony Taylor has announced his retirement from elite refereeing after a distinguished career spanning more than two decades and 831 matches.One of England’s most accomplished officials, the 47-year-old officiated 668 matches in the professional game in England. He brings the curtain down on his career following his final appointment – the World Cup last‑16 gamebetween Portugal and Spainin the US.Reflecting on his career, Taylor said: “Officiating at the elite level has been an immense privilege, but the pressure is intense and the scrutiny is constant. The time is now right to step aside and look forward to transitioning to the next chapter of my career.“I want to express my deepest gratitude to my two assistants, Gary and Adam, my refereeing colleagues and everyone connected with the game for all their support over the years. Most importantly, I want to thank my family and friends for their unwavering support throughout the immense sacrifices this career has demanded.”Over 16 Premier League seasons, Taylor took charge of 432 top-flight matches and officiated every major domestic cup final. His honours include the FA Cup finals in 2017 and 2020, the 2015 League Cup final, the 2015 Community Shield and the 2018 Championship playoff final.Taylor also enjoyed an outstanding international career, spending 14 years on the Fifa international list. During that time, he officiated at two World Cups (2022 and 2026), two European Championships (2020 and 2024) and two Club World Cups (2022 and 2025).Quick GuideHow do I sign up for sport breaking news alerts?ShowIn the Guardian app, tap the Profile settings button at the top right, then select Notifications. Turn on sport notifications.If you already have the Guardian app, make sure you’re on the most recent version.If you don't have the Guardian app, download it from theiOS App Storeon iPhone or theGoogle Play storeon Android by searching for 'The Guardian'.Was this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.Across the 163 international matches of which he took charge Taylor was entrusted with some of world football’s biggest fixtures, including the 2020 Uefa Super Cup, the 2021 Nations League final, the 2022 Club World Cup final, the 2023 Europa League final and the 2024 Champions League semi-final.Howard Webb, chief refereeing officer at Pro Ref, said: “Anthony has been a fantastic servant to the game over many years, both domestically and on the international stage. He has been repeatedly trusted to officiate in the biggest games including Portugal against Spain in this summer’s World Cup which ultimately capped off an outstanding career.“He’s consistently demonstrated over a sustained period why he is one of the best and most successful officials that English football has ever produced, but it’s not just his on-field achievements that see him as a role model to many. The professionalism he’s shown and the support for his colleagues and the next generation of officials looking to follow in his footsteps is testament to his character.“We thank him for his immense contribution to the game and wish him every success in the next chapter of his career.”Explore more on these topicsRefereesnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘The utmost respect for him’: Alex Ferguson pays tribute to Kevin Keegan | Newcastle United | The Guardian
description: Alex Ferguson has said Kevin Keegan’s Newcastle side of 1995-96 was their best ever and that ‘Kevin carried the love of the game on his sleeve’
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Alex Ferguson has said Kevin Keegan’s Newcastle side of 1995-96 was their best ever and that ‘Kevin carried the love of the game on his sleeve’
Content: ‘We had many duels when he was manager of Newcastle, but the job he did was fantastic,’ said Alex Ferguson.Photograph: Adam Butler/PAView image in fullscreen‘We had many duels when he was manager of Newcastle, but the job he did was fantastic,’ said Alex Ferguson.Photograph: Adam Butler/PANewcastle United‘The utmost respect for him’: Alex Ferguson pays tribute to Kevin KeeganFerguson says Keegan’s Newcastle was their best team‘Kevin carried the love of the game on his sleeve’PA MediaTue 21 Jul 2026 08.41 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSir Alex Ferguson has saluted Kevin Keegan for building Newcastle’s “greatest team”.Keegan, who had cancer, died on Mondayat the age of 75 and Ferguson has joined the tributes for one offootball’s most endearing characters.Ferguson and Keegan were involved a fierce battle for Premier League supremacy, with Ferguson’sManchester Unitedpipping Newcastle to the title in 1996. “I had the utmost respect for him,” Ferguson told Track Radio. “We had many duels when he was manager of Newcastle, but the job he did at Newcastle was fantastic. He re-energised that club and he brought that enthusiasm and fanaticism great supporters can give.Joy, tears and tension: Kevin Keegan’s three-part Newcastle stay was grippingRead more“And he also brought three great players to the club – [David] Ginola, [Faustino] Asprilla, Philippe Albert – and added a bit of class to the team. The challenges we had with Newcastle at that time were huge.”Keegan, frustrated by Ferguson’s comments in the media towards the end of the tense title fight,memorably ranted at the Scot in a television interview. Despite that, Ferguson remained friendly with the former Liverpool and England striker and admired Keegan’s impressive rebuilding job at Newcastle.The club were in danger of relegation to the third tier when Keegan took over, but he saved them from that fate and then won promotion to the Premier League in 1993. Keegan made Newcastle so dynamic they were referred to as “the entertainers”.Ferguson believes the title runners-up were the best team in the club’s history. “Kevin carried the love of the game on his sleeve, there’s no question about that, and that carried right on to the fans, to the players,” he said. “That period at Newcastle was of emotion, it was excitement. They brought these great players to the club and they added to it, and it gave Newcastle – I think – their best ever team.”View image in fullscreenTributes at St James’ Park for Kevin Keegan, who died on Monday.Photograph: Owen Humphreys/PAFerguson said he and Keegan had bumped into each other at a pub near Wentworth golf club a few weeks after that season. They spent the night around a piano with all thoughts of the rivalry forgotten. “The great thing about him, he held no grudges,” Ferguson said. “That was a fantastic quality he had and we had a great night.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“All aside whatever happened in previous weeks, that was soon forgotten. I really respected him for that.”Explore more on these topicsNewcastle UnitedAlex FergusonManchester UnitednewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Reclaim football’: Sepp Blatter joins La Liga chief in call for Gianni Infantino to resign | Gianni Infantino | The Guardian
description: Sepp Blatter has joined the attack on Gianni Infantino, his successor as the Fifa president, while Javier Tebas has criticised the lack of an opposition candidate next year
Author: Louise Taylor
Headline: Sepp Blatter has joined the attack on Gianni Infantino, his successor as the Fifa president, while Javier Tebas has criticised the lack of an opposition candidate next year
Content: Gianni Infantino has not had an opponent in a Fifa presidential election since 2016.Photograph: Emilee Chinn/Fifa/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenGianni Infantino has not had an opponent in a Fifa presidential election since 2016.Photograph: Emilee Chinn/Fifa/Getty ImagesGianni Infantino‘Reclaim football’: Sepp Blatter joins La Liga chief in call for Gianni Infantino to resignBlatter attacks successor as Fifa presidentJavier Tebas says system is ‘rotten at its core’Louise TaylorTue 21 Jul 2026 08.44 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Fifa president,Gianni Infantino, has come in for heavy criticism from his predecessor, Sepp Blatter, and the La Liga president, Javier Tebas, with the pair united in the belief that he should stand down.As Spain celebrated theirWorld Cupwin on Sunday, Tebas accused Infantino of “destroying the football industry” by prioritising the World Cup over national competitions. He claimed “his time is up”. Blatter has called for “fans, players and national associations to reclaim football and bring it back to its roots under new leadership”.David Squires on … Spain winning the World Cup final in the shadow of TrumpRead moreTebas is particularly opposed to the Fifa president’s enthusiasm forexpanding the 2030 tournamentto 64 teams from 48. “Increasing the number of national teams makes no sense,”Tebas told Italy’s La Gazzetta dello Sport.“The football industry is not just the World Cup. Not everything can revolve around the World Cup. It is national competitions that support this sport.“They [Fifa] are destroying the football industry, which generates tens of thousands of jobs, for an event that lasts 40 days and involves a minority of players. We need fewer national teams and more protection for national football, at all levels. They don’t realise they are making irresponsible decisions.”Tebas believes Infantino should resign, although he admits he finds it hard to envisage such a scenario. “He has the support of the system, of the federations. There is no opposition candidate, nobody wants to stand just to lose. It is a system rotten at its core.”Scandal-proof: why Infantino’s Fifa power can’t be touched – videoAfter 10 years at Fifa’s helm, Infantino is poised to be re‑elected for a fourth term in March,potentially standing unopposedfor the third time in a row, but Tebas believed the Swiss was a World Cup win away from being forced out. He argues that had the USA defeated Belgiumin their last‑16 gameInfantino might been mired in an unrecoverable scandal.The USA striker Folarin Balogun hada one-match ban suspendedby the Fifa disciplinary committee, facilitating his involvement against Belgium. The decision was taken after a phone call madeto Infantino by Donald Trump. Infantino said the decision to overturn Balogun’s ban was made independently by its disciplinary committee “based on the applicable regulations and the specific facts”.Tebas said: “The suspension of the American player’s ban was absolutely serious. They were lucky Belgium eliminated the USA because a case could have arisen that might have cost Infantino his job. Since Belgium won they were able to bury the matter. But these things are just the tip of the iceberg.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBlatter,a controversial figure during his tenure as Fifa president, appears to regard the Balogun case as a tipping point. “The longest World Cup has come to an end with the right champions,” he wrote on X. “But on the way to the final whistle, the tournament lost its credibility. Politics must not be allowed to overshadow the game.”Tebas feels much the same abouthydration breaksandextended half‑time intervals. “Fifa arranges things as it wants, for what it wants, for its own interests, certainly not football,” he said. “So if they need a 27-minute half-time break, they’ll have it.“Hydration breaks are a lie. We have them inLa Liga, but when it’s genuinely hot. The pitches in Dallas, Los Angeles, Atlanta had air conditioning; I had to put a jumper on in the stands. It was a lie, a break for advertising.”Fifa has been approached for comment.Explore more on these topicsGianni InfantinoFifaLa LigaWorld Cup 2026Football politicsWorld CupSepp BlatternewsShareReuse this content

title: Spurs break their women’s transfer record to sign Alice Sombath from Lyonnes | Tottenham Hotspur Women | The Guardian
description: Tottenham have signed the France defender Alice Sombath from Lyonnes for a club record fee
Author: Suzanne Wrack
Headline: Tottenham have signed the France defender Alice Sombath from Lyonnes for a club record fee
Content: Alice Sombath spent six years at Lyonnes after being signed from Paris Saint-Germain.Photograph: Tottenham FCView image in fullscreenAlice Sombath spent six years at Lyonnes after being signed from Paris Saint-Germain.Photograph: Tottenham FCTottenham Hotspur WomenSpurs break their women’s transfer record to sign Alice Sombath from LyonnesFrance defender is club’s sixth signing of the summerCoach Ho says she is aggressive and good on the ballSuzanne WrackTue 21 Jul 2026 04.47 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 17.17 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTottenham have signed the France defender Alice Sombath fromOL Lyonnesfor a club‑record fee. The price paid for the 22-year-old has not been disclosed but the previous club record, paid for Signe Gaupset in January, was reportedly more than £375,000.“The league is very competitive, every game is a tough game and that’s what I’m most excited about,” Sombath said. “I saw that Spurs had a very good season last year. My qualities can bring something to the team, even though I know they already have a lot.”Melvine Malard hungry for trophies after joining Chelsea from Manchester UnitedRead moreSombath spent six years at Lyonnes, the eight-time European champions, having joined from Paris Saint-Germain in 2020.She won the Champions League in her second season, featuring twice and being on the bench in the final, and won five league titles and two Coupe de France titles.Tottenham’s head coach, Martin Ho, said the defender “fits the profile of what we’re building”, adding: “She has already shown real maturity in her performances and we believe she has all the qualities to continue developing into a top player within our environment.“Alice is an aggressive defender who is comfortable in possession, competitive in her duels and has the ambition to keep improving every day. Those characteristics are really important to us and we’re excited to help her take the next step in her career while strengthening the squad.”Sombath is Spurs’ sixth signing of the summer, after Shekiera Martinez, Kirsty Hanson, Victoria Pelova, Caitlin Dijkstra and Selma Panengstuen. The club are delighted to have got these deals done early, allowing for a full pre-season, and that Sombath’s recruitment means they have secured the services of their six priority targets.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMoving the GoalpostsNo topic is too small or too big for us to cover as we deliver a twice-weekly roundup of the wonderful world of women’s footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionTottenham finished fifth in the Women’s Super League last season after a disappointing 11th the preceding season. They were four points behind fourth-placed Manchester United, but 19 behind the league winners, Manchester City.Explore more on these topicsTottenham Hotspur WomenOL LyonnesWomen's footballWomen's Super LeagueTransfer windownewsShareReuse this content

title: Madeleine McCann’s younger brother Sean to swim for Scotland at Glasgow 2026 | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: Madeleine McCann’s younger brother, Sean, has been selected to represent Scotland in swimming at the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: Madeleine McCann’s younger brother, Sean, has been selected to represent Scotland in swimming at the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow
Content: Sean McCann has swum for Scotland at youth level and hopes to compete at the 2028 LA Olympics.Photograph: Jamie Squire/Getty Images for Commonwealth SportView image in fullscreenSean McCann has swum for Scotland at youth level and hopes to compete at the 2028 LA Olympics.Photograph: Jamie Squire/Getty Images for Commonwealth SportCommonwealth Games 2026Madeleine McCann’s younger brother Sean to swim for Scotland at Glasgow 2026Freestyle swimmer eligible through father GerryMadeleine’s disappearance still under investigationYara El-ShabouryTue 21 Jul 2026 15.15 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 17.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMadeleine McCann’s younger brother, Sean, has been selected to represent Scotland in swimming at theCommonwealth Gamesin Glasgow.McCann, a student at Loughborough University, is expected to compete in a number of longer-distance freestyle events when competition begins at the Tollcross International Swimming Centre on Thursday, and hopes a strong performance in Glasgow will strengthen his bid for selection to Team Great Britain for the 2028 Olympic Games in Los Angeles. McCann is eligible to represent Scotland through his father, Gerry, who was born in Glasgow.Mollie O’Callaghan reveals she has spinal fractures ahead of Commonwealth GamesRead moreThe 21-year-old and his twin sister were two years oldwhen their sister Madeleine disappearedshortly before her fourth birthday during a family holiday in Praia da Luz, Portugal, in 2007. Her disappearance has become one of the world’s most high-profile missing person cases and subject to ongoing media attention, while aMetropolitan police investigationinto her disappearance has been open since 2011.McCann previously represented Scotland at the 2023 Commonwealth Youth Games in Trinidad and Tobago, where he finished fifth in the 400m freestyle and seventh in the 1500m freestyle. He also competed for Great Britain in the 10km at the 2025 European Open Water Championships in Croatia.In an Instagram videoposted by Loughborough Swimming last year, McCann said his ambition was to compete at the Commonwealth Games and the Olympics and explained how he got into swimming, saying: “When I was eight, my mum [Kate McCann] took me to Loughborough Town [swimming club] and I hated it at first, didn’t want to go, but after a while, it grew on me and I’m still here now.”Speaking to the Joe Humphries Memorial Trust, which in 2021-22 awarded him a grant, McCann said: “I train nine times a week in the pool, as well as three gym sessions, totalling over 20 hours of training per week. I’ve had to remain extremely dedicated, getting up at 4am multiple mornings each week to train, since the age of 11.”Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth GamesnewsShareReuse this content
