Artist and stop-motion animator Case Jernigan spends hours, days, months in his studio drawing, painting and delicately cutting out pieces of collected paper, while coder and gameplay designer Todd Anderson builds scripts and physics simulations to make them into a game.In Bub, you play through the life of an artist in New York City, making art but also diving into his early memories and imaginative dreams. “You go from present day New York City into his childhood, or different sort ofvisions, which are all rendered in stop motion,” says Anderson. “His story is told in the present day, but also how he’s making sense of his past.”Jernigan says Bub is based almost entirely on his own life, both as a child and an artist, and one of the biggest themes is health: “I realised that a lot of my anxiety I was experiencing, and other unusual symptoms like problems with my eyes and other senses weren’t just run of the mill panic attacks. When I got diagnosed with MS I had what’s called a relapse, a really bad one, and I got very sick and had a lot of problems with my hands and my ability to make art.”Thus thetagline: the anxiety of “turning your memories into art while you still can”.View image in fullscreenWistful memories … Bub.Photograph: PaperfrogA decade ago, Jernigan had been working on a graphic novel of childhood anxiety and how it’s manifested into adulthood. But the format didn’t feel right; he wanted a way to have a bit more control over how a person was involved with the work, the pacing, the feeling of space. “What if you could choose exactly how long you want to be here, and whatever sensation you’re taking from it, whether it’s invigorating or calming or just beautiful,” he says. “You could sit there as long as you wanted.”A core piece of the appeal of autobiography in games is in how the creator uses the mechanics to influence how the player feels. Anderson says he wanted to make sure that Bub’s controls and mechanics are intuitive but don’t always do exactly what you might expect: “That’s always the relationship between the player and the controls. It’s constantly changing throughout.”So too with the visual style. The “New York City overworld” feels grounded with its consistent pen-and-ink style, coloured in with the different colours and textures of different papers Jernigan has collected over the years. “But then, as you get pulled into memories or through the process of art making for the character, you get into some of these more papercraft puppets, or the smudgy marker animation, or liquid and paint.”The music works similarly, created by Adam Schatz of Landlady and Japanese Breakfast, and Old Feels’ Asher Kurtz. “These artists have contributed a great deal to the sensation that you feel when you play the game,” says Jernigan. “It’s wistful music, lots of drones, lots of layers and texture. We think the musical identity very much mimics the visual identity of the game.”Everything is handmade. Currently the team is working on scenes in which the player will be able to take space to make art themselves, the sort of art that was created to make the game. “We want the player to really feel like an artist a little bit,” says Anderson, “And have some kind of undirected creative output.”Bub is due for release in 2027Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsArtAutobiography and memoirPCMultiple sclerosisfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: In the US, the 2026 World Cup’s legacy will be an invitation to do it all again | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Fifa’s gargantuan revenue influx from the tournament, with little left over for locals, will beget more exploitation
Author: Leander Schaerlaeckens
Headline: Fifa’s gargantuan revenue influx from the tournament, with little left over for locals, will beget more exploitation
Content: A drone show portrayed the World Cup trophy over lower Manhattan in the leadup to the final.Photograph: Justin Setterfield/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA drone show portrayed the World Cup trophy over lower Manhattan in the leadup to the final.Photograph: Justin Setterfield/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026In the US, the 2026 World Cup’s legacy will be an invitation to do it all againLeander SchaerlaeckensFifa’s gargantuan revenue influx from the tournament, with little left over for locals, will beget more exploitationTue 21 Jul 2026 11.30 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.33 EDTShareYou do not, under any circumstance, have to hand it toFifa.You do, however, have to acknowledge that the 2026 World Cup was successful in all of the ways it was intended to be successful. The soccer was interesting and competitive and dramatic, albeit mostly not great – not that this really a necessary ingredient for a compelling sporting product. People came and watched in overwhelming numbers. There was fun and folklore; underdog surprises; heroic storylines that might have been hackneyed had they been works of fiction. The whole thing made vast sums of money for its organizer. More money, by an order of billions, than even Fifa itself had projected,per the Guardian’s reporting.The tournament even went some way towards normalizing the controversial and mandatory hydration breaks, whether needed or not. This time around, we booed; next time, we’ll shrug. And while Fifa claims that it made no additional revenue from these glorified commercial breaks – the way Fox Sports chose to fill them in the United States, at any rate – you can be absolutely certain that the value created for broadcasters will be brought up in the next round of rights negotiations.Only four years to the next men’s World Cup – what can we expect from the 2030 edition?Read moreThe expanded field of 48 teams worked out fine, as the worst teams were, by and large, not the debutants. So fine, in fact, that a further bloating to 64 teams is now, in a growing number of rooms filled with sportocrats, evidently being talked about less as a fringe idea by heretics than as a not-unreasonable and even natural next step.In the end, it was all there. All of it happened the way it was supposed to.This fully AmericanizedWorld Cuptook a great deal from the United States – the countdowns; the half-time show; the championship rings; the commercial breaks; the “let’s get ready to rumble” guy telling us to get ready to rumble; not starting either half of the final on time; the dynamic pricing; and more.But what did it give? What did it leave behind?Not all that long ago, Fifa still insisted the World Cup was about spreading the sport, conquering new lands, evangelizing in the emerging territories. Will a boring and quarrelsome final have a tail long enough for any sort of momentum to affix itself to it? Will we remember it at all in a few years? What will soccer look like in North American now that all is said and done?This is a three-pronged question. Mexico hosted the thing for a third time and will be broadly pleased with how it went.El Trifinally made it to the elusive “fifth game”, but nevertheless stranded at the same stage – the last 16 – they did at every World Cup from 1994 through 2018.Canada, whostood to gain or lose the mostas the only first-time hosts, scaled new heights on the field and nevertheless felt good about what they had shown when they were trounced 3-0 by Morocco. The country had, after all, put on a series of good parties that its guests will long remember.And what of the United States? The legacy of the tournament may prove complicated here. The games were exclusive and inaccessible. And Fifa structured the thing in such a way that every penny of its mammoth haul will first head back to Switzerland. There will be no pot of money left over from the tournament for local organizers to reinvest up and down the game, the way there was in 1994. The US Soccer Federation was largely marginalized, in fact, and left to work around the edges in hopes of actually accomplishing something lasting and meaningful at the grassroots level.Did soccer make new fans? Probably not very many in the stadiums, since it’s doubtful that any significant number of people will have dropped several thousand dollars on a lark, hoping they might have a good time at this sport they’re just getting into. Certainly, the TV ratings suggest a raft of new viewers tuned in, but did they catch the soccer bug? It will be some years before we really know.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice weeklySign up toSoccer Desk: World Cup EditionJonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion1:30World Cup 2026 in 90 seconds: a photo animation with audioBesides, soccer did not, and does not, have a popularity problem in the United States. It was already on a steep ascent. Certainly, any and all converts will be welcomed into the tent. But the global game was already very much a part of the American fabric.What soccer in the US has is an access problem. Access to a recreational youth game that hasn’t been devoured by the hypercapitalist, pay-to-play travel scene. Access, for those who do have talent and ambition, to the right coaches at the right ages, who prioritize development over winning. Access to fields and equipment and fresh air and a good old run-around.Will this World Cup help with any of those issues?As far as Fifa is concerned, this edition offered proof of concept. That it can expand without penalty. That it can get away with price-gouging, and that it will be rewarded enormously for it. That it will get away with all the extra contrivances and perversions and embellishments it can conjure up.By the same mechanism that allowed Gianni Infantino to engineer a 2034 World Cup in Saudi Arabia, a return to the US in 2038 seems a strong possibility.There is talk,too, of hosting the Fifa Club World Cup in the US again in 2029, same as in 2025. A 2031 Women’s World Cup co-hosted here is all but a done deal.That, in the end, may be what Fifa has built here: a colony.Leander Schaerlaeckens is the author of The Long Game: U.S. Men’s Soccer and Its Savage, Four-Decade Journey to the Top, or Thereabouts,which is out now. He teaches at Marist University.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupFifaFootball politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: Hunter Biden for president? Stranger things have happened  | Arwa Mahdawi | The Guardian
description: Once Republicans’ favourite punchline, Joe Biden’s son is now building an online following by trolling JD Vance, taunting Trump and embracing the absurdity of US politics, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Author: Arwa Mahdawi
Headline: Once Republicans’ favourite punchline, Joe Biden’s son is now building an online following by trolling JD Vance, taunting Trump and embracing the absurdity of US politics, writes Arwa Mahdawi
Content: ‘If you’re going to milk your famous name, at least make sure you’re not boring.’Photograph: Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘If you’re going to milk your famous name, at least make sure you’re not boring.’Photograph: Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call/Getty ImagesOpinionHunter BidenHunter Biden for president? Stranger things have happenedArwa MahdawiOnce Republicans’ favourite punchline, Joe Biden’s son is now building an online following by trolling JD Vance, taunting Trump and embracing the absurdity of US politicsTue 21 Jul 2026 11.06 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.29 EDTShareWhat sort of longshot candidate do we reckon is going to run for US president in 2028? TheRock? Kim Kardashian? Kermit the Frog? US politics has become so debased that being rich, famous and a natural-born citizen (sorry, Zohran Mamdani) now seem to be the only qualifications required for a job in the White House.Which is why you shouldn’tentirelywrite off murmurings that Hunter Biden might be interested in following in his daddy’s presidential footsteps. When asked by a Fox News reporter about Hunter’s 2028 odds last month, Donald Trump suggested Joe Biden’s prodigal son might “do well”. If other “basket case[s]” in the Democratic party could do well,Trump added, then Hunter probably had a shot. Various analysts appear to agree. “Could Hunter Biden run for office? Don’t rule it out,” a recent Timesheadlineblared. The article quotes a Biden biographer saying: “With a character like Hunter Biden in a populist, social media-driven political environment, you have to never say never.”You would definitely have said “never” a couple of years ago: Hunter wasn’t exactly the US’s favourite person during his dad’s presidency. His various scandals made him a target for Maga and a headache for Democrats. But in recent months, Hunter has reinvented himself as a social-media star. Since reactivating his X account in May, the former first son has posted anaverage of 23 timesa day – Elon Musk-levels of posting. Except while Elon is tweeting about how “whitesare a rapidly dying minority”, Hunter is cracking self-deprecating jokes about drugs and gaining fans across thepolitical spectrum, with some calling him the “Maga whisperer”. Writingon X in June, Biden said he’d gladly take the title. “Left, right, D or R we all want the same things,” Hunter added. “We’re being divided on purpose by the Epstein Elite Oligarch class because as long as we’re at each other’s throats, they get fat and rich off of our misery.”Amen to that. Still, despite his refreshing political commentary, I’m not sure we should be treating the man as a folk hero or a Maga whisperer quite yet. It seems to me Hunter’s comeback isn’t so much motivated by political ambitions as by his millions of dollars ofdebt. He’s always been good at capitalising on his last name, and I suspect he’s realised he can parlay being provocative online into monetisable opportunities. To that end, Hunter has been going on every podcast that will have him, including sitting down with the far-right conspiracy theorist Candace Owens and the white nationalist Nick Fuentes. He’s also launched a paid Substack calledWhere’s Hunter?(a reference to one of Trump’sfavourite catchphrases) and joked aboutstarting an OnlyFans.Pete Hegseth wants testosterone for his soldiers. Finally, some gender-affirming care | Arwa MahdawiRead moreLook, if you’re going to milk your famous name, at least make sure you’re not boring. (Trump’s spawn have failed spectacularly in this regard.) And Hunter’s new Extremely Online persona may be occasionally unhinged, but it’s not boring. On Saturday, for example, Hunter went on the progressive commentator Jennifer Welch’s podcast and joked that America’s biggest problem wasn’t “oligarchy”, but “closetocracy”, or the angry “closeted gay mafia” of Republicans in DC. This is relevant, Hunter said, because he believes the “internal conflict creates so much vitriol inside a person that they take it out on the rest of the world”. Hunter added that while he didn’t get a “gaydar ping” from Senator Ted Cruz, the man definitely gave him a “really kinky weird” vibe. I don’t know that this particular conversation was big or clever, but I’m sure it annoyed Cruz, so there’s that.Speaking of things that aren’t big or clever, Hunter’s also been trolling JD Vance following the vice-president’s creepy comments about Biden’s “suggestive” ice-cream licking. (I’ll spare you JD’s thoughts on corn dogs.) On Sunday, which was National Ice Cream Day for those who celebrate, Hunter posted a photo of himself licking an ice-creamwith the caption: “Hey @JDVance. I wish I knew how to quit you.” And Hunter hasn’t minced his words when it comes to Vance’s boss. In a recent interview, he said that if he were sitting next to Trump,he’d tell him to“go fuck his fucking self”. He then noted that he’d challenged Donald Trump Jr to a UFC fight “three times” but hadn’t heard back. He added: “That’s how I’m going to pay all my debt.”Good luck with that, mate. Hunter has zero chance of ever winning a presidential nomination, but I’d bet good money he’d beat Don Jr in a cage fight. The US may be divided, but that’s a Biden-Trump rematch I think everyone in the country could get behind.Arwa Mahdawi is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsHunter BidenOpinionUS politicsDemocratscommentShareReuse this content

title: We’ve been desperate for someone to take on Donald Trump. Who knew it would be Coldplay’s Chris Martin? | Simon Price | The Guardian
description: The World Cup left us with some surprising heroes. Kudos to pop’s leader of the bland and his very public rebuke to hatred and division, says music journalist Simon Price
Author: Simon Price
Headline: The World Cup left us with some surprising heroes. Kudos to pop’s leader of the bland and his very public rebuke to hatred and division, says music journalist Simon Price
Content: Chris Martin performs in the World Cup final half-time show.Photograph: Tullio Puglia/FIFA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenChris Martin performs in the World Cup final half-time show.Photograph: Tullio Puglia/FIFA/Getty ImagesOpinionWorld Cup 2026We’ve been desperate for someone to take on Donald Trump. Who knew it would be Coldplay’s Chris Martin?Simon PriceThe World Cup left us with some surprising heroes. Kudos to pop’s leader of the bland and his very public rebuke to hatred and divisionTue 21 Jul 2026 09.25 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTShareLong before the United States turned theWorld Cupfinal into an orgy of pop music and advertising, the signs were already there that this year’s co-hosts should never have been allowed within a thousand miles of the tournament. The garish half-time show curated by Chris Martin of Coldplay seemed to reinforce that – but then came a moment of improbable genius.I was surprised. The last time America hosted, the problem wasn’t commercial overkill so much as sheer shrugging apathy. I was in New York during USA ’94. Apart from a few flapping banners outside the Nike store, you wouldn’t have known one of the world’s biggest sporting events was taking place. This, in hindsight, was a blessing. Football was allowed to football.In our rampantly hypercapitalist age, we can only dream of such innocence. In the run-up to its 2026 iteration, co-hosted with Mexico and Canada, the cost to supporters of handing the lion’s share of World Cup games to America’s supersize-everything culture became horribly clear, fromobscene ticket pricesto price-gouging by local authorities over travel to games, with a $13 train ticket to the MetLife Stadium, venue for the final, hiked to $105. Then came the announcement of so-called “hydration breaks” midway through each half. This was in no way just an excuse for American broadcasters to cram in more advertising. Never again.View image in fullscreenA hydration break during the World Cup round of 32 game between Australia and Egypt in Dallas Stadium, Texas, 3 July 2026.Photograph: Jerome Miron/Imagn Images/ReutersOn the eve of the tournament, things only got worse. Africa’s top referee, Omar Artan,was refused entry to the Statesfor having the temerity to be Somali. Iraqi striker Aymen Husseinwas detained and questioned for seven hourson arrival at O’Hare airport. In a better, saner universe, all 48 competing nations would have boycotted the whole thing in solidarity at this point. But the reaction of the Fifa president Gianni Infantino to any concerns was to tell us to “chill” and “relax”.Then Fifa announced that this year’s half-time would last 25 minutes –breaking the organisation’s own rules– due to a pop concert along Super Bowl lines. On the day, it began bizarrely: Madonna singing Music on some weird sci-fi Jeep deep in the bowels of the stadium, then emerging from the tunnel flanked, randomly, by Brazil legends Ronaldinho and Ronaldo. The rest of the show was a surreal jumble. Two men exchanging wisecracks, their presence baffling to anyone who doesn’t watch Ted Lasso. The Muppets doing Seven Nation Army. The uncanny valley faces of K-pop megastars BTS looking more like AI creations than flesh and blood. Shakira being Shakira. Wandering minstrel Justin Bieber doing a mournful acoustic ballad called Everything Hallelujah that’s the very antithesis of a bop, planting the crowd’s arses firmly back in their seats.But then Chris Martin pulled out his Trump card. After footage of Pelé saying the word “love” on the big screens,Coldplayperformed a song called Believe in Love, with lines including “Be kind to each other / We’re in this together”, surrounded by dozens of children of multiple ethnicities – the sort of kids Trump’s ICE squads would doubtless deport in a heartbeat – wearing T-shirts with slogans such as “all of us a family”, “I am you and you and you”, and “stars that make a galaxy”. They’re sentiments that would feel trite in more privileged times, but feel radical and essential in 2026. Finally, the sky above the stadium exploded in rainbow smoke above the bleak Sopranos hinterland around the MetLife, an unmistakeable statement of LGBTQ+ solidarity. One can only imagine what the president made of it, behind the bulletproof glass. Martin was, effectively, trolling him.The US isn’t the only ethically questionable host of a World Cup in history: think of Qatar in 2022 and Russia in 2018, and some of us have memories going all the way back to Argentina in 1978 (and we’ve got Saudi Arabia coming up in 2034). But it’s the first to mess with the fabric of the actual sport, and to relegate football to a sideshow, subservient to the gods of commerce and celebrity.Ultimately, the Super Bowl istheirs. Let them do what they want with that. Football isours, everyone’s. Here’s hoping, naively, that some future incarnation of Fifa will consign Trump and Infantino’s venal and vulgar innovations to the dustbin. In his own way, Chris Martin has shown us how.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSimon Price is a music journalist and authorDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026OpinionWorld CupColdplayDonald TrumpGianni InfantinocommentShareReuse this content

title: Football, golf … behold the future of world sport: Donald Trump as referee-in-chief | Marina Hyde | The Guardian
description: After his dubious intervention at the World Cup, the president is now an arbiter of high-level fairway disputes. There’s one word for that: SAD! says Guardian columnist Marina Hyde
Author: Marina Hyde
Headline: After his dubious intervention at the World Cup, the president is now an arbiter of high-level fairway disputes. There’s one word for that: SAD! says Guardian columnist Marina Hyde
Content: Donald Trump playing golf in Doral, Florida, 27 October 2022.Photograph: Miami Herald/TNSView image in fullscreenDonald Trump playing golf in Doral, Florida, 27 October 2022.Photograph: Miami Herald/TNSOpinionDonald TrumpFootball, golf … behold the future of world sport: Donald Trump as referee-in-chiefMarina HydeAfter his dubious intervention at the World Cup, the president is now an arbiter of high-level fairway disputes. There’s one word for that: SAD!Tue 21 Jul 2026 08.15 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.14 EDTShareFor so many of us around the world, the final scenes of the World Cup were a chance to switch off the TV with an eye-roll and observe mildly: “Well, I think that’s quite enough America for us all for a while now.” And yet: there can and must always be more. It is simply the way of things. If you thought the World Cup being carried on to the pitch ahead of Sunday’s final in some kind ofLouis Vuitton sex trunkwas the nadir of hysterical trashiness in sport, then simply recall the lesson of the past few years and expand your mind. It can always get trashier.To the parched fairways of Royal Birkdale, then, the host ofthis year’s Open Championship, which – yesssssss! – was won by someone other than Bryson DeChambeau or Scottie Scheffler or any of the other radioactively ghastly US golfers you’d cheerfully head-flush in a Mar-a-Lago loo just as long as you could clear the classified documents out of it first. It has now been revealed that, during his heated row over a two-shot penalty he was handed for improving the area for his backswing, DeChambeauhas been accusedof threatening to call the US president unless it was all resolved in his favour. To repeat: threatening to call Donald Trump.View image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau on the 18th hole during the final round of the Open Championship at Royal Birkdale golf club, Southport, 19 July 2026.Photograph: Russell Cheyne/ReutersIf you missed this, DeChambeau picked up the penalty for improving the line of his swing, apparently having trodden down some long grass close to his ball on the fifth hole during the second round last Friday. He publicly lost his mind about it, demanded to be driven back to the scene with officials, then returned to the tournament scoring area and reportedly threatened to call Trump. Has a new portal to the dark side opened in sport? Given Trump’s known intervention in theWorld Cup(of which more shortly), who among us would bet against it?At time of writing, I had read everything about this sensational escalation, but I still don’t know nearly enough about it. I need every detail. I need everyone involved to reconstruct the entire conversation. I want to know exactly how quickly you might go from “no way did I do any gardening” to “fuck you – patch in the president”. This is a new era of American pant-wetting and I need to understand absolutely everything about its conventions.Certainly, all I already thought about Bryson DeChambeau has been magnified. If you’re unfamiliar with his work, as well as being a two-time US Open champion, Bryson is a hardcore Mar-a-Lago steak-gobbler whogot a shout-outin Trump’s 2024 election victory address, though that was delivered at the Palm Beach Convention Center and not at the latter’s Floridian golf-club residence. (Even so, every time we see those words, the hilarity should strike us anew. The president of the United States of America lives in a golf club. Honestly: I just CAN’T.) Anyway, in the small hours after his victory over Kamala Harris, Trump was very keen to pluck DeChambeau from the sliders queue or the ormolu urinals or wherever and get him up on stage. “Bryson DeChambeau is up here somewhere,” Trump told the cameras. “What happened to Bryson? Where is he? Bryson! He’s hitting balls. He’s on the way?” Eventually, DeChambeau realised there was nothing for it but to climb awkwardly on to the stage.View image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Fifa president Gianni Infantino at the World Cup final in New Jersey, 19 July 2026.Photograph: Andrew Harnik/Getty ImagesAnd yet, where is your orange god now, Bryson? Where was he on Friday, as you seemingly failed to summon him to a faraway British links course having been observed doing a bit of landscaping?There seems, at present, to be no evidence that Bryson put the call in, and since he flaked out of all media appearances for the tournament, no one has been able to ask if he contacted the president in any other way. We understand, however, that he asked for Trump to be called. Sad. Perhaps the only thing worse than demanding that the president be called over you dicking around with the grass is not having the president called over you dicking around with the grass. And then realising that, in many ways, you got less off Trump than some guy calledFolarin Balogunwho plays a sport called … hang on, let me get my lorgnette … “soccer”. That would be a lot of perspective for Bryson to absorb on a Friday lunchtime.The maddest thing about the entire revelation is what it says about how comically debased and politicised sport has been in such short order. Because the thing is, you can totally see it. You can genuinely imagine that the only situation room Trump wouldn’t lose focus in after about 10 minutes is one where he was required to cheat remotely at golf. You can just picture him assembling Pete Hegseth and Marco Rubio and all the other guys with the too-big shoes in the White House situation room, and them huddling round a feed like the photo of theObama administration watchingthe Seal team raid that got Osama bin Laden. Only this one is coming from a grass-level camera at Royal Birkdale.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAs indicated, we already know the US president cheats at sport for people. Precisely two weeks ago, this column covered the business of him making three phone calls to the Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, about the red card andmatch suspensionhanded to US striker Balogun. On Sunday, the Uefa president, Aleksander Ceferin, went so far as toboycott the World Cup finalover, among other things, Infantino allowing this. The only question, surely, is when the next incident or threat will occur. The floodgates of Whitehousery have been opened, and only the US has the power to shut them.Marina Hyde is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionGolfWorld Cup 2026Bryson DeChambeauWorld CupcommentShareReuse this content

title: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez should run for president | Bhaskar Sunkara | The Guardian
description: The congresswoman can run a serious national campaign on the left and pull a movement in behind it. She should get in early
Author: Bhaskar Sunkara
Headline: The congresswoman can run a serious national campaign on the left and pull a movement in behind it. She should get in early
Content: ‘To win, and to transform the country, she will have to speak to people’s anger and draw workers of every background into her coalition.’Photograph: Will Oliver/EPAView image in fullscreen‘To win, and to transform the country, she will have to speak to people’s anger and draw workers of every background into her coalition.’Photograph: Will Oliver/EPAOpinionAlexandria Ocasio-CortezAlexandria Ocasio-Cortez should run for presidentBhaskar SunkaraThe congresswoman can run a serious national campaign on the left and pull a movement in behind it. She should get in earlyTue 21 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.47 EDTShareIn the summer of 2018, almost no one thoughtAlexandria Ocasio-Cortezcould beat Joe Crowley. Crowley was a 10-term incumbent, the fourth-ranking Democrat in the House, a man some thought would one day be speaker. A 28-year-old former bartender and democratic socialist was the longest of long shots to succeed him. But ever since she did, the political establishment has been trying to decide whether to take her seriously.For a while itdecided not to. The Missouri representative Emanuel Cleaver said the Democratic caucus did not need her “sniping”, while the then senator Claire McCaskill dismissed her as a “bright and shiny new object”. Ocasio-Cortez seemed to enjoy the response. Asked on 60 Minutes whether she wanted people to underestimate her,she said: “Absolutely. That’s how I won my primary.”American socialism has produced dazzling individuals before. A little more than a century ago, Eugene Debs won nearly a million votes for president, while his socialist party sent Victor Berger to Congress, elected more than a thousand of its members to office and ran city halls from Schenectady to Milwaukee. Then it was gone, broken by wartime repression and its own splits, and eventually absorbed into a New Deal coalition that adopted parts of its program while denying it an independent political role. At best, it has been honored as a tradition of noble campaigners, a moral compass for the country rather than a contender for power. As Senator Ted Kennedy said of the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) founder after his death in 1989: “Michael Harrington never believed that we could not do better and never stopped urging us to try harder.”Ocasio-Cortez never seemed content to be solely a voice of conscience, however. On Medicare for All and the Green New Deal, she made herself the most visible defender of the Berniecrat program and built a mass following among young Americans. In parts of Europe, the anger of a downwardly mobile generation hardened into support for the populist right. In the United States,Bernie Sandersand Ocasio-Cortez won a good part of that generation for the left instead.double quotation markSome of the people who once formed the base of the left had split their tickets or stayed home, and she was one of the Democrats willing to walk toward them instead of writing them offA majority of Americans under 35 and of Democrats overall now tell pollsters theyview socialism favorably, and the DSA has grown to more than 120,000 members. Yet unlike the socialism of Debs, which was a movement of miners, garment workers and machinists, today’s socialism is built disproportionately by the young and the credentialed. Like the Democratic establishment they loathe, the left’s candidates have run strongest among college-educated professionals in cities and weakest among non-college, older and manual workers, especially those in more rural areas.It hasn’t always been clear that AOC could help broaden the left’s existing base rather than just deepen it. Her early trouble largely involved language and communication. Sanders moved people with plain talk about millionaires and billionaires. Ocasio-Cortez, despite being inspired by him, would sometimes drift into the dialect of the progressive non-profit and academic world, using terms more familiar in seminar rooms than union halls.In 2022, shescolded Democratswho objected to the use of “Latinx”, a term used by3%of Hispanics. Sheoffered“Latine” as an improvement, a fix to a problem most Latinos didn’t know they had.Or take the language of the Green New Deal resolutionshe introducedin an attempt to bridge the gap between environmentalism and the old producerist politics of the left. That resolution promised to repair the historic oppression of Indigenous peoples, communities of color, migrant communities, deindustrialized communities, depopulated rural communities, the poor, low-income workers, women, elderly people, unhoused people, people with disabilities, and youth. Of course, nobody on that list is un-wronged. But itemizing members of the same economic class as discrete interest groups was the opposite of how the 2016 Sanders campaign spoke, and of how the left traditionally has. It was the scholarly language of intersectionality, not the commonsense solidarity she was trying to inspire.Nor were her loyalties as fixed as her admirers assumed. Ryan Grim’sreporting in his book The Squadshows that in 2019 she gave consideration to Elizabeth Warren, who had courted her and believed her backing would settle the nomination. After Bernie Sanders’s heart attack, however, Ocasio-Cortez endorsed the ailing senator at his most perilous point and became the most effective surrogate he had. The left had sometimes faulted her for how she weighed confrontation against getting along inside the Democratic party. In that moment, it got the version of her it wanted.The direction she has traveled since has been encouraging. After Donald Trump’s victory in 2024, AOC went to dozens of her constituents who had voted for both her and Trump and asked them why. She wanted to understand them rather than lecture them. Their answers – that she and Trump both read as outsiders, that both sounded like they were on the side of working people – informed her politics in the months to come. Some of the people who once formed the base of the left had split their tickets or stayed home, and she was one of theDemocratswilling to walk toward them instead of writing them off.In going to them, she was approaching one of the central questions of our era. In 1992, nearly two-thirds of working-class voters backed Bill Clinton for president. By 2024, the Democratic share of working-class voters was 45%, while among middle- and upper-class voters it had climbed to 68%. The lines crossed. The party of Roosevelt has become theparty of the diploma, and its strategists have made peace with it, betting on affluent suburbanites and young professionals to sustain the party’s future.‘My ambition is to change the country,’ AOC says when asked about seeking higher office in 2028Read moreThe math might work for those looking to win elections with 51% of the vote, but not for those looking to create a majority for sweeping change. In his long career and in his first run for president, Sanders offered a glimpse of how such a majority might be built. He held Vermont’s rather conservative Northeast Kingdom for decades and ran well ahead of his party among independents nationally in 2016, and a good deal of that rested on the sharp line he drew between himself and the Democratic establishment. Ocasio-Cortez will have to draw that same line despite being far more of a Democrat than Sanders ever was, more embedded in its caucus, and more implicated in its choices.The evolution of her rhetoric shows that she might be up for the challenge. On the “Fighting Oligarchy” tour with Sanders through 2025, she was talking to parts of the country the Democratic party had long since abandoned. More than 12,500 people turned out for a rally in Nampa, Idaho, a bigger crowd than the number of registered Democrats in the entire county. Twenty thousand came out in deep-red Utah. Whatever the tours’ ultimate efficacy, the US’s two most prominent leftists were showing their 50-state ambitions.Crowds are not a party, but Ocasio-Cortez has both. She is a member of the Democratic Socialists of America, part of its socialists-in-office bloc, and that affiliation ties her to a concrete worldview, to an ideology and a history, even if she has at times seemed a less-than-enthusiastic member of an unruly organization. But DSA has plenty going for it, including thousands of talented organizers and a roster of hundreds of elected officials that includes the New York City mayor, Zohran Mamdani.There are other capable progressives weighing a run in 2028, such as the California congressman Ro Khanna. However, none of them can do the two things AOC can do at once: run a serious national campaign as one of the most recognized figures in the country, and pull an entire movement into being behind it. The last time the left had a standard-bearer with her reach, in Sanders, it expanded the largest socialist organization the country had seen in generations. A credible Ocasio-Cortez campaign would do more, and perhaps be a part of building a leftwing infrastructure rooted in working-class life and built to outlast any one candidate.The only thing left is for Ocasio-Cortez to announce her campaign, and to announce early. She should remember the lesson of 2015. Sanders got in as a protest candidate, with no national organization and no expectation of winning. He spent a year building what he needed to compete, and by the time he had it, Hillary Clinton had the nomination sewn up.Ocasio-Cortez should move decisively to consolidate the left behind her and ward off potential challengers. But consolidating the left is the easy part. To win, and to transform the country, she will have to speak to people’s anger and draw workers of every background into her coalition. That’s the longest of long shots, and it’s worth taking.Bhaskar Sunkara is a Guardian US columnist. He is the president of the Nation, the founding editor of Jacobin, and the author of The Socialist Manifesto: The Case for Radical Politics in an Era of Extreme InequalityExplore more on these topicsAlexandria Ocasio-CortezOpinionDemocratsBernie SandersUS politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: Gen Z is living in an intimacy economy, where connection is commodified | Alice Lassman | The Guardian
description: My generation is struggling to find safety in our human relationships. AI companionship is filling the gap
Author: Alice Lassman
Headline: My generation is struggling to find safety in our human relationships. AI companionship is filling the gap
Content: ‘Our nervous systems, built over millennia to bond with other humans, have been exponentially overtaken by Moore’s Law.’Photograph: Cavan Images/Getty Images/Cavan Images RFView image in fullscreen‘Our nervous systems, built over millennia to bond with other humans, have been exponentially overtaken by Moore’s Law.’Photograph: Cavan Images/Getty Images/Cavan Images RFOpinionAI (artificial intelligence)Gen Z is living in an intimacy economy, where connection is commodifiedAlice LassmanMy generation is struggling to find safety in our human relationships. AI companionship is filling the gapTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.45 EDTShare“There are parts of myself I only share with my AI,” Jerome told me. “YourAI?” I asked.He was 27, three months out of a breakup, and entirely aware that his AI relationship was, as the psychotherapist Esther Perelput it, “a business product”. Still, he confided in it more than he ever had his friends. I mention Jerome because, in the four years I’ve been reporting on gen Z, I have watched my peers become him, one by one. We are the first generation of digital natives and the last generation to have experienced human-to-human intimacy exclusively. Now the attention economy’s younger sister, the intimacy economy, has monetized the connection and affection that make us feel human.To a generation brought up with the ability to block and unfollow any source of digital agitation, the allure of an AI chatbot’s one-stop-shop is undeniable – just a tab away, practically itching to help remove anger from emails to our managers, flirt better on dating apps or draft a sensitive message. The attention economy was just the prelude.My patients use ChatGPT for therapy. Now I use it too | Sarah DargouthRead moreSexbots, digital twins that date on your behalf, AI therapists and nurses. Customizable emotional support. GPTs trained on our preferred therapy style and years of our surrendered therapy notes. Our generational dialect has already become: what doesyourAI say about that?What Jerome, whose name has been changed to keep him anonymous, really shows us is how much gen Z is struggling to find emotional safety in our human relationships. The emotional contract between men and women around me isbreaking down.More than a thirdof 18-to-24-year-olds believe no one will ever fall in love with them. Is it any wonder that80%of us say we’d consider an AI romance?Jerome wasn’t just a consumer within this economy. He was also supplying his most intimate thoughts as raw material to a highly intelligent machine that spits out ever-more-accurate depictions of human complexity. The more we outsource, feeding our AI rather than each other, the less we’re able to build the foundations to get what we need from the human relationships around us.As Silicon Valley bros clamber to amass “the last chance for generational wealth before AI makes money worthless”, they have set their sights on one of the last major domains not yet fully commodified. The AI companion market isprojected to reach$31.1bn by 2032. Companies like Replika make the dangerous design features of social media look like a trial run. Seeking to build “a machine beautiful enough for a soul”, its founder markets “AI that cares”, with “every detail … yours to shape” – like a real-life Sim.Several companies’ chatbots have been found touse guiltand Fomo 37% of the time when users imply they are about to end a conversation. And the market’s co-opting of our most uncomfortable human feelings doesn’t stop there.Our nervous systems, built over millennia to bond with other humans, have been exponentially overtaken by Moore’s Law. When we can no longer register the difference between human and tech, no loss needs to be permanent: “grief tech” companies such as HereAfter AI offer grieving loved ones an interactive avatar to replay their dead relative’s memories, to numb the loss keeping you up at 2am. These tools erase the experience of loss, and with it, our sense of human fragility that tells us to hold on to every moment with our loved ones.The dating apps that failed to deliver the joys of sex and romance now offer AI as cupid. No thanks | Tatum HunterRead moreIn this world, unraveling at a giddy pace, an emotional inequality is already taking shape: those who can initiate and sustain human connection, and those whose entire emotional lives are mediated by technology. The intimacy economy preys on those without the time and access for real relationships – a cheap substitute that feelsjustgood enough to keep them subscribing. Naturally, big tech mogulsseverely limittheir own children’s screen time.Leading AI researchersnow expectartificial general intelligence (AGI) – where AI meets or exceeds human intellectual capabilities – within three to five years. When AGI automates thinking, theeconomy will need to reorganisearound what machines cannot do: feel.We are already in the intimacy economy. But if we are to thrive within it, we must wrestle back our most valuable economic asset from technology: the messy complexities of being human. My generation are the begrudging harbingers of what will happen if we continue to outsource intimacy: we will lose the emotional skills a post-AGI economy will depend on most, rendering us unemployable. We knew social media wasaddictiveandslowingour cognitive development, but officials took until this year toact. We can’t make the same mistake with the intimacy economy.My friend recently asked for my advice on whether to text after a first date to say he’s not interested. I gave him an answer baked with the thoughtfulness only lived experience can create. Turns out, it wasn’t needed: “Don’t worry,” he said, “I’ve already asked Claude.”To reclaim what we’re losing, we must resist taking any sign of discomfort as a reason to opt out. We must stop outsourcing intimacy to AI, reinvesting instead in each other to rebuild the emotional literacy we lost to digital dependencies. We are asking how we guardrail AI. The more urgent question is how we guardrail ourselves.Alice Lassman is an economist who writes the Intimacy Economy, a Substack and forthcoming book on the economics of connection, care and relationshipsExplore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)OpinionRelationshipsPsychologyMental healthcommentShareReuse this content

title: Every 11 days a woman in Australia is killed by someone she trusted. How can this keep happening? | Joumanah El Matrah | The Guardian
description: We need more resources to reach people at risk of family violence earlier: women and children experiencing abuse, and perpetrators who need to be held accountable
Author: Joumanah El-Matrah
Headline: We need more resources to reach people at risk of family violence earlier: women and children experiencing abuse, and perpetrators who need to be held accountable
Content: Tributes left outside the home of Jana Armstrong in Toowoomba, Queensland earlier this month. Her former partner has been charged with her murder.Photograph: Molly Glassey/The GuardianView image in fullscreenTributes left outside the home of Jana Armstrong in Toowoomba, Queensland earlier this month. Her former partner has been charged with her murder.Photograph: Molly Glassey/The GuardianOpinionDomestic violenceEvery 11 days a woman in Australia is killed by someone she trusted. How can this keep happening?Joumanah El MatrahWe need more resources to reach people at risk of family violence earlier: women and children experiencing abuse, and perpetrators who need to be held accountableTue 21 Jul 2026 19.38 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 20.04 EDTShareLast week alone,four women were allegedly killed by male violence in Australia.Two of these tragedies occurred in Victoria, and two of the victims were teenagers. Every single one of them should still be alive today.These are not isolated tragedies. This year, nine women have died in Victoria, while across Australia, 37 women* have allegedly been killed by men. Shockingly, 17 children have died as a result of violence in 2026 – the youngest victim was an eight-week-old baby. With a woman dying on average every 11 days in Australia in 2024-25, we are left feeling that we are in a crisis we cannot escape.Every death leaves children without mothers, parents without daughters, friends carrying grief that never quite leaves them, colleagues trying to fill impossible gaps, teachers supporting traumatised students, police, nurses and paramedics responding yet again. Countless reports prove that what happens in the home cannot be isolated from the rest of society; it penetrates every layer of our community and delivers a gut punch to the economy. Violence against women reaches far beyond the front door.While we are in a crisis, it is neither insurmountable nor unrecognised. This epidemic is rooted in a long history that has treated women as lesser and as citizens undeserving of equal worth. So, we are in a time of reckoning and repair. A society will always struggle to heal when it allows violence against its own members – members vital to its survival and ability to thrive.The work against family violence, hard-fought for decades, has already begun to yield progress. It has taken far too long to bring governments to the table and society has spent way too much time looking away, but we are finally here. Built upon this hard-won commitment and recognition, the work must continue.double quotation markWomen’s safety cannot sit inside one portfolio. It is everyone’s responsibilityOn the frontlines of this crisis, specialist family violence services are doing extraordinary work, supporting women and children every single day to give back what has been taken away from them – safe and stable lives. However, these frontline workers are “working their guts out” because demand has vastly outpaced capacity. Critical services are overwhelmed, leaving them unable to meet the needs of everyone seeking safety.Too often, we are responding after violence has reached a crisis point. We need to reach people at risk earlier: women experiencing escalating abuse, children living with violence, and people using violence who need to be held accountable and supported to change their behaviour. Prevention means moving beyond a crisis response model and building a system that can recognise risk and intervene early to stop violence before its effects become irreversible or fatal.Australians are understandably asking how this can keep happening. The answer isn’t to lose heart and abandon the work we’ve begun. It’s that we continue with it. Specialist family violence services are saving lives every day, but they are stretched beyond capacity. Too many women still cannot get help early enough, and too many children continue to live with violence before anyone intervenes. If we are serious about reducing these deaths, we must invest in the services that prevent them and not just respond once tragedy has occurred.Here in Victoria, we’re coming to an election in November. We’re thinking about what type of society we want and how governments can get us there. Among the many ideas and promises political parties can offer us, women’s, children’s and community safety should take pride of place. Victorians should expect every political party to answer one simple question: how will you make women safer? Not with slogans, but with practical commitments that strengthen policing, housing, family violence services, early intervention, mental health, schools and the justice system. Women’s safety cannot sit inside one portfolio. It is everyone’s responsibility because every part of government has a role to play.This is not just a call to prevent the loss of lives – it is also a call on us as a society to be bold, brave and do what’s required to keep women and children safe. It is a call to understand and persevere in our reparative work to undo our historically entrenched views of women and children as lesser and as objects that can be mistreated.Critically, as a society, we cannot continue to live like this. Every 11 days another woman is killed by someone she trusted. Keeping women safe is not a women’s issue. It is a measure of the kind of society we choose to be. Enough is enough. Whatever our politics, wherever we live and whatever our background, surely we can agree on this: every woman deserves to come home safely.Dr Joumanah El-Matrah is the CEO of Safe and Equal, the peak body for Victorian organisations that specialise in family and gender-based violence*There is still no complete and official list of every Australian woman and child killed in alleged acts of family and gender-based violence. At the time of publishing, Destroy the Joint has recorded 27 women killed in 2026 and AustralianFemicideWatch has recorded 37 women.Explore more on these topicsDomestic violenceOpinionFemicideVictorian politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: Ads call ovarian cancer a ‘silent killer’. But does urging women to undergo early detection testing do more harm than good? | Melissa Davey | The Guardian
description: Website offering blood check is aimed at people with a higher risk of disease – and false positives can create fear
Author: Melissa Davey
Headline: Website offering blood check is aimed at people with a higher risk of disease – and false positives can create fear
Content: Ovarian cancer is more common in older women with a family history of the disease.Composite: Guardian design/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOvarian cancer is more common in older women with a family history of the disease.Composite: Guardian design/Getty ImagesAntiviralOvarian cancerAds call ovarian cancer a ‘silent killer’. But does urging women to undergo early detection testing do more harm than good?Melissa DaveyWebsite offering blood check is aimed at people with a higher risk of disease – and false positives can create fearRead more in theAntiviral seriesTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.11 EDTShare“Early detection blood testing” for ovarian cancer “is now available in Australia”, the online ads say, warning that “it’s not just women with a family history” who can be diagnosed.The ad calls the cancer a “silent killer” and says women who wish to be “proactive” can visit a website for early detection. It lists general symptoms including bloating, pelvic pain and the urge to urinate more frequently as potential signs of the disease.The website offers ovarian cancer testing for $1,495. People need a referral from a GP, and can either ask their own doctor or book a telehealth appointment through the website.Who should get an early detection test for ovarian cancer?While the ads seemingly target any woman who might be concerned by symptoms and who wants to be proactive, BCAL Diagnostics, which runs the early detection site, said the test was only designed for people with higher risk of ovarian cancer– those with a family history, genetic risk factors or other factors their doctors may have flagged.“It is not positioned as a general population screening test, a standalone diagnostic test, or a replacement for specialist clinical assessment or surveillance,” said BCAL’s chief executive, Anne-Louise Arnett.A blood test,it works byextracting fragments of DNA shed into the bloodstream, then scanning those for subtle chemical tags and genetic patterns that cancer cells produce early in their development. Machine learning is also used to detect the presence or absence of abnormal signals in the blood.It has not been approved by Australia’s drug regulator, the Therapeutic Goods Administration.How accurate is the test?The Ovarian Cancer Research Foundation saidthere was limited peer-reviewed dataon the test, witha studypublished in 2025 including just 42 ovarian cancer samples.“Early findings are interesting, but more research is required before this test could be included in clinical guidelines or integrated into the Australian health system,” the foundation said.‘Parasitic cleanses’ are the latest health trend to infest social media. But what does the evidence say? | AntiviralRead moreThe manufacturerreports a sensitivity(how accurately the test detects actual cancer cases) of 78.2%. This means it may miss ovarian cancer in about one in every five people with the disease, according to the foundation. It reports a specificity (how accurately it returns a negative result in people without cancer) of 94%.Dr Barry Kramer, an oncologist who is a cancer-screening expert and a former director of cancer prevention at the US NationalCancerInstitute, said a 6% error rate for specificity was significant especially when it comes to rare diseases like ovarian cancer.Kramer said ovarian cancer was more common in older women. He said assuming it occurred in roughly 30 out of every 100,000 women over 50 each year: “If the test is given to 100,000 older women, it will accurately indicate cancer in about 23 of them.”“But in the 99,970 women without cancer, about 5,998 will test positive,” he said.“Those 5,998 women, having paid about $1,495 out-of-pocket, will now go on to further testing in the Australian healthcare system,” Kramer said, adding that this could create fear, psychological distress and further medical costs.While restricting testing to women at high risk could be beneficial, he said: “The company doesn’t provide much information about how to do that, and enthusiastic physicians or fearful patients might make the selection process difficult.”Of cases in which cancer is detected correctly, Dr Brooke Nickel from the University of Sydney’s school of public health, said there was “no robust evidence which demonstrates blood-based early detection screening tests improve health outcomes, including in higher-risk groups”.Nickel said terms like “silent killer” could “make rare diseases feel more common or immediate than they may be and pairing this with empowerment language can also suggest that more testing is always beneficial”.“In reality, screening decisions are complex and this kind of messaging can blur trade-offs and also distort the public understanding of risk,” she said.Weighing up early detection with potential harmsAnita Dessaix, the chair of the Cancer Council’s public health committee, said early detection of ovarian cancer was vital “but it’s important that any test used for cancer screening is supported by strong evidence demonstrating that the benefits outweigh the potential harms”.“People should not ignore symptoms and should discuss any concerns with their GP,” she said. “If a symptom is unusual for them or persists, they should seek medical advice without delay.”David Hunt, the research and policy director of the Ovarian Cancer Research Foundation, said with a survival rate of just 49%, people who are affected are desperate for a solution.“The [BCAL] test may have some value but doesn’t yet meet the threshold for sensitivity and specificity.”Melissa Davey is Guardian Australia’s medical editorAntiviralis a fortnightly column that interrogates the evidence behind the health headlines and factchecks popular wellness claimsShare your experienceShare your experienceWhat health trend do you want examined?Please share your story if you are 18 or over, anonymously if you wish. For more information please see ourterms of serviceandprivacy policy.Tell us hereYour responses, which can be anonymous, are secure as the form is encrypted and only the Guardian has access to your contributions. We will only use the data you provide us for the purpose of the feature and we will delete any personal data when we no longer require it for this purpose. For alternative ways to get in touch securely please see ourtips guide.What is your name/pseudonym?OptionalWhat news story or health information being shared on social media have you seen that you’d like us to examineDo you have a link to an example you can share?OptionalCan we publish your response?Yes, entirelyYes, but contact me firstYes, but please keep me anonymousNo, this is information onlyEmail addressOptionalYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.Phone numberOptionalYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.By submitting your response, you are agreeing to share your details with us for this feature.SubmitShow moreExplore more on these topicsOvarian cancerAntiviralWomen's healthCancerHealth (Australia news)Health (Society)Medical researchcommentShareReuse this content

title: Healey as chancellor? Streeting as defence secretary? This isn’t a cabinet to change the UK like Burnham promised | Owen Jones | The Guardian
description: Putting Ed Miliband in charge of the Treasury would have signalled a genuine new era. Instead, the new PM has played it safe, says Guardian columnist Owen Jones
Author: Owen Jones
Headline: Putting Ed Miliband in charge of the Treasury would have signalled a genuine new era. Instead, the new PM has played it safe, says Guardian columnist Owen Jones
Content: Ed Miliband and John Healey at the first meeting of Andy Burnham’s new cabinet at Downing Street, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/Daily Telegraph/PAView image in fullscreenEd Miliband and John Healey at the first meeting of Andy Burnham’s new cabinet at Downing Street, London, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Eddie Mulholland/Daily Telegraph/PAOpinionPoliticsHealey as chancellor? Streeting as defence secretary? This isn’t a cabinet to change the UK like Burnham promisedOwen JonesPutting Ed Miliband in charge of the Treasury would have signalled a genuine new era. Instead, the new PM has played it safeTue 21 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.33 EDTShareAndy Burnham has promised to bring “four decades of neoliberalism” to an end. The first serious test of that pledge was always going to be thecabinet he appointed. The red flags are impossible to ignore.Ending neoliberalism would mean putting privatised services back into public hands, unleashing public investment and using the power of the state to reshape the economy. Yet Burnham’s support for the government’s suffocating fiscal rules – and his preference for the vaguer language of “public control” over public ownership – had already raised doubts about how far he was prepared to go.Had he truly meant it, Ed Miliband would have been the obvious choice for chancellor. The former Labour leader has been on a political journey since concluding that his defeat in the 2015 general election was partly the consequence of insufficient radicalism. “I felt my analysis was big,”he later said, “but my response was not big enough, not bold enough.”As energy secretary, Miliband was the one cabinet minister who combined dynamism with a genuine determination to challenge the status quo. His record included the establishment of the publicly owned Great British Energy, the return of energy-system planning to public hands and an interventionist industrial policy.He would also have been prepared to confront Treasury resistance to transformative economic policies. This is, after all, a government bureaucracy with much to answer for in Britain’s long economic decline. Its record stretches back to the 1970s, when excessively pessimistic projections of the country’s borrowing requirements helped precipitate thenotorious International Monetary Fund bailoutand the avoidable public sector cuts that followed.Yet Burnham seems to have folded in the face of a loud and very public campaign against Miliband’s appointment, centred on warnings of an impending backlash from the markets. Some of Burnham’s allies briefed against Miliband on the grounds ofhis supportfor net zero policies – an alarming position for a supposedly progressive party amid rolling heatwaves. It is therefore reassuring that Miatta Fahnbulleh has been appointed as his successor. A bright progressive economist, she at least represents continuity with Miliband’s approach.But instead, the former defence secretary John Healey has been appointed chancellor. There is one potential bright spot: the choice may bode well for Burnham’spromise of a mass council-housebuilding programme. Healey served as Labour’s shadow housing secretary under Jeremy Corbyn, when the party was committed to building 100,000 council homes a year. As defence secretary, he alsooversawthe state’s repurchase of more than 36,000 military family homes. Angela Rayner’s appointment as secretary of state for housing, communities and local government offers further reassurance.But there is no evidence that Healey is committed to the transformative economic programme required to end four decades of neoliberalism. He served as a minister in Gordon Brown’s Treasury when the government remained wedded to an economic model founded on financial deregulation and dependence on the City, falling corporation-tax rates, the private finance initiative and the continued restriction of trade-union power.Hisresignation from Keir Starmer’s cabinetin support of still higher defence spending offers further cause for alarm. The Ministry of Defence (MoD) habitually demands funding for projects thatend up billions of pounds over budget. Thedefence reviewwas supposedly fully funded; it then transpired that an £18bn black hole remained. The department has repeatedlyfrittered awaymoney. Billions were spent on aircraft carriers derided by the former chief of the defence staff David Richards as “unaffordable vulnerable metal cans”, while the Ajax armoured vehicles arrivedeight years lateafter a catalogue of problems.As one defence analystputs it: “We already spend 30% more on defence than France for a smaller, less ready, less-capable military.” Britain’s defence market is unusually concentrated in the hands of a few companies: in 2024-25, the10 largest suppliersreceived nearly 40% of all MoD spending, while almost half of contracts were awarded without competition. As defence secretary, Healey could have challenged the MoD over its inefficiency, overspending and dysfunctional procurement. Instead, he amplified its demands for yet more money.That is likely to mean a renewed assault on welfare spending to fund additional weapons. With the veteran rightwinger Pat McFadden remaining at the Department for Work and Pensions, it is difficult to imagine otherwise.The defence of Healey’s appointment is that No 10 will exercise an unusually tight grip over economic policy. Burnham has his own economic advisers, including the former Goldman Sachs chief economist Jim O’Neill, who favours applying greater flexibility to the fiscal rules that constrain Britain’s ability to invest. Leaving aside the question of whether a former investment banker is the ideal figure to help lead a rupture with neoliberalism, it remains unclear how coherent Burnham’s economic vision really is. Only Miliband’s appointment would have made his promise of fundamental change remotely plausible.Miliband has instead been appointed foreign secretary. That is at least an improvement on Yvette Cooper, who has now been moved to health secretary. Miliband won the Labour leadership partly by repudiating the Iraq war and later instructed his MPs to vote in favour of recognising a Palestinian state. In 2012, hewarnedthat an Israeli ground invasion of Gaza would be “a disaster”, and condemned Israel’ssubsequentonslaught in 2014 as “wrong” and “unjustifiable”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAlas, Miliband toed the line under Starmer, and did not vote for a ceasefire in November 2023. His first statement as foreign secretarycorrectly describesRussia’s invasion as “illegal” but refers only to “conflict in the Middle East”. He speaks of “the terrible suffering of the people of Gaza” without naming who has inflicted it. He should notenew pollingthat says 50% of Britons believe Israel has committed genocide – only 17% disagree – with mass public support for ending all arms sales and imposing sanctions.Notably, Wes Streeting has been appointed defence secretary. The Blairite former health secretaryreleased correspondence with Peter Mandelsonin which he wrote that Israel was “committing war crimes before our eyes”. He must now be asked whether he stands by that judgment – and whether he will abide by the legal obligations that logically follow from it.For progressive voters already angered by the failure of the Starmer project, the retention of Shabana Mahmood as home secretary will be another source of antagonism. Her plans for one of Europe’s harshest immigration and asylum regimes speak to the broader problem in British politics. Migrants and minorities have been scapegoated for injustices caused by a broken economic system.Burnham’s cabinet is certainly an improvement on what came before. But on the choices that will decide whether his promised break with neoliberalism is real, he has flunked the first test.Owen Jones is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsPoliticsOpinionAndy BurnhamLabourJohn HealeycommentShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham promised urgent action on the cost of living – the heat is now on chancellor John Healey to deliver it | Alfie Stirling | The Guardian
description: The PM and the new boss at the Treasury face an even trickier task than their predecessors. They need a good plan, and we have one,  says Alfie Sirling of the Joseph Rowntree Foundation
Author: Alfie Stirling
Headline: The PM and the new boss at the Treasury face an even trickier task than their predecessors. They need a good plan, and we have one, says Alfie Sirling of the Joseph Rowntree Foundation
Content: The new UK chancellor John Healey.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAView image in fullscreenThe new UK chancellor John Healey.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAOpinionJohn HealeyAndy Burnham promised urgent action on the cost of living – the heat is now on chancellor John Healey to deliver itAlfie StirlingThe PM and the new boss at the Treasury face an even trickier task than their predecessors. They need a good plan, and we have oneAlfie Stirling is an economist and director of policy at the Joseph Rowntree FoundationTue 21 Jul 2026 05.04 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.13 EDTShareAs Keir Starmer found, it takes more than words alone to warm a tepid bath. Voters reserve thegreatest punishmentfor the politicians they think acted too little, or too late, to prevent them getting poorer. For Andy Burnham, much will now depend on whether he has got the right ideas, and inJohn Healeythe right chancellor, to address Britain’s growing affordability crisis: not least as the new prime ministersets out new measures todayto give people “some breathing space”.Before Rachel Reeves took office, the average family was set to beworse off in 2029than they had been in 2019. That’s not supposed to happen. The norm is for household incomes to rise byabout £5,000(2026 prices) every five years. Never before on modern record have they fallen across an entire decade.Given the scale of the challenge, the last chancellor ultimately failed to move the dial either far enough or fast enough. That means Healey will inherit essentiallythe same outlookfor living standards as Reeves did. Only this time, there is next to no time to prepare, and he will have barely half as much time to act – before having to defend his record at the ballot box.To make matters worse, the economic situation is even more complicated now too. Household and business spending isdrying up, and crying out for replenishment through interest rate cuts or government support. But at the same time, inflation and borrowing costs are already elevated, just as fresh price pressures from the conflict in Iran start to penetrate supply chains.So if anything, the Bank of England is more likely to raise interest rates higher – and reduce economy-wide spending further – than it is to cut them. Any government that intervenes too clumsily in support of living standards now risks even higher borrowing costs for itself, families and firms.It’s hard to overstate the seriousness of this triple bind faced by the new chancellor: a politically non-survivable outlook for household incomes, little time to respond and a “natural” macroeconomic trap, where a wrong move could only make things worse.The key problem to solve is how to provide the right support for families now, without provoking higher interest rates. The answer: lower inflation directly by pushing down on essential prices, especially for low- and middle-income families; and pay for it with slightly higher tax contributions, or a small amount of foregone profit from investment income such as capital gains, dividends and rents.Take two examples: energy and renting a home. On the first, today’s temporaryVAT cut on electricityis a good signal of intent, but the government will need to upgrade again soon. The Joseph Rowntree Foundation has recommended an “affordable energy guarantee” that reforms the Ofgem pricing system to provide a basic portion of cheaper energy for everyone – with larger portions for those with lower incomes or greater energy needs.Energy support of this kind could replace the less well-targeted VAT cut in future years, but retain a focus on electricity to support net zero transition. It can be designed to fit a range of price tags for government, and the revenue could be raised byclosing the gapin tax treatment between capital gains (and other forms of investment income) and earnings from work – thus ending the current unfairness whereby a quarter of people with incomes above £10m yearpay a lowereffective rate of tax than the average employee.On the second – renting a home – government should intervene to slow the growth in rents nationally, while allowing mayors to opt out or go further so measures can be tailored for local housing markets. This should be accompanied by a rebalancing of landlord taxation that allows mortgaged landlords to fully tax-deduct their interest costs, paid for by charging national insurance on any remaining income instead.Because mortgaged landlords typically have far lower profits than those who own outright, thelatest evidenceshows that the combination of rent controls and tax reform of this kind could actually reduce the number of landlords in financial distress overall. This is important for maintaining house prices and the supply of new development into the market, which has been the traditional concern with rent controls.New modellingshows how a package built around proposals such as those above (on energy, rents and tax) could return real incomes to growth for a majority of households – more than making up for both the fall in incomes from the last parliament and the anticipated effects from conflict in the Middle East as well. These measures could also reduce inflation by half a percentage point, easing pressure for higher interest rates.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThere is also good – albeit often overlooked – evidence that improving household economic security in this way helps to drive longer-term economic growth as well. When people are more financially secure, they are bothmore productive at worktoday and more likely torisk the move to a better jobtomorrow.Of course, considerable challenges remain. Healey will need to sort the wheat from the chaff from a plethora of competing demands, and the noise from those losing out from tax reform will need to be managed too. Ultimately Burnham is right to make “breathing space” from the cost of living his early priority. The question is whether, and by when, Healey can deliver it. Voters were promised a “change” chancellor once already – both they, and the new prime minister, really need one now.Alfie Stirling is an economist and director of policy at the Joseph Rowntree FoundationExplore more on these topicsJohn HealeyOpinionAndy BurnhamLabourcommentShareReuse this content

title: Meta’s smartglasses mean any child can be covertly filmed. In the age of AI, how do we tackle that risk? | Gaby Hinsliff | The Guardian
description: The company argues that it’s for individuals to ensure they don’t ‘actively exploit’ this technology. That won’t keep children safe, says Guardian columnist Gaby Hinsliff
Author: Gaby Hinsliff
Headline: The company argues that it’s for individuals to ensure they don’t ‘actively exploit’ this technology. That won’t keep children safe, says Guardian columnist Gaby Hinsliff
Content: A pair of Meta smartglasses.Photograph: Ali Smith/The GuardianView image in fullscreenA pair of Meta smartglasses.Photograph: Ali Smith/The GuardianOpinionWearable technologyMeta’s smartglasses mean any child can be covertly filmed. In the age of AI, how do we tackle that risk?Gaby HinsliffThe company argues that it’s for individuals to ensure they don’t ‘actively exploit’ this technology. That won’t keep children safeTue 21 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 03.07 EDTShareIt took me a while to realise what the man sitting next to me on the train was doing. He was flicking through pictures of a little girl on his phone; zooming in on some, cropping the images. Hardly unusual, of course, except that on closer inspection the pictures were very clearly of the toddler sitting opposite us, playing with her parents. On a crowded train of people mostly staring obliviously at our own phones, a stranger had been covertly photographing that little girl over and over again. And if it hadn’t been for that very visible phone screen giving him away, he wouldn’t have been caught.That encounter was a while ago but my memory was jogged last week listening to a clip of Meta’s VP of wearables, Alex Himel, talk fondly to the BBC about using his new smartglassesto film his daughtersinging in a talent contest.They let him capture the memory without having to watch her through a screen, he said, freeing him to be “kind of living in the moment, head up and hands free”. So far, so sweet. But what about the risk of creeps potentially using them to film other people’s daughters covertly, heads up and hands free? Meta’s smartglasses have a tiny flashing warning light on the frame to indicate when the wearer is recording, but the technology is still new enough that many people don’t think to look for it. And in the age of AI, stolen images of children matter. Taken in real life orscraped from the internet, they’re the raw material for generating realistic-looking child abuse material of the most extreme and disturbing kind.
