(If that sounds like a grim but ultimately fairly victimless crime, theInternet Watch Foundation warnedin a recent report that AI-generated material risks fuelling sexual interest in children, normalising violence and increasing the risk of real-life offending.)When Google launched the first smartglasses in 2013, they didn’t catch on: too nerdy-looking but also potentially too creepy, given the possibilities they afforded stalkers, trolls, criminals gathering intelligence and law enforcement in repressive regimes.But big tech hasn’t given up on the dream of connecting humans more seamlessly to the internet, and newer versions learn from past mistakes. Meta’s are cooler, featuring collaborations with Ray-Ban and Kylie Jenner, and are pitched unashamedly at parents under the wholesome slogan: “never miss a moment”. Want to capture your baby’s milestones, or do all the things you surreptitiously do when you’re with the kids – checking emails, shopping online, listening to podcasts – without taking your eyes off them? It’s an appealing sell to busy, guilty, multi-tasking parents. But the safeguarding concerns around covert filming are real enough for many nurseries and schools to have startedbanning themfrom the premises.A twist in the tale, however, is that making them socially unacceptable risks creating casualties of its own: the technology is potentiallylife-changing for blind people, helping them navigate confidently around obstacles or instantly translate text to audio.The life-changing magic of wearing smartglasses | LettersRead moreMeta has argued that it is for individuals to ensure they don’t “actively exploit” the technology, which makes you wonder if it learned anything at all from watching humans get to grips with its social media platforms. Regulatory options might include new privacy legislation designed for the digital age, forcing manufacturers to make it far clearer when the glasses are recording – maybe even with an audible warning, like the beep of a reversing lorry – at least until we’re all primed to look twice at anyone in specs. But since that would make them insufferably embarrassing to wear, it’s unlikely to please tech companies seeking a lucrative mass market.Another option could be holding manufacturers, not wearers, liable for malicious usage – though that could mean years of painfully slow litigation, concluding long after the damage is done. The nuclear option, meanwhile, would be a before-market regulatory regime for novel technology recognising its capacity for social harm, much as we have for clearing new pharmaceutical drugs or novel foodstuffs.The best option is arguably the one history suggests is least likely to happen: for a myopic tech industry to start better anticipating, and designing out, potential harms that should be visible from space. There are some things, surely, that nobody needs smartglasses to see.Gaby Hinsliff is a Guardian columnistskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsWearable technologyOpinionMetaChild protectionPrivacyChildrencommentShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham’s plans could bring the UK closer to Europe than he realises | Paul Taylor | The Guardian
description: The new PM’s priorities are domestic, but he will soon realise that closer engagement with the EU is a no-brainer, says Paul Taylor of the European Policy Centre
Author: Paul Taylor
Headline: The new PM’s priorities are domestic, but he will soon realise that closer engagement with the EU is a no-brainer, says Paul Taylor of the European Policy Centre
Content: New British Prime Minister Andy Burnham gives his first speech to the country outside No 10 Downing StreetPhotograph: Hugo Philpott/UPI/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenNew British Prime Minister Andy Burnham gives his first speech to the country outside No 10 Downing StreetPhotograph: Hugo Philpott/UPI/ShutterstockOpinionAndy BurnhamAndy Burnham’s plans could bring the UK closer to Europe than he realisesPaul TaylorThe new PM’s priorities are domestic, but he will soon realise that closer engagement with the EU is a no-brainerTue 21 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 08.18 EDTShareWhat will European leaders make of Andy Burnham, the seventh UK prime minister they have had to deal with in a decade of chronic political instability in London unleashed by the fateful 2016 referendum vote to leave theEuropean Union?The “king of the north” is largely an unknown quantity in continental Europe and has given priority to a domestic agenda of devolving powers to regions and cities and extending state control of vital industries rather than to foreign policy. He may initially spend less time on diplomacy than his predecessor, Keir Starmer, whom critics branded “never here Keir”for his frequent foreign travelto support Ukraine, build European and Nato defence ties and try to rein in Donald Trump’s wrecking instincts in transatlantic relations.Starmer had begun to restore some trust between Brussels and London after a long period of mutual suspicion and exasperation at the UK’s failure to implement fully the withdrawal agreement hastily negotiated by Boris Johnson. Buthe was cautious in resetting ties with the EUto avoid angering Labour leave voters. And the Treasury was as ever loth to pay to play in Europe.Starmer was lauded for his co-leadership, with the French president Emmanuel Macron, of both the “coalition of the willing”working on security guarantees for Ukraineand the group of European nationspreparing to ensure safe passage in the strait of Hormuzonce the US and Iran stop fighting. Burnham is likely to maintain that role, which helped restore the UK’s standing as an important player in European security, albeit no longer in the EU core, rather than retreat into nationalist isolation or into Maga’s embrace as Nigel Farage’s Reform party would do.Like other domestically focused politicians, Burnham will soon realise that the UK’s economic stagnation, declining relative living standards and latent social discontent is at least partly due to Brexit’sdisastrous damping effects on trade and investment. Even without the compelling security and geopolitical reasons for rapprochement with the EU, closer engagement with Britain’s main export market would be a no-brainer to deliver healthier growth.As a new face in Downing Street, Burnham has an opportunity to review Labour’s red lines on relations with the EU – no single market, no customs union, no freedom of movement – and to pursue a more ambitious opening. He also has the charisma Starmer lacked to sell it to the public. Burnham will find European governments receptive on defence and security cooperation and increasingly willing to welcome the UK inside its “made in Europe”drive for greater European sovereigntyover key technologies and supply chains. But they remain wary of UK attempts to cherrypick the benefits of the EU market without the responsibilities, such as paying for market access and allowing free movement of persons, as Norway and Switzerland do.I agree with Joël Reland, of the UK in a Changing Europe thinktank,who noted last weekthat Burnham may sound less pro-European than Starmer, “but is formulating an economic and security agenda which could bring London into closer line with Brussels”.Reland highlights the “striking” similarity between Burnham’s ambition for “sovereign capabilities” in industrial sectors from shipbuilding to AI, to EU plans for greater “strategic autonomy”, ie reducing reliance on foreign suppliers in critical sectors.Moreover, Burnham faces political and social challenges familiar to his continental peers. Many European countries are experiencing a similar surge of national populism, backlash against immigration and anger at the political establishment over the cost of living and poor public services that fuelled Brexit. With populists such as Marine Le Pen in France and Alice Weidel in Germany rattling at the gates of power – egged on by Trump’s White House, Elon Musk and Vladimir Putin – the new Labour premier has an interest in linking arms with pro-European liberal democratic forces on the continent.View image in fullscreenKeir Starmer with the leaders of Poland, Italy, France and Germany ahead of Berlin talks on the crisis in the Strait of HormuzPhotograph: Michael Kappeler/AFP/Getty ImagesThe geopolitical case is compelling with Putin waging hybrid war against Europe, Trump seeking to disengage the US from European security and undercutting the European values of liberal democracy, rule of law and tolerance, and Xi Jinping flexing China’s export muscles and grip on critical raw materials.Building closer ties with Europe, starting with security and defence, also makes sense in domestic UK politics. Burnham must realise that Labour is not going to win the next general election as a halfway house party that is still afraid of upsetting Brexit voters. Labourhas been bleeding pro-European votersto the Liberal Democrats and the Greens, and bleeding working-class Brexit voters to Reform.He can start to reverse those losses if he proclaims Labour to be the practical pro-European force that can deliver the economic and political benefits of closer relations with the EU without being hamstrung by decade-old taboos. Hardcore Eurosceptics won’t vote Labour anyway.There are some indications that Burnham gets this, even if it is understandably not his top priority. He needs to find a way to link kickstarting the economy with closer cooperation with Europe. The first area may be in defence industrial cooperation, joining a follow-up tothe EU’s Safe joint procurement instrument. Another way could be by accepting an ambitious youth experience programme without quotas or prohibitive tuition fees. This would offer reciprocal opportunities for young Britons to study and work in Europe that Brexit made much more difficult.Temporary visas for European students and apprentices might result in higher immigration numbers but would benefit both the UK economy and its scientific and technological achievement levels.Burnham may well feel that a departure from Labour’s red lines needs a national democratic mandate at the next general election, but he should be bold and start making the case now as to why the UK would be better off in the single market, in preparation for the party’s next manifesto.Paul Taylor is a senior visiting fellow at the European Policy CentreExplore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionEuropeEuropean UnioncommentShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham as new UK PM faces the defining test for the centre-left. Australia will be watching closely | Tim Soutphommasane | The Guardian
description: Countering the populist challenge to liberal democracy requires something of a reset
Author: Tim Soutphommasane
Headline: Countering the populist challenge to liberal democracy requires something of a reset
Content: ‘For British Labour, Burnham is their great hope for revival. But the new PM faces many of the same challenges that confronted his predecessor.’Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘For British Labour, Burnham is their great hope for revival. But the new PM faces many of the same challenges that confronted his predecessor.’Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesOpinionAndy BurnhamAndy Burnham as new UK PM faces the defining test for the centre-left. Australia will be watching closelyTim SoutphommasaneCountering the populist challenge to liberal democracy requires something of a resetMon 20 Jul 2026 22.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 12.26 EDTShareThe British love a coronation. That’s how thearrival of Andy Burnham into 10 Downing Streetis being described in the UK. On Friday, Burnham was “crowned” as Labour leader, after being the only party MP to secure enough nominations following Keir Starmer’s resignation. He is now confirmed as Britain’s prime minister.Australians probably would call this something else: a leadership coup. For months, Burnham stalked Starmer to claim theLabourleadership. It was a meticulous campaign from the politician dubbed the “King of the North”. Via a byelection in the seat of Makerfield, Burnham has transplanted himself from the offices of the mayor of greater Manchester into the House of Commons and now Downing Street.That he has done so reflects Labour’s desperation. Only two years ago, a Starmer-led Labour had won a decisive election victory. But the glow of victory quickly faded.For nearly 18 months, the party has been in electoral freefall. Labour’s voting support, once hovering above 30%, plummeted to 17% in the spring. Nigel Farage’s Reform UK has grown into the ascendant force of British politics, having established a consistent lead in the polls since May 2025.The Andy Burnham I’ve met over the past 20 years gives me hope for British politics | John HarrisRead moreFor British Labour, Burnham is their great hope for revival. But the new PM faces many of the same challenges that confronted his predecessor in office. Is it possible to halt the march of populist nationalism? Is there life yet for centre-left politics? These are the big questions for British politics.It may not yet have reached the same urgency but the Australian Labor party faces a similar test. The resurgence of Pauline Hanson’s One Nation reveals potent voter discontent over issues including the cost of living and housing affordability. Frustration and distrust are growing. For Australian Labor, Starmer’s collapse should be a salutary lesson in how the authority of a commanding parliamentary majority can quickly disappear.We can be confident that a Burnham premiership will be different in style from Starmer’s. Burnham is a more natural politician than Starmer, unafraid of flaunting his northern authenticity. Labour can be assured they have a new leader who can make an argument, connect with voters and put forward a vision.This was the central deficiency of the Starmer premiership. British voters wanted change from a drawn-out period of austerity and were seeking leadership that could fix a country many experience as broken. Yet, right from its early days in office, the Starmer government set a grim and helpless tone. Starmer warned the public that “things will get worse before we get better” – a message aimed to manage expectations but which had the effect of demoralising voters.Burnham is already going about things differently. In a major speech last month,he told his audience: “It promises a new era of possibility for Britain. Possibility for places that haven’t felt it for a very long time … Let’s give them that feeling, that ability to hope, to aspire for better.”Of course, speaking of hope and delivering change aren’t the same things. As PM, Burnham will need to find ways to generate economic growth and fix the foundations of a British society showing signs of cracking. There are questions as to whether Burnham will truly mark a departure from Starmer – or whether in substance it will be more of the same but with a charismatic face.For now, having Burnham as PM means that Labour can be more confident about taking the fight to Reform. If the change in leadership works, it may well point to how centre-left political forces elsewhere can approach the task of neutering populist nationalism.One of the reasons that political parties such as Reform UK andOne Nationin Australia have gained support is that people feel angry at the system or “the establishment”. The Burnham approach is one that seeks not to compete on grievance but appeal to hope. Yet this can’t be about tone or sentiment alone – it must also be about vision and program.When Pauline Hanson and Tommy Robinson met, hatred was a given. More disturbing was the spectacle | Hugh RimintonRead moreBurnham should enjoy a brief summer honeymoon. He enters Downing Street at a time when populist forces are stumbling. Farage has been under significant scrutiny of his party’s receipt of donations and support, includingfrom a cryptocurrency billionaireand a convicted criminal. In response, Farage has resigned his seat in the House of Commons, triggering a byelection in Clacton, which shapes to be a farcical contest between Farage and theeccentric novelty candidate Count Binface.In a curious parallel, Hanson’s credibility and judgment have also come under the microscope. Her call for a “monocultural Australia” is a reminder that she is unchanged from her 1990s incarnation. Her visit to the UK and Europe, meanwhile, has featured an appearance in a podcast with the rightwing extremist and convicted criminal Stephen Yaxley-Lennon (also known as Tommy Robinson). Not the stuff you’d expect of someone positioning to be prime minister.There is no inevitability about either Reform or One Nation winning government in the UK or Australia. But countering the populist challenge to liberal democracy demands something of a reset. It requires leadership that is prepared to reconnect with people, to provide a vision of a better future and most of all to deliver for a public that is impatient for change. We will find out soon enough whether, in the UK’s case, British Labour has acted in time.Tim Soutphommasane is a political theorist and professor at the University of Oxford, and a former race discrimination commissioner of AustraliaExplore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionPauline HansonNigel FarageAustralian politicsLabor partyLabour party leadershipOne NationcommentShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on Ukraine’s challenges, inside and out: tensions at home arise from the struggle against Russia  | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: The row over the sacking of the popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, highlights questions about the country’s military and political direction
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: The row over the sacking of the popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, highlights questions about the country’s military and political direction
Content: A protest against the dismissal of Mykhailo Fedorov in Kharkiv, Ukraine, on 20 July 2026.Photograph: ShutterstockView image in fullscreenA protest against the dismissal of Mykhailo Fedorov in Kharkiv, Ukraine, on 20 July 2026.Photograph: ShutterstockOpinionUkraineThe Guardian view on Ukraine’s challenges, inside and out: tensions at home arise from the struggle against RussiaEditorialThe row over the sacking of the popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, highlights questions about the country’s military and political directionTue 21 Jul 2026 14.04 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 18.55 EDTShareEven as Ukraine’s civilians endurebrutal strikes by Russia, thousands have taken to the streets to protest against Volodymyr Zelenskyy’ssacking of Mykhailo Fedorov, the popular young defence minister, last week after he clashed with the commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi. On Tuesday night, demonstrators’ calls were answered as theUkrainian president fired the generaland offered Mr Fedorov a “prominent” role and promised an overhaul of the army command.Mr Fedorov, widely credited for significant improvements in the country’s military performance and reducing corruption, had made a scathingattack on Gen Syrskyiafter his dismissal – accusing him of blocking reforms and refusing to cooperate. The general seems to have seen the minister asexceeding his remitand effectively taking control of units. The pair also butted heads over Mr Fedorov’s plans to improve recruitment and training to reduce thedeep anger created by the conscription system. A Soviet-schooled military officer deeply embedded within institutions tussled with a young civilian disruptor taking a more agile, improvisational approach.View image in fullscreenMykhailo Fedorov.Photograph: Fredrik Sandberg/TT/ShutterstockThe danger is that the row imperils Ukraine’s regained momentum in Vladimir Putin’s war, after last summer’s nadir. Moscow hasfailed to translate a massive expansion of ground forcesinto a sustained and decisive advantage. Its spring offensive fell well short of ambitions. Kyiv is taking the fight to the aggressor. Civilian casualties on both sides rose 37% in the first half of this year, the UN said, reflecting increasing strikes on heavily populated cities far from the frontline. Impressive attacks on logistics and critical infrastructure deepinside Russiahave led to widespreadfuel rationing, diminishing the public appetite for war, though Moscow may now be stabilising supply. Crimea, annexed in 2014 and now strategically critical, is no longer a tourist idyllbut is strugglingwith petrol, water and electricity shortages.But in an article this week, Valerii Zaluzhnyi, ambassador to London and former commander-in-chief,warnedagainst believing that Russia had lost. It is still counting on its size and the gradual weakening of international support to exhaust Ukraine “economically, militarily and psychologically”.The US president, who likes winners, was surprisingly positive after meeting Mr Zelenskyy at the Nato summit this month,sayingthat there was “a lot of love in that room”. The longer his amicability can be sustained the better, but no one is counting on it. European enthusiasm for enhanced sanctionsis wavering. Western leaders are watching the domestic crisis with concern. They want to see stability.The most important issue, however, is the judgment of Ukrainians. They have understood that wartime can require different leadership, even if they had existing concerns about Mr Zelenskyy’s centralising tendencies, populism and suspicion of potential political rivals. But they have not handed him a blank cheque. There were concerns when hefired Mr Zaluzhnyias military chief in 2024. A year ago, protests forced him to U-turn after hestripped anti-corruption agencies of their independence.This crisis is at best an unwelcome distraction from Ukraine’s military and diplomatic successes. But it also demonstrates that while war has muted criticism and taken elections off the table, Mr Zelenskyy remains answerable to the public. That accountability, unimaginable in Russia, is another powerful reminder of whyUkraineis fighting, as well as a way to ensure that it does so effectively.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsUkraineOpinionVolodymyr ZelenskyyRussiaCrimeaeditorialsShareReuse this content

title: ‘The Kardashian era is over’: TikTok’s ‘povvo king’ takes on the billionaires | Television | The Guardian
description: Shabaz Ali stormed to TikTok fame mocking the designer ice cubes of the privileged. Now he’s on the BBC stoking revolt against the superrich
Author: Michael Hogan
Headline: Shabaz Ali stormed to TikTok fame mocking the designer ice cubes of the privileged. Now he’s on the BBC stoking revolt against the superrich
Content: ‘People are so sick of wealth inequality’…Shabaz Ali.Photograph: Georgie WildingView image in fullscreen‘People are so sick of wealth inequality’…Shabaz Ali.Photograph: Georgie WildingTelevision‘The Kardashian era is over’: TikTok’s ‘povvo king’ takes on the billionairesShabaz Ali stormed to TikTok fame mocking the designer ice cubes of the privileged. Now he’s on the BBC stoking revolt against the superrichMichael HoganTue 21 Jul 2026 09.26 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 13.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIt was ice cubes that tipped Shabaz Ali over the edge. “A woman online spent her entire day making content about ‘designer ice cubes’,” he recalls. “She’d freeze flavoured waters or fruit juices and show the whole process in tedious detail. I was like, ‘There’s no way she’s got a proper job or has ever struggled for money. Only someone wealthy would have the time to do this.’ It vexed me, so on a whim, I made a reaction video saying ‘On today’s episode of I’m Rich, You’re Poor …’ Soon my TikToks were getting 30m views and thousands of comments. It blew my mind.”Ali, AKA @ShabazSays, has since become a cult celebrity for his sarcastic commentary on how the other half live. His scathing takedowns of the “rich and ridiculous” have won him more than 4 million followers. Now he’s transferring his viral fame to our TV screens.It all began when former chemistry teacher Ali, 32, took to his bedroom in Blackburn during lockdown and amused himself by mocking the smugly privileged. In his crosshairs were those who humblebrag about their perfect lives and woo-woo habits. Tongue firmly in cheek, he styled himself as “King of the Povvos” and leader of the “#PovvoGang”.Allow TikTok content?This article includes content provided byTikTok. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continue“It was reclaiming an insult,” he says. “I grew up working class and heard ‘povvo’ so much. The community we’ve built is warm, honest and self-deprecating. They’re saying, ‘We don’t have much but at least we’re here’. Povvo is a badge of honour now.”The disconnect between influencers and their audience is increasingly jarring. “They post about bespoke Bentleys and private jets. Who are you making this for? [US socialite] Becca Bloom really bothers me. Millions of people watch her cats eating out of £20,000 bowls. She has a £100k Louis Vuitton trunk full of Hermès bags. Yet her followers genuinely think they’re friends.”In Ali’s corner of the internet, a backlash was brewing: “People were tagging me in dozens of videos per day, pointing out tone-deaf displays of wealth.” He calls it “a movement” that is helping to move the dial on unrealistic standards. “There’s been a massive shift in the last few years,” says Ali. “The Kardashian era is over. We don’t want airbrushed reality any more. We want it raw, authentic and unfiltered.”double quotation markThe illusion of working hard and getting rewarded has been shattered. Young people dream of opulence but can’t have itIn these days of doomscrolling, aspirational figures fill a void. “As a teacher, I saw how social media warped teenagers’ perception of the world. Wealth influencers, trad wives and Andrew Tate provide something that’s missing from their lives. The illusion of working hard and getting your reward has been shattered. They’re seeing their parents and teachers struggle. Young people dream of opulence but can’t have it. They’d say, ‘I don’t need to study, I’m going into business. Richard Branson didn’t do his GCSEs.’ I was like, ‘He’s one person!’ There are 3,400 billionaires in a global population of 8.3 billion. That’s a lot of disappointed people.”Amid the cost-of-living crisis, his videos provided a reality check. “I was telling my students, ‘This lavish stuff online isn’t right. It’s not real’. That’s why I started taking the mickey. It snowballed.”His BBC documentary, Shabaz Asks: Are Billionaires Bad?, finds Ali investigating extreme wealth and its real-world impact. With his trademark playful wit, he asks whether the mega-rich are a symptom of a broken culture or if they can be a force for good.Allow TikTok content?This article includes content provided byTikTok. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueWealth hoarding gives him “the ick”. “What annoys me most is that the gap is widening,” he says. “We’re getting more billionaires, who are finding more tax loopholes and ways to hoard assets. I genuinely think 90% of them don’t care. You can’t be a billionaire and be a good person.”In a bid to understand more, he attempts to meet a billionaire in person. Ali approaches the Kardashians, Beckhams, Trumps, Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos and Beyoncé for an interview. “Kim K definitely saw my message. She didn’t reply, though. A couple of people hearted it. That was it.”double quotation markI grew up working class and heard ‘povvo’ so much. The community we’ve built is warm, honest and self-deprecatingInstead he meets a range of people who are both pro and anti-billionaire. These include the Green party’s Zack Polanski. “He’s the only person on the party political spectrum who’s speaking out about billionaires,” says Ali. “Everybody else is too busy trying to protect their donors. Many of his arguments resonated. I’m a lifelong Labour voter but might I vote Green now? Possibly.”He spends time with Apprentice winner turned “eight-figure entrepreneur” Joseph Valente (“fascinating character but he uses a lot of hairspray”). He flies to the Colorado ski resort of Vail to stay with Phones 4U founder John Caudwell. “I hate billionaires but I liked John, which was hard,” admits Ali. “I just think he’s unaware of his own power and privilege. He told me, ‘You can’t whisper in the ears of governments. I had a meeting with Keir Starmer and he didn’t take my advice.’ The fact that he even said that sentence! You got into that room because of money. The prime minister might not have taken your advice but there’s someone whose advice he did take. I admire John’s philanthropy but fundamentally we still disagree. Your rich friends are still wankers, mate!”View image in fullscreen‘Fascinating character but he uses a lot of hairspray’ … with Apprentice winner Joseph Valente.Photograph: BBC/Hello Mary LimitedAli takes the opportunity to poke around inside Caudwell’s kitchen, hoping to find one of the vast “curated” fridges he satirises online. “I genuinely thought I was going to get the internet lifestyle with everything neat and labelled but it was like a normal person’s fridge. It was dirtier than mine!” Did making the film challenge Ali’s views? “It didn’t alter my beliefs, it cemented them. I still think we don’t need billionaires and they should be doing more for the rest of us.”He’s told that if we make billionaires pay more tax, they will leave the UK and take their wealth with him. “Let them go!” he says. “Let’s look over the hill and see if the grass is greener. It’s just scare tactics. They ain’t going anywhere. They’ve benefited too much from this country and have too much of a stake here to suddenly leave. Tax them, I say. Tax diamonds. Tax yachts.” He laughs. “If they don’t pay up, move them all to the Isle of Wight and turn it into a giant prison for rich people.”This future star’s debut documentary ends with a shameless appeal for a full series. “We’ve already started talking about the next film,” he says. “Skincare, cosmetics and plastic surgery is the route I’m keen to go down. Shabaz asks: can I turn back time? That’s the question I want to pose. Watch this space.”In the meantime, he believes society is approaching tipping point. “I think we’re on the brink of something big,” says Ali with a twinkle of zeal in his eyes. “People are so sick of wealth inequality. We’re French Revolution era, ready to overthrow Marie Antoinette.”Shabaz Asks: Are Billionaires Bad? airs on BBC Three at 9pm on Tuesday 28 Julyand will be available on iPlayerExplore more on these topicsTelevisionfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘His chords are like sheets of diamond ice’: the beguiling psych-folk of late cult songwriter Ed Askew | Folk music | The Guardian
description: The late songwriter released around 50 records, writing gay love songs and effortless folk songs from folk’s fringes. Fans including Bill Callahan explain his singular brilliance
Author: Kat Lister
Headline: The late songwriter released around 50 records, writing gay love songs and effortless folk songs from folk’s fringes. Fans including Bill Callahan explain his singular brilliance
Content: ‘There’s a holy aspect to it’ … Ed Askew.Photograph: Christian FilardoView image in fullscreen‘There’s a holy aspect to it’ … Ed Askew.Photograph: Christian FilardoFolk music‘His chords are like sheets of diamond ice’: the beguiling psych-folk of late cult songwriter Ed AskewThe late songwriter released around 50 records, writing gay love songs and effortless folk songs from folk’s fringes. Fans including Bill Callahan explain his singular brillianceKat ListerTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 06.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn a grainy television clip from 1986, a flame-haired and neckerchiefed troubadour plucks away at a lute-like instrument, harmonica under chin, the gentle ripples giving way to a voice full of longing. This performance, uploaded to YouTube in 2010, has generated only a handful of comments, but peruse any of them (“Ed the Great!”; “pure gold”) and you begin to understand the passion that Ed Askew – an under-the-radar US poet, artist and musician – has inspired over the years.“He was living in a world that was for people who didn’t fit in,” Askew’s collaborator, Jerry David DeCicca, says on the free-spiritedness of his friend, whose musical life began with discovering the 10-stringed tiple as a teenager in the 1950s and ended with his death a year ago, aged 84, on the verge of him releasing his album The Final Painting, now being released posthumously this month.Its producer, DeCicca, first fell for what he calls Askew’s “singularity” in the early 00s. But in recent years, he has captured the hearts of many more of music’s most characterful contemporary acts: from Sharon Van Etten and Bill Callahan, both of whom lend their vocals to The Final Painting, to Glasgow psych-folk band Trembling Bells who brought Askew to London’s Cafe Oto in 2017, and indie-Americana singer-songwriter Steve Gunn, who accompanied him at gigs in the early 2010s. “I got to tour with him in 2011 and felt his Jesus-y vibes,” Callahan says. “Jesus with a little scamp mixed in.” For Callahan, there is a “primordial” element in Askew’s music, that “makes it feel like the world is evolving right before your eyes when his song is playing. There’s a holy aspect to it – those piano chords constructed like sheets of diamond ice.”From the scent of a marigold to the death of a Kennedy, what stands out the most about Askew’s songwriting is his flâneur-like observations, evoking the quotidian and the tremendous. In 2011, Askew cited his early influences as Kurt Weill, John Cage and Erik Satie, while others have tried to compare Askew to the likes of Robert Wyatt, Gaslight-era Bob Dylan or the Incredible String Band. The truth is that his baroque songwriting bears more resemblance to the multiple-angled paintings of Cézanne – an artist he greatly admired – or the writing of Joe Brainard mixed with the poetry of Lorca, than it does any of its musical equivalents.View image in fullscreenUnconcerned with commercial success … Ed Askew, date unknown.Askew’s song Moon in the Mind appeared on the soundtrack of Netflix’s smash-hit series Baby Reindeer in 2024, an inclusion that was welcome and unexpected in the last year of his life, DeCicca says. But for the most part, Askew wasn’t concerned with acclaim or commerciality. “He just wasn’t wired that way,” DeCicca says; instead, Askew simply focused on writing his fine-spun songs on a day-to-day basis.He displayed that beatnik essence from the very beginning. Born in Connecticut in 1940, Askew studied painting at Yale School of Art in the early 1960s, before moving to New York for a brief spell, playing for tips in the “basket house” folk music venues of the East Village in 1967. It was here that he attracted the attention of New York-based record company ESP-Disk – usually more inclined towards free jazz giants such as Sun Ra and Pharoah Sanders – who put out his 1968 self-titled debut (later retitled Ask the Unicorn), but soon shelved his follow-up Little Eyes, which wasn’t released until 2002.View image in fullscreen‘I play music for you to feel’ … Performing in Köln in 2013.Photograph: Ullstein Bild/Getty ImagesOver the course of his life, Askew put out approximately 50 albums between labels, archival releases, live albums and self-releases, “with more still unreleased in his vault”, says DeCicca. This was mostly a home-recorded and self-published enterprise funded by house painting and art teaching, plus welfare assistance for a time – before he returned to the studio in 2011. Regarding a song on the new record titled Someone, with Callahan on vocals, DeCicca wonders if Askew is waiting in a social security office while they’re calling out numbers: “16”, “22” and “46” are delivered by Callahan with his usual deadpan delivery. “I think if most people wrote a song like that it would be about their agitation but Ed’s point of view is so different,” DeCicca says. But who knows if it’s really about social security at all, such is the impressionistic nature of Askew’s lyric writing.Jay Pluck, Askew’s close friend and collaborator, points out just how pioneering Askew’s psych-folk songwriting was in the 1960s. “It was quite brave for him to be writing openly gay love songs so early on,” he tells me. And yet: “Over the years, I noticed that no one really knew that he was gay and I don’t know why – therearegay love songs on Ask the Unicorn and Little Eyes.” Pluck mentions a later song called Lamar, recorded on cassette tape in the late-90s, in which Askew is “professing his love in a Greek paean kind of way”.View image in fullscreen‘A true artist’ … Ed Askew.Photograph: Christian FilardoWould Askew have described himself as a gay songwriter, I wonder? “I think he would,” Pluck answers – before pausing. “Althoughhecouldbe a contrarian. If you asked him if he thought he was a great gay artist, he’d say no. But if you left off ‘gay’ he’d be like, why?” Pluck laughs. For Askew, his sexuality was simply who he was, as much a part of his songs as anything else.Folk singer Michael Hurley at 80: ‘The way music comes to you, it’s like dreaming’Read moreYet for Askew, “like most obscure musicians, there was never that Searching for Sugar Man blast,” DeCicca says, citing the 2012 documentary that launched Mexican-American musician Rodríguez out of obscurity and into late-in-life fame. “What allowed Ed to gradually find a global audience was not one event shining a spotlight; it was that he lived, day in and day out, as a true artist, sharing meaningful breadcrumbs for more and more people to discover.”It’s a slow-burn tale in the age of the algorithm, and a fitting one for a musician who lived life on his own terms: “I play music for you to feel,” he once sang.“There is no trying in his music,” Callahan says. “It just is.”The Final Painting is released 31 July on Drag CityExplore more on these topicsFolk musicPop and rockfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilers | The Odyssey | The Guardian
description: Inside all the blink-and-you-miss-it moments, subtle easter eggs and poignant takeaways from Christopher Nolan’s ancient epic
Author: Owen Myers
Headline: Inside all the blink-and-you-miss-it moments, subtle easter eggs and poignant takeaways from Christopher Nolan’s ancient epic
Content: Matt Damon as Odysseus. The Odyssey grossed $264m on its opening weekend.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreenMatt Damon as Odysseus. The Odyssey grossed $264m on its opening weekend.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesNow you've seen itThe OdysseyThe Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilersInside all the blink-and-you-miss-it moments, subtle easter eggs and poignant takeaways from Christopher Nolan’s ancient epicThis article contains spoilers for The OdysseyOwen MyersTue 21 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Odysseylooks set to be the blockbuster hit of the summer, withglobal opening weekend grossesof $264m, critical rave reviews includingfive starsfrom the Guardian’s Peter Bradshaw, and reports of fanstraversing continentsto experienceChristopher Nolan’s epic tale of gods and monsters in one of the few dozen screens worldwide with the capability to screen Imax in 70mm. It also has the potential to rule the box office for the coming weeks, with few other true blockbusters to be released this summer, with the exception of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (hence the cast’shumiliation ritualof a press tour). Read on for a spoiler-packed breakdown of the epic battles, narrative twists and out-of-nowhere cameos that bring richness to Nolan’s idiosyncratic vision of ancient life.Sex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left outRead moreA monumental spectacleAfterOppenheimerwon best picture at the 2024 Oscars, Nolan knew he had a once-in-a-lifetime chance to realize a decades-long dream of bringing Homer to the big screen. “OK, I’ve now got an opportunity to make a film I wouldn’t be able to make otherwise,” he recentlytoldthe Guardian. You can see every penny of The Odyssey’s reported $250m budget on screen. The landscapes of Ithaca are breathtakingly beautiful, and there’s a creepy pall to our hero’s journey to the underworld that Nolan achieved by shooting on the black sands of Iceland’s beaches under the hazy light of the midnight sun. (Fun fact: Nolan also used Icelandic glaciers as the backdrop for the alien planet ofInterstellar.)Nolan filmed in six countries to achieve the range of landscapes in the film, which also included Scotland, Greece, Italy and Malta, and, more controversially, Western Sahara. Controlled by Morocco, the region is often referred to as Africa’s last colony, with the UN classifying it as a non-decolonized territory. “Audiences coming to see The Odyssey deserve better,” the artist and writer Mohamed Sleiman Labat wrote in anessayfor the Guardian. “Those cinematic shots sold to them as the places and moments where historical epics took place were captured at the expense of the Sahrawi people’s suffering.”The film’s epic scenes in the Mediterranean were filmed in the ocean, with Nolan eschewing CGI in favor of real roiling seas and Odysseus and his crew aboard the only functionalViking shipin the world (the delightfully named Draken Harald Hårfagre). A replica of the boat was built for scenes shot in a colossal water tank on the Universal lot. “There were these two jet engines, like 737 engines, blowing water on us,” Damon hassaid. “It was definitely about as hard as you can make water work in a controlled environment.”Technical wizardryView image in fullscreenNolan on set with cinematographer Hoyte van Hoytema.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesNolan has been a champion of Imax for two decades: in 2008, The Dark Knight was the first major Hollywood film to use the cameras for its action sequences. The camera set-up has been used increasingly prominently in his films since then. Nolan used half a dozen Imax cameras to shoot the film, all enclosed in soundproof “blimp” casing to mask their loud operational whirr.The team also worked with an LED company to create lighting rigs they called “pyrohedrons” (fire pyramids) to give the illusion that warm firelight was casting a glow over interior and night-time scenes. “I wanted the camera to see what the human eye would see,” Nolan told theNew York Times.Bringing the divine down to earthIn Homer’s epic poem, gods are “self-interested beings, whose interventions in human lives are motivated primarily by their own desires, whims, and preferences”, as classicist Emily Wilsonput itin her translation. Yet Nolan’s vision of how gods, demi-gods and mortals interact is somewhat earthier than in Homer, with Zendaya’s Athena less of a fearsome divine force than a pal and a confidante to Odysseus.Acting the fool: why have Hollywood press tours become so gimmicky?Read moreCharlize Theronis always a treat (even in her ropey Netflix fare), but Nolan’s interpretation of nymph Calypso may not feel strong enough for some viewers to work as a framing device for the bulk of the film. In Homer, the nymph is a cruel enslaver who holds Odysseus captive on her island; Nolan sees the character as lonely and somewhat pitiable. It’s worth wondering what Theron would have done with a spikier version of the role – she excels as acartoon villain.Like classicist Emily Hauser, who recentlywrotefor the Guardian about Nolan’s omissions, some viewers may also miss the Homeric princess Nausicaa. While interacting with Odysseus, she sniffily pooh-poohs him as “an unpromising man” – her barbs would have added a little light relief to the picture.Nolan’s womenWhenLupita Nyong’owas announced as Helen of Troy – the face that launched a thousand ships – rightwing commentators denounced the casting choice for predictably small-minded reasons. It is ironic that the character is afforded little screen time: when Tom Holland’s Telemachus goes to visit Helen and her husband, Menelaus (Jon Bernthal) in Sparta midway through the film, the famed beauty is near mute, with a grisly scar covering the left side of her face. It’s a bold choice from Nolan, but a little odd that we never find out the cause of her injury.View image in fullscreenAnne Hathaway and Tom Holland in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal Pictures/APOdysseus’s loyal wife Penelope (Anne Hathaway) is a steely and cunning presence despite the suitors’ invasion of her palace. “We wanted to make sure that she did not look like she was mourning her husband,”saidcostume designer Ellen Mirojnick, who dressed Hathaway in the red and blue colors of Ithaca. “She was always viewed as the queen.” The actor has likened her character to a slowly simmering volcano that eventually erupts in scenes where she curses the suitors and pushes her double-crossing servant Melantho (Mia Goth) to certain death while the palace is at war.One of the film’s most memorable scenes sees Odysseus and his men face the sirens, whose alluring song can lure sailors to their death. As their boat approaches their island, Odysseus is tethered to a mast to hear their beautiful yet terrible song while it is muffled to the audience’s ears, as if we, like Odysseus’s first mate Eurylochus (Himesh Patel), have our ears plugged with wax. The sound is “all the things you want it to be, then all the things you wish you never wished for”, says Odysseus, as Nolan powerfully leaves it to the audience’s imagination to fill in the blank.Surprise cameosView image in fullscreenJohn Leguizamo as Eumaeus.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesSome of the film’s most touching moments come from John Leguizamo, barely recognisable with white hair and a dirty smock in the role of Eumaeus, the nearly-blind swineherd that remains loyal to Odysseus to the very end. As Greek military leader Agamemnon, Benny Safdie strikes a fearsome presence in a smart bit of against-type casting from Nolan. Meanwhile, not all cameos were planned: a pod ofdolphinswho made a surprise appearance on a day of shooting ended up making the film’s final cut.Chastity, nodding and enormous pores: will women also love Nolan’s Odyssey?Read moreYet perhaps most surprising of all is the casting of rapperTravis Scottas a bard storyteller who opens the movie with a story of Odysseus’s heroic exploits at Troy. As he orates, the character pounds his staff on the floor in the tempo of dactylic hexameter (which as every good classicist knows, is the original meter of Homer’s poem). “I cast him because I wanted to nod towards the idea that this story has been handed down as oral poetry, which is analogous to rap,” Nolan said in an interview withTime.Oscar chancesView image in fullscreenTom Holland as Telemachus.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesIt’s likely far too early to make many accurate predictions about next year’s Oscars, given that many of the awardsiest-seeming films won’t premiere until fall film festivals. But Nolan is one of the Academy’s favorite directors, winning seven out of Oppenheimer’s 13 nominations in 2024 and a respectable three out of eight for Dunkirk a few years prior.The Odysseyseems like a lock for nominations in best picture, best director and most of the craft categories, leaving the acting categories as a toss-up.There’s no debate about who the male lead of the film is, but there’s all to play for in the supporting categories, withHollandandRobert Pattinsoneligible for supporting actor andZendaya,Nyong’oand Theron potential competitors in the supporting actress category – and Hathaway, if Universal decides not to run her in lead. But I’d personally lean towards Leguizamo for his turn as Eumaeus, andSamantha Morton’s staggering, scene-stealing role as Circe, a witch who turns Odysseus’s men into pigs.Being something of an outsider to the Hollywood star system hasn’t stopped Morton getting two Oscar nominations to date (for Sweet and Lowdown and In America). With the weight of Nolan’s film behind her, I’ll be gunning for her to go all the way this time.Examining the true cost of warView image in fullscreenRobert Pattinson in the film.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesThere’s a good case to be made that The Odyssey’s best sequence is its final 30 minutes, where Odysseus finally makes it back home with the help of Athena and sneaks into his Ithaca palace disguised as a beggar man. After Penelope challenges the men to string his bow – and Odysseus is the only one able to – he throws off his rags and reveals his true identity.His face-off with the suitors isn’t the merciless slaughter thatHomerdescribed – it’s a fair fight with both sides armed – but the suitors, led by Pattinson’s slimy Antinous, are no match for the master of the house. His recapturing of Ithaca is bittersweet, interspersed with flashbacks to the “fires of anarchy and pain” that blazed through Troy as the Greeks sacked the city. There’s no glory in war for Nolan, echoing the sentiments of Dunkirk and Oppenheimer. As Odysseus puts it: “We have broken the fragile bond between men.”There may be few true winners here, but Nolan’s cap to the story is somewhat more uplifting than Homer’s. Whereas the great poet ended The Odyssey with Odysseus planning “raids” to recoup his lost wealth, Nolan sees a rosier and less grimly mercenary future for Odysseus and Penelope. In a beautiful coast-side scene, the reunited couple embark on a journey west to honor the souls of his fallen men while Telemachus – who may be anywhere between 16 and 22 at this point; Holland certainlydoesn’t seem to know– stays to rule over Ithaca.Explore more on these topicsThe OdysseyNow you've seen itMatt DamonLupita Nyong'oSamantha MortonCharlize TheronAnne HathawayRobert PattinsonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Midnight at the Never Get review – Ben Platt soars in honey-hearted cabaret-musical of 60s sweethearts | Stage | The Guardian
description: Platt excels as a crooner looking back on falling for his songwriter-lover – dazzlingly played by Gregor Milne – in a show of beguiling, bittersweet nostalgia and pristine songs
Author: Kate Wyver
Headline: Platt excels as a crooner looking back on falling for his songwriter-lover – dazzlingly played by Gregor Milne – in a show of beguiling, bittersweet nostalgia and pristine songs
Content: Sky-gliding voice … Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get at the Menier Chocolate Factory, London.Photograph: Johan PerssonView image in fullscreenSky-gliding voice … Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get at the Menier Chocolate Factory, London.Photograph: Johan PerssonStageReviewMidnight at the Never Get review – Ben Platt soars in honey-hearted cabaret-musical of 60s sweetheartsMenier Chocolate Factory, LondonPlatt excels as a crooner looking back on falling for his songwriter-lover – dazzlingly played by Gregor Milne – in a show of beguiling, bittersweet nostalgia and pristine songsKate WyverTue 21 Jul 2026 06.30 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBen Platt’s sky-gliding voice was made for decades gone by. The Tony, Emmy and Grammy award-winning actor (Dear Evan Hansen, The Last Five Years) is beautifully cast in Mark Sonnenblick’s (KPop Demon Hunters) out-of-time love story, a honey-hearted cabaret-musical of two gay musicians trying to make it in 1960s New York.‘Being American feels helpless and scary’: actor Ben Platt on nerves, queer songs and why he couldn’t do it without his husbandRead moreWith theMenier Chocolate Factoryset up as a lamplit cabaret (set by Scott Pask), Platt is Trevor Copeland, a crooning bar-singer reliving the years he spent with songwriter Arthur Brightman, played with plate-spinning brilliance by Gregor Milne. The history of their love is told through stories shaped around American Songbook-style music, only occasionally syrupy, as the pair slip in and out of Sonnenblick’s inventions: now a jaunty knee-bouncing number; now a big-band ballad.While Platt is the show’s star, Milne is its anchor, at once believable as the troubled genius delivering his lover new songs like cups of tea, and the steady hand behind the piano, serving as the story’s pulse as well as the focal point for the flawless live band. There is a quiet power in the two men singing each other love songs, as they begin to do in public, with their clearly queer lyrics losing some audiences and gaining others. When politics comes knocking on their underground gay club’s doors, they face a fundamental choice: to protest, or assimilate. Central to their differences and their downfall, this dilemma gives grit to their otherwise largely jazz-smooth lives.View image in fullscreenDarkness seeps in … Gregor Milne and Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get.Photograph: Johan PerssonAs darkness seeps into the story, we start to make sense of our narrator’s gaps in time and memory. Director David Cromer (The Fear of 13) retains the show’s intimacy by keeping things simple, with a minor magic trick – worth keeping secret – wrapping up the story’s loose threads, though the gut-punch would hit even harder with a trim. Midnight at the Never Get sees music as both heartbreak and cure. With one eye on who’s about to walk in through the door, it reveals what love leaves you with when all else is lost: songs to sing at the top of your lungs to the memory of who you used to be.At Menier Chocolate Factory, London,until 12 SeptemberExplore more on these topicsStageTheatreMusicalsCabaretMenier Chocolate FactoryreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Social Darwinists to tech bros: tracing the long fight against equality in US | Books | The Guardian
description: Historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter Thiel
Author: Martin Pengelly
Headline: Historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter Thiel
Content: Peter Thiel and Elon Musk in 2000.Photograph: Paul Sakuma/Associated PressView image in fullscreenPeter Thiel and Elon Musk in 2000.Photograph: Paul Sakuma/Associated PressBooksSocial Darwinists to tech bros: tracing the long fight against equality in USHistorian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter ThielMartin PengellyTue 21 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 10.43 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s new book is published amid celebrations of the250th anniversaryof the declaration of independence and its truth “self-evident, that all men are created equal”. Country of Lords, however, is a history of Neo-Aristocrats, Social Darwinists, Tech Utopians, and the Long FightAgainstEquality in America.Concise and hard-hitting, the book arrives at a time of glaring social andeconomicinequality, Phillips-Fein seeingElon Musk“emerging as a trillionairevery much on people’s minds, with a deep level of discomfort, both fear and anger about what this portends”.‘People are picking the dumbest fights’: the tortured history of America’s culture warsRead more“It’s always hard to know what’s going to be happening when you have written a book,” she said. “But I think those questions about economic equality and the impact of extreme levels of economic hierarchy and concentration, and the questions, ‘Can you still have a democracy any longer? What are the ways that you can counter that? What alternate visions are there if you want to live in a world that’s not characterized that way?’, are present.”Phillips-Fein studies conservatism and “questions about capitalism and democracy”. Her first book,Invisible Hands, was a study of businessmen who fought the New Deal of the 1930s. Her second, Fear City, about the New York economic crisis of the 1970s, was afinalist for the Pulitzer prize. Hers is a major voice. It’s also unique. Not many books start with founder John Adams and end with Peter Thiel of Palantir, Musk’sfellow techno-fascist. Might such company seem unfair on Adams, the Boston lawyer who was part of a great revolution but feared what was unleashed?“I hope this comes through,” Phillips-Fein said. “Adams is very different from [steel magnate] Andrew Carnegie and [Yale professor]William Graham Sumnerand the late 19th-century social Darwinists, and the pro-slavery advocates. He’s also definitely very different from people who are saying extreme levels of economic inequality are good.” Adams, however, was “very ambivalent and skeptical of egalitarian claims … during and after the French Revolution: a deep sense of you can have too much egalitarianism.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: WW Norton & Company“In his writing, he does also give a strong kind of conservative temperament and foundational sense of the problems of arguing for greater equality and a deep wariness and sense of pessimism about a politics that claims to do that.”And so Adams takes his place in Country of Lords. Other subjects include preachers of race-based inequality from George Fitzhugh, a Confederate theorist of slavery as natural good, toTheodore Lothrop Stoddard, a hack journalist who had a huge bestseller in 1920 with The Rising Tide of Color Against White World-Supremacy, “part of a larger intellectual subculture of ‘scientific’ racism … in the aftermath of world war one”. There is also Carnegie, the Scottish-born tycoon who flourished in the Gilded Age of the late 19th century.“What makes Carnegie so interesting is that he at least allows himself to have some sense of ambivalence,” Phillips-Fein said. “He says that while the concentration of wealth is absolutely necessary and inevitable, and the people at the top … should have the ultimate say over resources, he is deeply aware that this is politically dangerous, that … it creates an unstable society in which the poor and the working class might actually hate the wealthy, and that’s not in the interest of the wealthy in the long run. Also, he has a sense that human brotherhood is lost, or some kind of community that had existed.”Carnegie’s father was a Chartist, seeking working-class rights. Carnegie’s major outlet for doubts about his wealth came through philanthropy, prominently the construction oflibrariesfor working people.“He had a very deep sense of paternalism,” Phillips-Fein said. “He was very invested in the idea of business as a romance and kind of a space of adventure and conquest and feudal benevolence. I think Carnegie’s answer was ultimately that the wealthy should have their money but they shouldn’t bequeath much to their descendants, they should invest it in massive philanthropic projects, and this was better than raising wages or other things like that … It is his level of guilt and ambivalence that I think does make him appealing, if also troubling. I think it raises a set of questions that he certainly was never really able to resolve.”The 1892 Carnegie Steel strike and its violent suppression devastated Homestead, Pennsylvania, and left Carnegie disillusioned. Now, in Washington, Carnegie Science is the Qatari embassy. At Mount Vernon, the Carnegie library hosts an Apple Store.Country of Lords is also a primer on modern inequality. In the early 1900s,Henry Fordmixed mass auto production with vicious racism. In mid-century,William Shockleywas a proto-tech mogul “who went completely off the rails”.View image in fullscreenAndrew Carnegie.Photograph: AP“He was a co-inventor of the transistor and he figures in different histories of Silicon Valley because he ran this company and wanted to maintain a tremendous amount of control, and as a result a bunch of the employees quit and founded their own firm [twoeventuallyfounding Intel] … So after Shockley Labs fell apart, Shockley started to become drawn to racist science, pseudoscience and IQ testing in particular. I don’t think anybody really knows exactly what triggered this, but he starts to talk a lot about it publicly in the mid-60s. He was at Stanford at the time. He was supposed to be teaching physics and engineering.”Shockley, Phillips-Fein says, was really “a uniquely horrible person, and probably quite unhappy as well.” If that sounds familiar, also like Musk, Shockley wasn’t unique. Linked to the meritocratic movement of the mid-century, he was “not isolated. He is actually part of what is a growing, troubling community of people who coalesce in the late 60s, early 70s, and who are really responding, not always directly, to the context of the civil rights movement.“It was obviously focused on overturning racist legislation that undergirded segregation, but it also made claims about the distribution of resources, saying the reasons for Black poverty are not innate, they reflect centuries of legal and political structures that have upheld white wealth and laid the foundation for exploitation of Black workers and working-class people, and the political disfranchisement has resulted in this distribution of resources. In other words, that’s nothing to do with merit at all.”In such soil, Phillips-Fein finds the roots of “the tech titans of today”, rejecting liberal ideals.“They are very skeptical and hostile towards elite institutions, or at least, have a very broad relationship to them,” she said. “So Peter Thiel embraces this set of ideas about competition drawn from theeconomist Joseph Schumpeter. He writes about how companies in a normal marketplace are competing at the margins … but every so often someone will come along and create a new invention, usually that winds up dominating the marketplace and changing it, and whoever does that first will completely change the marketplace.“Thiel has the same idea. He kind of writes about how, ‘I was growing up, I wanted to be the best, I wanted to go to these elite institutions. I went to law school, I had the straight and narrow path, but then I realized that all that was wrong, and actually you had to disrupt everything, create your own monopoly, a monopoly of one: nobody else can ever be you,’ and then the people who are kind of blessed with this genius to recognize how to create something completely new in the world, those are the people who will deserve to win in this race and deserve to command the resources of society.”The Country of Lords endures.Country of Lords, published by WW Norton & Company, isout nowExplore more on these topicsBooksHistory booksPeter ThielElon MuskUS politicsUS income inequalityfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘New Girl was so scary I had to stop smoking pot’: Jake Johnson on perseverance, pickleball and Prince | Movies | The Guardian
description: As Nick in the blockbuster TV comedy, the actor was adored by millions. So will his new comedy The Dink, which he jokes is a ‘sexier Challengers’, make him even more popular?
Author: Rebecca Nicholson
Headline: As Nick in the blockbuster TV comedy, the actor was adored by millions. So will his new comedy The Dink, which he jokes is a ‘sexier Challengers’, make him even more popular?
Content: ‘I was just trying to get a seat at the Hollywood table’ … Jake Johnson.Photograph: Robby Klein/Getty Images North AmericaView image in fullscreen‘I was just trying to get a seat at the Hollywood table’ … Jake Johnson.Photograph: Robby Klein/Getty Images North AmericaMoviesInterview‘New Girl was so scary I had to stop smoking pot’: Jake Johnson on perseverance, pickleball and PrinceRebecca NicholsonAs Nick in the blockbuster TV comedy, the actor was adored by millions. So will his new comedy The Dink, which he jokes is a ‘sexier Challengers’, make him even more popular?Tue 21 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 05.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleYou might assume that Jake Johnson would know a little something about pickleball. The actor is starring in The Dink, a sort of spiritual heir to Dodgeball – it has aBen Stillercameo – and he plays Dusty Boyd, a former tennis pro turned plucky pickleball upstart. Presumably Johnson took the gig because he has a deep love of the sport, a fondness for its pockmarked balls and weirdly smooth paddles? “No experience of it whatsoever,” says the actor, cheerfully, on a video call from Los Angeles.Yet this underdog of a racket sport, loved by retirees, found him anyway. At the climax of the movie, Dusty must play against an old foe, which meant Johnson had to undertake months of real-life training in pickleball and tennis, so it looked as if he could play. “It was like WWE. They call it fake wrestling. The outcome is there, but you still have to wrestle. That was probably the most stressful part of the film to me.”The Dink came to Johnson through its writer, Sean Clements, who had taken up the sport. “He started playing pickleball, and then became friends with this group of older women in Beverly Hills. He’s like, they talk so much shit, and get so competitive, it’s just so funny.” At the time, Johnson was searching for a specific kind of comedy. “I was looking to do something really fun and light, with big comedic set pieces. I wanted to make a movie that feels a little old school, there’s no messaging, it’s not trying to teach people anything.” It was just before the 2024 presidential election in the US. “I knew, however that goes, it’s going to get crazy for our country, and it’s going to be very divisive, and the internet is going to get all mad at each other about everything, and we’re all going to be fighting,” he says.The Dink was born from a desire to escape, if just for a moment. “I thought, if I’m going to make something, I don’t want to throw more wood on to that fire. You could say the world is burning, and you have so much great evidence. But can we just talk about this, for an hour and a half? Because while this is going on, there’s also life.”There is no denying that culture has taken on a new intensity in recent years. FromMarty SupremetoOne Battle After Another, movies and television are heightened, brittle and usually immensely stressful to watch. “You go: man, I know everybody is loving this, and it’s winning all the awards, but I can’t watch it. I’m already fucking stressed. Give me a break!”The Dink is that break. Dusty is “a goober”, according to Johnson. He works at his father’s country club and remains bitter about the injury that ended his promising tennis career, for which he blames Andy Roddick. When Dusty takes up pickleball, he joins a tough-talking, hard-hitting gang of older women, led by Mary Steenburgen’s Candace, who schools Dusty in sport and life. In a particularly lovely strand, Dusty and Candace develop a rock-solid intergenerational friendship. “I don’t agree with this idea that men and women can’t be friends, and that no matter what, it’s going to be screwed up with sex,” he says. “That feels so crazy old school and puritan to me.” Steenburgen is a legend of comedy, and Johnson was already a big fan. “Parenthood meant a ton to me when I first saw it. So did Step Brothers. I feel about her the way that Dusty feels about Candace. I don’t view her as myolderfriend. Mary’s my friend. I would hang out with Mary whenever.”View image in fullscreen‘I would hang out with her whenever’ … Johnson and Mary Steenburgen in The Dink.Photograph: Andrew Cooper/Courtesy of Apple TVHas Johnson seen that other great racket sports movie, the steamy, sweaty, Zendaya-starringChallengers? He has not, he says, but he defers to his co-star Aaron Chen’s response: “Chen refers to our movie as the sexier Challengers.”Johnson started acting in high school, in Chicago. He had dropped out at 15, unsure of the path his life was going to take, but went back to give it one last shot. “I couldn’t crack the code. I just knew that nothing was working. And then I did a play, and I was like, oh,thisis great.” It changed the course of his life. “I got credit for the first time from stuffy adults who did not like me,” he says.What was there to dislike? “I don’t know. Like, if there was a party at someone’s house and somebody found matches, all the parents assumed I brought the matches,” he says. “And a lot of the time, I had brought the matches.” Acting funnelled that restless energy into something positive. “I didn’t even realise it could be a real job. I didn’t realise that Cheers, which was my favourite show, was just a bunch of working actors who had a job. Once I started doing the math on it, I thought, I’ve got to go all in. That’s everything for me.”View image in fullscreen‘He called us by our character names’ … with Zooey Deschanel and Prince in New Girl.Photograph: Fox Television/REX/ShutterstockHe moved to Los Angeles and spent years as a jobbing actor, taking whatever he could, whenever he could. “I call it grinding,” he recalls. “If a commercial came, I’m taking it. If a co-star [role] comes, I’m taking it.” He appeared in Curb Your Enthusiasm as “Man on Cell Phone”, in Get Him to the Greek as “Jazz Man”. He describes Hollywood as “a really long dinner table with a bunch of seats, and the seats keep getting taken out, and then a new phase will come. You just want to stay at the table. At that point, I was just trying to get to the table.”Then came New Girl, the smash-hit sitcom that arrived in his life when he was 33. For seven years, he played the romantic lead, Nick, to Zooey Deschanel’s Jess. “New Girl was a very scary job, because all of them were really prepared. I had to stop smoking pot, because I was like, oh, these fools know their lines! I got to make some changes and do it fast, or else I’m going to lose this job,” he laughs.That job gave him his seat at the table. New Girl was such a smash that after a couple of years, Prince reached out and requested a cameo. He was a huge fan of the show, and would watch it on his tour bus. He appeared in 2014, in a post-Super Bowl episode that was watched by 26.3 million people. On set, Prince referred to Deschanel as Jess, and to Johnson as Nick. “I grew up loving Prince. We would take road trips from Chicago just to listen to his music in Minneapolis. So I thought, I’m going to show this man respect. I’m not going to be weird. And then I heard him say, ‘I’d like to meet Nick now.’ And he was so great.” They talked for hours about sport and TV. “Cheers, Roseanne and New Girl. Those were his three shows.”View image in fullscreenBox-office material … with Tom Cruise in The Mummy in 2017.Photograph: Collection Christophel/AlamyWhen New Girl blew up, Johnson’s life was transformed. “I think it exploded my brain for about six months. I spiralled out, and I didn’t know what it all meant. Bill Murray’s got a great quote that when you first get fame, you’ve got about a year where you can kind of be an asshole, and then if you stay an asshole after that, that just means you’re an asshole.” Johnson took a while to adjust to being recognised when out in the world, but it helped that he was already in his 30s. “Honestly, the dust just settled. I thought, this is the life that I wanted.”Johnson has been working solidly ever since, with a varied and busy career, ranging from blockbusters including Jurassic World and The Mummy, to the cruelly cancelled adult publishing comedy Minx (“that one makes me sad”) and, more recently, Apple’s sex-cam murder mystery Maximum Pleasure Guaranteed. He has also reunited with his old Drinking Buddies and Digging for Fire director Joe Swanberg, for The Sun Never Sets, with Dakota Fanning. When we speak, they have just closed a deal to take it to cinemas, with an expected release date later this year.double quotation markIf I didn't have kids I'd have a ponytail and would be doing this interview shirtlessStill, he says, there’s nothing like having kids to keep you grounded. In 2014, his wife, the artist Erin Payne, gave birth to their twin daughters. “Kids changed everything. I think if I didn’t have kids, I’d have a ponytail now, and I wouldn’t be wearing a shirt in this interview, because I wouldn’t believe in shirts,” he laughs. “Kids humble you, fast. They arenotfans of the old Jakester and his work.”Not even Spider-Man? (He voiced a version of Spider-Man in two animated films, Into the Spider-Verse and Across the Spider-Verse; a third is in production.) “They thought Hailee [Steinfeld]’s Spider-Gwen was cool. They thought my character was the most embarrassing, because he just ate pizza and was the old chubby one. The vibe was kind of like, why’d you say yes to that?” He had to tell them that’s not exactly how it works. “I was like, I don’t get to say, ‘Can I be the coolest one in there?’”It sounds as if Johnson has no problem staying humble. “Exactly right,” he says. “Unfortunately!”The Dink is out on Apple TV on 24 JulyExplore more on these topicsFilmBen StillerComedy filmsSport filmsComedyinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: Cool Machine by Colson Whitehead review – final part of the Harlem Trilogy is freewheeling fun | Fiction | The Guardian
description: The grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealer
Author: Xan Brooks
Headline: The grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealer
Content: Colson Whitehead.Photograph: PR/PR from little brownView image in fullscreenColson Whitehead.Photograph: PR/PR from little brownBook of the dayFictionReviewCool Machine by Colson Whitehead review – final part of the Harlem Trilogy is freewheeling funThe grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealerXan BrooksTue 21 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 02.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn the summer of 1977 a bolt of lightning struck a substation on the Hudson River, knocking out power across New York City. Two nights of darkness sparked a wave of looting as Harlem and Bronx teenagers swiped high-end items from the downtown stores. They took speaker systems and turntables to experiment with at home. They taught themselves how to scratch and mix, and sang self-styled scat lyrics into stolen microphones. Hip-hop, according to urban legend, was the child of a lightning strike and a robbing spree.It’s art from crime, thinks Ray Carney, the hardscrabble hero of Colson Whitehead’s rollicking Cool Machine, the final part of his Harlem Trilogy. Or possibly vice versa, given that the man dislikes rap music and nurses a sneaking affection for old-school smash-and-grab. For years Carney has led the sort of intrepid double life that would make Spider-Man proud, supplementing his furniture business on 125th Street with a lucrative sideline as an underworld fence. In his stealthy, dogged fashion, he helps facilitate a process he describes as “the churn”: the movement of stolen goods through New York. The churn fuels the economy and helps refresh the culture. It’s the city’s equivalent of an endocrine system.The Harlem Trilogy, like its hero, is a cool-headed trickster, more ambitious and far-reaching than its dimestore facade would suggest. Carney’s tale can be relished as a set of shaggy-dog crime capers that bound from the high-stakes heist to the mysterious bag of loot to the fraught getaway through the rat-infested back alley. But it is also – perhaps principally – a story of New York through the decades, and its semi-autonomous circles of black and white society.Harlem Shuffle, the first book in the series, hotwired the action to the 60s civil rights movement. The second, 2023’sCrook Manifesto, played out during the 70s “Fear City” era, when the infrastructure collapsed, the murder rate spiked and Carney’s store doubled as a blaxploitation film set.double quotation markWe're in the dirty, regenerative 1980s: hip-hop on the stereo, graffiti and a widening gulf between rich and poorNow along comes Cool Machine, the grandstanding final chapter, which pitches us into the dirty, regenerative 1980s. That means hip-hop on the stereo, graffiti on the subway cars and a widening gulf between rich and poor. It means, in this case, a plot that plays like pop-art Marxist noir, jam-packed with cultural references and thrumming with class and racial tensions.Searching for a secret route into the five-star Waldorf Astoria, Carney encounters a Morlock-style community in the train sheds beneath Manhattan, a subterranean tent city governed by the self-styled “Mayor Crotch”. The homeless people in the tunnels, Carney realises, are casualties of the prosperity above. “The richer those up top got, the more miserable and numerous those below became. And the more cast-offs and have-nots, the higher you have to build to get away from them.”Carney is a creature of the underworld too, but he’s also a middleman or a bridge, directing the traffic up and down to the point where the colour line becomes porous. Skipping through three interlinked tales from 1981 to 1986, Cool Machine uncovers the seeds of 21st-century cultural appropriation in the trade of African artefacts and sports memorabilia. White and black crooks squabble over the ownership of a Jesse Owens Olympic medal. A Ngil mask from Gabon electrifies the grungy East Village arts scene. A decade ago, Carney’s director friend Zippo was shooting blaxploitation pictures uptown. Now he’s hanging with the arthouse crowd, working on a redubbed version of Alfred Hitchcock’s 1963 classic in which the N-word is substituted for every mention of birds. Hitchcock’s original film was always a parable for racial desegregation, Zippo insists. So he’s simply taking the subtext and making it text.Whitehead initially envisioned the Harlem Trilogy as a jaunty entertainment, a break from the serious Pulitzer-winning business of The Underground Railroad and The Nickel Boys. Cool Machine specifically covers the New York of his youth, a scene he experienced first-hand, which only adds to the air of vivid, freewheeling fun. Whitehead maps the streets, labels the landmarks and dips in at intervals with handy background information. He takes a dark and dangerous corner of history and makes it feel illuminated, magnified, even borderline family-friendly.It’s the churn, paradoxically, that keeps the tale honest. It adds grit and grandeur to the sugar hit of nostalgia. The churn, Carney decides, applies to more than the flow of stolen goods. It is the great whip and roil of the city itself; the successive waves of immigrants washing through the individual neighbourhoods. “The enclaves and ghettoes rose and fell and were reborn.
