That was the churn, too – the underlying melody of death and rebirth in blocks and avenues. You wake up to discover the city has unveiled another version when you weren’t looking.” Carney is now in his 50s and has one eye on the exit door. He’s toiling to maintain his position, fighting the currents of a city that is as ageless and elemental as the Mississippi River. New York, he comes to realise, doesn’t care whether he lives or dies. It’s all-seeing and indifferent and just keeps rolling along.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toBookmarksDiscover new books and learn more about your favourite authors with our expert reviews, interviews and news stories. Literary delights delivered direct to youPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionCool Machine by Colson Whitehead is published by Fleet (£22). To support the Guardian, order your copy atguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsFictionBook of the dayreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Gruel and graft: museum asks if you could survive a Victorian workhouse | Museums | The Guardian
description: Revamped Ripon attraction reopens as a more immersive experience telling ‘national story’ of poverty and welfare
Author: Mark Brown
Headline: Revamped Ripon attraction reopens as a more immersive experience telling ‘national story’ of poverty and welfare
Content: Visitors to the museum, one of Britain’s most complete workhouses, can take part in activities including tasting gruel.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianView image in fullscreenVisitors to the museum, one of Britain’s most complete workhouses, can take part in activities including tasting gruel.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianMuseumsGruel and graft: museum asks if you could survive a Victorian workhouseRevamped Ripon attraction reopens as a more immersive experience telling ‘national story’ of poverty and welfareMark BrownNorth of England correspondentTue 21 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVisitors to one of Britain’s most complete surviving Victorian workhouses won’t have to strip and bathe in someone else’s bath water. But they might get some gruel, they can sweep the yard and there are “schoolmaster” volunteers happy to tell children how to behave.“You are literally going to walk in the footsteps of people who have experienced this place,” said the director of the RiponMuseumsTrust, Alexa Vernon.“I’m not sure we can serve gruel every day, but children will be able to get stuck into sewing and hunt for mice and listen to the sounds, and they might realise how lucky they are compared to Victorian children.”Vernon was speaking before the reopening of theRipon Workhouse Museumafter a £2.8m redevelopment.A key part of the revamp has been to make the experience more immersive with volunteers who might be baking bread or putting clothes through a mangle.View image in fullscreenThe museum intends to give a realistic experience of what it was like inside a Victorian workhouse.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianVisitors are asked if they would survive the Victorian workhouse. As they enter the vagrants’ block, the sound of unpleasant coughing fills the air. There are baths, a one-size-fits-all coarse men’s nightshirt and a reproduction “de-infestor” used to fumigate paupers’ and vagrants’ clothes.“All their possessions would be taken from them,” said the curator, Laura Allan. “They would be stripped. They would be bathed. If you were lucky you would be the first person in the bath water. If you were unlucky, you were the third.“It’s interesting that the reasons people became homeless in the past are very similar as today – so alcoholism, mental health, family breakdown, veterans returning from war.”Elsewhere in the museum, visitors can shut themselves in a vagrants’ cell, pick oakum from old ropes or pose for a family portrait in replica workhouse uniforms.View image in fullscreenThe museum’s reception area for vagrants and paupers.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianThe museum reopens to the public on Tuesday after major conservation and restoration work supported by theNational Lottery heritage fund.It became a museum in 2017, telling the story of the Victorian solution to poverty and homelessness – give people a bed and food, but make it as grim, unpleasant and rules-bound as possible.A revamp of the museum building had been badly needed, said Vernon. “You could honestly stick a screwdriver in the rotting window frames and it would stand up independently,” she said.“The roof was leaking. We had water damage throughout. The wiring was like spaghetti junction. We had unsupported arches. Our structural engineer said that technically the building shouldn’t stand up.”View image in fullscreenThe museum’s schoolroom.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianOn top of that the museum experience was more like walking round a 1970s council offices with bits of interpretation, unsurprising given that it was once office space forNorth Yorkshiresocial services.“Some of our Tripadvisor comments were along the lines of … it looks like they’ve not finished it,” Vernon said. “It wasn’t finished.”The lottery money has been transformative, and not just in fixing the leaking roof and rotting windows and painting the walls in horrible – but original – taupes, pinks and greens.One particular joy, curators say, has been the opportunity to open the cellar, allowing visitors to see where meat was slaughtered and stored.“This space, we think, was probably last opened in the 1930s,” said Allan. “This is probably exactly how it would have looked when it was a workhouse. It’s the only space that really has never been touched.”View image in fullscreenThe museum’s dining room.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianThe museum tells a story about Ripon but much more than that, said Vernon. “This is a national story,” she said. “Workhouses have an important story to tell about poverty, welfare and justice and there were workhouses up and down the country.“This was the Victorian solution to poverty.”The revamped museum is one of three in Ripon that tell vivid stories of Victorian life. There is also a courthouse museum and a prison and police museum.They tell historical stories, but their curators are passionate in their belief that they have contemporary resonance.“Poverty still exists, doesn’t it,” said Vernon. “People can come here and think about poverty, think about how we treat people and think about whether we are treating people more fairly now.”She hopes visitors to the museum will leave feeling more informed, “but also more compassionate, thoughtful and connected to the stories of those who came before us”.Explore more on these topicsMuseumsHeritagePovertyNorth YorkshirenewsShareReuse this content

title: TV tonight: Simon Pegg’s cyber-thriller The Undeclared War returns  | Television | The Guardian
description: Siân Brooke and Adrian Lester also star in the tense GCHQ drama. Plus: hideous creations on the Great British Sewing Bee. Here’s what to watch this evening
Author: Phil Harrison
Headline: Siân Brooke and Adrian Lester also star in the tense GCHQ drama. Plus: hideous creations on the Great British Sewing Bee. Here’s what to watch this evening
Content: Simon Pegg as Danny Patrick and Chloe Pirrie as Lt Col Roz Cavendish in The Undeclared War.Photograph: Simon RidgwayView image in fullscreenSimon Pegg as Danny Patrick and Chloe Pirrie as Lt Col Roz Cavendish in The Undeclared War.Photograph: Simon RidgwayTV tonightTelevisionTV tonight: Simon Pegg’s cyber-thriller The Undeclared War returnsSiân Brooke and Adrian Lester also star  in the tense GCHQ drama. Plus: hideous creations on the Great British Sewing Bee. Here’s what to watch this eveningPhil Harrison,Hannah J Davies,Ali Catterall,Jack SealeandSimon WardellTue 21 Jul 2026 01.15 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Undeclared War9pm, Channel 4Espionage, malware and Simon Pegg doing his best serious face feature as Peter Kosminsky’s cyber-thriller returns for a second season. When we left him, GCHQ director of operations Danny Patrick (Pegg) had discovered shocking secrets about both the internal workings of government and his own marriage. Perhaps inevitably, things are now even worse. Saara (Hannah Khalique-Brown) has observed irregularities in flows of information that can only mean one thing: a mole in the intelligence agency. Plenty of scowling and running down empty corridors ensues, with Adrian Lester back as the prime minister and Siân Brooke joining as Danny’s wife, a former MI6 member.Phil HarrisonThe Great British Sewing Bee8pm, BBC OneSophie Willan hosts the crafty competition, which continues with a week dedicated to colour and prints. The briefs for each round showcase a range of tastes and talents, but that doesn’t mean it’s a cinch. “This is absolutely hideous,” says one contestant, inspecting their own handiwork.Hannah J DaviesMurder 24/79pm, BBC OneAnother series of this raw and grimly intimate show that takes viewers into the inner workings of real murder cases. This time, Jo is found dead with a knife wound and her husband, Mohamed, is arrested. But with no fingerprints on the presumed weapon and blood patterns not indicating a struggle, something doesn’t add up.PHFaithless9pm, Sky AtlanticThe series concludes with a time-spanning double bill. In the late 1970s, actor Marianne (Frida Gustavsson) is having a torrid affair with director David (Gustav Lindh), which reignites when they work together at her theatre. Their increasingly reckless shenanigans reach fever pitch at a birthday party. By 1980, Marianne is divorcing her husband when a tense night-time meeting ends in tragedy.Ali CatterallJohnny Vegas’ Little Shop of Antiques9pm, QuestSeason two of a pleasantly lackadaisical reality show, and Vegas – plus his best mate Bev and girlfriend Vikki – has a new antiques shop to fit out, an electric van and dreams of taking the business online. Meanwhile, the endless need for new stock prompts a trip to Anglesey, and another haggling match with fearsome veteran Trish.Jack SealeFilm choiceThe Pillow Book (Peter Greenaway, 1996),1.10am, Film4Peter Greenaway found perfect source material for his perennial obsessions with art, sex and death (with a side order of lists) in the titular notebook penned by Sei Shōnagon, a lady in the imperial court of late-900s Japan. In this exquisitely stylish drama, Sei’s present-day counterpart is model Nagiko (Vivian Wu), who gets sexual gratification from calligraphers writing on her body. Then she starts a torrid affair with British translator Jerome (Ewan McGregor) and finds a very literary way to take revenge on the publisher who destroyed her father.Simon WardellLive sportThe Hundredwomen’scricket: MI London v Sunrisers Leeds,2.30pm, BBC TwoSouthern Brave v Welsh Fire is on Wednesday at 2.15pm.The Hundredmen’scricket: MI London v Sunrisers Leeds,6pm, BBC TwoSouthern Brave v Welsh Fire is on Wednesday at 6pm.This article was amended on 21 July 2026. An earlier version included a preview of Prison Wives of TikTok on U&W, but after publication the channel said that transmission of the programme had been postponed.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionTV tonightPeter KosminskySimon PeggfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: These Wild English by Nicola Wilding review – explaining a mother’s drift to the far right | Politics books | The Guardian
description: A documentary-maker probes family history and the darker corners of the internet to solve an urgent political puzzle
Author: Fiona Sturges
Headline: A documentary-maker probes family history and the darker corners of the internet to solve an urgent political puzzle
Content: Nicola Wilding at her home in Caldbeck, Cumbria.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianView image in fullscreenNicola Wilding at her home in Caldbeck, Cumbria.Photograph: Gary Calton/The GuardianPolitics booksReviewThese Wild English by Nicola Wilding review – explaining a mother’s drift to the far rightA documentary-maker probes family history and the darker corners of the internet to solve an urgent political puzzleFiona SturgesTue 21 Jul 2026 05.30 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 11.58 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen Sandra Forsyth began marching in support of the English Defence League, her daughter Nicola Wilding hoped it was a phase. The Cumbria-bred, Dover-dwelling Sandra was about to turn 60 and had been dealing with a lot of stress. Not only had her son been sentenced to two years in prison for armed robbery, but she was working at a home for adults with complex needs. She liked the job, but it could be gruelling – especially when clients hurled furniture at her or ripped sinks off walls.Then, in May 2013, came the death of Lee Rigby, a drummer with the Second Battalion of the Royal Regiment of Fusiliers who was murdered by two Islamist terrorists using machetes. Sandra didn’t know Rigby, Wilding notes, but his death nonetheless brought her pent-up rage to the surface. She began watching videos on Facebook by a man named Tommy Robinson, who wanted to know why the country’s brave soldiers were being murdered. By September of that year, she was protesting on the streets of London.Inspired by the puzzle of her mother’s late-life activism, These Wild English is a heartfelt and evocative memoir of Sandra who, up until her death in 2024, endured poverty, upheaval and abusive partners, as well as a heart condition leading to two rounds of surgery. “How did my mum, this proud tough woman who had brought up four kids pretty much alone, end up marching alongside far-right extremists chanting ‘No more refugees’?” Wilding asks. “And how did my brother – a kind, smart, funny man who loved his kids – how did he end up in prison? … Was it us? Did we do it to ourselves? Or was it something else – something bigger than us?”The “us” in this story is important. Wilding, the eldest of Forsyth’s children and one of the few in her family to find stability through education and a career, is a TV documentary maker specialising in poverty and rural working-class communities, though here she is no dispassionate observer. She also has an eye on the bigger picture, noting how wider events and the social and economic decisions taken in Westminster can affect the lives of vulnerable individuals and communities. For the Forsyth family, these have included the winter of discontent, mass unemployment and Thatcher’s right-to-buy scheme in the 1970s and 80s; the foot-and-mouth outbreak and the financial crisis in the 2000s; and the Tory austerity programme, Brexit referendum and resurgence of the far right in the 2010s.Wilding isn’t the first author to examine the alienation of the British working classes and the ways they have been exploited, pilloried and misunderstood: Kerry Hudson’sLowborn, Joel Budd’sUnderdogsand Rebecca Smith’sRuralhave all offered their own perspectives. In our social media-fanned, post-Brexit discourse, the white working classes are often cast as aggressors waging vicious war on immigrant communities, having been manipulated by self-interested politicians deliberately stoking division. Wilding has little time for such one-dimensional depictions, despite her clear dismay at her mother’s flirtation with the far right.Instead, writing in thoughtful and elegantly understated prose, she digs into the roots of Sandra’s dissatisfaction, telling a story that spans three generations, starting out with her early life in the 1960s on her father’s farm in Eden Valley, Cumbria, where she was tasked with looking after her siblings during the recurring periods when her own mother did a runner. Exasperated by the drudgery of parenting and rural living, Nana Forsyth would leave the family for weeks on end before returning home clutching an olive branch in the form of sweets for the kids and cigarettes for her husband.My mother was an excellent care worker. Why did she end up marching with the EDL?Read moreAt 18, Sandra became pregnant and attempted to make a life with the baby’s father, moving to a cottage up the road from the farm. But within a couple of years, he had left and Sandra was a penniless single mother. And so began a peripatetic pattern that would continue throughout her life: a new man, a new home, an implosion followed by deeper debt and the quest for a fresh start.In the book’s acknowledgments, Wilding credits the writer and farmerJames Rebanks, a former neighbour in Cumbria, for “seeing this book before I could”. It seems Wilding took some convincing to turn her documentarian’s gaze on her own flesh and blood, though, happily, she did. Full of grit and compassion, These Wild English blends the personal and political to illustrate the ways families are shaped by circumstance and inherited trauma, and can be buffeted by forces beyond their control. At its heart is Sandra, a protagonist for the ages who is wayward, charismatic, fierce, caring and – wisely or not – always up for a fight.These Wild English: A Family, a Class, a Country on Fire by Nicola Wilding is published by Profile (£16.99). To support the Guardian order your copy atguardianbookshop.com. Delivery charges may apply.Explore more on these topicsPolitics booksThe far rightSociety booksreviewsShareReuse this content

title: The Fires review – volcanoes and passions erupt in Iceland disaster-romance | Movies | The Guardian
description: A volcanologist and a photographer try to save Reykjavik in impressive and plausible drama
Author: Leslie Felperin
Headline: A volcanologist and a photographer try to save Reykjavik in impressive and plausible drama
Content: Genuine chemistry … Vigdís Hrefna Pálsdóttir and Pilou Asbaek in The Fires.Photograph: Signature EntertainmentView image in fullscreenGenuine chemistry … Vigdís Hrefna Pálsdóttir and Pilou Asbaek in The Fires.Photograph: Signature EntertainmentMoviesReviewThe Fires review – volcanoes and passions erupt in Iceland disaster-romanceA volcanologist and a photographer try to save Reykjavik in impressive and plausible dramaLeslie FelperinTue 21 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 04.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThis scientifically plausible, CGI-tastic disaster film will either do wonders for Iceland’s tourist industry or destroy it completely. Vigdís Hrefna Pálsdóttir stars as a volcanologist called Anna, who is descended from a short line of volcanologists (just her dad). When mini volcanos start erupting and earthquakes get more frequent, Anna begins to suspect the subterranean systems everyone thought were separate might all be fed from one big magma lake deep underground. Great gaseous clouds of geologic mumbo jumbo get spewed into the dialogue, backed up with sombre graphs on computer screens and people drawing stuff on blackboards. But the gist is that Reykjavik is in danger.Of course, the powers that be are sceptical about Anna’s theories and don’t want to cut off the island’s supply of sweet, sweet tourist money. Pretty soon, though, the signs are too numerous to ignore. And a big eruption either could last a day or two, or as Anna points out, for 30 years, like a volcano that erupted in the 9th century. (Good thing Icelandic history goes back that far.)While all this is brewing, Anna starts having the feels for hunky Danish photographer Thomas (Pilou Asbaek, the priapic bad boy Euron Greyjoy in Game of Thrones). Anna is inconveniently married to nice but dull Kristinn (Joi Johannsson), with whom she has daughter Salka (Steinunn Kaldal Jakobsdóttir). But Thomas’s beautiful pictures of gas clouds and of herself looking worried while observing said clouds, along with his groovy bachelor pad where he listens to Nick Cave, light a fire in Anna’s loins and soon they are having an affair. Given Iceland has a population roughly equal to Coventry’s, it doesn’t take long for word to get round about her and Thomas, giving those who doubt Anna’s volcanic theories an advantage.Even though this is a pretty lurid mashup of romance and catastrophism, and splattered with cliches from both genres, the end result is very watchable thanks in large part to the quality of the acting. Pálsdóttir and Asbaek have genuine chemistry, even if they start grinning a little too excessively when dancing together in a party scene, as if about to perform dental procedures on each other. Another bonus is the terrifically eerie orchestral score by up and coming composer Herdís Stefánsdóttir, who also wrote the music for two recent M Night Shyamalan features and some upmarket TV shows including The Essex Serpent. Stefánsdóttir’s score here is full of creepy discordance and ominous drones, which suits the sweeping footage of the famously beautiful/scary Icelandic landscape. Altogether, it is an impressively assembled package for director Ugla Hauksdóttir, making her feature debut.The Fires is on digital platforms from 27 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmAction and adventure filmsRomance filmsIcelandVolcanoesreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Chickenhare and the Secret of the Groundhog review – time-travel fun for plucky hybrid critters | Movies | The Guardian
description: A half-chicken half-hare, a skunk and a tortoise go in search of a 3,000-year-old groundhog in this wholesome animation with a surprisingly complex plot
Author: Leslie Felperin
Headline: A half-chicken half-hare, a skunk and a tortoise go in search of a 3,000-year-old groundhog in this wholesome animation with a surprisingly complex plot
Content: It’s all about the journey … Chickenhare and the Secret of the Groundhog.Photograph: I Heart CinemaView image in fullscreenIt’s all about the journey … Chickenhare and the Secret of the Groundhog.Photograph: I Heart CinemaMoviesReviewChickenhare and the Secret of the Groundhog review – time-travel fun for plucky hybrid crittersA half-chicken half-hare, a skunk and a tortoise go in search of a 3,000-year-old groundhog in this wholesome animation with a surprisingly complex plotLeslie FelperinTue 21 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 02.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAlthough this animated feature doesn’t have a title as utterly awesome as its predecessor,Chickenhare and the Hamster of Darkness, it’s still a perfectly adequate 90-ish minutes of creamy pastel-coloured picaresque entertainment. It feels like it’s skewed in the direction of younger viewers given the simple character designs, general wholesomeness and easy-on-the-jokes tone, but the plot is surprisingly complicated. The lead character is named Chickenhare because of course he is half-chicken half-hare, although in practice he looks more like a hare than a chicken other than a tuft of feathers on the crown of his head. He is best friends with a skunk named Meg, equipped with the awesome power of stink and who is very butch-coded with her waistcoat, faux-hawk and tomboy characteristics; there is also a tortoise named Abe, who as you might imagine is less keen on running about than the other two.After a pleasingly kinetic introductory action sequence that feels very Indiana Jones-inspired, during which the treasure-hunting trio run into a svelte but scratchy cat called Crolloq, an old school friend (girlfriend?) of Meg’s. Angry at Meg for assorted reasons, she leaves the other three in a pit but they eventually escape with the golden spork they were seeking all along, a doodah that proves useful later. One thing leads to another, like they do, and they end up on a new adventure in search of a 3,000-year-old groundhog with a backwards facing face who can turn back time. A new character accompanies them on the way: Gina, Chickenhare’s long-lost half-sister who is, wait for it, a harechicken – more aviform than mammalian.In truth, no one here is quite what they seem at first – and halfway through the movie the main trio get separated and then struggle with assorted backstories and obstacles before reuniting for a big climax when the whole menagerie get back together. The final message is about accepting your inner nature or some such platitude, but the journey is what really matters, especially all the feasting along the way and learning to forgive old friends for past slights.Chickenhare and the Secret of the Groundhog is in UK and Irish cinemas from 24 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmAnimation in filmFamily filmsScience fiction and fantasy filmsreviewsShareReuse this content

title: BBCPhil/Noseda/Selaocoe review – a proms concert that was everything the festival does best | Proms 2026 | The Guardian
description: A programme – Bach, Beethoven and a Jessie Montgomery premiere – that looked odd on paper made perfect sense in the hall, with Abel Selaocoe a mesmerising soloist in Montgomery’s ‘These Righteous Paths’
Author: Alexandra Coghlan
Headline: A programme – Bach, Beethoven and a Jessie Montgomery premiere – that looked odd on paper made perfect sense in the hall, with Abel Selaocoe a mesmerising soloist in Montgomery’s ‘These Righteous Paths’
Content: A storyteller through sound … cellist Abel Selaocoe with the Philharmonia Orchestra and Chorus, at the Royal Albert Hall.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouView image in fullscreenA storyteller through sound … cellist Abel Selaocoe with the Philharmonia Orchestra and Chorus, at the Royal Albert Hall.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouProms 2026ReviewBBCPhil/Noseda/Selaocoe review – a proms concert that was everything the festival does bestRoyal Albert Hall, LondonA programme – Bach, Beethoven and a Jessie Montgomery premiere –  that looked odd on paper made perfect sense in the hall, with Abel Selaocoe a mesmerising soloist in Montgomery’s ‘These Righteous Paths’Alexandra CoghlanTue 21 Jul 2026 09.16 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 09.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Thy magic power reunites / All that custom has divided / All men become brothers / Under the sway of thy gentle wings.” The BBC can’t have known when they booked Manchester’s BBC Philharmonic to perform Beethoven’s Ninth Symphony – culminating in Schiller’s ode to fellowship and unity – that it would mark Andy Burnham’s appointment as prime minister. Our new leader’s “gentle wings” will need the span of a 747 to embrace every warring faction beneath them, but what a welcome from aPromsconcert that was everything the festival does best.A strange programme on paper – Bach, Beethoven and a brand new cello concerto – made absolute sense in the hall, where musical past and future were held suspended together in a rapt continuous present. Bach’s Fantasia and Fugue in G minor set the tone. Arranged for orchestra by Andrew Davis, it had a distinct twinkle in its eye. Dutiful, even dogmatic, string exegesis takes a sudden sideways turn as the wind bend and distort the theme. And is that a celesta doubling the flute – a shimmering ghost in the orchestral machine, a childish distortion of the original organ? It’s wittily done, and under former chief conductor Gianandrea Noseda, the orchestra relished this fusion of sonic worlds.It was a process only amplified by the UK premiere of Jessie Montgomery’s These Righteous Paths – a cello concerto “born through” (in the composer’s own words) its soloist Abel Selaocoe, whose unique skills both enabled and inspired its unusual form. A tribute to Montgomery’s mother – playwright Robbie McCauley – the concerto’s three movements each take poems by McCauley as their starting point.View image in fullscreenFusing sonic worlds … Gianandrea Noseda conducting.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouThe texts are not always delivered directly, instead they inform the cello’s searching lines, wordless vocalise, mouth percussion, throat singing and African song – all performed by Selaocoe, who is less a singer or instrumentalist here than a storyteller through sound. There are nods to Bach and Elgar, but this concerto is entirely its own creation: an iridescent fabric woven between orchestra and soloist that dares imagine McCauley’s “new song … about something more”. Selaocoe is the mesmerising shaman at its centre.And then there was the Beethoven, deliberately classical and fleetfooted under Noseda, chasing the original shock of that extraordinary chorale finale. Classily delivered by a strong quartet of soloists and the Philharmonia Chorus, concealing the considerable strain of the composer’s demands, it was an argument not just for peace but progress, risk-taking, visionary optimism.Over to you, Andy.Available onBBC Soundsuntil 12 October.The Proms continue until 12 September.Explore more on these topicsProms 2026Classical musicBBC Philharmonic OrchestraPromsreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Splatoon Raiders review – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey | Games | The Guardian
description: The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill
Content: Better with friends … Splatoon Raiders.Photograph: NintendoView image in fullscreenBetter with friends … Splatoon Raiders.Photograph: NintendoGamesReviewSplatoon Raiders review – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to greyNintendo Switch 2The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-millJoshua RiveraTue 21 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 09.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSplatoon is among Nintendo’s most ingenious inventions of the last decade: a series of shooting games whose paint-splattered turf war nature means being bad at shooting is just as valuable as – if not more so than – being good. It is also blessed with an abundance of personality, with vibrant characters, a richly imagined world, and a hipness that the competition can only dream of achieving. Unfortunately, Splatoon Raiders makes it difficult to remember all of this.The first spinoff in the ink-soaked franchise, Splatoon Raiders is advertised as a single player-focused entry in this primarily multiplayer series. While this is true enough – one can play through the entire game solo just fine – Raiders is clearly best enjoyed cooperatively with up to three others. Players start each session on a hideout ship where they can upgrade their equipment and tweak their play style before heading out on bite-sized repeatable missions, and this structure is tailor-made for teaming up with friends or helping out strangers (the game lets you do either), as each person works towards creating their ultimate squid-kid avatar of chaos.Playing Splatoon Raiders solo feels like a lesser experience for this reason. Its levels are straightforwardly constructed to keep groups together. There is little of the puzzly rush of finding hidden depths with creative use of your tools. Instead, the fun is in putting together your kit. Splatoon’s weapons have always been goofy and playfully designed, but even better is learning how this new entry complements them with a suite of augmentable gadgets. As your options accrete and the game escalates in challenge, Raiders comes to life.View image in fullscreenGadgets bring the game to life … Splatoon Raiders.Photograph: NintendoSome may not get that far. Splatoon Raiders takes a long time to make it clear how completely the experience can change with the right kit, and while it offers a decent variety of challenges to tackle, players are meant to replay them. The minimal story, in which you are an unnamed mechanic stranded on a mysterious archipelago with the band Deep Cut (returning from Splatoon 3), is not terribly compelling. Which is surprising for a series with a history of casually revealing outrageous new details about its post-climate apocalypse world and its human-cephalopod inhabitants.Splatoon is Nintendo at its most offbeat, venturing forth in a direction few other game studios travel. And, according to sales, audiences outside of the company’s native Japan seem to struggle with how to receive it. In this context, Splatoon Raiders feels like a concession: Nintendo taking aside one of its rangier, peculiar creations and asking it to be a bit more normal. This is the biggest surprise about Splatoon Raiders: it is pedestrian. If you’ve played one of dozens of modern cooperative action games, you’ve played this. You have upgraded your gear and compared weapon stats and divined synergies between abilities in order to throw your character into the meat grinder over and over again, watching overwhelming hordes eventually become trivial as your stats improve, your arsenal more varied. There is only so much that Splatoon’s ink-fuelled weaponry and charming DIY design sensibilities can do to elevate this experience. Like its archipelago setting, Splatoon Raiders is far removed from what makes the main series so charming, and what’s left here feels a bit too much like watching paint dry.Splatoon Raiders is out 23 July; £41.99Explore more on these topicsGamesNintendoNintendo Switch 2Shooting gamesreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Entangled and Woven review – exquisitely spun textiles that cross borders, culture and time | Art and design | The Guardian
description: Manchester’s past as a centre of textile production forms the starting point of this seamless showcase of skills
Author: Hannah Clugston
Headline: Manchester’s past as a centre of textile production forms the starting point of this seamless showcase of skills
Content: Jounghye Yoo’s Gilded Veil at the Entangled and Woven.Photograph: the Whitworth, The University of Manchester. Photo by Michael Pollard.View image in fullscreenJounghye Yoo’s Gilded Veil at the Entangled and Woven.Photograph: the Whitworth, The University of Manchester. Photo by Michael Pollard.Art and designReviewEntangled and Woven review – exquisitely spun textiles that cross borders, culture and timeThe Whitworth, ManchesterManchester’s past as a centre of textile production forms the starting point of this seamless showcase of skillsHannah ClugstonTue 21 Jul 2026 08.04 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 21.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHow many hands did it take to make this show? That is to say, how many artisans, craftspeople, weavers and artists’ hands did these pieces pass through? For this is a textile exhibition that doesn’t just celebrate a short list of singular artists, but rather the many highly skilled craftspeople and communities repeating the stitches and specialities of their ancestors. Take Entangled Memories, Interwoven Stories by Péro for example: it was made by 50 artisans and took 18,000 hours to complete. And that’s one piece, in an exhibition that spans three galleries.These many hands and hours make Entangled and Woven an exquisitely beautiful exhibition. Every slip of fabric is layered with delicate detail and texture. Fabrics are airy and ethereal one moment, and weighty and dipped in deep indigo the next. It is hot, the fabrics sway and flicker in the fans, and hands flicker quickly in the video works capturing the creation of the pieces. I am mesmerised.View image in fullscreenPéro’s Entangled Memories, Interwoven Stories, and Somi Ko’s People of One, Continuum and Moment.Photograph: the Whitworth, The University of Manchester. Photo by Michael Pollard.What elevates this show from “it looks good” to “it’s great” is the fact it is also a true cultural exchange. The authentic type, where the artists have met in person, have visited each other’s countries, and have made brand new works in response to these research trips. The exhibition draws together eight artists and collectives from Korea and India, inviting them to incorporate new techniques into their own practices, creating something new that crosses borders, culture and time.The Whitworth holds around 20,000 textiles from across the globe, and for this show it presents several historic Indian and Korean works, some dating back to the 18th century. They are carefully placed around the gallery, sometimes mirroring the new works and other times educating us on technique. It is hard for these pieces – primarily flat on the wall in frames – to command presence alongside the impactful contemporary installations, but they serve as a good historical reference point and a link to Manchester’s past as a centre of textile production.In the middle of the first gallery is Gilded Veil_A Mysterious Curtain Lightly Draped in Gold by Jounghye Yoo. Suspended from a wooden frame in the ceiling is a tunnel of fabric sheets fading from indigo to red, bursting into glorious gold in the centre and back to red and finally indigo again. Inspired by the gate of the Kashi Vishwanath Temple in Varanasi, the shape of the sheer sheets is palatial, and as I wander through them they adjust slightly in the air, cocooning me in a surreal world drenched in colour.View image in fullscreenYoung In Hong’s Sacred Empires and White Cranes in Snowfall.Photograph: the Whitworth, The University of Manchester. Photo by Michael Pollard.The work was made in collaboration with Dr Ismail Mohammad Khatri, a master of ajrakh block printing from Gujarat, and his son, Juned Ismail. Ajrakh motifs dance with Korean embroidery using gold thread, and Indian cotton sits with Korean silk creating a work that effortlessly blends varied skills and collapses the separation between heritage, culture and artists – even between my body and the gallery space as I disappear into this veiled passage.Connection abounds between the artists themselves in the next gallery where Somi Ko, Young In Hong and fashion brand and cultural practice Péro play with weight, fragmentation, animals and teeny-tiny mirrors. Somi Ko’s People of One, Moment is a series of figures on Indian cotton made using traditional Indian mirror work. As I draw closer to the dancing bodies, I see shards of my own reflection split and shatter across the gallery, releasing me from my fleshy form.At the other end of the gallery, Young In Hong integrates this mirror work into Sacred Empires, a series of ornate flags produced in collaboration with Kala Raksha, a grassroots artisan collective in Kutch, home to seven minority craft communities. In Indian folk belief, the small reflective mirrors are seen as ritualistic devices that ward off evil spirits and mediate divine presence.The divine – or the spiritual realm – is also very present in the third gallery where Kaimurai, Sumakshi Singh and Yeonsoon Chang make the unseen seen. Singh crafts an ethereal, floating bridge to highlight links between Korea, India and the UK. She embroiders on water-soluble fabric so when the base is dissolved, all that is left is a delicate web of threads, glowing against the deep blue walls in the gallery.View image in fullscreenAn ethereal, floating bridge … Sumakshi Singh’s Spanning the Void.Photograph: the Whitworth, The University of Manchester. Photo by Michael Pollard.Kaimurai makes visible the process of meditation through stepped khadi sheets that reach upwards toward the spiritual realm. A combination of sculpture and painting, Kaimurai’s work is arresting and intriguing. Beginning with a bell hung over a hefty granite sphere, his sheets of khadi rise up in stages, each with a graduating splash of indigo that gets smaller and smaller as if reality were receding.Meanwhile, Chang ponders on the Buddhist concept of Worincheongang, which means “the moon reflected on a thousand rivers”. Her glowing structural pieces, dyed in indigo and shimmering as if possessing a divine power, enhance this sensation of togetherness both with the spiritual realm and the communities that dream under the self-same moon.Back in the main gallery, a mythical creature by Boito is made through the mingling of skills from across the Indian state of Odisha. A papier-mache chest comes from Raghurajpur; ringa textile and beads from Malkangiri; cane craft from Talabasta – the list goes on. It is an unusual creature, with a chicken’s head, elephant’s leg, human’s arm and camel’s hump, each boasting a different fabric and way of making. It is also the perfect symbol of what has happened – joyously – in Entangled and Woven: cultures combining to make the most striking works of art.Hyundai Translocal Series: Entangled and Woven is atthe Whitworth, Manchester until 3 January 2027Explore more on these topicsArt and designTextile artExhibitionsManchesterArtreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Exploring Germany’s East Frisian islands: mudflats, marshes and millions of birds | Germany holidays | The Guardian
description: The seven inhabited isles off the North Sea coast, part of a Unesco world heritage site, are a sanctuary for wildlife – with great hotels and restaurants too
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The seven inhabited isles off the North Sea coast, part of a Unesco world heritage site, are a sanctuary for wildlife – with great hotels and restaurants too
Content: The wide beaches of Norderney.Photograph: Jacques Nerwaque/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe wide beaches of Norderney.Photograph: Jacques Nerwaque/Getty ImagesGermany holidaysExploring Germany’s East Frisian islands: mudflats, marshes and millions of birdsThe seven inhabited isles off the North Sea coast, part of a Unesco world heritage site, are a sanctuary for wildlife – with great hotels and restaurants tooKate MannTue 21 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Tue 21 Jul 2026 08.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Can you hear that?” my guide Andrea Wellmeyer asks as I sink a little deeper into the wet ground, cold mud seeping between my toes. When the wind drops, I detect a faint but distinct crackling, similar to the rustle of a sweet wrapper or the bubbling of sparkling water. “That’s theWattknistern,” she says, “a natural phenomenon caused by the tens of thousands of mud shrimp that live here.”We’re standing in the Wadden Sea mudflats on Norderney, an island just off Germany’s North Sea coast. It’s Friday evening at low tide. In the town centre, bars are pumping with music, but here, just 15 minutes along the southern edge of the island, it’s a completely different story.“A lot of the flora and fauna is found under the surface,” Andrea says, showing me the tree-like tops of sand mason worm casings and the tips of marsh samphire growing upwards like tiny asparagus. Flipping over a cluster of wild oysters, she startles a crab that raises its pincers in the air before wriggling out of sight once more. Orange-beaked oystercatchers scurry back and forth on the exposed seabed and a pair of spoonbills wade gracefully through the shallow water beyond.View image in fullscreenA beach in Osterhook on the island of Langeoog.Photograph: Tasfoto/AlamyNorderney is one of the seven inhabited islands that make up the East Frisian islands (Ostfriesische Inseln)in Germany. The others are Borkum, Juist, Baltrum, Langeoog, Spiekeroog and Wangerooge. Stretching more than 50 miles from west to east, they are a popular holiday destination, known for their unique coastal landscapes and diverse wildlife, from grey seals and migrating birds to the creatures found in the mud.Each island has its own identity, from upmarket Juist with its horse-drawn transport to the historic red brick houses of sleepy Spiekeroog; but all have vast sandy beaches, grassy dunes and tidal mudflats. The islands belong to the larger Wadden Sea Unesco world heritage site and have been part of theLower Saxon Wadden Sea national parksince its formation in 1986.double quotation markHistoric seaside villas and retreats founded on the health benefits of briny sea air sit next to modern hotels and restaurantsNorderney was one of the first German seaside resorts in the North Sea. Today, historic seaside villas and retreats founded on the health benefits of briny sea air sit next to modern hotels and restaurants, such asInselloft Norderneyand Michelin-recommendedOktopussy, where summer scallops come with a caramelised miso-cauliflower purée. My favourite spot quickly becomesMarienhöhe, a dune-top pavilion built more than 100 years ago, where I eat delicately battered fish with warm potato salad.Despite being one of only two East Frisian islands where cars are permitted, the best way to explore Norderney is by bike. Leaving the town in the west, I zoom along the bike lanes that run through the dunes, stopping at a popular stretch of beach called Weiße Düne, before heading south towards the island’s tallest building, a lighthouse constructed between 1872 and 1874.View image in fullscreenWild oysters in the Wadden Sea.Photograph: Ron Giling/AlamySoon after, roughly halfway across the island, I reach a sign that says I can’t go any further on my bike. This is aRuhezone(quiet/restricted zone), a highly protected area where you can only walk on a couple of marked footpaths. A rusty shipwreck lies on the sand and seals bask in the sun at the far end.double quotation markMore than 10 million birds make a pit stop in the Wadden Sea every year, with spring and autumn the best time to see themThere are strict rules governing access in several sections of the island, part of a quest to let nature be nature. Heading out with an official guide helps you see and learn more. AlongsideWattwanderungen(mudflat hikes), you can also book salt marsh walks, birding excursions and boat trips to see the seals. The newly renovatedvisitor centreat Norderney harbour also has extensive information about the local ecosystems.My next stops are Spiekeroog and Langeoog, two calmer, car-free islands to the east. On Langeoog, I join a tour to learn more about the birds I have spotted over the last few days. “Many species need the Wadden Sea as the focal point of their migration, as a place to rest,” national park guide Fiona Wettstein says. “When they first arrive, they have completely burned through their fat reserves and practically drop down here.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice a weekSign up toThe TravellerGet travel inspiration, featured trips and local tips for your next break, as well as the latest deals from Guardian HolidaysEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionView image in fullscreenGreylag geese with chicks on Langeoog.Photograph: Image Professionals/AlamySetting off with bikes and binoculars, we spot red knots, redshanks, avocets and sandpipers, as well as squeaking lapwings with long crests and a short-eared owl swooping through the grasses. Geese with fluffy chicks in tow strut around the island, hissing loudly if you get too close. More than10 million birdsmake a pit stop in the Wadden Sea every year, with spring and autumn the best time to see them. The shoulder season is also a good time to visit to avoid the summer holiday crowds.I stay atSilt & Sand, a bright and airy design hotel just behind the dunes that opened last year. The expansive (almost 9-mile-long) beach on the other side is dotted withStrandkörbe, traditional hooded wicker armchairs found on beaches along the German coast.Despite the chain layout of the islands and their close proximity to one another, it isn’t possible to “hop” between them. Instead, you have to return to the mainland and make your way along the coast to a different ferry terminal each time, the exception being Juist and Norderney, which have a shared departure point in Norddeich. It is possible to walk from the mainland to some of the islands at low tide, but onlywith a certified guide.View image in fullscreenA hotel room at Silt & Sand on Langeoog.After my first few hours on Norderney, I give up on the weather forecast and start operating on a cloud by cloud basis instead, dark moody skies moving on as quickly as they appear. More reliable is the wind, churning the sea, bending the trees and blowing a fine mist of sand over the compact layer below. Having biked eastwards with ease, I’m met by a roaring resistance on my return, a fierce gust wrapping around my hat and prising it off.“This connection to the elements is part of the experience,” says Andrea. “Your entire system is challenged and pushed out of its comfort zone, something that doesn’t happen so often these days,” she adds as we make our way back to the familiarity and ease of the town.The trip was provided byTourismus Marketing Niedersachsenand theGerman National Tourist Office. Doubles atInselloft Norderneyfrom €160 and atSilt & Sandfrom €141 room-only.Book ferries through individual websites:Norderney and Juist;Spiekeroog.Explore more on these topicsGermany holidaysEurope holidaysfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: 101 uses for an empty vanilla pod | Chefs | The Guardian
description: Those expensive pods will bring their heavenly aroma to syrups, compotes, baked goods, desserts – and even your underwear drawer
Author: Anna Berrill
Headline: Those expensive pods will bring their heavenly aroma to syrups, compotes, baked goods, desserts – and even your underwear drawer
Content: Vanilla pods: a great natural scent.Photograph: Johner Images/Getty Images/Johner RFView image in fullscreenVanilla pods: a great natural scent.Photograph: Johner Images/Getty Images/Johner RFKitchen aideChefs101 uses for an empty vanilla podThose expensive pods will bring their heavenly aroma to syrups, compotes, baked goods, desserts – and even your underwear drawerAnna BerrillTue 21 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCan you do anything withvanilla pods once you’ve scraped out the seeds? They’resoexpensive.Jo, by email“I put them in my knicker drawer,” says Sarah Lemanski, owner ofNova Bakehousein Leeds. “They scent it nicely, that’s for sure.” But, culinarily speaking, and beyond custards and the like, Lemanski often uses them in vanilla syrup: “Chop the pod quite small, then dry-toast it with some of the sugar for the syrup – I use a 2:1 sugar-to-water ratio – to give it body.” The Guardian’s ownBenjamina Ebuehiis very much in agreement, and keeps a stash of said vanilla syrup in the fridge to fuel her matcha habit: “I can’t be paying £6 for oneeveryday.” (It would work well in iced coffee, too.)“Even when you’ve scraped out as much as you can from a pod, there are always some seeds left inside,” Ebuehi says, so there’s still so much flavour to be had in there. Perhaps the easiest way to keep the love alive is by sticking a spent pod in a fruit compote: “Once that’s cooled, get it in the fridge, but leave the pod in there, so it continues to seep out that flavour.” You could apply a similar tactic to jam.For Adam Sellar, executive baker atToklas Bakeryin London, it’s pod’s law to dry them out and blitz with caster sugar: “That gives a really good vanilla sugar for the likes of brioche, shortbread or any other simple biscuit.” Alternatively, grind the pods and add to salt: “That’s especially good with smoked sea salt,” Lemanski says, to add something a bit different to your next batch of chocolate chip cookies, say. “Remember that you can also freeze empty pods, then just make a big batch of vanilla sugar or vanilla salt, and gift it.”If tangy fermented kefir is part of your breakfast routine, that’s another good moment to use up an empty vanilla pod or two. “Leave them in the mix while it ferments, and they’ll lend it a gentle vanilla flavour without making it too sweet or dessert-like,” Sellar says. At the other end of the spectrum, spent pods would also be right at home in a bottle of whisky: “Don’t use a nice bottle, mind,” says Jan Ostle, ofWilsonsin Bristol. Rather, you want something “mediocre”. “Then, when you do Christmas drinks, you can be one of those really annoying people who prepared months ago.” Otherwise, he says, dry out the pods, mix with orange peel and black tea, then get the kettle on: “That would be beautiful.”Of course, brown butter (AKAbeurre noisette) can enhance the flavour of all the good things – cookies, cakes, brownies – so Lemanski would put empty pods to work in there, too. “I’ll either use the brown butter to baste brioche buns when they come out of the oven, or chill it down to solidify, then scoop on to pancakes or porridge.” Admittedly, we’re a way off porridge season at the moment, so right now Jo could do a lot worse than getting that vanilla in some good olive oil, Lemanski says: “Use it to finish desserts such as chocolate mousse, or yoghurt with raspberries and honey – it’ll make breakfast that bit more special.”Got a culinary dilemma? Emailfeast@theguardian.comExplore more on these topicsChefsKitchen aideFoodBakingHerbs and spicesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: José Pizarro’s recipe for tomato and bread salad with boquerones | Food | The Guardian
description: Spanish white pickled anchovies top this full-flavoured dish for hot summer days that’s easy to make and made for sharing
Author: José Pizarro
Headline: Spanish white pickled anchovies top this full-flavoured dish for hot summer days that’s easy to make and made for sharing
Content: Jose Pizarro’s tomato and bread salad with boquerones.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.View image in fullscreenJose Pizarro’s tomato and bread salad with boquerones.Photograph: Phoebe Pearson/The Guardian. Food styling: Hanna Miller. Prop styling: Lucy Turnbull. Food styling assistant: Thea Hudson.FoodJosé Pizarro’s recipe for tomato and bread salad with boqueronesSpanish white pickled anchovies top this full-flavoured dish that’s easy to make and made for sharingJosé PizarroTue 21 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn Spain, some of our most loved dishes come from making something special out of very little. This salad is exactly that kind of food: it’s about using what is already in the house and turning it into something generous and full-flavoured. I have always loved how these dishes bring people together, because they’re designed to be placed in the middle of the table for everyone to share. They remind me of long, relaxed lunches that slowly turn into the afternoon and evening, and where conversation matters as much as the food. Simple, honest and full of character, this is the kind of cooking that never goes out of style.Tomato and bread salad with boqueronesPrep10 minCook15 minSteep10 min+Serves4-6400g day-old white bread– sourdough, ciabatta or pane pugliese4 tbsp olive oilFlaky sea salt and freshly ground black pepper2 garlic cloves, peeled and finely sliced500g ripe tomatoes, sliced1 small cucumber, deseeded and chopped2 tbsp sherry vinegar4 tbsp extra-virgin olive oil150g boqueronesTear the bread into pieces. Heat half the oil with the garlic in a large frying pan, and fry over a low heat for 30 seconds. Add half the bread, turn up the heat and fry until golden. Tip the fried bread into a serving bowl, then add the rest of the oil to the pan and fry the remaining bread until golden. Add this to the bowl, and season with a little flaky sea salt.Add the tomatoes and cucumber to the bowl and toss. Put the vinegar and extra-virgin olive oil in a blender, add sea salt and freshly ground black pepper, blitz and pour over the salad.Toss to coat, then leave the salad to sit and steep for 10-15 minutes, so the bread soaks up all the juices. Arrange the boquerones over the top and serve.Explore more on these topicsFoodSummer food and drinkSaladFishTomatoesBreadVegetablesrecipesShareReuse this content
